topics.title: Software Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the canonical **Avizienis Taxonomy (IEEE 1028 / ISO 25010 aligned)**, the terminology surrounding software imperfections is rigorously stratified into four distinct abstractions. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects mastery of this terminology verbatim.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definitions (Memorize Verbatim for KTU Valuation)**
>
> 1. **Fault (Defect / Bug)** — The *root cause* inside the software artifact. It is a static condition in the code, model, document, or specification that *may* manifest as a failure. A fault is the "seed."
> 2. **Error (Mistake)** — The *human action* that produced the fault. The mental or typographical slip made by the developer during requirements elicitation, modeling, or coding. The error is the "moment of creation."
> 3. **Failure** — The *observable deviation* from the specified behavior when the program is executed. It is the runtime manifestation visible to the user. The failure is the "symptom."
> 4. **Malfunction** — A failure observed at the boundary of the system, distinct from internal deviations.

The **causal chain** is therefore:
$$\text{Error (human slip)} \longrightarrow \text{Fault (latent defect)} \longrightarrow \text{Failure (observable misbehavior)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Diagnostic Chain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Think of a medical diagnostic sequence.
> - A surgeon makes a *mistake* (Error) and nicks a nerve during an operation.
> - That nicked nerve is a *fault* dormant inside the patient's body.
> - When the patient later tries to walk, the leg *collapses* — the *failure* is observed.
>
> The fault existed long before the failure manifested. Similarly, a software fault may lie dormant in production code for years before an unusual input triggers the failure.

## 1.3 Software Faults in the MBSD Context

In **Model-Based Software Development (MBD)**, faults are not limited to source code. They can propagate through multiple abstraction layers:

| Layer | Typical Fault Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirements Model** | Ambiguous stakeholder intent | "System must be fast" (un-quantified) |
| **Design Model** (UML/SysML) | Incorrect state transitions | Deadlock state in statechart |
| **Formal Model** (Z, B, VDM) | Type inconsistency, schema error | $x \in \mathbb{Z}$ declared, but $\sqrt{x}$ used |
| **Transformation Rules** | Model-to-code generator bug | Wrong loop unrolling |
| **Generated Code** | Codegen template defect | Off-by-one in array bounds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *unique advantage* of MBD is that faults introduced at higher abstraction levels (requirements, formal specification) can be detected by **model checkers** *before* any code is generated. This **shifts fault detection leftward** in the development lifecycle, dramatically reducing downstream cost.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

The following reliability and fault metrics are standardized by **IEEE 1633 / IEC 61508** and appear frequently in KTU questions:

- **MTTF** — Mean Time To Failure
- **MTTR** — Mean Time To Repair / Recovery
- **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures
- **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** — faults per unit execution time
- **Availability ($A$)** — proportion of uptime
- **Reliability $R(t)$** — probability of no failure up to time $t$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Software Faults (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

### A. By Origin
1. **Specification Faults** — arise from incorrect, incomplete, or inconsistent requirements.
2. **Design Faults** — architectural or algorithmic mistakes.
3. **Coding Faults** — syntax errors, logic errors, data type mismatches.
4. **Integration Faults** — interface mismatches between modules.
5. **Test Faults** — incomplete or incorrect test cases.
6. **Configuration / Deployment Faults** — environment-specific issues.

### B. By Severity (Orthogonal Defect Classification — ODC)
- **Function** — incorrect output
- **Interface** — wrong data exchanged between components
- **Checking** — missing/incorrect validation
- **Assignment** — initialization or state-update error
- **Timing/Serialization** — race conditions, deadlocks
- **Build/Package** — compilation or linkage error

### C. By Phase of Introduction
- **Pre-release (Type A)** — detected before deployment
- **Post-release (Type B)** — escaped to field

## 2.2 Operational Fault Lifecycle in MBD

The MBD fault lifecycle is a **closed-loop refinement** cycle:

1. **Model Construction** — abstract model $M_0$ built.
2. **Formal Verification** — model checker explores state space; counterexamples = fault candidates.
3. **Fault Localization** — bisimulation or slicing narrows fault to a region.
4. **Model Refinement** — region is corrected, producing $M_1$.
5. **Code Generation** — corrected model $\rightarrow$ code; residual faults measured.
6. **Runtime Fault Tolerance** — built-in detectors and reconfiguration.

## 2.3 Software Reliability Growth Models (SRGM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board frequently tests the **Musa Basic Execution Time Model**. Master its formula.

The **Musa Basic Model** assumes fault detection follows a non-homogeneous Poisson process. The expected cumulative number of faults detected by execution time $\tau$ is:

$$m(\tau) = N \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_0 \tau}\right)$$

where:
- $N$ = total initial fault content
- $\lambda_0$ = initial fault detection rate
- $\tau$ = execution time (in CPU-hours or test-hours)

The **instantaneous failure intensity** $\lambda(\tau)$ is:

$$\lambda(\tau) = N \cdot \lambda_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda_0 \tau}$$

The **mean time to next failure** at time $\tau$ is:

$$\text{MTNF}(\tau) = \frac{1}{\lambda(\tau)} = \frac{e^{\lambda_0 \tau}}{N \lambda_0}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum required** for any KTU numerical on software faults. All symbols are **standard IEEE 1633 notation**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless probability; assumes exponential |
| 2 | Failure rate (HW analog) | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$ | Failures per hour |
| 3 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Hours |
| 4 | Steady-state Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}}$ | $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| 5 | Musa expected faults | $m(\tau) = N(1 - e^{-\lambda_0 \tau})$ | Count |
| 6 | Musa failure intensity | $\lambda(\tau) = N \lambda_0 e^{-\lambda_0 \tau}$ | Failures per unit time |
| 7 | Mean Time To Next Failure | $\text{MTNF}(\tau) = \dfrac{e^{\lambda_0 \tau}}{N \lambda_0}$ | Hours |
| 8 | Fault density (post-test) | $\text{FD} = \dfrac{\text{Remaining Faults}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Defects per KLOC |
| 9 | Hazard function | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Conditional failure rate |
| 10 | Bathtub hazard sum | $h(t) = h_I(t) + h_W(t) + h_F(t)$ | Infant mortality, wear-out, intrinsic |

> **Critical LaTeX Note for Students:** In the table above, all absolute value / conditional bars use **\dfrac** and **\frac** style fractions inside the markdown table. Never write `|x|` with raw pipes — KTU answer sheet auto-rendering breaks. Always use `\vert x \vert`.

## 2.5 The Software "Bathtub Curve" (Engineering Utility)

Unlike hardware, software reliability follows a **modified bathtub curve**:

- **Infant Mortality Phase (Burn-in):** High initial fault rate as residual defects surface. MBD verification reduces this phase.
- **Useful Life Phase:** Low, approximately constant failure intensity $\lambda(\tau)$ (Musa's assumption).
- **Wear-out Phase:** Software *does not* physically wear out; instead, *specification drift* and environment changes create a "pseudo wear-out" where residual latent faults and unmodeled scenarios rise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The bathtub concept is used in **safety-critical systems** (aerospace, nuclear) to plan release windows. MBD allows the infant-mortality phase to be *simulated exhaustively in the model* before deployment, leaving only the useful-life phase for field operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Exponential Hazard to Reliability

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU Part B (7-mark) question**. Learn the chain of substitutions, not just the final form.

**Step 1 — Start with the hazard rate definition.**
For a non-negative random variable $T$ (time to failure), the hazard function is:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constant-hazard assumption.**
For hardware (and as a simplification for software's useful-life phase), assume $h(t) = \lambda$, a constant. Substitute:

$$\lambda = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 3 — Convert the conditional probability using the law of total probability.**

$$P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t) = \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t)}{P(T \ge t)} = \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

where $F(t)$ is the cumulative distribution function and $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ is the reliability.

**Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange.**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t + \Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Replace $dF(t)/dt$ with $f(t)$ (probability density function) and note that $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$.**

$$\lambda = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{d}{dt} \ln R(t)$$

**Step 6 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**

$$\int_0^t \lambda \, d\tau = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

**Step 7 — Apply the initial condition $R(0) = 1$ (no failure at time zero).**

$$\lambda t = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 8 — Exponentiate to obtain the closed-form reliability.**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result Box:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — the **Exponential Reliability Law** used universally in KTU numericals.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) from the Exponential

**Step 1 — Definition of expected value.**

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Use the identity $E[T] = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$ (true for any non-negative $T$).**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^{\infty} = \left(0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> **Result:** $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ — the cornerstone of KTU reliability numericals.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A system exhibits a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0025$ failures per hour. Compute (a) MTTF, (b) Reliability at $t = 100$ hours, (c) MTBF if MTTR is 4 hours, (d) Availability.

### Part (a) — MTTF
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0025} = 400 \text{ hours}$$

### Part (b) — Reliability at $t = 100$ h
$$R(100) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 100} = e^{-0.0025 \times 100} = e^{-0.25}$$
$$e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$$

So $R(100) \approx 0.7788$, i.e., **77.88%** probability of survival to 100 hours.

### Part (c) — MTBF
$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 400 + 4 = 404 \text{ hours}$$

### Part (d) — Availability
$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{400}{404} \approx 0.9901$$

So $A \approx 0.9901$, i.e., **99.01% uptime**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative SRGM Summary

| Property | Musa Basic | Logarithmic | Musa-Okumoto |
|---|---|---|---|
| Expected faults $m(\tau)$ | $N(1 - e^{-\lambda_0 \tau})$ | $\dfrac{1}{\theta}\ln(1 + \theta \mu \tau)$ | $N \ln(1 + \mu \tau / N)$ |
| Failure intensity $\lambda(\tau)$ | $N \lambda_0 e^{-\lambda_0 \tau}$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{1 + \theta \mu \tau}$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{1 + \mu \tau / N}$ |
| Diminishing returns | Exponential | Logarithmic | Logarithmic |
| Concave shape of $m(\tau)$ | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| KTU typical marks | 7 | 3–5 (definition) | 3 (mention) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Software Fault Causal Chain & Lifecycle

```mermaid
    node3 -->|"causes"| node4
    node5 -->|"identifies"| node2
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX["Time tau"]:::axisNode
    axisY["Faults / Intensity"]:::axisNode
    cumM["Cumulative m(tau) = N(1 - e^(-lambda tau))"]:::cumNode
    falI["Intensity lambda(tau) = N lambda e^(-lambda tau)"]:::falNode
    asymp["Asymptote at N (Total Faults)"]:::asymNode
    peak["Peak intensity at tau = 0"]:::peakNode
    decline["Exponential decline phase"]:::declNode

    axisX --- cumM
    axisY --- falI
    cumM --> asymp
    falI --> peak
    falI --> decline
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

### Question 1. **[3 Marks]**
**Distinguish clearly between a software fault, a software error, and a software failure. Use the Avizienis taxonomy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — bullet-by-bullet for valuation clarity):**
- **(1 mark)** **Error (Mistake):** A human action that produces an incorrect result. Example: a developer typing `i <= n` instead of `i < n` while concentrating on algorithm design.
- **(1 mark)** **Fault (Defect / Bug):** The static manifestation of an error within the software artifact — the incorrect `i <= n` code residing in the source file. It is latent and dormant.
- **(1 mark)** **Failure:** The observed deviation from specified behavior when the program executes. The faulty `i <= n` causes an *out-of-bounds write* at $i = n$, leading to a crash or memory corruption at runtime.

**Causal chain** (bonus line students often add for the "extra mile" mark):
$$\text{Error (human slip)} \longrightarrow \text{Fault (latent code defect)} \longrightarrow \text{Failure (runtime crash)}$$

---

### Question 2. **[3 Marks]**
**State and explain the Bathtub Curve of software reliability. How does Model-Based Development modify the infant-mortality phase?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — explicit for valuation):**
- **(1 mark)** The Bathtub Curve plots hazard $h(t)$ versus time and has three regions: (i) **Infant Mortality** — decreasing hazard as latent defects are detected; (ii) **Useful Life** — approximately constant hazard; (iii) **Wear-out** — increasing hazard. For software, wear-out is *pseudo* — driven by specification drift, not physical decay.
- **(1 mark)** The infant-mortality phase in classical development is bridged only through extensive field testing or beta releases. Costly and time-consuming.
- **(1 mark)** In MBD, the infant-mortality phase is **simulated and resolved within the model itself** using model checkers (e.g., SPIN, NuSMV) and theorem provers. Counterexamples generated by the checker pinpoint faults before code generation, compressing the infant-mortality phase dramatically.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1, Pattern Adapted]**

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **Musa Basic Execution Time Model** for software reliability growth. Derive the expected cumulative number of failures $m(\tau)$ and the failure intensity $\lambda(\tau)$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A software system is tested with the following observed Musa parameters: total fault content $N = 200$, initial fault-detection rate $\lambda_0 = 0.04$ per CPU-hour. Compute the expected number of failures detected and the failure intensity at $\tau = 10$ CPU-hours. Also compute the Mean Time to Next Failure at that instant. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the model assumptions (1 mark).**
- The system is operated under a constant test-execution profile.
- Faults are independent and detected one at a time.
- Fault-detection rate is proportional to remaining faults.

**Step 2 — Form the first-order differential equation (2 marks).**
The number of remaining faults decreases proportionally to current failures:

$$\frac{d}{d\tau}\bigl(N - m(\tau)\bigr) = -\lambda_0 \bigl(N - m(\tau)\bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the ODE (2 marks).**

$$\int \frac{d(N - m)}{N - m} = -\int \lambda_0 \, d\tau$$

$$\ln\bigl(N - m(\tau)\bigr) = -\lambda_0 \tau + C$$

Apply $m(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C = \ln N$, so:

$$N - m(\tau) = N e^{-\lambda_0 \tau}$$

$$\boxed{m(\tau) = N\bigl(1 - e^{-\lambda_0 \tau}\bigr)}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the failure intensity (1 mark).**

$$\lambda(\tau) = \frac{dm}{d\tau} = N \lambda_0 e^{-\lambda_0 \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Block diagram (1 mark).**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    paramN["N (Total Faults)"]:::inNode
    paramL["lambda_0 (Initial Rate)"]:::inNode
    tauIn["tau (Execution Time)"]:::inNode
    coreM["Musa ODE Solver"]:::coreNode
    outM["m(tau) Expected Failures"]:::outNode
    outL["lambda(tau) Failure Intensity"]:::outNode
    outM2["MTNF(tau) = 1/lambda(tau)"]:::outNode

    paramN --> coreM
    paramL --> coreM
    tauIn --> coreM
    coreM --> outM
    coreM --> outL
    coreM --> outM2

    classDef inNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef coreNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f
    classDef outNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d
```

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $N = 200$, $\lambda_0 = 0.04$ per CPU-h, $\tau = 10$ CPU-h.

**Step 1 — Compute expected cumulative failures (2 marks).**

$$m(10) = 200 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-0.04 \times 10}\right) = 200 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-0.4}\right)$$

$$e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703$$

$$m(10) = 200 \cdot (1 - 0.6703) = 200 \cdot 0.3297 \approx 65.94 \text{ failures}$$

**Step 2 — Compute failure intensity (2 marks).**

$$\lambda(10) = 200 \cdot 0.04 \cdot e^{-0.4} = 8 \cdot 0.6703 \approx 5.3624 \text{ failures/CPU-h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute MTNF (2 marks).**

$$\text{MTNF}(10) = \frac{1}{\lambda(10)} = \frac{1}{5.3624} \approx 0.1865 \text{ CPU-h}$$

$$\text{MTNF}(10) \approx 11.19 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark).**
At $\tau = 10$ CPU-h, on average the next fault will surface in about **11.19 minutes** of further execution; this is used to plan test cycles and release criteria.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **four levels of fault tolerance** as described in the Avizienis taxonomy. With examples, discuss how Model-Based Development enables design-level fault tolerance. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A server system has MTTF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 10 hours. Compute (i) MTBF, (ii) Steady-State Availability, (iii) the Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours assuming exponential distribution, and (iv) the probability that the system will fail *before* 1000 hours. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define fault tolerance (1 mark).**
Fault tolerance is the ability of a system to continue providing service in the presence of *active faults*. Per Avizienis, fault tolerance has four sub-properties forming a hierarchy.

**Step 2 — Fault Removal (1 mark).**
Reduction of the *number and severity* of faults. In MBD: model checking removes design faults before code is generated.

**Step 3 — Fault Forecasting (1 mark).**
Estimation of the *presence, creation, and consequences* of faults. In MBD: probabilistic model checking, PRISM tool, fault-tree analysis on the formal model.

**Step 4 — Fault Prevention (1 mark).**
Prevention of fault *occurrence or introduction*. In MBD: type-checked formal specifications (Z, B) prevent entire classes of faults (e.g., type mismatches, uninitialized variables).

**Step 5 — Fault Tolerance proper (1 mark).**
Preservation of service despite *active* faults. In MBD: code generators can be instrumented to insert N-version programming or recovery blocks directly from the model specification.

**Step 6 — MBD-specific example (1 mark).**
A UML statechart can declare **alternative transitions** for exceptional conditions; the generated code automatically includes exception-handler scaffolding, eliminating manual error-prone coding.

**Step 7 — Distinction summary (1 mark).**
- *Prevention* stops faults from being created.
- *Removal* finds and eliminates existing faults.
- *Forecasting* estimates their impact.
- *Tolerance* masks their effect at runtime.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** MTTF = 5000 h, MTTR = 10 h.

**Step 1 — Compute MTBF (1.5 marks).**

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 5000 + 10 = 5010 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Steady-State Availability (1.5 marks).**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{5000}{5010} \approx 0.9980$$

So availability $\approx$ **99.80%**.

**Step 3 — Compute failure rate (0.5 mark).**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{5000} = 0.0002 \text{ failures/h}$$

**Step 4 — Reliability at $t = 1000$ h (2 marks).**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 1000} = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2}$$

$$e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$$

So $R(1000) \approx 0.8187$, i.e., **81.87%** probability of survival to 1000 h.

**Step 5 — Probability of failure before 1000 h (1.5 marks).**

$$F(1000) = 1 - R(1000) = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813$$

So the probability of failure before 1000 h $\approx$ **18.13%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Software Faults Questions**
>
> 1. **Conflating Fault and Failure (–2 marks typical):** The most common error. Remember: a fault is *latent*, a failure is *observed*. Writing "the system has a failure in the code" is technically wrong; the code has a *fault*, the execution has a *failure*.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Musa Numerical (–1 mark):** Always state units explicitly — e.g., "$\lambda_0 = 0.04$ per CPU-hour." KTU evaluators are instructed to deduct for missing units.
> 3. **Mixing up MTBF and MTTF (–1.5 marks):** MTBF includes repair time; MTTF does not. Repairable systems use MTBF; non-repairable components use MTTF. Spell out which one you are computing.
> 4. **Skipping the Causal Chain in definitions (–1 mark):** A complete fault definition MUST include the Error → Fault → Failure chain. Writing only "a bug in the code" earns partial credit at best.
> 5. **Using raw pipe `|` for absolute value in tables (–0.5 mark):** Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ in LaTeX. Markdown tables break on raw pipes.
> 6. **Confusing Musa failure intensity with hardware $\lambda$ (–1 mark):** Musa's $\lambda(\tau)$ is *decreasing* (improving reliability), whereas hardware $\lambda$ is *constant*. State this distinction when relevant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Error** = human slip; **Fault** = latent defect; **Failure** = observed misbehavior.
- [ ] **Causal chain:** Error $\rightarrow$ Fault $\rightarrow$ Failure.
- [ ] Reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is for the *useful-life* (constant-hazard) phase.
- [ ] $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ for exponential distribution.
- [ ] $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$; **Availability** = $\text{MTTF} / \text{MTBF}$.
- [ ] **Musa Basic Model:** $m(\tau) = N(1 - e^{-\lambda_0 \tau})$ and $\lambda(\tau) = N \lambda_0 e^{-\lambda_0 \tau}$.
- [ ] **Musa MTNF** at time $\tau$: $\text{MTNF}(\tau) = e^{\lambda_0 \tau} / (N \lambda_0)$.
- [ ] MBD shifts fault detection **leftward** — from runtime to model-check time.
- [ ] Software **does not physically wear out**; it undergoes *specification drift*.
- [ ] **Bathtub Curve phases:** Infant mortality $\rightarrow$ Useful life $\rightarrow$ Wear-out.
- [ ] **Avizienis Fault-Tolerance Hierarchy:** Prevention $\rightarrow$ Removal $\rightarrow$ Forecasting $\rightarrow$ Tolerance.
- [ ] Always carry **units** in Musa and reliability numericals.
- [ ] Use $\vert \cdot \vert$, never raw `|`, for absolute values in LaTeX tables.
- [ ] In MBD, faults can exist in *requirements, design, formal, transformation,* and *generated-code* layers.
- [ ] Model checkers (SPIN, NuSMV, PRISM) are the **primary fault detectors** in MBD.
- [ ] Failure intensity $\lambda(\tau)$ in Musa *decreases* with $\tau$ — opposite to hardware.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Model Checking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Model Checking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Introduction to Model Checking

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Model Checking** is an automated, formal verification technique used in software and hardware engineering to systematically verify whether a finite-state model of a system satisfies a given correctness property (specification). It was pioneered by **Edmund M. Clarke**, **E. Allen Emerson**, and **Joseph Sifakis**, who were jointly awarded the **2007 ACM A.M. Turing Award** for their seminal work on this technique.

Formally, model checking is the algorithmic problem of deciding whether a structure $M$ (the *model* of the system) satisfies a formula $\varphi$ (the *specification*), written as:

$$M \models \varphi$$

The structure $M$ is a **Kripke Structure** (a finite-state transition system with labeled states), and $\varphi$ is expressed in a **temporal logic** such as **LTL** (Linear Temporal Logic), **CTL** (Computation Tree Logic), or **CTL\***.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — What is Model Checking?**
> Model checking is a *push-button* verification technology. Unlike theorem proving (which requires human-guided proofs), model checking is fully automatic — you provide the model and the property, and the tool either confirms correctness or returns a counterexample (a concrete execution trace that violates the property).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction — Model Checking vs. Testing**
> - **Testing** checks specific executions of the system (sample-based verification).
> - **Model Checking** checks *all possible executions* (exhaustive verification).
> Testing can find bugs but cannot prove their absence. Model checking can prove the *absence* of certain classes of bugs in the model.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blueprint Inspector"

Imagine you are an architect who has just received a complex blueprint of a new airport terminal. Before construction begins, you want to verify two things:

1. **Will every passenger eventually reach a gate?** (Liveness property)
2. **Will two passengers ever be assigned the same seat at the same time?** (Safety/Mutual Exclusion property)

You *could* simulate thousands of random passenger paths through the blueprint (testing). However, model checking is like hiring a **hyper-fast, infinitely patient inspector** who:

- Builds a **mathematical miniature** of the airport (the *Kripke structure*).
- Translates your two questions into **formal logic** (e.g., $\forall \text{paths}, \Diamond \, \text{at\_gate}$).
- **Exhaustively walks through every possible path** in the miniature.
- Returns a green checkmark ✅ if all paths satisfy the rules, or hands you a **map of the exact wrong path** (a *counterexample*) if any path violates them.

> [!NOTE]
> This inspector never gets tired, but has one weakness: if the airport is too big (millions of states), the inspection takes forever or runs out of memory. This is the famous **State Space Explosion Problem**, which we will discuss in Section 1.4.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Model Checking

The model checking ecosystem rests on three foundational pillars:

| Pillar | Mathematical Object | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Modeling** | Kripke Structure $M = (S, S_0, R, AP, L)$ | Represents the finite-state behavior of the system. |
| **Specification** | Temporal Logic Formula $\varphi$ (LTL / CTL) | Expresses the desired correctness property. |
| **Verification** | Decision Procedure (Model-Checking Algorithm) | Automates the check $M \models \varphi$. |

---

## 1.4 Strengths and Limitations

| Strengths | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Fully automatic (no expert intervention) | **State Space Explosion** — number of states grows exponentially. |
| Produces counterexamples on failure (great debugging aid) | Restricted to **finite-state** systems (infinite-state requires abstraction). |
| Applies to both software and hardware | Property must be expressed in formal logic (learning curve). |
| High industrial adoption (Intel, Microsoft, Amazon, NASA) | The model is an abstraction — "you verify the model, not the system." |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — State Complexity**
> A system with $n$ boolean variables has up to $\mathbf{2^n}$ reachable states. A modest 50-variable system can thus yield $\approx 1.12 \times 10^{15}$ states — far exceeding practical memory limits. This is why techniques like **abstraction**, **symmetry reduction**, and **partial-order reduction** are central to modern model checking research.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The Model Checking Workflow (Operational Phases)

The end-to-end model checking process is a four-phase pipeline:

1. **Modeling Phase**
   - The engineer builds a finite-state model of the system under verification (SUV).
   - The model is a **Kripke structure** capturing all relevant behaviors of the design.
   - Concurrency, communication, and control flow are encoded as transitions.

2. **Specification Phase**
   - The desired properties are expressed in a temporal logic:
     - **LTL** (Linear Temporal Logic) — properties over single execution paths.
     - **CTL** (Computation Tree Logic) — branching-time properties over the entire computation tree.
     - **CTL\*** — a superset that combines both.

3. **Verification (Run) Phase**
   - The model checker (e.g., **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, **UPPAAL**, **CBMC**) performs an exhaustive state-space exploration.
   - It systematically verifies $M \models \varphi$ using graph algorithms (DFS, BFS, fixpoint computation).

4. **Result Analysis Phase**
   - If the property holds → ✅ confirmation is returned.
   - If the property fails → ❌ a **counterexample** (a concrete execution path violating $\varphi$) is generated for debugging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Analogy**
> Think of the four phases as: *(1) building a digital twin, (2) writing a contract, (3) running an audit, (4) reading the audit report.* If the audit fails, the report does not just say "wrong" — it shows exactly *which* clause was broken and *where*.

---

## 2.2 Kripke Structure — The Heart of the Model

A **Kripke structure** is the canonical finite-state model used in model checking. It is a 5-tuple:

$$M = (S,\ S_0,\ R,\ AP,\ L)$$

| Component | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Finite set of states | $S$ | All possible configurations of the system. |
| Initial states | $S_0 \subseteq S$ | States where execution begins. |
| Transition relation | $R \subseteq S \times S$ | All possible state changes (assumed *total* — every state has at least one successor). |
| Atomic propositions | $AP$ | Boolean variables describing state properties (e.g., `x = 1`, `lock = held`). |
| Labeling function | $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ | Maps each state to the set of atomic propositions true in that state. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "total" transitions?**
> A Kripke structure is required to be **total** (every state has at least one outgoing transition). If a state has no successor, an artificial **sink (self-loop) state** is added. This guarantees every infinite path can be extended, which is essential for defining *temporal* properties.

---

## 2.3 Temporal Logic — Specifying What the System Must Do

Temporal logic extends classical propositional logic with operators that reason about **time** (the order of states along execution paths).

### 2.3.1 Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) — Single-Path View

LTL formulas are evaluated along a single infinite path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$:

| Operator | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Next** | $X\,\varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds in the *next* state ($s_1$). |
| **Finally / Eventually** | $F\,\varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds in *some future* state of the path. |
| **Globally / Always** | $G\,\varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds in *every* future state of the path. |
| **Until** | $\varphi\,U\,\psi$ | $\varphi$ holds continuously *until* $\psi$ becomes true. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful LTL Abbreviations**
> - $F\,\varphi \equiv \text{true}\,U\,\varphi$
> - $G\,\varphi \equiv \neg F\,\neg\varphi$

### 2.3.2 Computation Tree Logic (CTL) — Branching-Time View

CTL prefixes every temporal operator with a **path quantifier**:

| Path Quantifier | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $A$ | "**For All** paths from the current state…" |
| $E$ | "**There Exists** a path from the current state…" |

Basic CTL operators (must appear in pairs: a path quantifier + a temporal operator):

| CTL Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $AX\,\varphi$ | On all paths, $\varphi$ holds in the next state. |
| $EX\,\varphi$ | There exists a path where $\varphi$ holds in the next state. |
| $AF\,\varphi$ | On all paths, $\varphi$ eventually holds. |
| $EF\,\varphi$ | There exists a path where $\varphi$ eventually holds. |
| $AG\,\varphi$ | On all paths, $\varphi$ holds globally. |
| $EG\,\varphi$ | There exists a path where $\varphi$ holds globally. |
| $A[\varphi\,U\,\psi]$ | On all paths, $\varphi$ holds until $\psi$ holds. |
| $E[\varphi\,U\,\psi]$ | There exists a path where $\varphi$ holds until $\psi$ holds. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Logics?**
> - **LTL** is best for properties about a *single* execution (e.g., "every request is eventually followed by a response" — $G(\text{req} \rightarrow F\,\text{resp})$).
> - **CTL** is best for *branching* properties (e.g., "from every state, it is *possible* to reach the goal" — $EF\,\text{goal}$).
> - **CTL\*** is the expressive superset; LTL and CTL are *incomparable* subsets of CTL\*.

---

## 2.4 The Verification Algorithm — How the Check is Performed

For a Kripke structure $M$ and a CTL formula $\varphi$:

1. **Parse** $\varphi$ and decompose it into sub-formulas (syntactic tree).
2. For each sub-formula $\psi_i$, mark every state in $S$ where $\psi_i$ holds — this is a **fixpoint computation** using operators $\exists \square$, $\forall \square$, $\exists \Diamond$, $\forall \Diamond$.
3. Propagate results bottom-up until the root formula is evaluated.
4. Check whether **all** initial states $S_0$ are labeled with $\varphi$. If yes → $M \models \varphi$.

For LTL, the algorithm is conceptually different: it builds a **Büchi automaton** for $\neg \varphi$ and checks for the emptiness of $M \times A_{\neg \varphi}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - **Hardware design**: Intel, AMD, and IBM use model checking (e.g., formal verification of floating-point units, cache coherence protocols) to catch design bugs *before* fabrication — saving millions in respin costs.
> - **Software**: Microsoft uses **SLAM** (predecessor of Static Driver Verifier) to verify Windows device drivers; Amazon uses **TLA+** to verify distributed systems like DynamoDB and S3.
> - **Embedded / Safety-Critical**: UPPAAL is used to verify real-time controllers in automotive and avionics systems (DO-178C compliance).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Quick Reference

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $M = (S, S_0, R, AP, L)$ | Kripke structure (5-tuple) | Universal model format. |
| $M \models \varphi$ | "$M$ satisfies $\varphi$" | The central model-checking decision. |
| $\vert S \vert \leq \prod_{i=1}^{n} d_i$ | Upper bound on state count | Where $d_i$ is the domain size of the $i$-th state variable. |
| $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ | An infinite execution path | Foundation of LTL semantics. |
| $G\,\varphi \equiv \neg F \neg \varphi$ | LTL duality | Convert "always" to "never eventually not". |
| $F\,\varphi \equiv \text{true}\,U\,\varphi$ | LTL "Finally" via "Until" | Useful for LTL-to-Büchi translation. |
| $AG\,\varphi$ | Invariant property | Most common safety property. |
| $AF\,\varphi$ | Guarantee property | Most common liveness property. |
| $AG(\text{req} \rightarrow AF\,\text{resp})$ | Request-response (liveness) | Standard in protocol verification. |
| $AG\,\neg(\text{crit}_1 \wedge \text{crit}_2)$ | Mutual exclusion | Standard in concurrency verification. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars (`|`) inside table cells** — they break Markdown table syntax. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in math mode. *(This very row demonstrates the safe encoding.)*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Worked Example: Model Checking a Mutual-Exclusion Protocol

To make the theory concrete, we trace through the complete model checking of a **two-process mutual-exclusion system**. This is the canonical example used in Clarke & Emerson's original 1981 paper and remains the KTU gold standard.

---

## 3.1 System Description

Two processes, $P_1$ and $P_2$, share a single critical resource. Each process cycles through three states:

- $N$ — Non-critical section (idle, doing local work).
- $T$ — Trying section (requesting the lock).
- $C$ — Critical section (holding the lock, accessing the shared resource).

The legal transitions for each process are:

$$N \rightarrow T \rightarrow C \rightarrow N$$

The combined system state is the pair $(p_1, p_2)$ where $p_i \in \{N, T, C\}$. The lock-granting policy is **asymmetric**: if $P_1$ is in $C$, $P_2$ stays in $T$; if $P_2$ is in $C$, $P_1$ stays in $T$. Either process may enter $T$ from $N$ at any time.

---

## 3.2 Building the Kripke Structure

The 5-tuple is:

$$
M = (S,\ S_0,\ R,\ AP,\ L)
$$

with:
- $S$ = all reachable pairs of process states.
- $S_0 = \{(N, N)\}$ — both processes start in the non-critical section.
- $R$ = the transition relation enumerated below.
- $AP = \{P_1\text{.C},\ P_2\text{.C},\ P_1\text{.T},\ P_2\text{.T}\}$ — atomic propositions indicating where each process is.
- $L(s)$ labels each state with the propositions true in that state.

### Step 1 — Enumerate reachable states

Starting from $(N, N)$, we perform a breadth-first expansion. At each state, every process may move forward by one step *unless blocked by the other holding the lock*.

The reachable set is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \{\, &(N,N),\ (T,N),\ (C,N),\ (N,T),\ (N,C),\ (T,T), \\
&(C,T),\ (T,C)\,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives $\vert S \vert = 8$ states — small enough for manual verification.

### Step 2 — Enumerate transitions

For every state, we list each process's possible next move:

| From State | Allowed Next States | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $(N,N)$ | $(T,N),\ (N,T)$ | Either process may enter the trying section. |
| $(T,N)$ | $(C,N),\ (T,T)$ | $P_1$ enters $C$; $P_2$ may enter $T$. |
| $(C,N)$ | $(N,N)$ | $P_1$ exits $C$; $P_2$ is still in $N$. |
| $(N,T)$ | $(T,T),\ (N,C)$ | $P_2$ enters $C$; $P_1$ may enter $T$. |
| $(N,C)$ | $(N,N)$ | $P_2$ exits $C$; $P_1$ is still in $N$. |
| $(T,T)$ | $(C,T),\ (T,C)$ | Either process may win the lock (nondeterminism). |
| $(C,T)$ | $(T,T)$ | $P_1$ exits $C$; $P_2$ is still trying. |
| $(T,C)$ | $(T,T)$ | $P_2$ exits $C$; $P_1$ is still trying. |

So the transition relation $R$ consists of the 10 edges listed in the rightmost column above.

### Step 3 — Label each state

The labeling function $L$ assigns the true atomic propositions:

| State | $L(\text{state})$ |
|---|---|
| $(N,N)$ | $\varnothing$ |
| $(T,N)$ | $\{P_1\text{.T}\}$ |
| $(C,N)$ | $\{P_1\text{.C}\}$ |
| $(N,T)$ | $\{P_2\text{.T}\}$ |
| $(N,C)$ | $\{P_2\text{.C}\}$ |
| $(T,T)$ | $\{P_1\text{.T},\ P_2\text{.T}\}$ |
| $(C,T)$ | $\{P_1\text{.C},\ P_2\text{.T}\}$ |
| $(T,C)$ | $\{P_1\text{.T},\ P_2\text{.C}\}$ |

---

## 3.3 Specifying the Properties

We express three standard correctness properties in CTL:

### Property 1 — Mutual Exclusion (Safety)

"It is never the case that both processes are in the critical section simultaneously."

$$
\varphi_1 \;\equiv\; AG\,\neg(P_1\text{.C} \wedge P_2\text{.C})
$$

### Property 2 — Liveness (Progress)

"If a process is in the trying section, it will eventually enter the critical section."

$$
\varphi_2 \;\equiv\; AG\,(P_1\text{.T} \rightarrow AF\,P_1\text{.C}) \;\wedge\; AG\,(P_2\text{.T} \rightarrow AF\,P_2\text{.C})
$$

### Property 3 — Deadlock-Freedom

"There is no deadlock state — a state from which no further progress is possible."

$$
\varphi_3 \;\equiv\; AG\,EF\,(P_1\text{.C} \vee P_2\text{.C})
$$

---

## 3.4 Executing the Verification Algorithm

We now execute the model checking algorithm for $\varphi_1$. The fixpoint computation proceeds in stages.

### Stage 1 — Atomic proposition

By definition of $L$, no state has both $P_1\text{.C}$ and $P_2\text{.C}$ true simultaneously (looking at the table in §3.2 Step 3, $P_1\text{.C}$ and $P_2\text{.C}$ never co-occur). Hence:

$$
\text{Sat}(\neg(P_1\text{.C} \wedge P_2\text{.C})) \;=\; S \quad \text{(all 8 states satisfy it)}
$$

### Stage 2 — $AG\,\psi$ fixpoint

For a formula $AG\,\psi$, the satisfying set is the **greatest fixpoint** of the operator:

$$
\tau(Z) \;=\; \psi \;\cap\; \text{Pre}_\forall(Z)
$$

where $\text{Pre}_\forall(Z) = \{s \in S \mid \forall s' \in R(s),\ s' \in Z\}$.

We compute iteratively, starting with $Z_0 = S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= S = \{(N,N), (T,N), (C,N), (N,T), (N,C), (T,T), (C,T), (T,C)\} \\
Z_1 &= \tau(Z_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

We must exclude any state with a successor outside $Z_0$. But $Z_0 = S$, and *every* state has a successor in $S$ (the structure is total). Therefore:

$$
Z_1 \;=\; S
$$

The fixpoint is reached in one step: $Z^* = S$. Thus:

$$
\text{Sat}(AG\,\neg(P_1\text{.C} \wedge P_2\text{.C})) \;=\; S
$$

### Stage 3 — Check the initial state

The initial state $(N,N) \in \text{Sat}(\varphi_1) = S$. Therefore:

$$
M \models \varphi_1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion**: The mutual-exclusion property is satisfied. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary**
> - **Modeling** abstracted the two-process system as an 8-state Kripke structure.
> - **Specification** translated "mutual exclusion" into $AG\,\neg(P_1\text{.C} \wedge P_2\text{.C})$.
> - **Verification** confirmed via a one-step greatest fixpoint that all states (and in particular the initial state) satisfy the formula.
> - If any state had violated $\varphi_1$, the tool would have produced a *counterexample trace* (a path of states) leading to it.

---

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Properties vs. Outcomes

| Property | Formula | Result | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutual Exclusion | $AG\,\neg(P_1\text{.C} \wedge P_2\text{.C})$ | ✅ Holds | No two processes ever share the critical section. |
| Liveness (P1) | $AG\,(P_1\text{.T} \rightarrow AF\,P_1\text{.C})$ | ✅ Holds | $P_1$ is not starved. |
| Liveness (P2) | $AG\,(P_2\text{.T} \rightarrow AF\,P_2\text{.C})$ | ✅ Holds | $P_2$ is not starved. |
| Deadlock-Freedom | $AG\,EF\,(P_1\text{.C} \vee P_2\text{.C})$ | ✅ Holds | Some process can always reach $C$. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Model Checking Workflow (Process Flow)

This diagram captures the canonical model checking pipeline — from abstract system to verified result — including the critical feedback loop on counterexample generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System Under Verification"] --> B["Modeling Phase"]
    B --> C["Kripke Structure M"]
    D["Requirements Spec"] --> E["Specification Phase"]
    E --> F["Temporal Logic Formula phi"]
    C --> G["Verification Engine"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"M models phi?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["VERIFIED - Property Holds"]
    H -- "No" --> J["Counterexample Trace"]
    J --> K["Debug and Refine Model"]
    K --> B
    I --> L["Document and Deploy"]
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- The **left branch** (top-down) shows the *forward* path: model the system, specify the property, and verify.
- The **right branch** shows the *output*: a verdict and, on failure, a counterexample that drives model refinement.
- The **feedback loop** from the counterexample back to the modeling phase is the most powerful feature of model checking — every false result is *actionable*.

---

## 4.2 Kripke Structure of the Mutual-Exclusion Example

This diagram visualizes the 8-state Kripke structure built in Section 3. State labels are the *atomic propositions* true in that state.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0(["s0: N N"])
    s1(["s1: P1.T"])
    s2(["s2: P1.C"])
    s3(["s3: P2.T"])
    s4(["s4: P2.C"])
    s5(["s5: P1.T P2.T"])
    s6(["s6: P1.C P2.T"])
    s7(["s7: P1.T P2.C"])

    s0 -- "P1 moves to T" --> s1
    s0 -- "P2 moves to T" --> s3
    s1 -- "P1 enters C" --> s2
    s1 -- "P2 moves to T" --> s5
    s2 -- "P1 exits C" --> s0
    s3 -- "P1 moves to T" --> s5
    s3 -- "P2 enters C" --> s4
    s4 -- "P2 exits C" --> s0
    s5 -- "P1 wins lock" --> s6
    s5 -- "P2 wins lock" --> s7
    s6 -- "P1 exits C" --> s5
    s7 -- "P2 exits C" --> s5
```

**How to read the diagram**:
- Each **node** (`s0`–`s7`) is a reachable system state. The label after the colon shows which atomic propositions hold.
- Each **directed edge** is a transition in $R$. Edges are annotated with the *event* (process movement) that triggers them.
- The structure has **no self-loops** on the two non-final states (`s6`, `s7`) — they return to the contention state `s5`, modelling the exit-from-critical-section behavior.
- The single sink in this finite example is the set of all 8 states (no state is dead — deadlock-freedom is satisfied).

---

## 4.3 Classification of Model Checking Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Temporal Properties"]
    P --> S["Safety Properties"]
    P --> L["Liveness Properties"]
    P --> F["Fairness Properties"]

    S --> S1["AG phi - Nothing bad ever happens"]
    S --> S2["AG not bad - Mutual exclusion"]

    L --> L1["AF phi - Something good eventually happens"]
    L --> L2["AG req to AF resp - Request-response"]

    F --> F1["Fairness Constraints - Every process gets a turn"]
    F --> F2["Used to rule out unrealistic counterexamples"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why fairness matters**: Without fairness assumptions, a model checker may flag a property as *violated* by an unrealistic path (e.g., where a process is *infinitely* denied the lock). Fairness constraints — **unconditional**, **strong**, or **weak** — restrict the verifier to *realistic* execution paths.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> - Part A: 2 questions × **3 marks** = 6 marks (Short answer / definition).
> - Part B: 1 question × **14 marks** (with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B) = 14 marks.
> - Module-level internal choice applies — students answer EITHER Q-A OR Q-B.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**Define model checking. List any two advantages of model checking over traditional testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **(1 mark)** **Model checking** is an automatic, formal verification technique that exhaustively checks whether a finite-state model $M$ of a system satisfies a temporal-logic specification $\varphi$, written $M \models \varphi$.
- **(1 mark)** *Advantage 1 — Exhaustiveness*: It checks **all possible execution paths**, whereas testing checks only sampled executions.
- **(1 mark)** *Advantage 2 — Counterexamples*: On failure, it produces a **concrete counterexample trace** pinpointing the bug, which testing cannot guarantee.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**What is a Kripke structure? List its components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **(1 mark)** A **Kripke structure** is a finite-state transition system used as the standard semantic model in model checking. It represents all possible behaviors of a reactive system.
- **(2 marks)** It is a 5-tuple $M = (S,\ S_0,\ R,\ AP,\ L)$, where:
  - $S$ — finite set of **states**,
  - $S_0 \subseteq S$ — set of **initial states**,
  - $R \subseteq S \times S$ — **transition relation** (assumed total),
  - $AP$ — set of **atomic propositions**,
  - $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ — **labeling function** mapping each state to the propositions true in it.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note**: KTU ESE Module Internal Choice. Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 — Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the three main temporal operators in LTL — $X$, $F$, $G$ — with one example formula each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **state space explosion problem** in model checking. List and briefly explain any **two techniques** used to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — LTL Temporal Operators (7 marks)**:

LTL is a *linear-time* logic: formulas are interpreted over a single infinite path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$

1. **Next operator — $X\,\varphi$** *(2 marks)*
   - $X\,\varphi$ holds at position $i$ along $\pi$ iff $\varphi$ holds at position $i+1$.
   - Formally: $\pi \models X\,\varphi \iff \pi^{i+1} \models \varphi$.
   - **Example**: $X\,(x = 1)$ — "in the next state, the variable $x$ will equal 1".

2. **Finally / Eventually — $F\,\varphi$** *(2 marks)*
   - $F\,\varphi$ holds at position $i$ iff there exists some $j \geq i$ such that $\varphi$ holds at $j$.
   - Formally: $\pi \models F\,\varphi \iff \exists j \geq i,\ \pi^j \models \varphi$.
   - **Example**: $F\,(\text{error})$ — "the system will eventually enter an error state".

3. **Globally / Always — $G\,\varphi$** *(2 marks)*
   - $G\,\varphi$ holds at position $i$ iff $\varphi$ holds at every position $j \geq i$.
   - Formally: $\pi \models G\,\varphi \iff \forall j \geq i,\ \pi^j \models \varphi$.
   - **Example**: $G\,(P_1\text{.C} \rightarrow \neg P_2\text{.C})$ — "globally, if process 1 is in the critical section, process 2 is not".

4. **Important duality** *(1 mark)*:
   $$G\,\varphi \;\equiv\; \neg F\,\neg\varphi \quad \text{and} \quad F\,\varphi \;\equiv\; \neg G\,\neg\varphi$$
   This duality is the foundation of many LTL-to-automata translations.

**(b) — State Space Explosion (7 marks)**:

1. **Problem statement** *(2 marks)*:
   The number of states in the Kripke structure grows **exponentially** with the number of concurrent components, variables, or data values. For $n$ boolean variables, the state space has up to $2^n$ states. A system with just 50 boolean variables yields $> 10^{15}$ states — far exceeding the memory of any current computer. This makes naive exhaustive verification infeasible for real-world systems.

2. **Mitigation Technique 1 — Symbolic Model Checking using BDDs** *(2.5 marks)*:
   Instead of enumerating states individually, **Binary Decision Diagrams (BDDs)** represent the transition relation and reachable-state sets as *compact Boolean functions*. Pioneered by **Ken McMillan** (and later **Bryant**), this approach allowed the verification of industrial circuits with $> 10^{20}$ states. Tools: **NuSMV**, **VIS**.

3. **Mitigation Technique 2 — Partial-Order Reduction** *(2.5 marks)*:
   In concurrent systems, many interleavings of independent transitions are *equivalent* with respect to a given property. Partial-order reduction exploits this by considering only one representative from each equivalence class, drastically reducing the explored state space. Tools: **SPIN** uses this technique extensively via its `never` claim mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Other techniques** (for breadth, not required for full marks): **Abstraction** (modeling away irrelevant details), **Symmetry Reduction** (exploiting identical components), **Counterexample-Guided Abstraction Refinement — CEGAR** (iterative abstraction refinement), and **Bounded Model Checking (BMC)** using SAT solvers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **general model checking process**. Describe each phase briefly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **LTL** and **CTL** with suitable examples. Show that CTL and LTL are *incomparable* in expressive power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Model Checking Process (7 marks)**:

The block diagram of the model checking process has **four phases**:

| Phase | Input | Output | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Modeling** | System description (code / circuit) | Kripke structure $M$ | Build a finite-state model capturing all relevant behaviors. |
| **2. Specification** | Property in natural language | Temporal logic formula $\varphi$ | Translate the property into LTL / CTL. |
| **3. Verification** | $M$ and $\varphi$ | TRUE / FALSE + counterexample | Run the model checker algorithm. |
| **4. Analysis** | Result | Verdict | If FALSE, debug using the counterexample. If TRUE, proceed with confidence. |

**Block diagram (textual representation for answer script)**:
```
[System] → (Modeling) → [Kripke M]   ─┐
                                       ├→ (Verification) → {YES / NO + Counterexample}
[Property] → (Specification) → [φ]   ─┘
```

*(If examiners expect a Mermaid / hand-drawn diagram, the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes can be reproduced verbatim.)*

**(b) — LTL vs. CTL (7 marks)**:

| Aspect | LTL | CTL |
|---|---|---|
| **Time view** | Linear (single path) | Branching (computation tree) |
| **Operator structure** | Temporal operators only | Path quantifier + temporal operator (mandatory pairing) |
| **Typical use** | "What happens on *this* execution?" | "What is *possible* / *inevitable* across all executions?" |
| **Example** | $G\,(\text{req} \rightarrow F\,\text{resp})$ | $AG\,(P_1\text{.T} \rightarrow AF\,P_1\text{.C})$ |
| **Expressibility** | $F\,G\,p$ (eventually permanently $p$) — valid in LTL | $AG\,EF\,p$ (always possibly $p$) — valid in CTL but **not** expressible in LTL |

**Proof of Incomparability** *(3 marks)*:
- **LTL property not in CTL**: $F\,G\,p$ — "eventually $p$ holds forever". This is valid LTL but cannot be expressed in CTL.
- **CTL property not in LTL**: $AG\,EF\,p$ — "on every path, there is always a path where $p$ eventually holds". This is valid CTL but cannot be expressed in LTL.
- Therefore, neither logic is a subset of the other → **LTL ⊄ CTL and CTL ⊄ LTL**. They are **incomparable** subsets of the larger logic **CTL\***.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on Model-Checking Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing LTL and CTL syntax** *(−2 marks)*: Writing "$G\,\varphi$" or "$F\,q$" without the mandatory path quantifier in CTL. *Remember*: in CTL, every temporal operator must be prefixed by $A$ or $E$.
>
> 2. **Mixing Kripke structure components** *(−1 to −2 marks)*: Forgetting that the transition relation $R$ must be **total** (every state has a successor). Forgetting to include the labeling function $L$ in the 5-tuple.
>
> 3. **Writing $\vert S \vert$ in a Markdown / answer-script table cell without escaping** *(−0.5 marks if scripting format is being graded)*: Use $\mid S \mid$ or $\vert S \vert$ in math mode. In plain prose, write "the cardinality of S".
>
> 4. **Failing to state both halves of LTL/CTL incomparability** *(−1.5 marks)*: To prove incomparability, you must exhibit **one** LTL property not in CTL **and** one CTL property not in LTL. Showing only one direction is *incomplete*.
>
> 5. **Omitting the counterexample in failed verification** *(−1 mark)*: Whenever $M \not\models \varphi$, the model checker's most valuable output is the counterexample trace. Examiners reward students who mention its debugging role.
>
> 6. **Conflating model checking with theorem proving** *(−1 mark)*: Model checking is *automatic* and *finite-state*; theorem proving is *interactive* and works on *infinite-state* systems. Examiners look for this distinction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Model checking** = automatic verification of a finite-state Kripke structure $M$ against a temporal-logic specification $\varphi$, written $M \models \varphi$.
- The three pillars are **Modeling**, **Specification**, and **Verification**.
- A **Kripke structure** is the 5-tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, AP, L)$ with $R$ total.
- **LTL** = linear-time temporal logic (path quantifier implicit, single path). Operators: $X, F, G, U$.
- **CTL** = branching-time computation tree logic. Operators must be paired: $A$/$E$ with $X, F, G, U$.
- **Safety properties** use $AG\,\varphi$; **liveness properties** use $AF\,\varphi$ or $G(\text{req} \rightarrow F\,\text{resp})$.
- **Fairness** is required to rule out unrealistic counterexamples in liveness verification.
- **State space explosion** is the central scalability challenge — mitigated by BDDs, partial-order reduction, abstraction, symmetry reduction, and bounded model checking.
- Model checking is widely used in **hardware design (Intel, AMD)**, **driver verification (Microsoft SLAM)**, **distributed systems (Amazon TLA+)**, and **safety-critical embedded systems (UPPAAL)**.
- The **2007 Turing Award** recognized Clarke, Emerson, and Sifakis for founding model checking — a *must-mention* historical point in KTU answers.
- Always pair model-checking answers with a **counterexample discussion** to demonstrate full conceptual mastery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Automated Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Automated Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automated Testing** is the practice of using specialized software tools, frameworks, and scripts to execute test cases automatically, compare actual outcomes against expected outcomes, and report results — without manual human intervention during execution. In the context of **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, automated testing leverages executable models (state machines, UML diagrams, formal specifications) to automatically generate, derive, or drive test cases, thereby validating the conformance of the software under test (SUT) to its specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Automated Testing is the application of software engineering tools and methodologies to control the execution of tests, the comparison of actual outputs with predicted outputs, and the reporting of results, with the aim of increasing testing efficiency, repeatability, and coverage while reducing human effort and error."*

The **IEEE Standard 829-2008** and the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board) Syllabus** form the primary global reference frameworks adopted by KTU for testing-related questions.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Test Case** | A set of preconditions, inputs, actions, and expected results developed to verify a specific requirement. |
| **Test Suite** | A collection of test cases grouped for execution. |
| **Test Oracle** | A mechanism that determines whether a test passed or failed by comparing actual vs. expected output. |
| **SUT (System Under Test)** | The software artifact being tested. |
| **Test Harness** | The supporting code/utilities required to run automated tests (drivers, stubs, fixtures). |
| **Regression Testing** | Re-running existing tests after a change to ensure no new defects are introduced. |
| **Test Coverage** | The percentage of code paths, branches, or requirements exercised by a test suite. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Car Inspector"

Imagine a car manufacturing factory:

- **Manual Testing** is like a human mechanic driving each car off the assembly line, manually checking the brakes, lights, engine sound, and dashboard. It works, but it's slow, expensive, inconsistent, and gets tired by the 500th car.
- **Automated Testing** is like installing a **robotic inspection tunnel** at the end of the assembly line. The robot runs the same precise sequence — checks brake pressure, beams light sensors at the headlights, scans for engine vibration patterns — on **every single car, 24/7, with zero fatigue**. If anything deviates from spec, the robot flags the car and ejects it from the line.

Just like the robot inspection tunnel can be **reused for every new car model** by simply updating the inspection script, automated test scripts can be reused across multiple versions of the same software.

## 1.4 Why Automated Testing? (KTU High-Yield Motivation)

> [!NOTE]
> The "Why" of automated testing is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU ESE and is graded very strictly for clarity of reasons.

1. **Speed**: Tests that take hours manually complete in seconds/minutes.
2. **Repeatability**: Same script runs identically every time → eliminates human error.
3. **Reusability**: One test script can be reused across hundreds of builds.
4. **Coverage**: Enables execution of thousands of test cases impractical to run manually.
5. **Continuous Integration (CI)**: Enabler for **DevOps** — runs on every commit.
6. **Cost-Effectiveness (Long-term)**: High initial cost, low marginal cost.
7. **Early Bug Detection**: Aligns with **Shift-Left Testing** philosophy.
8. **Reliable Regression**: Critical for iterative/agile/MBSD workflows.

## 1.5 When to Automize vs. When NOT to Automize

| Automate ✅ | Do NOT Automate ❌ |
|------------|---------------------|
| Regression test suites | One-time/throwaway tests |
| Data-driven repetitive tests | Tests requiring subjective human judgement (UX, aesthetics) |
| Performance/load/stress tests | Tests with frequently changing requirements (UI prototypes) |
| Tests run across multiple environments | Exploratory testing |
| Smoke and sanity tests | Ad-hoc usability tests |
| API and integration tests | Tests that yield no predictable test oracle |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Test Automation Pyramid (Mike Cohn's Model)

This is the **single most important diagram** in software testing and is examined almost every year in KTU.

The pyramid is divided into **three (or four) layers**:

```
           /\
          /  \         🔺 UI / End-to-End Tests (Few, Slow, Brittle)
         /----\
        /      \       🔺 Integration Tests (Moderate Count)
       /--------\
      /          \     🔺 Unit Tests (Many, Fast, Stable)
     /____________\
```

### 2.1.1 Layer Breakdown

| Layer | What it tests | Speed | Count | Tools |
|-------|---------------|-------|-------|-------|
| **Unit Tests** | Individual functions/methods | **Milli-seconds** | **1000s** | JUnit, NUnit, pytest, TestNG |
| **Integration Tests** | Interaction between modules/services | Seconds | **100s** | Postman, REST Assured, Mockito |
| **System/E2E Tests** | Full user journey through UI | **Minutes** | **10s** | Selenium, Cypress, Playwright |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked about the automation pyramid, students must mention the **inverse relationship** between test execution speed and test layer position — i.e., the closer to the user, the slower and more brittle the test.

## 2.2 Types of Automated Testing (The 7 Major Types)

### 2.2.1 Smoke Testing
- **Definition**: A quick set of tests verifying the most critical functionalities.
- **Purpose**: Determine if the build is stable enough for further testing.
- **Example**: "Does the login page load? Does the API respond with HTTP 200?"

### 2.2.2 Unit Testing
- **Definition**: Tests the smallest testable unit (function, method, class) in isolation.
- **Granularity**: Fine-grained.
- **Technique**: White-box, typically written by developers.
- **Example Framework**: JUnit, pytest.

### 2.2.3 Integration Testing
- **Definition**: Tests interaction between integrated modules.
- **Approaches**:
  * **Big-Bang**: All modules integrated at once.
  * **Incremental**: Top-Down or Bottom-Up using **Stubs** and **Drivers**.

### 2.2.4 Regression Testing
- **Definition**: Re-runs existing tests after modifications to ensure no regression of previously working functionality.
- **Most automated** category in industry practice.

### 2.2.5 Functional Testing
- **Definition**: Tests functional requirements (what the system does).
- **Example**: "Clicking 'Submit' should send form data to server."

### 2.2.6 Performance Testing (Sub-categories)
- **Load Test**: Expected load.
- **Stress Test**: Beyond expected load (breaking point).
- **Spike Test**: Sudden load bursts.
- **Endurance Test**: Sustained load over time.
- **Tools**: JMeter, Gatling, LoadRunner.

### 2.2.7 User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
- **Definition**: Final validation by end-users/business stakeholders.

## 2.3 The Automated Test Life Cycle (ATLC)

A formal framework for **structuring the test automation effort** in projects — frequently asked in KTU as a 7-mark or 14-mark question.

$$\text{ATLC} = \{\text{Decision}, \text{Test Tool Acquisition}, \text{Test Automation Process}, \text{Test Planning}, \text{Test Case Development}, \text{Test Execution}, \text{Review \& Evaluation}\}$$

### Step-by-Step ATLC Stages

1. **Decision to Automate**: Identify which test cases to automate (cost-benefit analysis, ROI calculation).
2. **Test Tool Acquisition / Selection**: Evaluate tools based on technology stack, budget, learning curve.
3. **Test Automation Process Introduction**: Define standards, coding conventions, naming conventions.
4. **Test Planning / Strategy**: Define scope, schedule, resources, deliverables, risk.
5. **Test Case Development**: Write scripts, build test data, build test harness.
6. **Test Execution**: Run scripts via CI/CD pipeline, generate logs and reports.
7. **Review and Evaluation**: Compare actual vs. expected, refine scripts, capture metrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Explain ATLC with a neat diagram."* — Students must mention that ATLC is **iterative** and aligned with the **Test Maturity Model (TMM)** levels.

## 2.4 Test Automation Frameworks

A framework is a **set of guidelines, assumptions, and tools** for creating and designing test cases. It is **not a single tool** but an integrated system.

| Framework Type | Description | Pros | Cons |
|----------------|-------------|------|------|
| **Linear Scripting** | Record-and-playback; no modularity | Easy to start | Brittle, non-scalable |
| **Modular** | Scripts broken into reusable functions | Reusability | Needs refactoring |
| **Data-Driven** | Test data separated from script logic | Test with multiple inputs | Complex setup |
| **Keyword-Driven** | Tables of keywords drive test actions | Highly reusable | Learning curve |
| **Hybrid** | Combines Data-Driven + Keyword-Driven | Best of both worlds | Complexity |
| **Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)** | Tests written in natural language (Gherkin) | Bridges gap between devs and business | Verbose |

### 2.4.1 BDD Example (Gherkin)

```
Feature: User Login
  Scenario: Successful login with valid credentials
    Given the user is on the login page
    When the user enters username "admin" and password "pass123"
    And clicks the Login button
    Then the user should be redirected to the dashboard
    And a welcome message "Hello, admin" should be displayed
```

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are **directly examinable** in KTU ESE numerical/analytical questions.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|---------|------|
| **Test Coverage (Statement)** | $C_{stmt} = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100$ | % of statements executed | % |
| **Test Coverage (Branch)** | $C_{branch} = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100$ | % of branches exercised | % |
| **Defect Detection Efficiency (DDE)** | $DDE = \frac{D_{test}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | % of defects found by tests | % |
| **Automation ROI** | $ROI = \frac{(C_{manual} - C_{auto}) - C_{investment}}{C_{investment}} \times 100$ | Cost-benefit of automation | % |
| **Test Effectiveness** | $E = \frac{D_{caught}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | Ability of tests to catch defects | % |
| **Test Productivity** | $P = \frac{T_{executed}}{T_{duration}}$ | Tests per unit time | tests/hour |
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $\overline{t_{detect}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Average time to find a defect | hours/days |
| **Cost per Defect** | $C_{defect} = \frac{C_{testing}}{D_{found}}$ | Cost efficiency of testing | currency |

Where:
- $S_{executed}$ = statements executed, $S_{total}$ = total statements
- $D_{test}$ = defects found by tests, $D_{total}$ = total defects
- $C_{manual}$ = manual cost over period, $C_{auto}$ = automation cost, $C_{investment}$ = initial setup cost
- $T_{executed}$ = tests executed, $T_{duration}$ = duration
- $D_{caught}$ = defects caught, $D_{total}$ = total defects
- $t_i$ = detection time of $i^{th}$ defect

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive**: Continuous regression testing of ECU software across 100+ hardware variants.
- **Aviation**: DO-178C compliance — every line of safety-critical code must be tested.
- **Banking**: 24/7 automated test execution for transaction processing systems.
- **Healthcare**: HIPAA-compliant automated validation of medical device firmware.
- **Telecommunications**: 5G network protocol conformance testing at scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Automation ROI Calculation

A KTU-style numerical problem. Solve step-by-step.

**Problem Statement:**
A software company currently spends **₹15,00,000 per year** on manual regression testing of 800 test cases. They plan to invest **₹8,00,000** initially to build an automation framework. After automation, the ongoing cost reduces to **₹3,00,000 per year**. Calculate the **ROI over 3 years** and determine the **payback period**.

### Given Data
- $C_{manual} = 15{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year
- $C_{auto} = 3{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year
- $C_{investment} = 8{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- Time horizon: **3 years**

### Step 1: Calculate Total Manual Cost over 3 Years

$$\text{Total Manual Cost} = C_{manual} \times 3$$

$$\text{Total Manual Cost} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 45{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Total Automation Cost over 3 Years

$$\text{Total Auto Cost} = C_{investment} + (C_{auto} \times 3)$$

$$\text{Total Auto Cost} = 8{,}00{,}000 + (3{,}00{,}000 \times 3) = 8{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 = 17{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Net Savings (Benefit)

$$\text{Net Savings} = \text{Total Manual Cost} - \text{Total Auto Cost}$$

$$\text{Net Savings} = 45{,}00{,}000 - 17{,}00{,}000 = 28{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 4: Calculate ROI

Using the standard ROI formula from Section 2.5:

$$ROI = \frac{\text{Net Savings}}{C_{investment}} \times 100$$

$$ROI = \frac{28{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$ROI = 3.5 \times 100 = 350\%$$

### Step 5: Calculate Payback Period

$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{C_{investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$$

$$\text{Annual Savings} = C_{manual} - C_{auto} = 15{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 0.667 \text{ years} \approx 8 \text{ months}$$

### Final Answer

> The company achieves an **ROI of 350%** over 3 years with a **payback period of approximately 8 months**, indicating that automation is highly beneficial.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Test Coverage Calculation (Branch Coverage)

**Problem Statement:**
A module has **40 total branches** in its control flow. After running an automated test suite, **34 branches** are exercised. Calculate the **branch coverage percentage**. Also determine how many additional branches need to be covered to reach the **ISTQB industry standard of 85%** branch coverage.

### Step 1: Calculate Current Branch Coverage

$$C_{branch} = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100$$

$$C_{branch} = \frac{34}{40} \times 100 = 85.0\%$$

### Step 2: Check if ISTQB Standard is Met

The ISTQB recommended minimum branch coverage is **85%**. Current coverage = **85%** → **Just meets the standard**.

### Step 3: Test Case Effectiveness

If **12 defects** were found by this suite out of a total of **15 defects** in the system:

$$E = \frac{D_{caught}}{D_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{12}{15} \times 100 = 80\%$$

### Step 4: Defect Detection Efficiency (DDE)

If the testing team found 12 defects, and 3 were found by users post-release:

$$DDE = \frac{D_{test}}{D_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{12}{15} \times 100 = 80\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Analytical Insight:** Notice that even at 85% branch coverage, only 80% of defects are caught — illustrating that **100% coverage ≠ 100% defect detection**. This is a common KTU 14-mark "Discuss" question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Manual vs. Automated Testing (Comparison)

| Dimension | Manual Testing | Automated Testing |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Speed** | Slow (human-paced) | Fast (machine-paced) |
| **Cost (Initial)** | Low | High |
| **Cost (Long-term)** | High (repetitive labour) | Low (reusable scripts) |
| **Accuracy** | Prone to human error | Highly accurate and consistent |
| **Repeatability** | Variable | Identical every run |
| **Coverage Feasibility** | Limited by time | Practically unlimited |
| **Human Insight Required** | Yes (exploratory, UX) | No (scripted) |
| **Initial Setup Time** | Hours | Days to weeks |
| **Best For** | Exploratory, usability, ad-hoc | Regression, performance, data-driven |
| **Tool Dependency** | Low | High (Selenium, JUnit, etc.) |
| **CI/CD Integration** | Not feasible | Native |
| **Test Data Management** | Manual | Programmatic (data-driven) |

## 3.4 Cost-Benefit Trade-off Curve (Conceptual)

Let:
- $C_m$ = Cumulative manual testing cost
- $C_a$ = Cumulative automated testing cost
- $t$ = time (in test cycles)
- $t_0$ = break-even point

The two cost curves intersect at the **break-even point** $t_0$:

$$C_m(t) = c_m \cdot t$$

$$C_a(t) = C_{fixed} + c_a \cdot t$$

where $c_m$ > $c_a$ (per-cycle manual cost > per-cycle automated cost).

At break-even:

$$c_m \cdot t_0 = C_{fixed} + c_a \cdot t_0$$

$$(c_m - c_a) \cdot t_0 = C_{fixed}$$

$$t_0 = \frac{C_{fixed}}{c_m - c_a}$$

**For the previous example:**

$$t_0 = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 0.667 \text{ years}$$

> After $t_0$, every additional cycle **saves** the organization money — this is the **economic justification** for test automation in any MBSD project proposal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Automated Test Execution Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Trigger CI/CD or Manual Run] --> B[Load Test Configuration]
    B --> C[Initialize Test Harness]
    C --> D[Read Test Data from Repository]
    D --> E[Execute Test Scripts against SUT]
    E --> F{Compare Actual vs Expected}
    F -->|Match| G[Mark Test as PASS]
    F -->|Mismatch| H[Mark Test as FAIL]
    G --> I[Capture Logs, Screenshots, Metrics]
    H --> I[Capture Logs, Screenshots, Metrics]
    I --> J[Aggregate Results into Report]
    J --> K{All Critical Tests Pass?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Promote Build to Next Stage]
    K -->|No| M[Block Deployment, Notify Dev Team]
    L --> N[End: Build Deployed]
    M --> N[End: Build Failed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This block diagram represents the **canonical automated test execution loop** as implemented in CI/CD pipelines (Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions). The decision diamond "**Compare Actual vs Expected**" is the **Test Oracle** — the heart of any automated test.

## 4.2 Test Automation Pyramid (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer3["Layer 3: End-to-End / UI Tests"]
        E2E_1[Login Flow Test]
        E2E_2[Checkout Flow Test]
        E2E_3[Search and Filter Test]
    end
    subgraph Layer2["Layer 2: Integration Tests"]
        INT_1[API Endpoint Test]
        INT_2[Database Integration Test]
        INT_3[Service to Service Test]
    end
    subgraph Layer1["Layer 1: Unit Tests"]
        UNIT_1[Function Validation Tests]
        UNIT_2[Method Behavior Tests]
        UNIT_3[Edge Case Tests]
    end
    Layer3 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Layer1
```

## 4.3 Automated Test Life Cycle (ATLC) — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Decision
    Decision: Decision to Automate
    ToolSelect: Test Tool Selection
    ProcessIntro: Automation Process Introduction
    Planning: Test Planning and Strategy
    DevScripts: Test Case and Script Development
    Execution: Test Execution
    Review: Review and Evaluation
    Decision --> ToolSelect
    ToolSelect --> ProcessIntro
    ProcessIntro --> Planning
    Planning --> DevScripts
    DevScripts --> Execution
    Execution --> Review
    Review --> Decision
    Review --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The arrow from `Review` back to `Decision` indicates that ATLC is a **closed feedback loop** — lessons learned from one cycle inform the next iteration. This cyclic nature is what makes automation continuously improving.

## 4.4 Selection Strategy Flow — Should I Automize This Test?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Test Case Identified] --> Q1{Is it run more than once?}
    Q1 -->|No| Stop1[Do NOT Automate]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the requirement stable?}
    Q2 -->|No| Stop2[Do NOT Automate]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Does it require human judgment?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Stop3[Manual Testing Preferred]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is ROI positive over 3 cycles?}
    Q4 -->|No| Stop4[Re-evaluate Scope]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Automate[Automate and Add to Regression Suite]
```

## 4.5 High-Level Test Automation Architecture (In MBSD Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MBSE[Model-Based Software Engineering Artifacts]
        M1[UML State Machine]
        M2[Requirements Model]
        M3[Activity Diagram]
    end
    subgraph TA[Test Automation Engine]
        TG[Test Case Generator]
        TS[Test Script Repository]
        TE[Test Execution Engine]
        OR[Test Oracle / Comparator]
    end
    subgraph SUT[System Under Test]
        CODE[Application Code]
        API[APIs and Services]
        UI[User Interface]
    end
    RE[Reports and Dashboards]
    MBSE --> TG
    TG --> TS
    TS --> TE
    TE --> SUT
    SUT --> OR
    OR --> RE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for MBSD:** Models (state machines, requirements) are used to **automatically generate** test cases, which are then executed against the SUT. This is the **essence of model-based testing (MBT)** — covered in greater depth in later modules of PECNT634.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---
