topics.title: Learning MBSD from the Perspective of Architecture Analysis and Design Language (AADL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Learning MBSD from the Perspective of Architecture Analysis and Design Language (AADL)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of AADL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Analysis and Design Language (AADL)** is a standardized, model-based engineering notation (standardized as **SAE AS5506/AS5506/2** and standardized internationally as **ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010**) specifically designed to support the **model-based engineering** of **embedded real-time, safety-critical, and distributed software-intensive systems**.

AADL is one of the cornerstone languages in **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)** because it allows the software architect to express the **runtime architecture** of a system (i.e., how the software will be deployed, scheduled, and interconnected on the execution platform) in a way that is both human-readable and **machine-analyzable**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, AADL is treated as the **primary vehicle** through which students learn *how architecture can be modeled, analyzed, validated, and even auto-generated* before a single line of implementation code is written.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint" View

Imagine you are building a high-rise apartment building.

- The **architectural blueprint** does not lay a single brick. Instead, it precisely specifies the load-bearing pillars, the electrical conduits, the plumbing network, the elevator shafts, and the safety exits.
- The blueprint also specifies *properties*: maximum occupancy per floor, fire-resistance rating of the walls, height clearance for elevators, etc.
- Once the blueprint is ready, a structural engineer can simulate an earthquake on the blueprint to verify safety — **without physically building the tower**.

AADL plays the *exact same role* in software engineering for embedded systems.

| Civil Engineering | AADL Equivalent (Software Engineering) |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Blueprint of a building | AADL model of the software system |
| Load-bearing pillar | **Process / Thread** component |
| Electrical conduit | **Connection** (port-to-port) |
| Foundation / soil | **Processor / Memory** hardware |
| Building regulations | **AADL Properties** (timing, memory) |
| Earthquake simulation | **Model Analysis** (schedulability, safety) |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a blueprint separates the *what* (rooms, doors) from the *how* (materials, dimensions), AADL separates the **component type** (the abstract interface/contract) from the **component implementation** (the concrete realization). This separation is the foundation of MBSD.

## 1.3 Why AADL Was Needed — Historical Motivation

Before AADL, embedded systems were specified using a mix of:
- Natural-language requirement documents
- UML diagrams (which were largely *structural* and *design-time* — not analyzable for runtime)
- Hand-written timing budgets in spreadsheets

This led to the well-known **"integration crisis"** — when hardware and software were finally combined, hidden timing and resource bugs surfaced, costing up to **200–500×** more to fix than if caught in design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AADL was developed by the **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** primarily because the **aerospace and automotive industries** (Boeing, Airbus, Lockheed Martin, Toyota, Honeywell) demanded a *single standard* in which an architecture could be:
> 1. **Modeled** precisely with well-defined semantics.
> 2. **Analyzed** automatically for timing, memory, and safety.
> 3. **Exchanged** between tool vendors (OSATE, Stood, Cheddar, etc.) without losing information.

The standard was first released in **2004** as AS5506, with major revisions in **2009, 2012, 2017, and 2022**.

## 1.4 The Core AADL Philosophy — *Separation of Concerns*

AADL is built on a strict separation of four modeling dimensions:

1. **Component categories** — *What kind of thing is it?* (software, hardware, system, abstract)
2. **Component classifier** — *What is its interface?* (Component Type) and *What is its internal structure?* (Component Implementation)
3. **Properties** — *What are its non-functional characteristics?* (timing, memory, reliability, security)
4. **Annexes** — *What additional behavior/error/contract semantics does it carry?*

This separation is the *single most important concept* you must internalize for the KTU examination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AADL Component Classification System

AADL organizes all modeling elements into **four major component categories** (sometimes called *metaclasses*). Each category contains several *component classifiers*.

### 2.1.1 Software Components (the "logical" software units that run on hardware)

| Component Classifier | KTU Definition | Intuitive Role |
|----------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Data** | Represents typed data at runtime (C struct, Ada record) | Passive data container — does not execute |
| **Subprogram** | A callable routine (C function, Ada procedure) | Smallest unit of executable code |
| **Subprogram Group** | A library of related subprograms | Logical grouping, no runtime footprint |
| **Thread** | A schedulable unit of concurrent execution | The fundamental "active" software unit |
| **Thread Group** | A cluster of threads sharing an address space | Used to model multi-threaded processes |
| **Process** | A protected address space (memory partition) | Strong isolation boundary |

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU exam trap: students confuse `Process` and `Thread`. **A Process is a memory boundary (partition); a Thread is a schedulable unit of execution.** A Process contains Threads; a Thread does *not* contain a Process.

### 2.1.2 Execution-Platform Components (the "hardware" and runtime substrate)

| Component Classifier | Definition | Intuitive Role |
|----------------------|------------|-----------------|
| **Processor** | A hardware execution unit (CPU, microcontroller, core) | Schedules and dispatches threads |
| **Memory** | A storage component (RAM, ROM, flash) | Stores code, data, and stack |
| **Bus** | A communication medium (CAN, Ethernet, I²C) | Connects processors, memory, and devices |
| **Virtual Processor** | A logical execution partition (hypervisor VM) | Schedules a subset of threads |
| **Virtual Bus** | A logical communication protocol stack | Abstraction of a logical bus protocol |

### 2.1.3 System Components

| Component Classifier | Definition |
|----------------------|------------|
| **System** | The top-level composite — the entire system under design, containing software, hardware, and sub-systems |

### 2.1.4 Abstract Components

| Component Classifier | Definition |
|----------------------|------------|
| **Abstract** | A placeholder with no predefined category — used for generic modeling or for components that don't fit elsewhere (e.g., a "Human" operator) |

## 2.2 AADL Features — How Components Communicate

AADL components exchange information through **features** (the *external interface* of a component).

| Feature Type | Used In | Purpose | Example |
|--------------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Port** (data, event, event data) | Process, Thread, System, Processor, Device | Send/receive data or events | `data port sensor_input` |
| **Parameter** | Subprogram | Call arguments | `in parameter cmd: Command` |
| **Subprogram Access** | Process, Thread, Processor | Required subprogram call | `requires subprogram access` |
| **Data Access** (read, write, read-write) | Process, Thread, Memory | Shared memory access | `requires data access config` |
| **Bus Access** | Processor, Memory, Device | Connection to a bus | `requires bus access CAN_Bus` |
| **Feature Group** | Any | Logical grouping of features | `sensors: feature group` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ports** are subdivided into:
> - `event port` — asynchronous event (no data payload), e.g., an interrupt
> - **data port** — carries typed data on every dispatch (latest value semantics)
> - `event data port` — carries both an event (queue) and data payload
> - `data port` *with* `in` or `out` direction — directionality of the dataflow

## 2.3 AADL Properties — The Quantitative Backbone of MBSD

**Properties** are *named, typed, user-attachable attributes* that give AADL components their non-functional, analyzable character. They are what make the model **machine-checkable**.

AADL ships with **standardized property sets**:

| Property Set | High-Yield Properties (KTU must-know) | Meaning |
|--------------|----------------------------------------|---------|
| **Timing_Properties** | `Period`, `Deadline`, `Compute_Execution_Time`, `Dispatch_Offset` | Schedulability input |
| **Memory_Properties** | `Source_Code_Size`, `Source_Data_Size`, `Stack_Size` | Memory budget |
| **Communication_Properties** | `Latency`, `Bandwidth_Budget`, `Connection_Transmission_Time` | Network analysis |
| **Thread_Properties** | `Dispatch_Protocol`, `Priority`, `Compute_Entrypoint` | Thread behavior |
| **Process_Properties** | `Execution_Time`, `Period` | Process-level budgets |
| **Deployment_Properties** | `Actual_Processor_Binding`, `Actual_Memory_Binding` | Binding constraints |
| **Reliability_Properties** | `Mean_Time_To_Failure` (MTTF) | Reliability analysis |
| **Security_Properties** | `Security_Level` | Security classification |

> [!NOTE]
> In the textual AADL syntax, properties are applied in a `properties` block. Example:
> ```
> thread SensorThread
>   features
>     raw: in data port SensorRaw;
>   properties
>     Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic;
>     Period => 10 ms;
>     Deadline => 12 ms;
>     Compute_Execution_Time => range 1ms .. 3ms;
> end SensorThread;
> ```

## 2.4 Connections, Flows, and Modes

### 2.4.1 Connections
Connections physically link features between subcomponents. AADL enforces the **type compatibility rule**: you can only connect two ports of the same `data type` and matching direction.

- **Port-to-port** (most common): `p1.out_port -> p2.in_port`
- **Port-to-component** (delegation)
- **Subprogram access-to-subprogram** (subprogram call)
- **Data access-to-data** (shared memory reference)
- **Bus access-to-bus** (binding to communication bus)

### 2.4.2 Flow Declarations — End-to-End Analysis
AADL allows you to declare **end-to-end flows** that cross multiple components:
- `flow source` — origin of a flow
- `flow sink` — destination
- `flow path` — composite flow through subcomponents

This enables **latency and reliability analysis** across the entire system path.

### 2.4.3 Modes and Mode Transitions
A real-time system often has multiple operational states (e.g., `Initialization`, `Flight`, `Emergency`). AADL `modes` allow you to specify which subcomponents, connections, and flows are **active in which mode**, and the `mode transitions` define how the system moves between modes (triggered by events).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modes** are a *high-frequency KTU exam topic*. A typical 7-mark question will ask: *"Explain the role of modes and mode transitions in AADL with a suitable example."* Be prepared to draw a `mode transition` diagram.

## 2.5 AADL Annexes — Extending the Core Language

The core AADL is *declarative and structural*; it describes *what exists*, not *what happens dynamically*. To express behavior, errors, and contracts, the standard provides **Annex sublanguages**:

| Annex Sublanguage | Purpose | KTU Relevance |
|-------------------|---------|---------------|
| **Behavior Annex** | State-transition behavior inside components (state machines) | High — behavioral model |
| **Error Model Annex** | Fault propagation, error states, and recovery | High — safety analysis |
| **ARINC653 Annex** | Avionics-specific partitioning | Medium |
| **STOOD Annex** | Automatic code generation from AADL | Medium — MBSD toolchain |
| **Requirements Annex** | Traceability from requirements to architecture | High |
| **Meta-Model Annex** | Extending AADL with user-defined concepts | Low |

## 2.6 The KTU High-Yield AADL Cheat Sheet (Formula / Construct Table)

> The following is the **single most important table** for your Module 1 examination.

| # | AADL Construct | KTU Definition (one-line) | Use Case |
|---|----------------|---------------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Component Type** | Declares the external interface (features + properties) | Defines the *contract* |
| 2 | **Component Implementation** | Declares the internal structure (subcomponents + connections) | Defines the *realization* |
| 3 | **Package** | Namespacing mechanism for grouping AADL declarations | Avoids naming conflicts |
| 4 | **System** | Top-level composite (root of architecture) | Overall system boundary |
| 5 | **Process** | Protected address space (memory partition) | Software runtime boundary |
| 6 | **Thread** | Schedulable unit of concurrent execution | Task in RTOS terms |
| 7 | **Data** | Passive data type at runtime | Sensor reading, state variable |
| 8 | **Subprogram** | Callable routine | Function / method |
| 9 | **Processor** | Hardware execution unit | CPU, MCU, core |
| 10 | **Memory** | Storage component | RAM, ROM, flash |
| 11 | **Bus** | Communication medium | CAN, Ethernet, I²C |
| 12 | **Device** | External sensor/actuator hardware | Camera, motor, GPS |
| 13 | **Port** (data, event, event data) | Communication endpoint | Inter-component dataflow |
| 14 | **Connection** | Link between two features | Wiring of the architecture |
| 15 | **Property** | Named, typed non-functional attribute | Timing, memory, reliability |
| 16 | **Flow (source/sink/path)** | End-to-end propagation path | Latency, reliability analysis |
| 17 | **Mode** | Named operational state of the system | Initialization, Normal, Fault |
| 18 | **Mode Transition** | Triggered shift between modes | State change semantics |
| 19 | **Annex** | Sublanguage extension | Behavior, error modeling |
| 20 | **Binding** (`Actual_Processor_Binding`) | Mapping of software to hardware | Deployment analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical / Semantic Operators in AADL** (used in property values):
> - **range** `a .. b` — used for non-deterministic budgets, e.g., `Compute_Execution_Time => range 1ms .. 3ms`
> - **applies to** — declares the scope of a property
> - **inheritance** — implementations inherit from parent implementations with `extends` keyword
> - **reference** — links a property value to another component (semantic dependency)
> - **classifier match** rule — the formal type-checking rule that ensures two connected ports share a *compatible data classifier*

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Methodology of AADL-Based MBSD

AADL is not just a notation — it is the front-end of a rigorous MBSD workflow. Below is the **exhaustive 7-step derivation of the MBSD lifecycle as it applies to AADL**. This is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

### Step 1 — Requirement Capture (using the Requirements Annex)
The MBSD process begins with natural-language requirements (e.g., "The system shall sample the temperature every 10 ms with a maximum latency of 12 ms"). These are imported into the model via the **Requirements Annex** as:

```
requirement R_TempSampling
  properties
    Required_Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic;
    Required_Period => 10 ms;
    Required_Deadline => 12 ms;
end R_TempSampling;
```

The *Required\_\** prefix is **mandatory** in the Requirements Annex — this is a KTU high-yield detail.

### Step 2 — Architecture Type Modeling
For each subsystem, the architect writes a **component type** that declares the *external contract* (features). Example:

```
thread sensor_thread
  features
    raw_in : in data port Temperature_Raw;
    valid_out : out event data port Temperature_Valid;
  properties
    Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic;
end sensor_thread;
```

Notice: **no internal structure is specified at the type level** — this enforces the *interface-implementation separation* principle of MBSD.

### Step 3 — Architecture Implementation Modeling
The architect then writes a **component implementation** that specifies the *internal structure*:

```
thread implementation sensor_thread.basic
  subcomponents
    reading : subprogram read_temperature;
  calls
    seq1 : { reading: read_temperature; };
  connections
    param1 : parameter reading.cmd -> raw_in;
  properties
    Period => 10 ms applies to reading;
    Compute_Execution_Time => 2 ms .. 4 ms applies to reading;
end sensor_thread.basic;
```

The key insight: the implementation *extends* the type and *adds* the subcomponents, connections, calls, and properties.

### Step 4 — Property Assignment (the Quantitative Phase)
This is where AADL becomes *analyzable*. The architect attaches **timing, memory, and reliability** values. The model now contains all the data required for analysis.

### Step 5 — Deployment / Binding
The architect maps software components onto hardware components using **Actual\_\*** properties:

```
system implementation TempMonitor.i
  subcomponents
    CPU : processor CortexM4;
    APP : process TemperatureApp.i;
    RAM : memory SRAM_64K;
  properties
    Actual_Processor_Binding => reference CPU applies to APP;
    Actual_Memory_Binding => reference RAM applies to APP;
end TempMonitor.i;
```

This step enables **schedulability, memory-overflow, and bus-bandwidth analysis**.

### Step 6 — Model Analysis (the "M" payoff of MBSD)
The AADL model is fed to analyzers (OSATE, Cheddar, MAST, AIRES, FLAVOR):
- **Schedulability analysis** — verifies that all thread deadlines are met.
- **Latency analysis** — end-to-end flow latency must satisfy `Required_Latency`.
- **Memory analysis** — total memory footprint must fit in `Actual_Memory_Binding`.
- **Safety analysis** — using the Error Model Annex.
- **Reliability analysis** — using flow reliability propagation.

### Step 7 — Code Generation and Continuous Verification
Tools like **Stood, OCARINA, OPMW-Pro** can auto-generate **Glue-Code skeletons** (Ada/C skeletons) directly from the AADL model. Generated code is *guaranteed* to be consistent with the architecture — the integration crisis is **eliminated**.

## 3.2 The AADL Type-Implementation Inheritance Derivation

A very common KTU question asks to **prove / explain the inheritance semantics of AADL implementations**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> Inheritance in AADL is *unidirectional* and *additive* — child implementations **extend** parents, **add** new subcomponents, **refine** connections, and **narrow** property values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } &P = \text{parent implementation} \\
\text{Let }&C = \text{child implementation}, \quad C \; \text{extends} \; P \\
\text{Then } &\text{Components}(C) \; = \; \text{Components}(P) \cup \text{Components}_{C}^{\text{new}} \\
\text{and }&\text{Properties}(C) \; \preceq \; \text{Properties}(P) \quad \text{(child values are within parent ranges)} \\
\text{and }&\text{Connections}(C) \; \supseteq \; \text{Connections}(P) \quad \text{(child may add but not remove)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanatory step-by-step**:
1. A child implementation `C` declares `extends P` in its header.
2. The AADL tool **inherits** all subcomponents of `P` into `C`'s namespace.
3. The child can **add** new subcomponents with the `subcomponents` clause.
4. The child can **add** new connections in the `connections` clause.
5. The child can **refine** an inherited property only if the new value is **semantically narrower** (e.g., parent says `Period => range 5ms .. 20ms`, child may say `Period => 10ms` — a single point is narrower than a range).
6. The child **cannot** remove an inherited subcomponent (this would break the architecture's contract).

## 3.3 The "End-to-End Latency" Analytical Formula

For a KTU numerical/conceptual question, the latency of an end-to-end flow is computed as follows. Let a flow path be:

$$
P = \left\langle C_1, C_2, C_3, \ldots, C_n \right\rangle
$$

where $C_i$ is a component (or subcomponent) on the path. The total latency is the sum of each leg's latency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}}(P) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} L_{\text{leg}}(C_i, C_{i+1}) \\
L_{\text{leg}}(C_i, C_{i+1}) &= L_{\text{process}}(C_i) + L_{\text{queue}}(C_i) + L_{\text{transmit}}(C_i, C_{i+1}) \\
L_{\text{process}}(C_i) &= \text{Compute\_Execution\_Time}(C_i) \\
L_{\text{queue}}(C_i) &= \max_{j \in \text{inputs}} \text{Delay}(j) \\
L_{\text{transmit}}(C_i, C_{i+1}) &= \text{Connection\_Transmission\_Time}(\text{conn}(C_i, C_{i+1}))
\end{aligned}
$$

The **schedulability constraint** is then:

$$
\forall i, \; L_{\text{total}}(P_i) \;\le\; \text{Deadline}(P_i)
$$

If the constraint is violated, the architecture **fails the MBSD analysis** and must be re-designed (e.g., a faster processor, a different scheduling policy, or a redesign of the data path).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why AADL is the *enabler of MBSD*: the analysis is **derived from the model itself**, not from a separate, manually maintained spreadsheet. The model is the **single source of truth**.

## 3.4 The Mode-Transition Derivation

Modes are essentially a *switching* of the active subcomponent/connection set. Formally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } &M = \{m_0, m_1, \ldots, m_k\} \quad \text{be the set of modes} \\
\text{Let } &A(m) \subseteq \text{Subcomponents} \quad \text{be the active set in mode } m \\
\text{Let } &\delta: M \times E \rightarrow M \quad \text{be the mode-transition function} \\
\text{where } &E = \text{set of triggering events}
\end{aligned}
$$

A mode transition $\delta(m_i, e) = m_j$ is valid **iff** the triggering event $e$ is declared in the source mode $m_i$ and the destination mode $m_j$ has all required subcomponents instantiated (either always-active or mode-specific).

The **invariants** are:
- The system is *always* in exactly one mode.
- A subcomponent can be `in modes (m1, m2)` — i.e., active in only certain modes.
- Default mode is declared with `initial mode`.

## 3.5 The Analytical Matrix — AADL Construct vs. Engineering Activity

| AADL Construct | Engineering Activity it Supports | KTU Key Term to Remember |
|----------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Component Type | Requirement-to-interface mapping | **Contract** |
| Component Implementation | Design realization | **Realization** |
| Properties | Non-functional specification | **Quantitative Model** |
| Connections | Integration specification | **Wiring Diagram** |
| Flows | Cross-cutting analysis | **End-to-End Path** |
| Modes | Operational state machine | **Operational Variability** |
| Annex Behavior | Behavioral specification | **State-Transition Logic** |
| Annex Error | Fault propagation | **Safety Case** |
| Binding | Deployment to hardware | **Allocation** |
| Package | Reuse and modularity | **Namespacing** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The AADL Component Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **full AADL component taxonomy** as required by the KTU syllabus. Every block uses an alphanumeric node identifier with a clean uppercase label (per the compilation safeguard rule).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootAADL["AADL Component Categories"]
    swCat["Software Components"]
    hwCat["Execution Platform Components"]
    sysCat["System Components"]
    absCat["Abstract Components"]

    swData["Data"]
    swSub["Subprogram"]
    swSubGrp["Subprogram Group"]
    swThread["Thread"]
    swThreadGrp["Thread Group"]
    swProc["Process"]

    hwProc["Processor"]
    hwVirtProc["Virtual Processor"]
    hwMem["Memory"]
    hwBus["Bus"]
    hwVirtBus["Virtual Bus"]
    hwDev["Device"]

    sysSys["System"]
    absAbs["Abstract"]

    rootAADL --> swCat
    rootAADL --> hwCat
    rootAADL --> sysCat
    rootAADL --> absCat

    swCat --> swData
    swCat --> swSub
    swCat --> swSubGrp
    swCat --> swThread
    swCat --> swThreadGrp
    swCat --> swProc

    hwCat --> hwProc
    hwCat --> hwVirtProc
    hwCat --> hwMem
    hwCat --> hwBus
    hwCat --> hwVirtBus
    hwCat --> hwDev

    sysCat --> sysSys
    absCat --> absAbs

    swThread -->|"runs in"| swProc
    swProc -->|"allocated to"| hwProc
    hwProc -->|"communicates via"| hwBus
    hwProc -->|"uses"| hwMem
    hwDev -->|"connects to"| hwBus
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The downward arrows show the **is-a** relationship (taxonomy). The lateral arrows (e.g., `runs in`, `allocated to`) show the **runtime interaction** relationship — these are the bindings that the AADL `Actual_*` properties formalize.

## 4.2 The AADL Type vs. Implementation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    typeBlock["Component Type: sensor_thread"]
    featList["Features: raw_in, valid_out"]
    propList["Type Properties: Dispatch_Protocol"]

    implBlock["Component Implementation: sensor_thread.basic"]
    subList["Subcomponents: read_temperature"]
    connList["Connections: param1"]
    callList["Calls: seq1"]
    implProp["Implementation Properties: Period, Compute_Execution_Time"]

    typeBlock -. extends .-> implBlock
    typeBlock --- featList
    typeBlock --- propList
    implBlock --- subList
    implBlock --- connList
    implBlock --- callList
    implBlock --- implProp
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrow `extends` is the **inheritance link** between type and implementation. The solid lines show the **clauses** of each declaration.

## 4.3 The AADL-Based MBSD Workflow (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1: Capture Requirements (Requirements Annex)"]
    step2["Step 2: Model Component Types (Interfaces)"]
    step3["Step 3: Model Component Implementations (Structure)"]
    step4["Step 4: Assign Properties (Timing, Memory, Reliability)"]
    step5["Step 5: Bind Software to Hardware (Deployment)"]
    step6["Step 6: Model Analysis (Schedulability, Latency, Safety)"]
    step7["Step 7: Auto-Generate Skeletal Code (Glue Code)"]
    step8["Step 8: Continuous Verification & Iteration"]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 -->|"If violation found"| step3
    step6 -->|"If all checks pass"| step7
    step7 --> step8
    step8 -->|"Refine on real feedback"| step4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **back-arrow** from Step 6 to Step 3 is the *single most important* aspect of MBSD: analysis drives design refinement. This is what distinguishes MBSD from the old **document-driven** (V-model) approach where errors were caught only at integration.

## 4.4 AADL Mode-Transition Schematic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Normal: event init_complete
    Normal --> Degraded: event sensor_fault
    Degraded --> Normal: event fault_recovered
    Normal --> Emergency: event critical_overheat
    Emergency --> Init: event manual_reset
    Degraded --> Emergency: event second_fault
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each state in this diagram corresponds to an AADL `mode` declaration. The events are the **triggers** of the `mode transitions`. The AADL model enforces that subcomponents needed only in a given mode are declared with `in modes (...)`.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

> *Define Architecture Analysis and Design Language (AADL). List its four main component categories with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- AADL is a **standardized model-based engineering language (SAE AS5506)** used to specify the **software and hardware architecture** of **embedded real-time, safety-critical systems**. It supports both *abstract specification* and *machine-analyzable* models. (1 mark)
- The **four main component categories** are: (2 marks)
  1. **Software Components** — e.g., `Process`, `Thread`, `Data`, `Subprogram`
  2. **Execution Platform (Hardware) Components** — e.g., `Processor`, `Memory`, `Bus`, `Device`
  3. **System Components** — e.g., `System` (the composite root)
  4. **Abstract Components** — e.g., `Abstract` (generic placeholder for non-standard entities)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

> *What is the difference between a Component Type and a Component Implementation in AADL? Why is this separation important in MBSD?*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- A **Component Type** declares the **external interface** of a component — its *features* (ports, parameters) and *type-level properties*. It defines the **contract**. (1 mark)
- A **Component Implementation** declares the **internal structure** — its *subcomponents*, *connections*, *calls*, and *implementation-level properties*. It defines the **realization**. (1 mark)
- This separation is the foundation of **MBSD's substitutability**: the same type can have *multiple implementations* (e.g., a software thread type can have an implementation for a target A, and another for target B). The *interface contract is preserved* while the realization differs. This enables **re-use, variant management, and seamless code generation**. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

> **(a) Explain the role of AADL in Model-Based Software Development. Discuss how AADL supports architecture modeling, analysis, and code generation. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) With a suitable example, explain the use of AADL Properties (Timing\_Properties, Memory\_Properties, and Communication\_Properties) in modeling an embedded real-time system. Show how property-based analysis is performed. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

AADL is the **architectural spine** of MBSD for embedded real-time systems. Its role can be broken down into five concrete functions:

1. **Architecture Modeling** — AADL provides a **formal, standardized vocabulary** to describe the *runtime architecture* of a system: its software components (threads, processes, subprograms), hardware components (processors, memory, buses), and the connections between them. Unlike UML, which is *general-purpose*, AADL is *specifically tailored* to embedded systems — with built-in concepts for schedulable threads, protected address spaces, and hardware buses. (2 marks)

2. **Quantitative Specification via Properties** — AADL attaches **typed properties** to every component. These include timing budgets (`Period`, `Deadline`, `Compute_Execution_Time`), memory budgets (`Source_Code_Size`, `Stack_Size`), and communication budgets (`Latency`, `Bandwidth_Budget`). The model is therefore **not just structural but also quantitative**. (1 mark)

3. **Architecture Analysis** — Because the model is precise and the properties are typed, **automatic analyzers** can perform:
   - *Schedulability analysis* (rate-monotonic, deadline-monotonic) — checking that all thread deadlines are met.
   - *Latency analysis* — checking that end-to-end flows meet their required latency.
   - *Memory analysis* — checking that total memory footprint fits within the available memory.
   - *Safety analysis* (via Error Model Annex) — checking fault propagation.
   - *Reliability analysis* — checking MTTF of end-to-end flows. (2 marks)

4. **Code Generation** — AADL models can be processed by **code generators** (Stood, OCARINA, Ocarina-GlueC) to produce **skeletal code** — the *glue code* that wires together threads, processes, and devices. Generated code is **guaranteed consistent** with the architecture. (1 mark)

5. **Continuous Verification & Iteration** — Because the model is the **single source of truth**, modifications to the architecture automatically propagate to analysis and code, eliminating the "integration crisis" of document-driven development. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Example**: Consider a temperature monitoring system with two periodic threads — `sensor_thread` (period 10 ms) and `control_thread` (period 20 ms). The architecture is:
- `Process TempApp` (memory partition)
  - `Thread sensor_thread` (samples temperature)
  - `Thread control_thread` (computes fan speed)
- `Processor CortexM4` (CPU)
- `Memory SRAM_32K` (data memory)
- `Bus I2C` (sensor interface)

**Property assignment**:
```
thread sensor_thread
  features
    raw_in : in data port Temperature_Raw;
    valid_out : out event data port Temperature_Valid;
  properties
    Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic;
end sensor_thread;

thread implementation sensor_thread.basic
  subcomponents
    read_op : subprogram read_temperature;
  calls
    s1 : { read_op: read_temperature; };
  properties
    Period => 10 ms applies to read_op;
    Compute_Execution_Time => range 1ms .. 3ms applies to read_op;
    Deadline => 12 ms applies to read_op;
    Source_Code_Size => 4 KB applies to read_op;
    Stack_Size => 512 B applies to read_op;
end sensor_thread.basic;

system implementation TempMonitor.i
  subcomponents
    cpu : processor CortexM4;
    app : process TempApp.i;
    sram : memory SRAM_32K;
  properties
    Actual_Processor_Binding => reference cpu applies to app;
    Actual_Memory_Binding => reference sram applies to app;
end TempMonitor.i;
```

**Analysis performed** (1+1+1+1+2 = 6 marks for analysis steps + 1 mark for conclusion):

1. **Schedulability** — Using the Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) bound:
$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{3}{10} + \frac{5}{20} = 0.30 + 0.25 = 0.55
$$
For $n=2$ threads, the RMS bound is $U_{\max} = 2(2^{1/2} - 1) \approx 0.828$.
Since $0.55 \le 0.828$, the system is **schedulable** (✓).

2. **Memory Analysis** — Sum the code sizes:
$$
\text{Total Code} = 4 \text{ KB} (\text{sensor}) + 8 \text{ KB} (\text{control}) = 12 \text{ KB}
$$
$$
\text{Total Data} = 512 \text{ B} + 1 \text{ KB} = 1.5 \text{ KB}
$$
Since $12 \text{ KB} + 1.5 \text{ KB} = 13.5 \text{ KB} \le 32 \text{ KB}$, the system **fits in SRAM** (✓).

3. **Latency Analysis** — End-to-end flow `sensor → control → actuator`:
$$
L_{\text{total}} = (3 \text{ ms}) + (\text{queue} \approx 0) + (\text{transmit} \approx 1 \text{ ms}) + (5 \text{ ms}) = 9 \text{ ms}
$$
Required deadline is 20 ms. Since $9 \text{ ms} \le 20 \text{ ms}$, the deadline is met (✓).

4. **Bandwidth Analysis** — I²C bus load:
$$
\text{Bus Load} = \frac{\text{Messages per second} \times \text{Size}}{\text{Bus Capacity}} = \frac{100 \times 16 \text{ bits}}{400 \text{ kbps}} = 0.4\%
$$
Well within budget (✓).

5. **Conclusion** — All four analyses pass, so the architecture is **ready for code generation** via the Stood/OCARINA tool. Any change in `Period` or `Compute_Execution_Time` would automatically trigger a re-analysis — true MBSD.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

> **(a) Explain the concept of modes and mode transitions in AADL. With a suitable example, demonstrate how modes are used to model the operational variability of an embedded system. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) Discuss the AADL annexes — Behavior Annex, Error Model Annex, and Requirements Annex — and explain how they extend the core AADL to support behavioral, safety, and traceability concerns in MBSD. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Conceptual Explanation** (3 marks):
- **Modes** in AADL model the *distinct operational states* of a system (e.g., `Init`, `Normal`, `Emergency`, `Sleep`).
- A system is *always* in exactly one mode at runtime.
- Each mode declares a **specific active set of subcomponents, connections, and flows**.
- **Mode Transitions** are *event-triggered* changes of the system's mode. The transition is declared in the source mode and points to a destination mode.
- The keyword `initial mode` designates the starting mode.

**Example — UAV Flight Controller** (4 marks):

```
system implementation UAV.i
  subcomponents
    flight_ctrl : process FlightCtrl.i;
    sensor_thread : thread SensorThread;
    nav_thread : thread NavThread;
    emergency_thread : thread EmergencyThread;
  modes
    init : initial mode;
    normal : mode;
    emergency : mode;
    landed : mode;
  mode transitions
    init -[ event system_init_complete ]-> normal;
    normal -[ event sensor_fault_detected ]-> emergency;
    normal -[ event mission_complete ]-> landed;
    emergency -[ event manual_pilot_override ]-> landed;
end UAV.i;

thread implementation SensorThread
  modes
    active : initial mode;
    standby : mode;
end SensorThread;
```

**Demonstration of use**:
- In the `init` mode, only the initialization subcomponent is active.
- In the `normal` mode, `sensor_thread` is in `active` mode and `nav_thread` is enabled.
- In the `emergency` mode, `sensor_thread` switches to `standby` and `emergency_thread` becomes active.
- In the `landed` mode, all real-time threads are suspended.

This allows the **same architectural model** to capture radically different runtime configurations, which is essential for **operational variability in MBSD**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Overview** (1 mark):
AADL annexes are **standardized extensions** that supplement the core declarative language. They enable the model to express *behavior, errors, and requirements traceability* without breaking AADL's analyzability.

**Behavior Annex** (2 marks):
- Adds a **state-transition sublanguage** *inside* components.
- Allows the architect to write **state machines** for components, e.g., a `Thread` can declare a state machine with states like `Idle`, `Executing`, `Waiting`, `Error`.
- Each transition is triggered by an **event port** and may have **actions** (subprogram calls, port outputs).
- **MBSD payoff**: the state machine is *analyzable* (e.g., for reachability, deadlocks) and can be **simulated** by tools like OSATE.

**Error Model Annex** (2 marks):
- Models **fault propagation** through the architecture.
- Each component can declare **error states** (e.g., `Operational`, `Degraded`, `Failed`) and **error events** (e.g., `MemoryError`, `BusError`).
- The annex supports **error propagation** across component boundaries via **error propagations** on features.
- **MBSD payoff**: enables **safety analysis** (e.g., FTA — Fault Tree Analysis, FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) directly on the architecture model, generating safety evidence for certification (e.g., DO-178C in avionics).

**Requirements Annex** (2 marks):
- Allows **formal requirement statements** to be embedded in the AADL model.
- Each requirement has a unique identifier, a `Required_*` property set (e.g., `Required_Period`, `Required_Latency`), and a `for` clause linking it to a component.
- **MBSD payoff**: enables **bidirectional traceability** — from requirement → architecture → code → test. This is essential for certification and is a key part of the MBSD's "single source of truth" philosophy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in AADL Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing `Process` and `Thread`** — A `Process` is a *memory partition*; a `Thread` is a *schedulable execution unit*. Writing "a Thread contains Processes" will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the `initial mode`** declaration — AADL requires *exactly one* `initial mode` per system. Omitting it is a syntax error and will cost a mark.
> 3. **Confusing `data port` and `event data port`** — A `data port` carries *latest value*; an `event data port` carries a *queue* of events. Mismatch loses marks.
> 4. **Treating AADL as a UML variant** — AADL is *not* UML. It is a **standardized model with formal semantics for analysis**. Writing "AADL is a UML profile" is a factual error that will lose 3 marks.
> 5. **Omitting `applies to` clause in properties** — Property declarations are **scoped**. A property `Period => 10 ms` without `applies to` is **invalid** in most cases.
> 6. **Not stating SAE/ISO standard numbers** — KTU examiners expect a mention of **SAE AS5506** and **ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010** when defining AADL.
> 7. **Missing the four-category structure** — AADL has **exactly four** component categories: software, execution platform, system, abstract. Forgetting the *abstract* category is a common 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] AADL = **Architecture Analysis and Design Language**, standardized as **SAE AS5506** and **ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010**.
- [x] AADL is the **primary architectural language** for MBSD in embedded real-time, safety-critical systems.
- [x] The **four component categories** are: **Software, Execution Platform (Hardware), System, Abstract**.
- [x] **Component Type** declares the *contract* (features + type properties); **Component Implementation** declares the *realization* (subcomponents, connections, properties).
- [x] A **Process** is a *memory partition*; a **Thread** is a *schedulable execution unit*. Threads run *inside* Processes.
- [x] **Properties** are typed, named, and scoped via `applies to`. Standard sets: *Timing, Memory, Communication, Thread, Process, Deployment, Reliability, Security*.
- [x] **Features** are external interfaces: ports (data, event, event data), parameters, subprogram access, data access, bus access, feature groups.
- [x] **Connections** link features. Type compatibility rule must hold.
- [x] **Flows** enable end-to-end analysis: `flow source`, `flow sink`, `flow path`.
- [x] **Modes and Mode Transitions** capture operational variability; `initial mode` is mandatory.
- [x] **Annex sublanguages** extend the core: *Behavior Annex*, *Error Model Annex*, *Requirements Annex*, *ARINC653 Annex*, *STOOD Annex*.
- [x] AADL enables **automatic analysis**: schedulability, latency, memory footprint, safety (FMEA/FTA), reliability (MTTF).
- [x] AADL models can be **auto-translated** into skeletal code (glue code) — true MBSD payoff.
- [x] The MBSD workflow with AADL: **Requirements → Type Modeling → Implementation Modeling → Properties → Binding → Analysis → Code Generation → Continuous Verification**.
- [x] The **RMS schedulability bound** for $n$ threads: $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- [x] The **end-to-end latency** is the sum of processing, queuing, and transmission delays: $L_{\text{total}} = \sum L_{\text{leg}}$.
- [x] The architectural model is the **single source of truth** — modifications propagate automatically to analysis and code.
- [x] Real-world analogy: AADL is to embedded software what an *architectural blueprint* is to a building.
- [x] KTU high-yield tip: When asked to "explain AADL," *always* mention the standard number, the four categories, the type/implementation split, the role of properties, and the analysis types.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MBSD Based Software Development - Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** MBSD Based Software Development - Requirements # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, requirements engineering refers to the systematic capture, modeling, analysis, validation, and traceability of stakeholder needs through **executable and semi-formal models**, rather than through traditional unstructured natural-language documents. Per the KTU 2024 scheme, MBSD requirements are treated as **first-class model artifacts** (often represented using notations such as **SysML**, **UML**, **KAOS**, or **i\***) that drive downstream design, code generation, and verification activities through bidirectional traceability links. The IEEE 29148:2018 standard (referenced by KTU) defines a **requirement** as a statement that translates or expresses a need and its associated constraints and conditions, while in MBSD, this need is represented as an **abstract syntactic-semantic construct** inside a model. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A requirement in MBSD is NOT just a sentence in a Word document. It is a **traceable, verifiable model element** connected to use cases, state machines, structural blocks, and test cases via dedicated dependency relations (`<>`, `<>`, `<>`, `<>`). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are building a house. In the **traditional approach**, the architect writes a long paragraph: *"The house should have 4 rooms, be earthquake-resistant, and cost less than 50 lakhs."* This paragraph gets passed to the engineer, who interprets it differently than the client wanted — leading to mismatches and rework. In the **MBSD approach**, the architect instead produces a **scale 3D model** with tagged elements: `Room.R1` (size = 20m²), `Material.Wall` (strength = seismic-zone-5), `Budget.Constraint` (≤ 50L). Every requirement is a **labeled, connected, measurable element** in the model. If the budget changes, you can visually see which rooms, walls, and windows are affected. The model becomes the **single source of truth (SSOT)**. | Traditional Requirements | MBSD Requirements | |---|---| | Plain text in SRS document | Model elements with formal semantics | | Ambiguous natural language | Structured SysML/UML/KAOS notations | | Manual traceability | Automated traceability links | | Static, hard to simulate | Executable, simulatable, verifiable | | Decoupled from design | Tightly coupled to design & test models | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** In MBSD, the requirement model is **simulatable** — you can run it, test scenarios against it, and detect conflicts before any code is written. This is the essence of *Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)* philosophy. ## 1.3 Types of Requirements in MBSD Per KTU PECNT634 Module 2 syllabus: 1. **Stakeholder Requirements (StR):** High-level "why" — goals, missions, and constraints of the system from the user/business perspective. 2. **System Requirements (SyR):** Refined "what" — functional and non-functional system-level requirements. 3. **Software Requirements (SwR):** Detailed "how much / how well" — decomposed into the software component. These are hierarchically linked in MBSD through SysML `<>` and `<>` dependencies. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The MBSD Requirements Engineering Process (Structured Logic) The MBSD requirements lifecycle follows a **closed-loop, iterative, model-centric** workflow: 1. **Elicitation & Stakeholder Analysis** - Identify actors, goals, and concerns using goal-oriented methods (e.g., **i\*** for goal modeling, **KAOS** for goal-obstacle refinement). - Output: Initial stakeholder model + goal hierarchy. 2. **Modeling & Specification** - Convert goals into structured requirements diagrams: - **SysML Requirement Diagram** for hierarchical requirement decomposition. - **Use Case Diagram** for functional behavior boundaries. - **Block Definition Diagram (BDD)** for system structure. - Output: Formal, semi-executable requirement models. 3. **Analysis & Negotiation** - Detect conflicts (e.g., safety vs. cost, performance vs. battery life) using formal methods. - Use **goal obstruction analysis** (KAOS) or **NFR (Non-Functional Requirements) framework** for trade-offs. 4. **Validation & Verification (V&V)** - **Validation:** Are we building the right system? (against stakeholder needs). - **Verification:** Are we building the system right? (against specified requirements). - In MBSD: validation via simulation of executable requirement models; verification via model checking, test-case generation. 5. **Traceability & Evolution Management** - Maintain bidirectional links between requirements, design models, code, and tests. - Manage requirements volatility via **Change Impact Analysis** on the model graph. ## 2.2 Why MBSD Transforms Requirements Engineering | Traditional Pain Point | MBSD Solution | |---|---| | Ambiguity in natural language | Formal semantics (SysML/KAOS) | | Stale documentation | Model = live document | | Manual traceability matrix | Automatic dependency graphs | | Late defect detection | Early simulation & analysis | | Inconsistent stakeholder views | Unified model (SSOT) | | Difficulty handling NFRs | NFR modeling frameworks integrated | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (For Software Metrics) Although requirements engineering is qualitative, KTU examiners frequently test **software metrics** linked to MBSD requirements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the following table, the vertical pipe symbol used inside mathematical expressions (e.g., set notation, cardinality) is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. | Metric | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | KTU Exam Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Requirement Stability Index (RSI)** | $RSI = 1 - \dfrac{R_c}{R_t}$ | $R_c$ = changed requirements, $R_t$ = total requirements | dimensionless (0–1) | High | | **Traceability Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{R_{tr}}{R_t} \times 100\%$ | $R_{tr}$ = traced requirements | percent (%) | Very High | | **Requirement Volatility (RV)** | $RV = \dfrac{R_c}{R_t} \times 100\%$ | Measures requirement churn | percent (%) | High | | **Model Completeness (MC)** | $MC = \dfrac{R_{impl}}{R_t} \times 100\%$ | $R_{impl}$ = requirements implemented | percent (%) | High | | **Verification Coverage (VC)** | $VC = \dfrac{R_{ver}}{R_t} \times 100\%$ | $R_{ver}$ = verified requirements | percent (%) | Medium | | **Average Fan-out (Trace Depth)** | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert T_i \vert}{n}$ | $\vert T_i \vert$ = outgoing trace links from req $i$ | links / requirement | Medium | | **Defect Density per Requirement (DDR)** | $DDR = \dfrac{D_{found}}{R_t}$ | $D_{found}$ = defects linked to requirements | defects / requirement | Low | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive (ISO 26262):** MBSD requirements with SysML ensure functional safety traceability. - **Avionics (DO-178C):** Bidirectional traceability between requirements, design models, and test cases is mandatory. - **Medical Devices (FDA, IEC 62304):** Model-based requirements serve as auditable artifacts for regulatory submission. - **IoT / Embedded Systems:** SysML models can be auto-generated into C/C++/Simulink code. > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 evaluations, examiners reward **diagram-based answers**. A student who draws a SysML Requirement Diagram alongside prose typically scores 2–3 marks higher than a pure text answer for the same question. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing MBSD Requirement Metrics **Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):** A model-based development project for an Automated Teller Machine (ATM) has the following metrics tracked at the end of Iteration 3: - Total requirements in the SysML model: $R_t = 250$ - Requirements with at least one trace link (forward or backward): $R_{tr} = 215$ - Requirements that have been changed since the previous iteration: $R_c = 40$ - Requirements that have been implemented and tested: $R_{impl} = 190$ - Requirements formally verified against stakeholder goals: $R_{ver} = 175$ - Number of post-release defects attributed to requirement gaps: $D_{found} = 12$ **Required:** Compute (a) Traceability Coverage, (b) Requirement Stability Index, (c) Model Completeness, (d) Verification Coverage, (e) Defect Density per Requirement. Comment on project health. --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution #### (a) Traceability Coverage (TC) $$ \begin{aligned} TC &= \dfrac{R_{tr}}{R_t} \times 100\% \\ &= \dfrac{215}{250} \times 100\% \\ &= 0.86 \times 100\% \\ &= 86\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 86% of requirements are linked to other model artifacts (use cases, design blocks, test cases). Industry benchmark (KTU expectation): **≥ 90%** for safety-critical, **≥ 80%** for general systems. The project is **borderline** and requires improvement. --- #### (b) Requirement Stability Index (RSI) $$ \begin{aligned} RSI &= 1 - \dfrac{R_c}{R_t} \\ &= 1 - \dfrac{40}{250} \\ &= 1 - 0.16 \\ &= 0.84 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** An RSI of **0.84** indicates moderate stability. KTU benchmark: $RSI \geq 0.90$ for a mature iteration. With 16% of requirements churning, the project may face **change-impact risk**. --- #### (c) Model Completeness (MC) $$ \begin{aligned} MC &= \dfrac{R_{impl}}{R_t} \times 100\% \\ &= \dfrac{190}{250} \times 100\% \\ &= 76\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 76% implementation completeness is reasonable for Iteration 3 (assumed to be mid-project). However, **24% gap** must be tracked through the backlog. --- #### (d) Verification Coverage (VC) $$ \begin{aligned} VC &= \dfrac{R_{ver}}{R_t} \times 100\% \\ &= \dfrac{175}{250} \times 100\% \\ &= 70\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 70% verified — KTU benchmark target ≥ 85% before release. The project has a **verification debt** of 15% that must be cleared. --- #### (e) Defect Density per Requirement (DDR) $$ \begin{aligned} DDR &= \dfrac{D_{found}}{R_t} \\ &= \dfrac{12}{250} \\ &= 0.048 \ \text{defects per requirement} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Industry acceptable: $DDR \leq 0.05$. The project is at the edge — slight increase may indicate process issues. Often this correlates with low TC. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Health Diagnosis | Metric | Computed Value | KTU Target | Verdict | Action Needed | |---|---|---|---|---| | TC | 86% | ≥ 90% | Borderline | Add trace links for remaining 14% | | RSI | 0.84 | ≥ 0.90 | Moderate | Freeze baseline, control changes | | MC | 76% | ≥ 75% (iteration-dependent) | Healthy | Continue backlog burndown | | VC | 70% | ≥ 85% | Weak | Schedule V&V activities | | DDR | 0.048 | ≤ 0.05 | At threshold | Improve elicitation quality | ## 3.4 Derivation: Requirement Volatility vs. Defect Density Correlation A well-known empirical relation in MBSD literature (used in KTU problem framing): $$ \begin{aligned} DDR_{predicted} &= \alpha \cdot RV + \beta \cdot (1 - TC) + \gamma \\ \end{aligned} $$ Where empirical coefficients (typical): $\alpha = 0.10$, $\beta = 0.15$, $\gamma = 0.01$. Substituting our values: $$ \begin{aligned} RV &= \dfrac{40}{250} = 0.16 \\ TC &= 0.86 \\ DDR_{predicted} &= (0.10 \times 0.16) + (0.15 \times (1 - 0.86)) + 0.01 \\ &= 0.016 + 0.021 + 0.01 \\ &= 0.047 \ \text{defects / requirement} \end{aligned} $$ This matches our observed DDR of 0.048 with **< 2% error**, confirming the model is well-calibrated. > [!IMPORTANT] > The above formula is **not** expected to be memorized verbatim, but students must understand that **higher volatility** and **lower traceability** → **more defects**. This is a high-yield KTU concept. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: MBSD Requirements Engineering Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Stakeholder Goals"] --> A1["Goal Modeling
i-star or KAOS"] A1 --> A2["Stakeholder Requirements"] A2 --> A3["SysML Requirement Diagram"] A3 --> A4["System Requirements"] A4 --> A5["Software Requirements"] A5 --> A6["Use Case Diagram"] A6 --> A7["Structural Model BDD"] A5 --> A8["Behavior Model STM"] A7 --> A9["Traceability Matrix"] A8 --> A9 A9 --> A10["Simulation and Model Checking"] A10 --> A11["Validation Verdict"] A11 -->|Pass| A12["Code Generation"] A11 -->|Fail| A13["Requirement Refinement"] A13 --> A3 A12 --> A14["Test Cases Auto Generated"] A14 --> A15["Verification Report"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: SysML Requirement Decomposition Pattern ```mermaid flowchart TB R0["REQ001
Safe ATM Transaction"] R1["REQ001.1
PIN Validation"] R2["REQ001.2
Encryption AES256"] R3["REQ001.3
Session Timeout 60s"] R4["REQ001.3.1
Auto Logout"] R5["REQ001.3.2
Lock Account after 3 fails"] R0 --> R1 R0 --> R2 R0 --> R3 R3 --> R4 R3 --> R5 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Traceability Relationship Types ```mermaid flowchart LR STK["Stakeholder"] -->|raises| GR["Goal"] GR -->|refined to| REQ["Requirement"] REQ -->|satisfied by| DSG["Design Model"] REQ -->|refined by| UC["Use Case"] REQ -->|verified by| TST["Test Case"] DSG -->|generates| CODE["Source Code"] TST -->|executes| CODE CODE -->|validates| REQ ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: NFR Conflict Resolution Pattern ```mermaid flowchart TD B0["NFR Performance
Response time <= 2s"] B1["NFR Security
AES-256 encryption"] B2["NFR Cost
Budget <= 50k"] B3["Conflict Detected
Performance vs Cost"] B4["Trade-off Analysis
Model Simulation"] B5["Resolved NFR Set"] B0 --> B3 B2 --> B3 B1 --> B4 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** When drawing these diagrams in exams, label your edges with SysML stereotype names (`<>`, `<>`, `<>`, `<>`) — this earns extra marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q:** Differentiate between traditional requirements engineering and MBSD-based requirements engineering. List any three advantages of the latter. **Model Answer (≈120 words):** | Aspect | Traditional RE | MBSD-based RE | |---|---|---| | **Notation** | Natural language | SysML / KAOS / i\* models | | **Traceability** | Manual, error-prone | Automatic via model links | | **Simulation** | Not possible | Executable requirement models | | **Documentation** | Separate SRS doc | Model is the documentation | | **Consistency** | Hard to maintain | Model checker enforces it | **Three Advantages of MBSD:** 1. **Early defect detection** through executable simulation of requirement models before coding. 2. **Automatic traceability** across stakeholder → system → software → test artifacts. 3. **Code generation** directly from validated requirements, reducing manual coding errors. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q:** Define *Requirement Traceability* and explain the two main types with examples. **Model Answer:** Requirement Traceability is the ability to follow the life of a requirement **forward and backward** through the development lifecycle using documented links. **1. Forward Traceability (Forward-from):** - Links requirements → design, code, tests. - Example: `REQ-Authentication → UseCase-Login → Module-Auth → TestCase-TC01`. - **Purpose:** Ensures every requirement is implemented and tested. **2. Backward Traceability (Backward-from):** - Links design/code/tests → original requirement. - Example: `TestCase-TC01 → REQ-Authentication → Goal-Security`. - **Purpose:** Justifies that every artifact exists because of a valid requirement. > [!NOTE] > A third type — **bidirectional traceability** — is implicit in MBSD because model links naturally support both directions. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme] **Q:** Model-Based Software Development relies heavily on formal requirements modeling. **(a)** Explain the SysML Requirement Diagram with a neat sketch. Discuss its key components and the stereotype relationships used in requirement modeling. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A critical-care patient monitoring system has the following MBSD metrics recorded at the end of Sprint 4: - Total requirements: $R_t = 320$ - Traced requirements: $R_{tr} = 288$ - Changed requirements since Sprint 3: $R_c = 48$ - Verified requirements: $R_{ver} = 256$ - Defects attributed to unclear requirements: $D = 20$ Compute (i) Traceability Coverage, (ii) Requirement Stability Index, (iii) Verification Coverage, and (iv) Defect Density. Comment on the suitability of the system for safety-critical deployment. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution for A(a) — SysML Requirement Diagram (7 Marks) **Key Components (4 marks):** 1. **Requirement Element:** Rectangular box with `«requirement»` stereotype, containing `Id`, `Text`, `Priority`, `Status`, `Source`, `Risk`, `Verification Method`. 2. **Containment (Composition):** Solid line with filled diamond — represents *decomposition* of a parent requirement into sub-requirements. 3. **Derive Requirement (`<>`):** Dashed arrow with `«deriveReqt»` — shows that one requirement is derived/refined from another (e.g., stakeholder → system requirement). 4. **Satisfy (`<>`):** Dashed arrow with `«satisfy»` — shows that a design model element satisfies a requirement. 5. **Verify (`<>`):** Dashed arrow with `«verify»` — links test cases to requirements. 6. **Trace (`<>`):** Dashed arrow with `«trace»` — generic bidirectional traceability. 7. **Copy (`<>`):** Used when the same requirement appears in multiple diagrams. 8. **Refine (`<>`):** Used when a requirement is refined into a more concrete one. **Neat Sketch (3 marks):** - Draw a parent requirement box (e.g., REQ-001: "Monitor Heart Rate"). - Decompose into three child requirements via composition. - Show a `Block` element with `<>` arrow to the parent. - Show a `TestCase` with `<>` arrow to the child. - Use a `«trace»` arrow between two related requirements. --- #### Solution for A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks) **(i) Traceability Coverage:** $$ \begin{aligned} TC &= \dfrac{R_{tr}}{R_t} \times 100\% = \dfrac{288}{320} \times 100\% = 90\% \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Requirement Stability Index:** $$ \begin{aligned} RSI &= 1 - \dfrac{R_c}{R_t} = 1 - \dfrac{48}{320} = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85 \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Verification Coverage:** $$ \begin{aligned} VC &= \dfrac{R_{ver}}{R_t} \times 100\% = \dfrac{256}{320} \times 100\% = 80\% \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Defect Density per Requirement:** $$ \begin{aligned} DDR &= \dfrac{D}{R_t} = \dfrac{20}{320} = 0.0625 \ \text{defects/requirement} \end{aligned} $$ **Suitability Comment (2 marks):** - TC = 90% meets the **safety-critical threshold** (≥ 90% per IEC 62304). - RSI = 0.85 is **moderate** — 15% volatility is high for safety-critical; baseline freeze is recommended. - VC = 80% is **below** the safety-critical target (≥ 95%). - DDR = 0.0625 **exceeds** the acceptable ceiling of 0.05. - **Verdict:** The system is **NOT yet suitable** for safety-critical deployment. Additional V&V iterations, stricter change control, and improved requirements elicitation are required. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **Q:** Requirements play a central role in MBSD-based development. **(a)** Explain the concept of **Goal-Oriented Requirements Engineering (GORE)** using the KAOS framework. How does KAOS support requirements derivation in MBSD? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a Smart Traffic Control System, derive a requirement hierarchy using SysML notation. Identify at least 4 sub-requirements and link them using appropriate stereotypes. Justify how this hierarchy supports traceability and verification. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution for B(a) — GORE & KAOS (7 Marks) **Definition (2 marks):** Goal-Oriented Requirements Engineering (GORE) is a paradigm where requirements are derived systematically from high-level **goals** (stakeholder objectives) using techniques like KAOS, i\*, and Tropos. KAOS (Knowledge Acquisition in Object-Oriented Systems) is one of the most influential GORE frameworks. **Core Concepts of KAOS (3 marks):** 1. **Goals:** Prescriptive statements of stakeholder intent (e.g., "Maximize throughput"). 2. **Obstacles:** Conditions that prevent goal satisfaction (e.g., "Sensor malfunction"). 3. **Agents:** Entities (human/software/hardware) responsible for satisfying goals. 4. **Operations / Objects:** Domain entities over which goals operate. 5. **AND/OR Refinement:** Goals are decomposed into sub-goals using AND-decomposition (all sub-goals must be satisfied) or OR-decomposition (any one sub-goal may satisfy the parent). **How KAOS Supports MBSD (2 marks):** - Provides **formal semantics** — goals can be expressed in temporal logic (LTL/CTL) and model-checked. - Enables **obstacle analysis** for early conflict detection. - Generates **operationalized requirements** that map directly to SysML requirement diagrams. - Supports **traceability** from stakeholder intent down to software specifications through AND/OR refinement trees. --- #### Solution for B(b) — Smart Traffic SysML Hierarchy (7 Marks) **Parent Requirement:** - `REQ-001: Optimize Urban Traffic Flow` **Sub-Requirements (with stereotypes):** 1. `REQ-001.1: Real-time Vehicle Detection` (derived via `<>` from REQ-001). 2. `REQ-001.2: Adaptive Signal Timing` (`<>` → Block: SignalController). 3. `REQ-001.3: Emergency Vehicle Preemption` (`<>` → TestCase: TC-Emerg). 4. `REQ-001.4: Data Logging and Reporting` (`<>` from REQ-001). 5. `REQ-001.5: Fault Tolerance (sensor fail-safe)` (`<>` to REQ-001.1). **Hierarchy Sketch (3 marks):** Draw a SysML Requirement Diagram with REQ-001 at the top, 5 sub-requirements via composition, and arrows to design blocks (SignalController, SensorArray) with `<>`, plus test cases with `<>`. **Justification (2 marks):** - **Traceability:** Every sub-requirement is linked to its parent AND to a design/test artifact, enabling change-impact analysis. - **Verification:** Each `<>` link enables automatic test-case execution and coverage reporting. - **Change Management:** Modifying REQ-001.2's timing constraint propagates visibly through the model, allowing easy stakeholder review. - **Compliance:** This structure supports ISO 26262 / IEC 61508 audits where requirement hierarchy must be provably complete. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the denominator** in RSI/RSV formulas — many students compute $\dfrac{R_c}{R_t}$ and stop, forgetting the $1 -$ prefix for RSI. > 2. **Drawing UML use-case diagrams when SysML is asked** — these are different notations. SysML has dedicated `«requirement»` elements. > 3. **Confusing traceability types** — backward traceability is from artifact → requirement, not requirement → artifact. > 4. **Ignoring units** — DDR is in *defects per requirement*, not raw count. Always normalize. > 5. **Not mentioning NFRs** — in patient/safety systems, examiners expect explicit discussion of safety, security, and reliability as first-class NFRs. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **MBSD Requirement = Model Element**, not a sentence — it has ID, text, priority, status, verification method. - **SysML Requirement Diagram** uses stereotypes: `«requirement»`, `«deriveReqt»`, `«satisfy»`, `«verify»`, `«trace»`, `«refine»`, `«copy»`. - **Three Levels of Requirements:** Stakeholder → System → Software (hierarchically linked). - **GORE frameworks:** KAOS (with AND/OR goal refinement), i\* (with strategic dependency modeling). - **NFR Modeling:** Must explicitly handle trade-offs (performance vs. cost, safety vs. usability). - **Key Metrics to Memorize:** - $TC = \dfrac{R_{tr}}{R_t} \times 100\%$ (target ≥ 90% for safety-critical) - $RSI = 1 - \dfrac{R_c}{R_t}$ (target ≥ 0.90) - $RV = \dfrac{R_c}{R_t} \times 100\%$ - $MC = \dfrac{R_{impl}}{R_t} \times 100\%$ - $VC = \dfrac{R_{ver}}{R_t} \times 100\%$ (target ≥ 95% for safety-critical) - $DDR = \dfrac{D}{R_t}$ (target ≤ 0.05) - **Traceability Types:** Forward (req → code/test) and Backward (code/test → req). MBSD enables bidirectional via model links. - **Validation vs. Verification:** Validation = right system? Verification = system right? - **Change Impact:** Higher volatility + lower traceability → higher defect density. - **Real-world standards:** ISO 26262 (automotive), DO-178C (avionics), IEC 62304 (medical), IEC 61508 (industrial safety). - **KTU-Favorite Topics:** SysML diagrams, KAOS goal refinement, metric calculation, NFR trade-off analysis. - **Diagrams Always Score:** In every Part B answer, draw at least one SysML requirement diagram — this alone can earn 2–3 bonus marks. - **Safety-critical targets:** TC ≥ 90%, VC ≥ 95%, RSI ≥ 0.90, DDR ≤ 0.05. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Strategy:** When you see "MBSD requirements" in an exam question, your answer should always contain: **(1)** definition, **(2)** SysML or KAOS diagram, **(3)** at least one metric calculation if numerical, and **(4)** a real-world standard reference. This 4-part structure is the KTU examiner's gold standard.

topics.title: Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Analysis in Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, **Analysis** refers to the systematic, model-driven investigation of a system's requirements, structure, and behavior to produce precise, executable, and verifiable analysis models that bridge the gap between informal stakeholder requirements and formal software design. The analysis phase is the **first modeling activity** in the MBSD lifecycle, where raw requirements are transformed into semantically rich, platform-independent models (PIMs) that capture the *what* (functional and non-functional requirements) before any *how* (implementation) is decided.

According to the **OMG (Object Management Group)** standards adopted in MBSD curricula, analysis is performed using standardized modeling languages such as **UML 2.5** (Unified Modeling Language) and **SysML** (Systems Modeling Language), along with profile extensions and OCL (Object Constraint Language) for precise specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Analysis):**
> The analysis phase in MBSD emphasizes the construction of **Use-Case Models**, **Analysis Class Models**, **Dynamic Behavior Models**, and the application of **analysis patterns** and **design heuristics** to ensure traceability from requirements to design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a new hospital**.

- You don't start by buying bricks or hiring masons (that would be *implementation*).
- Instead, you first **analyze** the client's needs: how many patients, what departments, what traffic flow, what safety regulations.
- You draw **blueprints**: a floor plan (static structure), an electrical wiring diagram (interactions), and a fire-evacuation sequence (dynamic behavior).
- These blueprints are **models** — abstract, precise, and verifiable *before* construction.

In the same way, **Analysis in MBSD** is the *blueprint-drawing* phase of software. Stakeholders give you vague, conflicting needs (like a client saying "I want a fast, secure, and user-friendly app"). Your job as an analyst is to:

1. **Elicit** the real needs.
2. **Model** them precisely using UML/SysML.
3. **Validate** the models with stakeholders.
4. **Trace** each requirement to a model element.

The result is a set of **Platform-Independent Models (PIMs)** that any competent development team can take forward to design and code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:**
> - *Requirements Engineering* (Module 1 territory) → produces textual, often ambiguous **requirements documents**.
> - *Analysis Modeling* (Module 2) → transforms those requirements into **formal, graphical, and semi-formal models** with executable semantics.

## 1.3 Why Analysis is Critical in MBSD

| Aspect | Without MBSD Analysis | With MBSD Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Errors Caught** | Late, during testing (costly) | Early, during modeling (cheap) |
| **Stakeholder Validation** | Through code demos (risky) | Through model walkthroughs (safe) |
| **Traceability** | Manual, often broken | Automated via model relations |
| **Communication** | Text-based, ambiguous | Graphical, standardized (UML) |
| **Reusability** | Low (tangled code) | High (analysis patterns) |

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Model**: An abstract, simplified representation of a system focusing on specific concerns.
- **PIM (Platform-Independent Model)**: A model that does not refer to implementation technology.
- **PSM (Platform-Specific Model)**: A model tailored to a specific platform (e.g., Java EE, .NET).
- **MDA (Model-Driven Architecture)**: OMG's framework with **CIM → PIM → PSM** transformations.
- **Use Case**: A description of a system's behavior under specific conditions from an actor's perspective.
- **Analysis Class**: A stereotyped UML class representing a conceptual role (*Entity*, *Boundary*, *Control*).
- **OCL (Object Constraint Language)**: A formal language for writing invariants and constraints on UML models.
- **Round-Trip Engineering**: The bidirectional synchronization between models and code.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Analysis Workflow in MBSD

The analysis phase follows a **systematic, iterative workflow**. Each step produces specific model artifacts that feed into the next.

### Step 1: Requirements Consolidation
- Inputs: Stakeholder requests, business goals, regulatory constraints, domain knowledge.
- Activity: Resolve conflicts, eliminate redundancies, prioritize (using **MoSCoW**: *Must*, *Should*, *Could*, *Won't*).
- Output: **Requirements Specification Document (RSD)** + initial **Use-Case Diagram**.

### Step 2: Use-Case Modeling (Functional Analysis)
- Identify **actors** (human users, external systems, devices).
- Identify **use cases** (goals an actor wants to achieve).
- Establish **relationships**:
  - `<<include>>` — mandatory shared behavior (e.g., *Authentication* is included in every secure use case).
  - `<<extend>>` — optional, conditional behavior (e.g., *Apply Discount* extends *Checkout*).
  - **Generalization** — actor or use-case inheritance.
- Write **use-case narratives** (the *fully-dressed* format): preconditions, postconditions, main flow, alternative flows, exception flows.

### Step 3: Static Analysis — Building the Analysis Class Model
- Identify **analysis classes** using the **"Three Amigos" stereotype rule**:
  - **Boundary classes** (the *interface* layer — windows, APIs, web pages, sensors).
  - **Entity classes** (the *data* layer — persistent information, domain objects).
  - **Control classes** (the *logic* layer — coordinators, transactions, use-case controllers).
- Apply **CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator)** cards to assign responsibilities.
- Establish **associations**, **aggregations**, **compositions**, and **generalizations** (the "**is-a**" vs. "**has-a**" distinction).

### Step 4: Dynamic Analysis — Building the Behavior Model
- For each use case, construct:
  - **Sequence Diagrams** (time-ordered object interactions).
  - **Communication Diagrams** (link-centric interactions).
  - **State Machine Diagrams** (for objects with significant lifecycles, like *Order* or *Loan*).
  - **Activity Diagrams** (for workflows and parallel processes).

### Step 5: Constraint Specification with OCL
- Use **OCL** to express invariants, pre/postconditions, and derived attributes.
- Example: `context Order inv: self.items->forAll(i | i.quantity > 0)`
  - *Meaning*: For every order, all items must have a positive quantity.

### Step 6: Model Validation & Verification
- **Validation**: Are we building the *right* system? (Check against stakeholder needs.)
- **Verification**: Are we building the system *right*? (Check internal consistency, OCL invariants.)
- Techniques: **Walkthroughs**, **Inspections**, **Model Checking** (formal), **Simulation** (executable UML).

## 2.2 Analysis Patterns

**Analysis Patterns** are reusable, proven solutions to recurring analysis problems. They are not code templates but **conceptual templates** that suggest classes, responsibilities, and collaborations.

| Pattern | Purpose | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Entity-Interface-Control** | Three-tier conceptual separation | Web apps, embedded systems |
| **Transaction Script** | Procedural handling of use cases | Simple CRUD apps |
| **Domain Model** | Rich object model mirroring real-world | Complex business domains (banking) |
| **Table Module** | One class per database table | Data-centric enterprise apps |
| **Service Layer** | Defines application boundary and business rules | SOA, microservices |
| **Observer** | Decouples subject from dependent observers | Event-driven UIs, MVC |
| **State** | Encapsulates state-specific behavior | Order processing, UI controls |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Analysis patterns differ from **Design Patterns (GoF)**. Analysis patterns operate at the *conceptual* level (no language constructs), while design patterns are *language-specific* (e.g., Java `interface`, C++ template).

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Rule | Application in Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Use-Case Realization | $UC_{i} \to \{ SD_{i}, CD_{i}, CD_{class} \}$ | Each use case maps to a sequence + class diagram slice |
| Stereotype Rule | $\text{Class} \in \{ B, E, C \}$ | Boundary, Entity, Control |
| Cardinality | $1..1, 0..*, 1..*$ | UML multiplicities on associations |
| OCL Invariant | $\text{context T inv: } P$ | $P$ must always be true for type $T$ |
| Model Completeness | $C = \frac{\vert R_{covered} \vert}{\vert R_{total} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Traceability metric (0–100) |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (for State Machines) | $V(G) = E - N + 2$ | Where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes |
| Coupling Metric | $C_{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{i}$ | $c_{i}$ = coupling of module $i$ to others |
| Cohesion Metric | $LCOM$ (Lack of Cohesion of Methods) | Lower is better |
| Refactoring Threshold | $V(G) \leq 10$ | Recommended per method/operation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Be ready to compute **cyclomatic complexity** for control flow or state diagrams — it's a favorite 7-mark question in the ESE.

## 2.4 The Three Layers of Analysis

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PRESENTATION / BOUNDARY LAYER              │  ← UI, screens, APIs
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LOGIC / CONTROL LAYER                      │  ← Use-case controllers, transactions
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  DATA / ENTITY LAYER                        │  ← Domain objects, persistence
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

This **layered architecture** is the de-facto result of analysis modeling and is preserved through design and implementation. It promotes **separation of concerns**, **testability**, and **maintainability**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR)**: SysML analysis models drive ECU software generation.
- **Aviation (DO-178C)**: Model-based analysis is required for safety-critical certification.
- **Healthcare (IEC 62304)**: Analysis models support medical device software validation.
- **Finance**: Domain models formalize regulatory compliance (Basel III, GDPR).
- **IoT/Embedded**: State-machine analysis of sensor-actuator systems enables formal verification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Derivations

## 3.1 Procedure: Deriving an Analysis Class Model from Use Cases

This is a **textual-NLP-inspired** but UML-formal procedure that examiners love to test.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Parse the Use-Case Narrative.**
Read the fully-dressed use case. Highlight **nouns** (potential classes/attributes) and **verbs** (potential operations/responsibilities).

**Step 2 — Filter Nouns using the "Classes" Test.**
A noun is a candidate class if it:
- Has *independent existence* (not just a property of another).
- Has *responsibilities* (operations).
- Has *attributes* and *state*.
- Is *mentioned in multiple use cases* (reusable).

**Step 3 — Apply Stereotype Assignment.**
