> 1. **Confusing viewpoints with OMG layers.** CIM/PIM/PSM are *viewpoints* (3). M0/M1/M2/M3 are *meta-layers* (4). Do not mix them.
> 2. **Forgetting the Platform Model.** Many students answer "PIM → PSM" without mentioning the PM. Since the PM is a *directive input*, omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Treating code generation as the *only* transformation.** Remember: M2M is also a transformation, and arguably the *most important* one.
> 4. **Mixing up OCL and XMI roles.** OCL is for **constraints**; XMI is for **interchange**. Reversing these is a sure half-mark loss.
> 5. **Not drawing the transformation diagram.** In 14-mark questions, the diagram carries at least 1–2 marks. Always include the Mermaid-style chain.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Definition (verbatim, OMG):** *"An approach to IT system specification, organized so that functional requirements can be met independently of the specific platform(s) used in implementation."* (2 marks)
* **Three viewpoints:** **CIM → PIM → PSM** — business, structure, platform. (1 mark)
* **Four OMG pillars:** **UML, MOF, OCL, XMI**. (1 mark)
* **OMG four-layer meta-architecture:** **M0 (objects), M1 (model), M2 (meta-model), M3 (meta-meta-model / MOF)**. (1 mark)
* **Six assumptions of MDA:** models-are-primary, separation, standards, transformability, platform-modelled, portability. (1 mark)
* **Transformations:** **M2M** (PIM to PSM, often via QVT) and **M2T** (PSM to Code). Both are mandatory mentions. (1 mark)
* **Platform Model (PM):** A **directive input** to PIM → PSM transformation. Re-targeting to a new platform requires *only* changing the PM. (1 mark)
* **Single source of truth:** The **PIM** is the *primary, reusable* artefact. All platform code is derived from it. (1 mark)
* **Common exam trick question:** *"Does MDA replace UML?"* — **No.** MDA *uses* UML as its primary notation; UML is a pillar, not a competitor.
* **Final one-liner for viva:** *"MDA says: change the model, not the code."* ✔
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of MBSD Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of MBSD Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)** is a software engineering paradigm in which **formal, abstract models** are treated as the **primary artifacts** of the software development lifecycle, from which executable code, documentation, and test cases are systematically derived through **automated or semi-automated transformations**.

MBSD is also commonly referred to as **Model-Driven Development (MDD)** or **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**. It shifts the developer's focus from writing implementation code (in a specific programming language) to **constructing precise models** using a Domain-Specific Modeling Language (DSML) or a general-purpose modeling standard such as the **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** or **Systems Modeling Language (SysML)**.

The methodology is grounded in the principle that *"models are first-class citizens of the engineering process"* — a concept formalized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)** through its **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)** standard.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> Imagine you are constructing a house. In a *traditional (code-centric)* approach, the builder would start laying bricks and pouring concrete directly, then describe what was built. In the *MBSD (model-centric)* approach, the architect first creates a **detailed, formal blueprint** — including structural, electrical, and plumbing models. Every contractor reads the **same blueprint**, and the final construction is essentially a *direct realization* of the model. If the client wants a change (e.g., add a window), you modify the **blueprint first**, then propagate the change to the construction.

| Traditional Approach | MBSD Approach |
|---|---|
| **Code is the truth** | **Model is the truth** |
| Manual translation of design to code | Automated/semi-automated code generation |
| High maintenance cost | Model-level modifications, regenerated code |
| Tight coupling to platform | Platform-independent models reused across targets |

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Model:** An abstract, simplified representation of a system using a well-defined notation.
> - **Metamodel:** A model that defines the structure (syntax, semantics, rules) of other models.
> - **Transformation:** A rule-based mapping from a source model to a target model.
> - **Code Generation:** Automatic production of source code from a model.
> - **DSL (Domain-Specific Language):** A modeling language tailored to a specific application domain.
> - **CIM / PIM / PSM:** The three canonical abstraction levels in MDA (see §2).
> - **Round-trip Engineering:** Keeping model and code synchronized in both directions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Core Principles of MBSD

MBSD is built upon **four foundational pillars**. Every KTU exam question on this topic will likely test at least one of these.

1. **Abstraction through Modeling**
   Systems are described at multiple, well-separated levels of abstraction. Each level hides unnecessary implementation detail and exposes only what is essential at that level.

2. **Models as Primary Artifacts**
   Models are *not* documentation that accompanies code — they **are** the development artifact. Code, tests, and configuration are *by-products* derived from models.

3. **Automated Transformations**
   Movement between abstraction levels is performed by **formal, repeatable transformation rules** (e.g., model-to-model, model-to-text). This eliminates manual translation errors.

4. **Domain-Specific Semantics**
   Modeling languages and tools are designed to reflect the **vocabulary and constraints of the problem domain**, not the target platform.

## 2.2 The OMG MDA Abstraction Pyramid

The **Object Management Group (OMG)** — the standards body behind CORBA, UML, and SysML — formalized the MDA reference architecture. It defines **three layers of models**:

- **CIM (Computation-Independent Model)** — also called the *domain model* or *business model*. It describes the system's purpose and environment in business terms, hiding all computational detail. Audience: domain experts, business analysts.
- **PIM (Platform-Independent Model)** — describes the system's structure and behaviour *without* committing to a specific execution platform (e.g., Java EE, .NET, Android). It captures logical entities, operations, and interactions. Audience: software architects.
- **PSM (Platform-Specific Model)** — refines the PIM by adding platform-specific details (e.g., "use Servlet API", "use Hibernate ORM"). Each PSM is tightly bound to one execution platform. Audience: software implementers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformation Chain in MDA:**
> **CIM → (annotated) → PIM → (refined) → PSM → (code-gen) → Source Code / Executable**

The mapping from CIM → PIM is largely manual (driven by requirements analysis). The mapping from PIM → PSM and from PSM → Code can be **highly automated** using transformation engines such as **Acceleo, QVT, ATL, or Xtend**.

## 2.3 MBSD Lifecycle Phases

The MBSD process is **iterative** and **model-centric** at every stage:

1. **Domain Analysis** — Build the CIM; capture stakeholder requirements as use cases, activity diagrams, and stakeholder maps.
2. **System Analysis** — Construct PIMs using class diagrams, sequence diagrams, state machines; define non-functional constraints.
3. **Platform Selection** — Choose target platform(s) (web, mobile, embedded, cloud). Each platform selection defines a separate PSM branch.
4. **Model Refinement** — Transform PIM into one or more PSMs using model-to-model (M2M) transformations.
5. **Code Generation** — Apply model-to-text (M2T) transformations to produce compilable source code.
6. **Testing** — Generate test cases from models (model-based testing, MBT); verify the code conforms to the model.
7. **Deployment & Maintenance** — Models are versioned; changes to models trigger regeneration. **Round-trip engineering** keeps code and model synchronized when manual code edits are required.

## 2.4 Benefits of MBSD

- **Higher Productivity** — automation removes repetitive coding.
- **Platform Portability** — the same PIM can be deployed to multiple PSMs.
- **Improved Communication** — models are easier for non-programmers to validate.
- **Earlier Defect Detection** — model-level consistency checks catch errors before code is generated.
- **Documentation by Construction** — the model *is* the documentation; they never diverge.

## 2.5 Challenges of MBSD

- **High Initial Learning Curve** — mastering DSMLs and metamodeling.
- **Tool Maturity** — transformation engines and editors vary in quality.
- **Performance Constraints** — generated code may not match hand-optimized code.
- **Legacy Integration** — applying MBSD to existing codebases is non-trivial.
- **Model Management** — versioning, diffing, and merging of large models.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision cheat sheet** combining all key acronyms, definitions, and process characteristics examiners test in KTU 2024 Scheme papers.

| Term / Concept | Full Form | Definition / Role in MBSD | Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MBSD** | Model-Based Software Development | Paradigm where models are the primary development artifact | **High** |
| **MDA** | Model-Driven Architecture | OMG standard defining CIM, PIM, PSM layers | **High** |
| **CIM** | Computation-Independent Model | Business / domain model, no computational detail | **High** |
| **PIM** | Platform-Independent Model | Logical model, no commitment to execution platform | **High** |
| **PSM** | Platform-Specific Model | Refined model bound to one execution platform | **High** |
| **M2M** | Model-to-Model Transformation | Mapping source model → target model (e.g., PIM → PSM) | **Medium** |
| **M2T** | Model-to-Text Transformation | Mapping model → source code (e.g., PSM → Java) | **Medium** |
| **QVT** | Query/View/Transformation | OMG standard for M2M transformations | **Low–Medium** |
| **DSML** | Domain-Specific Modeling Language | Modeling language tailored to a domain | **Medium** |
| **MBT** | Model-Based Testing | Generating test cases from behavioural models | **Medium** |
| **OMG** | Object Management Group | Standards body behind MDA, UML, SysML | **Low** |
| **UML** | Unified Modeling Language | General-purpose visual modeling notation | **Medium** |
| **SysML** | Systems Modeling Language | UML extension for systems engineering | **Low** |
| **Round-trip Eng.** | Round-Trip Engineering | Synchronizing model and code in both directions | **Medium** |
| **Metamodel** | Metamodel | A model that defines the rules of other models | **Medium** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:**
> MBSD is the backbone of **embedded systems, automotive software (AUTOSAR), avionics (DO-178C), industrial control, and large-scale enterprise application development**. Companies like **Bosch, Airbus, Toyota, and Siemens** use MBSD principles to manage complexity, achieve certification, and enable cross-platform reuse. In the era of **cyber-physical systems (CPS)** and **digital twins**, MBSD is becoming an industry-essential skill.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed MBSD Methodology — Exhaustive Walk-through

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **complete, step-by-step MBSD workflow** as taught under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Every transition between artifacts is explicitly justified.

### Step 1 — Capture the Domain (CIM Construction)

The first activity is **building a Computation-Independent Model**. This is the *why* and *what* of the system — it contains:
- **Business goals** of the system
- **Stakeholders** and their concerns
- **Domain vocabulary** (a glossary of terms)
- **High-level processes** (activity diagrams, BPMN)

**Notation used:** Activity diagrams, use-case diagrams, stakeholder maps, business rule catalogs.
**Output artifact:** A CIM that business analysts and domain experts can validate.

### Step 2 — Translate Requirements to a PIM

The CIM is *annotated* with software-relevant concerns, then designers construct a **Platform-Independent Model**. The PIM expresses:
- **Structural view** → class diagrams
- **Behavioural view** → state machines, sequence diagrams
- **Interaction view** → communication diagrams
- **Constraint view** → OCL (Object Constraint Language) expressions

**Output artifact:** A PIM that captures logical entities and their relationships *without* choosing Java, .NET, Python, or any specific platform.

### Step 3 — Choose the Target Platform(s)

For each deployment scenario, an **execution platform** is selected. Examples:
- Web → Java EE / Spring
- Mobile → Android (Kotlin) / iOS (Swift)
- Embedded → C / C++ with RTOS

This decision is **external** to the PIM — it determines which PSM transformations will be applied.

### Step 4 — Apply M2M Transformation: PIM → PSM

For the chosen platform, an **M2M transformation engine** (e.g., **Acceleo, ATL, QVT, Xtend**) applies rule-based mappings that inject platform-specific types and patterns into the PIM.

For instance, a PIM class `Order` with a 1-to-many association to `OrderItem` may be transformed into a PSM where:
- `Order` is mapped to a **JPA `@Entity`** in Java
- `OrderItem` is mapped to a related `@OneToMany` collection
- A persistence XML descriptor is added

This step is **automated and repeatable**.

### Step 5 — Apply M2T Transformation: PSM → Code

The PSM is processed by a **model-to-text (M2T) engine** that produces source files, configuration files, and deployment descriptors. Templates define how each metaclass is rendered as text.

**Output artifacts:** Source code, build scripts (e.g., `pom.xml`, `Makefile`), test stubs, configuration files.

### Step 6 — Compile, Deploy, and Test

Standard compilation, packaging, and deployment follow. Importantly, **test cases can also be generated from the model** (Model-Based Testing) — for example, from a state machine, all transition paths are enumerated to produce test sequences.

### Step 7 — Maintain via Round-Trip Engineering

If developers must hand-edit generated code (for performance reasons, for example), the changes are propagated **back into the model** so that future regenerations preserve them. This is **round-trip engineering** and requires sophisticated tool support to avoid inconsistencies.

## 3.2 Transformation Chain — Formal Notation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to express the transformation chain in **symbolic form**. The standard notation is:

$$
T_{\text{M2M}} : \text{PIM} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{PSM}
$$

$$
T_{\text{M2T}} : \text{PSM} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Code}
$$

$$
T_{\text{End-to-End}} \;=\; T_{\text{M2T}} \circ T_{\text{M2M}} \;:\; \text{PIM} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Code}
$$

Where $\circ$ denotes **function composition**. The composition means "apply $T_{\text{M2M}}$ first, then apply $T_{\text{M2T}}$ to the result."

The **abstraction distance** between PIM and Code can be expressed as:

$$
D_{\text{abs}} \;=\; \text{depth}(\text{PSM}) \;-\; \text{depth}(\text{PIM})
$$

A higher $D_{\text{abs}}$ indicates **more platform-specific detail** must be injected by the transformation rules.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Traditional vs MBSD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is **frequently asked in KTU Part B questions (14 marks)**. Memorize the contrasts — they often form the core of a 7-mark sub-question.

| Dimension | Traditional / Code-Centric | MBSD / Model-Centric |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Artifact** | Source code | Formal model |
| **Documentation** | Maintained separately, often outdated | Model *is* the documentation |
| **Abstraction Levels** | Two (design + code) | Three or more (CIM, PIM, PSM, code) |
| **Platform Switching** | Manual rewrite of large code portions | Regenerate from same PIM into new PSM |
| **Error Detection** | Mostly at compile / run time | Possible at model-level (consistency checks) |
| **Stakeholder Involvement** | Limited to requirements phase | Continuous, model-based review |
| **Tooling Dependence** | Compilers, IDEs | Modeling editors + transformation engines + generators |
| **Reusability** | Function / class libraries | Reusable PIMs across projects / platforms |
| **Initial Cost** | Lower | Higher (training, tool licensing) |
| **Long-term Maintenance Cost** | Higher (manual sync) | Lower (model-level changes propagated) |

## 3.4 Verification — Why MBSD Improves Correctness

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Consistency Condition:**
> A model $M$ is *internally consistent* if and only if for every OCL constraint $C$ defined in its metamodel, $C(M) = \text{true}$.

A model $M$ is *consistent with its code* $K$ if:

$$
K \;=\; T_{\text{M2T}}(\,T_{\text{M2M}}(M_{\text{PIM}})\,)
$$

Any deviation between $K$ and the right-hand side indicates a **synchronization defect** that round-trip engineering must repair.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MBSD End-to-End Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete MBSD methodology pipeline** from business intent to deployed executable. All node IDs are alphanumeric and safe from reserved-keyword conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Domain Analysis] --> A2[Build CIM]
    A2 --> A3{CIM Validated by Stakeholders?}
    A3 -- No --> A1
    A3 -- Yes --> B1[Step 2: System Analysis]
    B1 --> B2[Construct PIM with Class State and Sequence Diagrams]
    B2 --> C1[Step 3: Select Target Platform]
    C1 --> D1[Step 4: Apply M2M Transformation]
    D1 --> D2[Generate PSM]
    D2 --> E1[Step 5: Apply M2T Transformation]
    E1 --> E2[Produce Source Code and Config Files]
    E2 --> F1[Step 6: Compile and Deploy]
    F1 --> F2[Step 7a: Execute Model Based Testing]
    F2 --> G1{Defects Detected?}
    G1 -- Yes --> A1
    G1 -- No --> H1[Step 7b: Round Trip Engineering Sync]
    H1 --> I1[System in Production with Living Model]
```

## 4.2 MDA Abstraction Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4[Layer 4: Executable Code and Deployment Artifacts] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3: PSM Platform Specific Model] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2: PIM Platform Independent Model] --> L1
    L1[Layer 1: CIM Computation Independent Model]
    L1 -. Annotated with software concerns .-> L2
    L2 -. Refined via M2M transformation .-> L3
    L3 -. Generated via M2T transformation .-> L4
```

## 4.3 Round-Trip Engineering Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[PIM Model] -- M2M transform --> M2[PSM Model]
    M2 -- M2T transform --> M3[Source Code]
    M3 -. Manual edit for optimization .-> M4[Modified Code]
    M4 -. Reverse sync into model .-> M2
    M2 -. Propagate update .-> M1
```

## 4.4 Traditional vs MBSD — Process Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Code Centric Process]
        T1[Requirements Doc] --> T2[Design Doc] --> T3[Source Code] --> T4[Manual Test Cases] --> T5[Deployed System]
    end
    subgraph MBSD_PROC[MBSD Process]
        M1A[CIM Model] --> M1B[PIM Model] --> M1C[PSM Model] --> M1D[Generated Code] --> M1E[Generated Test Cases] --> M1F[Deployed System]
        M1F -. Round trip sync .-> M1C
        M1C -. Update model from feedback .-> M1B
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]  
> **Define Model-Based Software Development (MBSD). List any two distinguishing features of MBSD compared to traditional software development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Model-Based Software Development (MBSD) is a software engineering methodology in which *formal, abstract models* are the primary artifacts of the development lifecycle. Code, tests, and documentation are automatically or semi-automatically derived from these models through well-defined transformations, rather than being written by hand.

**Two distinguishing features (1 mark — ½ mark each):**
1. **Models are first-class artifacts**, not just documentation. Changes are made at the model level and propagated to code.
2. **Platform-independence** is preserved at the logical level (PIM), enabling the same model to be deployed to multiple target platforms via different PSM transformations.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]  
> **What is the role of the OMG in Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)? Name the three MDA model layers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of OMG (2 marks):** The Object Management Group (OMG) is the international standards consortium that **defined, standardized, and maintains** the Model-Driven Architecture (MDA) framework. It publishes the specifications for UML, the Meta-Object Facility (MOF), the Query/View/Transformation (QVT) language, and the layered model abstractions (CIM, PIM, PSM) that underpin MDA-compliant MBSD tools.

**Three MDA layers (1 mark — ⅓ mark each):**
1. **CIM** — Computation-Independent Model
2. **PIM** — Platform-Independent Model
3. **PSM** — Platform-Specific Model

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Pair**  
> (Answer **any one** of the following:)

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**  
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **principles and lifecycle phases** of the Model-Based Software Development (MBSD) methodology. Discuss the role of *transformation engines* in each phase. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **MDA architecture**. Explain the difference between **PIM and PSM** with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Principles and lifecycle of MBSD (7 marks):**

**Principles (2 marks):** MBSD rests on four principles:
1. **Abstraction through modeling** — systems described at multiple levels.
2. **Models as primary artifacts** — code is derived, not authored.
3. **Automated transformations** — M2M and M2T mappings replace manual translation.
4. **Domain-specific semantics** — DSMLs capture domain vocabulary.

**Lifecycle phases with transformation-engine role (5 marks — 1 per phase):**

1. **Domain Analysis → CIM:** Transformation engines are *not* used here. Domain experts manually construct the CIM using activity diagrams, use cases, and glossaries.
2. **System Analysis → PIM:** Designers use UML/SysML tools (e.g., Eclipse Papyrus, MagicDraw) to author the PIM. Lightweight validation engines (OCL interpreters) check OCL constraints.
3. **Platform Selection:** No transformation; an explicit platform choice (Java EE, .NET, Android, etc.) is documented.
4. **PIM → PSM (M2M transformation):** Engines like **ATL, QVT, or Xtend** apply rule-based mappings to inject platform-specific types, persistence annotations, and middleware bindings.
5. **PSM → Code (M2T transformation):** Engines like **Acceleo, Xtend, MOFScript** render templates to produce compilable source code and configuration files.
6. **Testing:** MBT engines (e.g., **Conformiq, MaTeLo**) generate test sequences from state machines and activity diagrams.
7. **Maintenance — Round-trip Engineering:** Synchronization engines propagate manual code edits back into the model and vice versa, preserving consistency.

**(b) MDA Architecture with block diagram (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
+----------------------------------------+
|  CIM (Business / Domain Model)         |
|  - Stakeholders, business rules        |
+----------------------------------------+
              |  Manual annotation
              v
+----------------------------------------+
|  PIM (Platform-Independent Model)      |
|  - Classes, state machines, OCL       |
+----------------------------------------+
              |  M2M transformation
              v
+----------------------------------------+
|  PSM (Platform-Specific Model)         |
|  - Java/JPA? .NET/Entity Framework?   |
+----------------------------------------+
              |  M2T transformation
              v
+----------------------------------------+
|  Source Code, Build Scripts, Tests     |
+----------------------------------------+
```

**PIM vs PSM explanation with example (4 marks):**

A **PIM** is *platform-neutral*. For example, a PIM class `Order` with attribute `totalAmount: Real` and an association `contains → OrderItem` is a logical description usable anywhere.

A **PSM** *binds* the PIM to a specific execution platform. For the same `Order` class targeting Java EE, the PSM version would be:
- Annotated with `@Entity` (JPA persistence)
- Attribute typed as `BigDecimal` (currency-safe type on the JVM)
- Association mapped with `@OneToMany(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)`

For the .NET target, the same logical class becomes a C# `class Order` with `[Table("Orders")]` Entity Framework attributes and `decimal TotalAmount`.

**Key contrasts:**

| Aspect | PIM | PSM |
|---|---|---|
| Platform references | None | Explicit (e.g., `java.util.List`, `System.Data.Entity`) |
| Reusability across platforms | High | Low (locked to one platform) |
| Audience | Architects | Implementers |
| Level of detail | Logical | Implementation-oriented |

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**  
> **(a)** Compare the **traditional code-centric** software development approach with the **MBSD** approach across at least **six dimensions**. Justify why MBSD is preferred for embedded and safety-critical systems. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Explain **model-to-model (M2M) and model-to-text (M2T) transformations** with examples. Discuss the **benefits and challenges** of adopting MBSD in industry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison and justification (7 marks):**

**Six-dimension comparison table (5 marks — ½ mark per correct row + header):**

| Dimension | Traditional | MBSD |
|---|---|---|
| Primary artifact | Source code | Formal model |
| Documentation | Manual, often outdated | Auto-derived from model |
| Abstraction levels | 2 (design, code) | 3+ (CIM, PIM, PSM, code) |
| Platform portability | Manual rewrite | Regenerate from same PIM |
| Error detection | Compile / runtime | Model-level + compile time |
| Stakeholder review | Limited | Continuous, visual |

Additional dimensions for higher marks: tooling dependence, reusability, round-trip support, training cost, maintenance cost.

**Why MBSD suits embedded / safety-critical systems (2 marks):**
1. **Traceability** — every line of generated code can be traced back to a model element, satisfying certification standards such as **DO-178C (avionics)** and **ISO 26262 (automotive)**.
2. **Early defect detection** — model-level static analysis catches design defects before deployment, reducing the cost of late-stage corrections in safety-critical pipelines.
3. **Platform reuse** — the same control logic (PIM) can be deployed to multiple automotive ECUs (PSM variants).
4. **Automation reduces human error** — fewer hand-written lines means fewer injection points for safety bugs.

**(b) M2M and M2T transformations + benefits & challenges (7 marks):**

**M2M transformation (2 marks):**
A Model-to-Model transformation maps a *source model* conforming to a *source metamodel* to a *target model* conforming to a *target metamodel*.
- *Example:* A PIM class `Account` with a `balance: Real` attribute is transformed into a PSM class `Account` with `balance: BigDecimal` (Java type) and a `@Column(precision=15, scale=2)` JPA annotation. Tools: **ATL, QVT, Xtend**.

**M2T transformation (2 marks):**
A Model-to-Text transformation renders a model into one or more *textual artifacts* (source code, XML, JSON, HTML) using a template-based engine.
- *Example:* A PSM entity `Order` is rendered as a Java class with imports, annotations, getters, setters, and `equals`/`hashCode` methods via an **Acceleo** template. Tools: **Acceleo, MOFScript, Xtend, JET**.

**Benefits of MBSD in industry (2 marks):**
- Higher productivity, multi-platform reuse, automatic documentation, early defect detection, better stakeholder communication, easier certification traceability.

**Challenges of MBSD in industry (1 mark):**
- Steep learning curve, immature / proprietary tools, generated code performance limitations, model versioning difficulties, integration with legacy codebases.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing CIM with PIM** — students often write that the CIM "describes classes and methods". The CIM must be *purely business-oriented* with **no software terms** (no classes, no methods, no UML stereotypes). It talks about *business processes and stakeholders*.
> 2. **Writing "MBSD generates code automatically" without qualifying** — the *entire* code is rarely auto-generated. The *majority* is, but platform glue code and performance optimizations are often manual. Examiners deduct marks for over-generalization.
> 3. **Skipping the transformation chain** — many students list CIM/PIM/PSM but fail to state the **transformation type** between each layer. Always write "M2M" between PIM and PSM, and "M2T" between PSM and Code.
> 4. **Forgetting round-trip engineering** in the lifecycle — the question on MBSD phases must mention how models and code stay synchronized. Skipping this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **No real-world example** in long answers — KTU examiners reward at least **one concrete example** (e.g., AUTOSAR for automotive, Simulink for control systems) to anchor abstract definitions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Overview of MBSD Methodology**

- ✅ **MBSD = Model-Driven Development = Model-Centric Software Engineering.** Models are the **primary artifacts**, not documentation.
- ✅ **MDA** is the OMG standard that defines the three-layer model architecture: **CIM → PIM → PSM → Code**.
- ✅ **CIM** = business / domain view, **no software jargon**, audience is *business analysts and stakeholders*.
- ✅ **PIM** = logical, **platform-neutral** software view, audience is *software architects*.
- ✅ **PSM** = platform-bound, includes *specific technologies* (Java EE, .NET, Android, AUTOSAR, etc.), audience is *implementers*.
- ✅ **M2M transformation** = PIM → PSM. Tools: **ATL, QVT, Xtend**.
- ✅ **M2T transformation** = PSM → Code. Tools: **Acceleo, MOFScript, Xtend, JET**.
- ✅ **Four principles of MBSD:** Abstraction, Models as primary artifacts, Automated transformations, Domain-specific semantics.
- ✅ **Seven lifecycle phases:** Domain Analysis → System Analysis → Platform Selection → M2M Transformation → M2T Transformation → Testing → Round-Trip Maintenance.
- ✅ **Five benefits:** Higher productivity, platform portability, automatic documentation, early defect detection, better stakeholder communication.
- ✅ **Five challenges:** Learning curve, tool maturity, generated-code performance, model management, legacy integration.
- ✅ **Round-trip engineering** = bidirectional synchronization between model and code.
- ✅ **Key industrial domains:** Embedded systems, automotive (AUTOSAR), avionics (DO-178C), industrial control, cyber-physical systems, digital twins.
- ✅ **Standard bodies / languages to know:** OMG (standards), UML (general modeling), SysML (systems extension), OCL (constraint language), MOF (metamodeling foundation).
- ✅ **Exam trap to avoid:** Never describe the CIM using software-engineering terms. Keep it strictly *business-level*.

> **🎯 Examiner's Final Tip:** In a 14-mark KTU question, structure your answer as: **(1)** definition, **(2)** diagram, **(3)** layered explanation with example, **(4)** benefits, **(5)** challenges. This 5-part structure covers every marking checkpoint a KTU 2024 Scheme paper typically allocates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Modelling Levels - Computation Independent Model (CIM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** The Modelling Levels - Computation Independent Model (CIM) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (OMG / MDA Standard) According to the **Object Management Group (OMG)** specification of **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)**, the **Computation Independent Model (CIM)** is the highest level of abstraction in the modelling hierarchy. It captures the **business domain, operational context, requirements, and semantics of the system** *without specifying* (or even implying) how those requirements will be realized in software, hardware, or any computational technology. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECNT634 – Module 2):** > A *Computation Independent Model* is a view of a system that "shows only the business requirements, business processes, and the operational environment of the system, hiding all details of how the system will be built using computer software." The CIM is also commonly referred to as the **Domain Model** or **Business Model** because it answers the question: > *"WHAT the system must do for the business — NOT how it will do it."* | Attribute | CIM Value | |---|---| | Abstraction Level | Highest (closest to the real world) | | Primary Audience | Domain experts, business analysts, stakeholders | | Technology Detail | None (platform-independent) | | Formal OMG Name | Computation Independent Viewpoint | | Typical Notations | UML Use-Case Diagrams, BPMN, Activity Diagrams, Domain Class Diagrams | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are briefing an **architect** about a new house. You do not start by talking about cement grade, rebar diameter, or the Java compiler you will use to model it. Instead, you say: - "There is a **family of four** living here." - "They need **three bedrooms**, a kitchen facing east, and a pooja room." - "The grandmother should not have to climb stairs." That plain-English, **family-centric brief** is your **CIM**. The architect's later blueprint — with concrete mix ratios, plumbing layouts, and electrical wiring — is the **PIM** and **PSM**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** The CIM speaks the **language of the business**, not the language of computers. If the business changes, only the CIM changes; the technology (PIM/PSM) can be re-generated or re-mapped without rewriting the core business rules. ## 1.3 Why CIM Exists in MDA The OMG introduced the CIM layer to solve three persistent problems in software engineering: 1. **Communication Gap** — Business stakeholders rarely understand UML class diagrams or deployment views. CIM bridges this gap using familiar business vocabulary. 2. **Requirement Volatility** — Business rules change often. Capturing them in a *computation-independent* layer isolates change from implementation. 3. **Traceability** — A formal CIM creates a verifiable lineage from *stakeholder need → system requirement → platform implementation*. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** CIM = *"What & Why"* | PIM = *"How (software shape)"* | PSM = *"How (specific technology)"*. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Modelling Levels of MDA — Structured Logic Breakdown The MDA framework, standardized by the OMG, defines a layered modelling philosophy. Each layer hides complexity from the layer above it. ### Level 1 — Computation Independent Model (CIM) - **Focus:** Business requirements, domain entities, external actors, workflow. - **Hides:** Software classes, APIs, databases, frameworks, platforms. - **Stakeholders:** Business owners, analysts, end-users. - **Notation Palette:** Use-case diagrams, BPMN process flows, business glossary, domain models. ### Level 2 — Platform Independent Model (PIM) - **Focus:** Logical software structure (classes, interfaces, operations, state machines). - **Hides:** Specific middleware, OS, programming language, vendor products. - **Stakeholders:** Software architects, designers. - **Notation Palette:** UML Class, Sequence, State, Component diagrams. ### Level 3 — Platform Specific Model (PSM) - **Focus:** Concrete implementation choices (e.g., EJB, Spring, .NET, MySQL). - **Stakeholders:** Developers, DevOps. - **Notation Palette:** Extended UML with technology stereotypes, deployment diagrams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transformation Rule (Exam Favourite):** > CIM → PIM is a *requirements-to-software* mapping (manual or semi-automated). > PIM → PSM is a *model-to-code* mapping (often automated by tools like Acceleo, EMF, ANDROMDA). ## 2.2 Internal Anatomy of a CIM — What Must Be Inside A complete CIM, as expected by KTU examiners, contains **four core artefacts**: 1. **Business Glossary** — Precise definitions of every domain term (e.g., *Loan Applicant*, *Credit Score*). 2. **Business Use-Case Diagram** — Actors (external entities) and use-cases (business goals). 3. **Business Process Model** — Activity/BPMN flow showing the sequence of business activities. 4. **Domain Class Diagram (Conceptual)** — High-level entities and their relationships (no methods, no visibility, no attributes shown with types — only conceptual names). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — CIM at a Glance | Concept | Detail Expected in Exam | |---|---| | Full Form | Computation Independent Model | | OMG Document | MDA Guide (omg.org/mda) | | Alternative Name | Domain Model / Business Model | | Abstraction Level | Highest in MDA | | Technology Knowledge Required | **None** | | Primary Notation | UML Use-Case + BPMN | | Audience | Business stakeholders | | Transformation Direction | CIM refines into PIM | | Output Artefact Type | Requirements specification in visual form | | Relationship to Requirements | CIM *is* the formal requirements model | ## 2.4 Boundary Conditions — What CIM MUST NOT Contain KTU examiners frequently test whether the student can distinguish CIM from PIM. The following are **strictly forbidden** inside a CIM: - Class attributes with data types (e.g., `String`, `int`, `Double`) - Methods, operations, or signatures - Visibility modifiers (`+`, `-`, `#`) - Inheritance, polymorphism, or design patterns - Database tables, primary keys, foreign keys - Any mention of Java, .NET, Python, REST, SOAP, SQL, XML, JSON - Architecture tiers (presentation, business, data) > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Drawing UML class diagrams with attributes and operations at the CIM level. This is *not* CIM — it is already a PIM. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In industry, the CIM is delivered as a **Business Requirements Document (BRD)** or a **BPMN-based process map**. Tools used include: - **Bizagi Modeler** — for BPMN business process design - **Visual Paradigm** — for UML use-case + domain class modelling - **IBM Blueworks Live** — for business process catalogues - **ARIS** — for enterprise architecture business views The CIM is consumed by **BA teams** and handed over to **architecture teams** who convert it into a PIM. This division of labour is what makes MDA commercially viable. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — From Real-World Need to CIM ## 3.1 Methodology: Building a CIM (Derivation-Style Walkthrough) Because software modelling is not algebraic, we present the *derivation* of a CIM as a **structured 6-step analytical chain**. Each step produces an artefact and feeds the next. ### Step 1 — Identify the Business Domain Boundary > *Textual logic:* What is the system, and what is NOT the system? > *Output:* A **System Context Statement**. **Example (KTU-style sample):** For an *Online Train Ticket Booking* portal, the domain boundary includes searching trains, booking seats, processing payments, and cancelling tickets. The domain boundary **excludes** the railway signalling system and the maintenance schedule database. ### Step 2 — Discover External Actors > *Textual logic:* Who interacts with the business processes from outside the boundary? > *Output:* A list of **Primary and Secondary Actors**. | Actor | Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Passenger | Primary | Searches and books tickets | | Payment Gateway | Secondary | Validates payment (external system) | | Administrator | Primary | Manages routes and schedules | ### Step 3 — Elicit Business Use-Cases > *Textual logic:* For each actor, what business goals must be satisfied? > *Output:* A **Use-Case Diagram** (the visual core of the CIM). Business use-cases use **business verbs** — *Register Passenger, Book Ticket, Refund Amount* — never technical verbs like *Insert Record* or *Persist Object*. ### Step 4 — Model the Business Process > *Textual logic:* In what order do the use-cases unfold? What are the decision points? > *Output:* A **BPMN / Activity Diagram**. For the booking use-case: *Search → Select Train → Check Seat Availability → Make Payment → Confirm Booking → Issue Ticket*. ### Step 5 — Construct the Conceptual Domain Model > *Textual logic:* What noun-like entities exist in this domain? How are they related? > *Output:* A **Conceptual Class Diagram** (no methods, no typed attributes). Entities: *Passenger, Ticket, Train, Route, Payment, Schedule*. Relationships: *Train runs on Route*, *Passenger holds Ticket*, *Ticket references Schedule*. ### Step 6 — Build the Business Glossary > *Textual logic:* Every domain term must be unambiguously defined so that analysts and developers share one vocabulary. > *Output:* A **Glossary Table** with term, definition, and synonyms. | Term | Definition | Synonyms | |---|---|---| | Passenger | A registered user who can book tickets | Customer, Traveller | | Schedule | A time-table for a train on a route | Timetable | | Refund | Money returned on cancellation | Reversal | > [!IMPORTANT] > The deliverable at the end of Step 6 is the **complete CIM**. Handing this artefact to an architect allows them to derive the PIM by adding operations, attributes with types, and design patterns. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — "Hospital Appointment System" CIM Excerpt We now demonstrate the *minimum acceptable* CIM content for a hospital appointment subsystem. **Business Use-Cases (Step 3 output):** - *Book Appointment* - *Reschedule Appointment* - *Cancel Appointment* - *View Doctor Availability* **BPMN Steps for *Book Appointment* (Step 4 output):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Flow:} \quad & \text{Patient} \rightarrow \text{Select Specialization} \rightarrow \text{View Available Slots} \\ & \rightarrow \text{[Slot Available?]} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Confirm Booking} \rightarrow \text{Send SMS Confirmation} \\ & \rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Join Waitlist} \rightarrow \text{End} \end{aligned} $$ > *Conversion Logic:* The diamond `[Slot Available?]` is a **decision gateway** — a purely business decision, not an `if-else` in code. This is why BPMN is preferred at CIM level over programming flowcharts. **Conceptual Domain Model (Step 5 output):** Entities: *Patient, Doctor, Appointment, Specialization, Slot*. Relationship: *Doctor offers Slot*, *Patient makes Appointment*, *Appointment occupies Slot*. Note that there is **no `appointmentId: int`**, **no `save()` method**, **no `<>` stereotype** — those belong to the PIM. ## 3.3 CIM-to-PIM Hand-off Checklist | # | Hand-off Item | Producer | Consumer | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Business Use-Case Diagram | BA | Architect | | 2 | Business Glossary | BA | Architect + Developer | | 3 | BPMN Process Model | BA | Architect | | 4 | Conceptual Class Diagram | BA / Domain Expert | Architect (to enrich into PIM) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The MDA Modelling Pyramid — Showing CIM at the Top The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the three MDA layers and explicitly positions the CIM at the apex of abstraction. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CIM LAYER
Computation Independent Model
Business Requirements Domain View"] --> B["PIM LAYER
Platform Independent Model
Logical Software Structure"] B --> C["PSM LAYER
Platform Specific Model
Technology Specific Implementation"] C --> D["SOURCE CODE
Java .NET Python etc"] A1["Business Analyst"] -. uses .-> A B1["Software Architect"] -. uses .-> B C1["Developer"] -. uses .-> C D1["Build Tool"] -. generates .-> D style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **yellow top layer** is the CIM — purely business-focused. - The **blue middle layer** is the PIM — adds software structure. - The **green lower layer** is the PSM — adds platform decisions. - The **purple bottom** is the executable code, derived from the PSM. ## 4.2 Internal Structure of a Complete CIM Artefact This block-level functional architecture shows the four mandatory components inside a CIM. ```mermaid flowchart LR G["GLOSSARY
Business Terms"] --> C["CIM ARTEFACT"] U["USE CASE DIAGRAM
Actors and Business Goals"] --> C P["PROCESS MODEL
BPMN Activity Flow"] --> C D["DOMAIN MODEL
Conceptual Classes"] --> C C --> H["HAND OFF TO PIM TEAM"] style C fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:3px style H fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 CIM-to-PIM Transformation Flow This diagram shows how each CIM artefact is *consumed* by the architect and *expanded* into PIM artefacts. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Glossary"] --> B1["Domain Classes with Attributes"] A2["Use Case Diagram"] --> B2["System Sequence Diagrams"] A3["BPMN Process"] --> B3["Activity and Sequence Diagrams"] A4["Conceptual Classes"] --> B4["PIM Class Diagram with Operations"] B1 --> PIM["PIM ARTEFACT"] B2 --> PIM B3 --> PIM B4 --> PIM style PIM fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When asked *"How does CIM relate to PIM?"*, the safe answer is — *"The CIM provides the conceptual vocabulary and behavioural boundaries; the PIM refines each CIM element by adding typed attributes, operations, and design constraints."* --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define Computation Independent Model (CIM). Mention any two notations used to represent it.* **Model Answer:** A **Computation Independent Model (CIM)** is a view of a system that captures the business requirements, business processes, and operational context of the system while hiding all details of how the system will be built using computer software. It is the highest level of abstraction in the OMG Model-Driven Architecture. Two common notations used to represent a CIM are: 1. **UML Use-Case Diagram** — captures actors and business goals. 2. **BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation)** — captures business workflows and decisions. *(Acceptable alternatives: Activity Diagram, Domain Class Diagram, Business Glossary.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *List four artefacts that constitute a complete CIM.* **Model Answer:** A complete CIM consists of the following four artefacts: 1. **Business Glossary** — definitions of all domain terms. 2. **Business Use-Case Diagram** — actors and business use-cases. 3. **Business Process Model** — BPMN/Activity flow of business activities. 4. **Conceptual Domain Class Diagram** — high-level entities and their relationships (no methods, no typed attributes). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the concept of Computation Independent Model (CIM) in detail. Discuss its role in the Model-Driven Architecture (MDA) framework. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** With a suitable example, illustrate the four artefacts of a CIM for an *Online Food Delivery System*. *(7 Marks)* --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** The **Computation Independent Model (CIM)** is the highest abstraction layer in the OMG **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)**. It describes a system purely from a business perspective — its requirements, processes, stakeholders, and operational vocabulary — without specifying any computational or technological detail. **Role of CIM in MDA:** 1. **Bridge Between Business and IT** — The CIM is created and understood by business analysts and domain experts. It translates stakeholder needs into a formal visual model. 2. **Requirements Formalisation** — It elevates a textual requirements document into a structured, verifiable model consisting of use-cases, business processes, and conceptual entities. 3. **Stable Foundation for Lower Layers** — Since the CIM is technology-free, business rule changes only affect this layer. The PIM and PSM can be regenerated or refined without disturbing the business definition. 4. **Traceability Anchor** — Each PIM element can be traced back to a CIM artefact, ensuring end-to-end requirements coverage. 5. **Stakeholder Validation** — Because the CIM uses business vocabulary, business owners can directly review and approve it, reducing requirement ambiguity. In the MDA transformation chain, the CIM feeds into the **PIM** (Platform Independent Model) which in turn is refined into one or more **PSMs** (Platform Specific Models) and finally into source code. **Key Characteristics of CIM:** - Independent of any software platform - Independent of programming language - Uses business vocabulary - Owned by business stakeholders - Forms the input for software architects *(7 marks awarded for definition + role + characteristics.)* --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Example: Online Food Delivery System — Four CIM Artefacts** **1. Business Glossary (Sample):** | Term | Definition | |---|---| | Customer | A registered user who places food orders | | Restaurant | A registered food vendor listed on the platform | | Order | A request from a customer for food items | | Delivery Agent | A person who transports the order | | Menu | List of food items offered by a restaurant | **2. Business Use-Case Diagram (Textual Representation):** - *Actors:* Customer, Restaurant, Delivery Agent, Payment Gateway - *Use-Cases:* Register User, Browse Restaurants, Place Order, Track Order, Make Payment, Rate Restaurant **3. Business Process Model (BPMN excerpt for Place Order):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Flow:} \quad & \text{Customer} \rightarrow \text{Select Restaurant} \rightarrow \text{Add Items to Cart} \\ & \rightarrow \text{Choose Payment Method} \rightarrow \text{[Payment Success?]} \\ & \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Notify Restaurant} \rightarrow \text{Assign Delivery Agent} \rightarrow \text{End} \\ & \rightarrow \text{No} \rightarrow \text{Show Error} \rightarrow \text{End} \end{aligned} $$ **4. Conceptual Domain Model (Entities & Relationships):** - Entities: *Customer, Restaurant, Order, OrderItem, MenuItem, DeliveryAgent, Payment* - Relationships: - *Customer places Order* - *Order contains OrderItems* - *OrderItem references MenuItem* - *Restaurant offers MenuItem* - *DeliveryAgent delivers Order* - *Order has Payment* Notice that none of these contain attributes with data types, methods, or any technology choice — exactly what defines a CIM. *(7 marks awarded for correctness, completeness, and absence of technology leakage.)* --- ### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative for Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Differentiate between CIM, PIM, and PSM with respect to abstraction level, audience, notation, and technological detail. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Explain why the CIM should NOT contain any programming language constructs, database tables, or framework details. Justify with an example showing the harm of leaking technology into a CIM. *(7 Marks)* --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** The three layers of MDA — CIM, PIM, and PSM — differ systematically along four dimensions: | Dimension | CIM | PIM | PSM | |---|---|---|---| | Abstraction Level | Highest (business view) | Middle (logical software) | Lowest (technology specific) | | Primary Audience | Business analysts, stakeholders | Software architects, designers | Developers, DevOps engineers | | Typical Notation | Use-Case, BPMN, Activity, Glossary | UML Class, Sequence, State, Component | Extended UML with stereotypes, Deployment diagrams | | Technological Detail | None | Platform-independent, but software-aware | Concrete — Java EE, Spring, .NET, MySQL, REST, etc. | | Transformation Output | Feeds into PIM | Refined into one or more PSMs | Generates source code | | Example Term Used | *Book Ticket* | *TicketBookingService* | *SpringBean ticketBookingServiceImpl* | The CIM provides the *what*, the PIM provides the *software-shaped how*, and the PSM provides the *technology-specific how*. *(7 marks distributed across the four dimensions with at least one clear example each.)* --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** The CIM must remain **technology-free** because its purpose is to be a stable, business-owned specification. Including programming constructs, database tables, or framework details breaks the abstraction and creates three concrete problems: **Problem 1 — Loss of Business Readability:** If a CIM contains `customerId: int` or `Hibernate Entity` stereotypes, business stakeholders can no longer validate the model. They never asked for `int`; they asked for *Customer Identity*. **Problem 2 — Premature Design Lock-In:** Once `getCustomerById(int id)` appears in a CIM, the team is forced into a particular technology. If the organization later migrates from Java to .NET or from MySQL to PostgreSQL, the CIM — the supposed "stable foundation" — must be rewritten. The whole purpose of MDA is defeated. **Problem 3 — Traceability Corruption:** If a CIM contains both *business* and *technology* elements, it becomes impossible to map *one PIM element to exactly one business requirement*. The transformation chain breaks. **Illustrative Example of Harm:** Consider a CIM for a *Library Management System*. Suppose a junior analyst draws: > `Book` *–attribute→* `isbn: String`, *operation→* `saveToDatabase()` This is **wrong** at CIM level. Now imagine the organization decides to expose the system as a stateless REST API with NoSQL storage. The CIM must be rewritten because it contains the old assumption. The architect must also re-validate every business use-case. Hours of rework, traced directly to technology leakage in the CIM. The correct CIM entry for *Book* would simply be: > *Entity: Book* — *Relationship: Book is held by Member, Book belongs to Category.* No types, no operations, no persistence calls. **Conclusion:** Keeping the CIM technology-free guarantees that the business specification outlives any specific technology choice, fulfilling the MDA promise of portability, longevity, and clear traceability. *(7 marks awarded for the three problems + illustrative example + corrective statement.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing CIM with PIM** — Adding attributes with data types or methods in CIM diagrams (loss of 2–3 marks per occurrence). > 2. **Using programming verbs in use-cases** — e.g., *Save Data* instead of *Register Customer*. > 3. **Skipping the Business Glossary** — Examiners expect all four CIM artefacts, not just the use-case diagram. > 4. **Memorizing without understanding the transformation flow** — Writing *"CIM converts to PIM via a tool"* without explaining *what* converts is treated as incomplete. > 5. **Mixing notation styles** — Drawing a flowchart where a BPMN diagram is expected loses presentation marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **CIM stands for Computation Independent Model** — the topmost layer of OMG MDA. - It describes the **WHAT and WHY** of a system, never the HOW. - **Primary notations:** UML Use-Case Diagram, BPMN, Activity Diagram, Conceptual Class Diagram, Business Glossary. - **Mandatory four artefacts:** Glossary, Use-Case Diagram, Process Model, Conceptual Domain Model. - **Audience:** Business analysts, domain experts, stakeholders — *not* developers. - **No technology leakage:** No data types on attributes, no methods, no frameworks, no database tables, no programming language references. - **Transformation direction:** CIM → PIM → PSM → Code. - **Also called:** Domain Model, Business Model. - **Memory phrase:** *"CIM speaks business; PIM speaks software; PSM speaks technology."* - **Highest exam yield topics:** definition, four artefacts, comparison with PIM and PSM, justification of technology-freedom, worked example. - **Always state the OMG source** when defining CIM in KTU answers — *"According to the OMG MDA Guide…"* fetches extra credit. ---

topics.title: Platform Independent Model (PIM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Platform Independent Model (PIM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Platform Independent Model (PIM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (OMG / MDA Standard)**
> A **Platform Independent Model (PIM)** is a **high-level abstraction of a software system** that is described using a domain-specific or general-purpose modeling language (such as **UML**) **without any reference to a specific execution platform, middleware, database, operating system, or programming language**. In the OMG's **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)**, the PIM represents business functionality and behaviour purely in terms of domain concepts, and is later transformed (via automated or semi-automated mappings) into one or more **Platform Specific Models (PSMs)**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Architect's Blueprint Analogy"** 🏛️
> Imagine you commission an architect to design a house. The architect first produces a **conceptual drawing** that shows *rooms, doors, flow, and purpose* — but says **nothing** about whether the house will be built using brick, wood, or concrete. That abstract drawing is the **PIM**.
> Later, you decide the house will be built with **red brick in Kerala** or **timber in Canada**. The specialised drawings (with material specifications, local building codes, foundation type) are the **PSMs**.
> The PIM stays **the same** — only the *implementation* changes. That is the entire philosophy of MDA.

### 1.2 Where Does the PIM Sit in MDA?

The OMG defines **four layers of abstraction** in MDA:

| Abstraction Layer | What It Describes | Platform Dependency |
|---|---|---|
| **M0** | Real running system (objects in memory) | Concrete instance |
| **M1** | Instance model of the application | Platform Specific |
| **M2** | Metamodel (e.g., UML, MOF) | Meta-level |
| **M3** | Meta-metamodel (MOF itself) | Foundational |

The **PIM lives at the M1 layer**, expressed in conformance with a metamodel (M2) like UML, but **without binding to any specific technology platform**.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of a PIM

- ✅ **Pure business semantics** — captures *what* the system does, not *how* it is deployed.
- ✅ **Expressed in UML** (class, state, sequence, activity, use-case diagrams).
- ✅ **No technology artefacts** — no `javax.persistence.Entity`, no `<asp:Button>`, no `CREATE TABLE`.
- ✅ **Reusable across platforms** — the *same* PIM can yield J2EE-PSM, .NET-PSM, CORBA-PSM.
- ✅ **Transformation-ready** — designed to be input for automated QVT/ATL transformations.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU One-Liner to Memorise:**
> *"A PIM describes business functionality and behaviour in UML, abstracted from any underlying technology platform, enabling the same model to be mapped onto multiple PSMs."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MDA Triad — Relationship Between CIM, PIM, and PSM

MDA does **not** operate on the PIM alone. The full lifecycle comprises three modelling tiers:

### 🔹 2.1.1 Computation Independent Model (CIM)
- Also called **domain model**.
- Captures the **business environment** in the language of business stakeholders (not developers).
- Answers: *"What does the business need?"*
- Example: *"A bank needs to allow customers to transfer funds between accounts."*

### 🔹 2.1.2 Platform Independent Model (PIM)
- Bridges CIM and PSM.
- Captures **system structure and behaviour** using UML but **ignores** any execution platform.
- Answers: *"What must the software do — structurally and behaviourally?"*
- Example: A `BankAccount` class with a `transferTo(otherAccount, amount)` operation.

### 🔹 2.1.3 Platform Specific Model (PSM)
- A **refinement of the PIM** annotated with platform-specific details (e.g., Java EE annotations, SQL types, WSDL ports).
- Answers: *"How will this run on platform X?"*
- Example: A `BankAccount` class annotated with `@Entity`, `@Table(name="ACCOUNT")`.

### 🔹 2.1.4 Why This Order Matters
The MDA promise is that **changes in platform do not invalidate business logic**. If tomorrow you migrate from J2EE to Spring Boot, you re-transform the *same PIM* — the **business rules survive intact**.

---

## 2.2 PIM in the OMG Standards Stack

A PIM is built upon a well-defined metamodel stack:

| Layer | OMG Standard | Purpose in PIM Construction |
|---|---|---|
| M3 | **MOF** (Meta-Object Facility) | Defines the rules for defining metamodels |
| M2 | **UML** (Unified Modeling Language) | The metamodel PIMs conform to |
| M1 | **PIM / PSM / Application Models** | Your actual models |
| M0 | **Runtime Objects** | Live instances |

Supporting standards used **alongside** the PIM:
- **OCL** (Object Constraint Language) — to write **platform-independent invariants** on the PIM (e.g., `context BankAccount inv: balance >= 0`).
- **XMI** (XML Metadata Interchange) — to **serialise** PIMs for tool interchange.
- **QVT** (Query/View/Transformation) — to **transform** PIM → PSM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Examiners love asking *"Which OMG standard is used to write constraints on a PIM?"* — the answer is **OCL**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although PIM is conceptual, the *transformations* involving it are formal. Below is the cheat sheet of formal notations you must remember:

| Concept | Formal Notation / Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| MDA Transformation Function (PIM → PSM) | $T: PIM \rightarrow PSM$ | A mapping $T$ that transforms a Platform Independent Model into a Platform Specific Model |
| Multi-platform fan-out | $T_{i}(PIM) = PSM_{i}, \; i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ | The *same* PIM is mapped to $n$ different PSMs |
| PIM Conformance to UML | $PIM \models UML$ | "PIM conforms to (is an instance of) the UML metamodel" |
| MOF Four-Layer Architecture | $M_{i} \models M_{i+1}, \; i = 0, 1, 2$ | Each layer $M_i$ is an instance of the layer above |
| OCL Invariant | $\text{context } C \text{ inv: } \phi$ | Platform-independent constraint $\phi$ on class $C$ |
| Vertical Reuse Ratio | $R_{v} = \dfrac{\text{Platforms supported}}{\text{PIMs maintained}}$ | Measures how many platforms a single PIM serves |
| Transformation Round-trip (ideal) | $PSM_{i} \rightarrow PIM \rightarrow PSM_{j} \equiv PSM_{i}$ | Round-trip preservation property |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **automotive, aerospace, and embedded systems**, the same business-logic PIM is transformed into PSMs for **AUTOSAR**, **VxWorks**, and **Linux** simultaneously — saving thousands of engineering hours per platform migration.

---

## 2.4 Anatomy of a PIM — What It Contains vs. What It Excludes

> [!NOTE]
> **A Valid PIM MUST contain:**
> - Class diagrams with **domain classes**, attributes, associations, multiplicities.
> - Use-case diagrams describing functional requirements.
> - State machines / sequence diagrams showing **business behaviour**.
> - **OCL constraints** expressing business invariants.
> - No `import` statements; no `package` mapping to file systems; no DB types.

> [!WARNING]
> **A PIM MUST NOT contain:**
> - Any `java.*`, `javax.*`, `org.omg.*`, `System.*` references.
> - SQL DDL/DML (`CREATE TABLE`, `SELECT`).
> - WSDL / REST / SOAP endpoint definitions.
> - Operating-system calls (`fork()`, `CreateThread`).
> - Vendor-specific annotations (`@Entity`, `[DataContract]`).

If any of these appear, the model has **leaked into PSM territory** and is no longer a true PIM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — PIM Properties vs. PSM Properties

| Property | Platform Independent Model (PIM) | Platform Specific Model (PSM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Capture business semantics | Capture platform-specific implementation |
| **Modelling Language** | UML (profile-free or with limited stereotypes) | UML + platform-specific profile (e.g., EJB profile, .NET profile) |
| **Audience** | Business analysts, domain experts, architects | Platform developers, integrators |
| **Granularity** | Coarse-grained, conceptual | Fine-grained, executable |
| **Tool Support** | RSA, Papyrus, MagicDraw, Enterprise Architect | Same tools + code generators (e.g., Acceleo, AndroMDA) |
| **Lifespan** | Long-lived (decades — survives tech change) | Short-lived (depends on platform lifecycle) |
| **Example Element** | `class Customer { name: String }` | `class Customer { @Entity @Table(name="CUST") String name; }` |
| **Testability** | Validated against CIM requirements | Validated by unit tests, integration tests |
| **Reusability** | High — reusable across platforms | Low — bound to one stack |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Building a PIM from a CIM (Worked Example)

**Scenario:** A university registrar's office wants a system to **enrol students in courses**.

### **Step 1 — Capture the CIM (Domain Vocabulary)**
Stakeholders say:
- *"A student can enrol in many courses."*
- *"A course has limited capacity."*
- *"A student cannot enrol in the same course twice."*

These statements use **plain business language**. This is the **CIM**.

### **Step 2 — Derive UML Class Diagram (PIM-Level)**

Extract classes, attributes, associations from the CIM:

- `Student` class → attributes: `studentId`, `name`, `email`.
- `Course` class → attributes: `courseCode`, `title`, `capacity`.
- Association: `Student *—* Course` (many-to-many), named `Enrolment`.

### **Step 3 — Express Behaviour with UML Operations**

On the `Student` class:
- `enrolIn(course: Course)`
- `dropCourse(course: Course)`

On the `Course` class:
- `getAvailableSeats(): Integer`

### **Step 4 — Add OCL Platform-Independent Constraints**

Now express the business rules formally, **without any platform reference**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{context Course} \\
& \text{inv NoOverEnrolment:} \\
& \quad \text{self.enrolledStudents} \rightarrow \text{size()} \le \text{self.capacity} \\[6pt]
& \text{context Student} \\
& \text{inv NoDoubleEnrolment:} \\
& \quad \text{Student.allInstances()}\rightarrow\text{forAll}(s1, s2 \mid \\
& \quad \; s1 <> s2 \text{ implies } s1.enrolledCourses \rightarrow \text{intersection}(s2.enrolledCourses) \rightarrow \text{isEmpty()})
\end{aligned}
$$

> Every line above is **platform-neutral**. It can be evaluated in Java, C#, Python, or any environment — the *meaning* is unchanged.

### **Step 5 — Validate Against CIM**
- Does `enrolIn` reflect the CIM's intent? ✅
- Do the OCL rules match "limited capacity" and "no duplicate enrolment"? ✅

The model is now a **valid PIM**.

### **Step 6 — Transformation (Out of Scope for Module 2, but conceptually)**
Apply transformations $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ to yield:
- $T_1(PIM) \rightarrow$ `J2EE-PSM` (with `@Entity`, `EJB` annotations).
- $T_2(PIM) \rightarrow$ `Spring-Boot-PSM` (with `@Document`, MongoDB annotations).
- $T_3(PIM) \rightarrow$ `.NET-PSM` (with `[Table]`, `Entity Framework`).

The PIM remains **untouched** — this is the core MDA promise.

---

## 3.3 PIM Construction — Formal Algorithm

For KTU derivations, the construction of a PIM from a CIM can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \textbf{Inputs:} \; CIM = (E, R) \\
& \quad \text{where } E = \text{set of domain entities, } R = \text{set of business rules} \\
& \\
& \textbf{Step 1 (Identify Classes):} \\
& \quad C = \{c_i \mid c_i \in E \text{ and } c_i \text{ is a noun of interest}\} \\
& \\
& \textbf{Step 2 (Identify Attributes):} \\
& \quad \forall c_i \in C, \; A(c_i) = \{a_{ij}\} \text{ where } a_{ij} \text{ is a CIM property of } c_i \\
& \\
& \textbf{Step 3 (Identify Associations):} \\
& \quad X = \{(c_i, c_j, m_i, m_j, l) \mid \text{CIM states a relationship}\} \\
& \\
& \textbf{Step 4 (Identify Operations):} \\
& \quad O(c_i) = \{op_k\} \text{ derived from CIM verbs on } c_i \\
& \\
& \textbf{Step 5 (Encode Constraints via OCL):} \\
& \quad \Phi = \{\phi_m\} \text{ where each } \phi_m \text{ encodes one } r_m \in R \\
& \\
& \textbf{Output:} \; PIM = (C, A, X, O, \Phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive a PIM from a given CIM description," always list these **five elements explicitly**: **Classes, Attributes, Associations, Operations, Constraints (OCL)**. Examiners award marks for completeness of all five.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The MDA Layered Architecture with PIM at the Centre

The diagram below shows how a PIM relates to other MDA artefacts and the OMG standards stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph M3["M3 - MOF Layer"]
        MOF["MOF - Meta Object Facility"]
    end
    subgraph M2["M2 - Metamodel Layer"]
        UML["UML 2.x Metamodel"]
        CWM["CWM - Common Warehouse Model"]
    end
    subgraph M1["M1 - Model Layer (The Realm of PIM)"]
        CIM["CIM - Domain Model"]
        PIM["PIM - Platform Independent Model"]
        PSM1["PSM 1 - J2EE"]
        PSM2["PSM 2 - .NET"]
        PSM3["PSM 3 - CORBA"]
    end
    subgraph M0["M0 - Runtime Layer"]
        RUNTIME["Live Objects in Memory"]
    end
    MOF --defines--> UML
    MOF --defines--> CWM
    UML --conforms to--> PIM
    UML --conforms to--> CIM
    PIM --transformed by T1--> PSM1
    PIM --transformed by T2--> PSM2
    PIM --transformed by T3--> PSM3
    PSM1 --instantiated as--> RUNTIME
    PSM2 --instantiated as--> RUNTIME
    PSM3 --instantiated as--> RUNTIME
    PIM -.annotated with.-> OCL["OCL Constraints"]
    PIM -.serialised via.-> XMI["XMI Files"]
    PIM -.transformed using.-> QVT["QVT Mappings"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PIM Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CIM - Business Requirements"] --> B["Requirement Analyst"]
    B --> C["Class Identification"]
    C --> D["UML Class Diagram - PIM"]
    D --> E["OCL Constraint Modelling"]
    E --> F["Validated PIM Artefact"]
    F --> G1["QVT Transformation 1"]
    F --> G2["QVT Transformation 2"]
    F --> G3["QVT Transformation n"]
    G1 --> H1["Java PSM"]
    G2 --> H2["C Sharp PSM"]
    G3 --> H3["Python PSM"]
    H1 --> I1["Java Code"]
    H2 --> I2["C Sharp Code"]
    H3 --> I3["Python Code"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Anatomy of a PIM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PIMA["PIM INTERNAL STRUCTURE"]
        P1["Domain Class Diagrams"]
        P2["Use Case Diagrams"]
        P3["Sequence and State Diagrams"]
        P4["OCL Constraints"]
        P5["Activity Diagrams for Business Workflows"]
    end
    P1 --> MERGE["Unified PIM Model"]
    P2 --> MERGE
    P3 --> MERGE
    P4 --> MERGE
    P5 --> MERGE
    MERGE -.excludes.-> X1["No Java or C Sharp Code"]
    MERGE -.excludes.-> X2["No SQL or NoSQL Schema"]
    MERGE -.excludes.-> X3["No Vendor Annotations"]
    MERGE -.excludes.-> X4["No Network Protocol Details"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - In the **M0–M3** stack, the **PIM resides at M1**, conforming to UML at M2, which itself is defined by MOF at M3.
> - The **construction pipeline** shows that a single validated PIM is the *input* to all platform-specific transformations, reinforcing the "**one source, many targets**" MDA principle.
> - The **anatomy schematic** reinforces what *must* and *must not* appear inside a PIM — the exclusions are as important as the inclusions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Platform Independent Model (PIM) in the context of MDA. List any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Platform Independent Model (PIM)** is a model of a software system that expresses its structure and behaviour using UML but **does not refer to any specific execution platform, middleware, programming language, or database technology**. It captures the *business functionality* of the system at a high level of abstraction.

**Two characteristics:**
1. It is **platform-neutral**, containing only domain concepts and OCL constraints.
2. It is **reusable** across multiple platforms — the same PIM can be transformed into different PSMs (J2EE, .NET, CORBA).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a CIM and a PIM. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CIM | PIM |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Describes the business environment in plain language | Describes system structure and behaviour |
| **Audience** | Business stakeholders | Software architects and developers |
| **Notation** | Natural language + business glossary | UML diagrams + OCL |
| **Example** | *"Customers can place orders for products."* | A `Customer` class with a `placeOrder(product)` operation and an association to `Order` |

---

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> Each long question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MDA architecture** highlighting the role of the PIM. Show how a single PIM is transformed into multiple PSMs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)** is an OMG framework that separates *business functionality* from *implementation technology*. It comprises four MOF-defined layers (M0–M3) and three model tiers:

1. **CIM (Computation Independent Model)** — the domain view.
2. **PIM (Platform Independent Model)** — the system view, expressed in UML.
3. **PSM (Platform Specific Model)** — the implementation view for one specific technology.

The **role of the PIM** is to act as the **stable, long-lived abstraction** of business logic. Once validated against the CIM, the PIM serves as the **single source of truth** that is then transformed by platform-specific mappings $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ into multiple PSMs.

```
CIM  --(requirements)-->  PIM  --(T1)-->  PSM-Java
                              --(T2)-->  PSM-DotNet
                              --(T3)-->  PSM-CORBA
```

Because the PIM contains **no platform dependencies**, the same PIM can be re-targeted to a new platform simply by adding a new transformation, without rewriting business logic.

**(b)** Design a PIM for a **Library Management System** using UML class notation. The system must allow members to borrow and return books, with the constraint that *a member cannot borrow more than five books at a time* and *a book cannot be issued if all copies are currently borrowed*. Express the constraints in OCL. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Class Diagram (PIM):**

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
|---|---|---|
| `Member` | `memberId: String`, `name: String`, `borrowedBooks: Set<Book>` | `borrowBook(b: Book)`, `returnBook(b: Book)` |
| `Book` | `isbn: String`, `title: String`, `totalCopies: Integer`, `availableCopies: Integer` | `isAvailable(): Boolean` |
| `Loan` | `loanId: String`, `loanDate: Date`, `dueDate: Date`, `member: Member`, `book: Book` | (association class) |

**Associations:**
- `Member 1—* Loan` (a member can have many loans).
- `Book 1—* Loan` (a book copy can be loaned many times).

**OCL Constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{context Member} \\
& \text{inv MaxFiveBooks:} \\
& \quad \text{self.loans} \rightarrow \text{size()} \le 5 \\[6pt]
& \text{context Book} \\
& \text{inv CannotIssueWhenNoCopies:} \\
& \quad \text{self.availableCopies} \ge 0 \\
& \quad \text{and self.availableCopies} \le \text{self.totalCopies} \\[6pt]
& \text{context Loan} \\
& \text{inv DueDateAfterLoanDate:} \\
& \quad \text{self.dueDate} > \text{self.loanDate}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The model is **pure PIM** — it has no `java.util.Date`, no SQL types, no annotations. It can be transformed into a Java PSM, a .NET PSM, or a Python PSM identically.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain **OCL** and its role in defining **platform-independent constraints** on a PIM. Give two examples of OCL constraints. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Object Constraint Language (OCL)** is a formal, declarative language standardised by the OMG for expressing **constraints, invariants, pre/post-conditions, and query operations** on UML models. OCL is **purely declarative** — it describes *what* must be true, not *how* to compute it. It is **side-effect free**, meaning an OCL expression cannot modify the model.

**Role in PIM:**
- OCL is the **mechanism** by which business rules are encoded into a PIM **without committing to any implementation language**.
- An OCL constraint on a PIM remains **valid in any PSM** derived from it, because OCL is interpreted against UML elements, not against Java/C# objects.
- OCL makes the PIM **mathematically verifiable** — tools can perform automated consistency checks at design time.

**Example 1 (Invariant):** Every bank account must have a non-negative balance.

$$
\text{context Account inv: self.balance >= 0}
$$

**Example 2 (Pre-condition):** A withdrawal operation must request a positive amount.

$$
\text{context Account::withdraw(amount: Real) pre: amount > 0}
$$

**Example 3 (Query):** Get the count of overdue loans for a member.

$$
\text{context Member def: overdueCount : Integer = self.loans -> select(l | l.dueDate < today) -> size()}
$$

**(b)** Compare and contrast **PIM** and **PSM** with respect to their **purpose, contents, audience, longevity, and transformation mechanism**. Which one is harder to maintain and why? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | PIM | PSM |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Capture business logic independent of platform | Refine PIM for one specific platform |
| **Contents** | UML classes, attributes, OCL constraints, domain operations | UML + platform stereotypes, vendor annotations, persistence mappings |
| **Audience** | Architects, domain experts, business analysts | Platform developers, integrators |
| **Longevity** | Long-lived (decades) | Short-lived (depends on platform lifecycle) |
| **Transformation Mechanism** | Input to QVT/ATL mappings | Output of QVT/ATL mappings |
| **Reusability** | High | Low |
