| **Examples** | `Customer` with `name: String` | `@Entity class Customer { @Column String name; }` |

**Which is harder to maintain?**

The **PSM is harder to maintain**, for three reasons:

1. **Tight coupling to platform** — when a platform version changes (e.g., Java EE → Jakarta EE, .NET Framework → .NET Core), the PSM must be regenerated or manually patched.
2. **Vendor lock-in** — PSMs embed proprietary extensions that are not portable.
3. **Multiple PSMs per PIM** — maintaining *n* PSMs increases the total maintenance surface area.

In contrast, a well-designed PIM needs maintenance **only when the business changes**, not when the technology changes. This is the central economic argument for MDA.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing PIM with CIM** — many students write CIM-style prose in PIM answers. *PIM answers must use UML notation and OCL.*
> 2. **Including platform code in PIM** — anything like `@Entity`, `System.out.println`, `import javax.*` **immediately disqualifies** the model as a PIM. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this.
> 3. **Forgetting OCL** — when asked for "PIM constraints," OCL is **mandatory**. Writing rules in English only loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the transformation step** — when explaining MDA, always show **how the PIM yields multiple PSMs**. This is the *raison d'être* of the PIM.
> 5. **No class-diagram drawing** — for 7-mark "design a PIM" questions, a textual class table is acceptable, but a **hand-drawn or ASCII UML class diagram is highly preferred** and often carries a **bonus 1 mark** for visual clarity.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- 🔹 **Definition** — A PIM is a **UML-based, platform-neutral** model of a software system that captures business logic only.
- 🔹 **MDA Tiers** — `CIM → PIM → PSM` (with `CIM` being business-focused, `PIM` being system-focused, `PSM` being platform-focused).
- 🔹 **MOF Layers** — PIM lives at **M1**, conforms to UML at M2, defined by MOF at M3.
- 🔹 **Supporting Standards** — **OCL** (constraints), **XMI** (serialisation), **QVT** (transformation).
- 🔹 **PIM Contents** — Class diagrams + Use cases + Sequence/State diagrams + OCL invariants.
- 🔹 **PIM Exclusions** — No Java/.NET code, no SQL, no vendor annotations, no OS calls.
- 🔹 **Key Benefit** — **Portability + Productivity + Communication** across stakeholders.
- 🔹 **Transformation Function** — $T: PIM \rightarrow PSM$ (same PIM, many PSMs).
- 🔹 **Tools** — RSA, Papyrus, MagicDraw, Enterprise Architect, AndroMDA, Acceleo.
- 🔹 **Real-world Use** — Automotive (AUTOSAR), Banking (multi-platform deployment), Embedded systems.
- 🔹 **Exam Mantra** — *"If it mentions a technology, it's not a PIM."*

> 📚 **End of Module 2 Notes — Platform Independent Model (PIM)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Platform Specific Model (PSM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Platform Specific Model (PSM) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Platform Specific Model (PSM)** — A software model defined under the **Object Management Group (OMG)** **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)** framework. A PSM is a model of a subsystem that contains sufficient platform-specific information (e.g., language constructs, runtime APIs, middleware services) to allow a **direct code generation** for a specific target platform such as J2EE, .NET, CORBA, Android, or EJB. In simple KTU 2024 syllabus terms, a **PSM is the lowest-level model in the MDA pyramid** that sits exactly one step away from the source code. It is derived by transforming a **Platform Independent Model (PIM)** through a set of formally defined **mapping rules** that map logical constructs to their platform-specific counterparts (e.g., a UML class → an EJB Entity Bean). > [!NOTE] > **Key idea for exams:** PSM = *PIM + platform-specific details*. Removing the platform details from a PSM gives back the PIM. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architectural Blueprint vs. The Actual Building" Imagine you are constructing a house: | Layer | Real-World Analogy | MDA Equivalent | |---|---|---| | Idea / Requirements | "I want a 3-bedroom house" | **CIM** (Computation Independent Model) | | Architect's Blueprint | "3 rooms, kitchen, wiring layout" — no brand specified | **PIM** (Platform Independent Model) | | Engineer's Working Drawing | Specific wiring by Havells, tiles by Kajaria | **PSM** (Platform Specific Model) | | Actual House | Bricks, mortar, paint | **Source Code** | A PSM is like the engineer's working drawing — it still describes the system using modeling notation (UML), but every component is tied to a **particular vendor, technology, or framework**. Just as the engineer's drawing is one-step away from construction, the PSM is one-step away from compilable code. --- ## 1.3 PSM in the MDA Layered Architecture ``` ┌───────────────────────────────┐ │ CIM ── Business View │ ← Stakeholders / Analysts ├───────────────────────────────┤ │ PIM ── Logical View │ ← System Architects ├───────────────────────────────┤ │ PSM ── Technical View │ ← Platform Engineers ← [FOCUS] ├───────────────────────────────┤ │ CODE ── Implementation │ ← Developers └───────────────────────────────┘ ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick:** *"**C**ompute → **P**latform-**I**ndependent → **P**latform-**S**pecific → **C**ode"* > Mnemonic: **CIPSC** (think "Chips") — as you go down, abstraction decreases. --- ## 1.4 Characteristics of a Valid PSM A model qualifies as a PSM under OMG/MDA standards **if and only if** it satisfies: - ✅ It is derived **deterministically** from a PIM via well-defined transformation rules. - ✅ It uses platform-specific types, APIs, and stereotypes (e.g., `<>`, `<>`). - ✅ It preserves **100 % behavioural and structural semantics** of the original PIM. - ✅ It can be **directly translated** into source code by a code generator with minimal human intervention. - ✅ It conforms to a **specific technology's metamodel** (e.g., J2EE metamodel, .NET metamodel). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes ## 2.1 The MDA Transformation Chain — Why PSM Exists The fundamental objective of MDA (and therefore PSM) is to **separate business logic from platform technology**. This separation addresses one of the oldest problems in software engineering — *platform lock-in*. ### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Flow 1. **Step 1 — PIM Authoring:** Architects model the system using UML, free of platform concerns. 2. **Step 2 — Platform Selection:** A target platform is chosen (e.g., J2EE for a banking application). 3. **Step 3 — Transformation Rule Selection:** A QVT (Query/View/Transformation) or MOF rule set is chosen that knows how to map PIM constructs to platform constructs. 4. **Step 4 — PIM → PSM Transformation:** Rules are applied; PIM is enriched with stereotypes, tagged values, and platform-specific types. 5. **Step 5 — PSM Validation:** The resulting PSM is checked against the platform metamodel. 6. **Step 6 — Code Generation:** A code generator produces source files from the PSM. --- ## 2.2 PIM → PSM Mapping Rules — The Heart of MDA The transformation is governed by **mapping rules**. Below is the canonical KTU-highlighted mapping table: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Mapping Rules (PIM → PSM)** > > 1. **Class** in PIM → **Component / Bean** in PSM (e.g., EJB Session Bean, .NET Assembly). > 2. **Attribute** in PIM → **Field with typed platform data** (e.g., `String` → `varchar(255)` in DB). > 3. **Operation** in PIM → **Method with platform-specific signature** (e.g., throws `RemoteException`). > 4. **Association** in PIM → **Collection reference** (e.g., `List`, `ArrayList`). > 5. **State Machine** in PIM → **State-pattern class hierarchy** in PSM. > 6. **Use Case Realization** in PIM → **Servlet / Controller / Workflow** in PSM. > 7. **Persistence Class** (stereotype `<>`) → **Entity Bean / JPA Entity / DAO**. > 8. **User Interface Class** → **JSP / ASPX / Android Activity**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Notation / Symbol | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | MDA Layers | CIM, PIM, PSM, Code | 4-layer abstraction pyramid of OMG-MDA | | 2 | Transformation Direction | `f : PIM → PSM` | Function f maps PIM elements to PSM elements | | 3 | Preservation Property | `sem(PSM) ≡ sem(PIM)` | Semantics must be preserved | | 4 | Traceability | `trace(e_PIM, e_PSM)` | Every PSM element traces back to a PIM element | | 5 | Standard for Transformation | **QVT**, **MOF 2.0 QVT** | OMG standard transformation language | | 6 | Metamodel Standard | **MOF** (Meta-Object Facility) | Defines structure of modelling languages | | 7 | Code Gen Function | `g : PSM → Code` | Code generator g produces source code | | 8 | Round-trip Engineering | `g ∘ f (PIM) → Code` | Composition of transformations | | 9 | Common PSM Targets | J2EE, .NET, CORBA, Android, ROS, Cloud | Industrial target platforms | | 10 | Industrial PSM Tools | AndroMDA, ArcStyler, IBM Rational, EMF, Acceleo | MDA-compliant tools | > [!TIP] > Use `f` for PIM → PSM, `g` for PSM → Code. The composition **g ∘ f** is the **full MDA pipeline**. --- ## 2.4 Why is PSM Important in Real Engineering? - **Portability:** Re-generate PSM for a new platform by simply changing the transformation rules — the PIM remains unchanged. - **Productivity:** Code generation from PSM can automate 60–80 % of boilerplate code. - **Consistency:** Standards-based modelling eliminates syntactic variations across developers. - **Maintenance:** A change in the PIM propagates to all PSMs and consequently to all platforms uniformly. - **Documentation:** The PSM serves as a *living, executable, technical contract* between architects and developers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Walkthrough & Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example — "Library Management System" PIM → PSM Transformation Consider the following **PIM** written in plain UML: ``` Class: Book Attributes: - title : String - isbn : String - price : double Operations: + getTitle() : String + setPrice(p : double) : void ``` ### Step 1 — Platform Selection For this walkthrough, we select **J2EE + EJB 3.0** as the target platform. ### Step 2 — Apply Stereotype for Persistence ``` <> Class: Book ``` The `<>` stereotype is added because the class maps to database storage. ### Step 3 — Attribute Type Mapping PIM uses abstract `String` and `double`. The J2EE PSM replaces these with platform-specific Java types: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{title} : \text{String} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{title} : \text{java.lang.String} \\ \text{isbn} : \text{String} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{isbn} : \text{java.lang.String},\;\text{@Column(length=13)} \\ \text{price} : \text{double} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{price} : \text{double},\;\text{@Column(precision=2)} \end{aligned} $$ Each annotation (`@Column`, `@Entity`) is a **platform-specific tagged value** injected during transformation. ### Step 4 — Operation Signature Mapping PIM operation `setPrice(p : double) : void` is mapped to: $$ \text{setPrice}(\text{double p}) : \text{void} \;\;\xrightarrow{f}\;\; \text{public void setPrice}(\text{double p})\;\text{throws RemoteException} $$ ### Step 5 — Final PSM (UML with Platform Stereotypes) ``` <> Class: Book Attributes: - title : java.lang.String [length = 200] - isbn : java.lang.String [length = 13, unique] - price : double [precision = 2] Operations: + getTitle() : java.lang.String + setPrice(double p) : void throws RemoteException ``` ### Step 6 — Code Generation A code generator (e.g., Acceleo) reads the PSM and emits Java code: $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{@Entity} \\ \texttt{public class Book } \{ \\ \quad \texttt{@Column(length=200) private String title;} \\ \quad \texttt{@Column(length=13, unique=true) private String isbn;} \\ \quad \texttt{@Column(precision=2) private double price;} \\ \quad \texttt{public String getTitle() \{ return title; \}} \\ \quad \texttt{public void setPrice(double p) throws RemoteException \{ price = p; \}} \\ \} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quality Attributes Achieved by PSM | Quality Attribute | Achieved by PSM? | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | Platform Independence | Indirect (via PIM) | PIM is unchanged; PSM regenerated | | Maintainability | High | Centralized rules; one fix propagates | | Traceability | Guaranteed | Each PSM element maps to a PIM element | | Tool Interoperability | High | PSM conforms to MOF metamodel | | Performance Tuning | Platform-Aware | PSM allows fine-grained optimizations | | Round-Trip Engineering | Supported | Reverse-engineering code back to PSM | --- ## 3.3 Mathematical / Logical View of Transformation Let us formalize the transformation as a function: $$ f : \text{PIM} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{PSM} $$ The function `f` is composed of a set of mapping rules: $$ f \;=\; \{r_1,\, r_2,\, r_3,\, \ldots,\, r_n\} $$ Each rule `r_i` is of the form: $$ r_i : \text{SourcePattern} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{TargetPattern}\;\vert\;\text{Guard} $$ - **SourcePattern** — a pattern matched in the PIM. - **TargetPattern** — the corresponding pattern produced in the PSM. - **Guard** — an OCL (Object Constraint Language) condition that must hold true for the rule to fire. > [!NOTE] > **For KTU exams:** If asked *"What are the components of a transformation rule?"*, always answer with the triple **SourcePattern → TargetPattern | Guard** and cite the standard **QVT**. --- ## 3.4 Common Pitfalls in PIM → PSM Transformation | # | Pitfall | Consequence | Remedy | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Adding business logic in PSM | Violates separation of concerns | Keep logic in PIM only | | 2 | One-to-many PIM elements collapsed silently | Loss of semantics | Use explicit `trace` relations | | 3 | Platform-specific types leaking into PIM | Defeats MDA's purpose | Enforce PIM metamodel rules | | 4 | Non-deterministic rules | Different PSMs each run | Use QVT Relations (declarative) | | 5 | Missing reverse engineering | No round-trip | Use tools supporting round-trip | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 MDA Pyramid — Role of PSM in the Abstraction Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph layer4["CODE LAYER"] codeA["Java Source Files"] codeB["XML Deployment Descriptors"] codeC["EJB JAR / WAR Files"] end subgraph layer3["PSM LAYER - PLATFORM SPECIFIC MODEL"] psmA["UML + J2EE Stereotypes"] psmB["Entity Beans, Session Beans"] psmC["Servlet and JSP Nodes"] psmD["JDBC Persistence Mapping"] end subgraph layer2["PIM LAYER - PLATFORM INDEPENDENT MODEL"] pimA["Pure UML Class Diagrams"] pimB["Use Case Realizations"] pimC["Business State Machines"] end subgraph layer1["CIM LAYER - COMPUTATION INDEPENDENT MODEL"] cimA["Business Process Models"] cimB["Domain Glossaries"] cimC["Stakeholder Requirements"] end layer1 --> layer2 layer2 --> layer3 layer3 --> layer4 style layer1 fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px style layer2 fill:#D4E6F1,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px style layer3 fill:#AED6F1,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px style layer4 fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 PIM → PSM Transformation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PIM Repository
UML 2.0 XMI Files"] --> B["Transformation Engine
QVT or MOFScript"] B --> C{"Platform
Selector"} C -->|J2EE| D1["J2EE PSM
with EJB Stereotypes"] C -->|.NET| D2["NET PSM
with Assembly Stereotypes"] C -->|Android| D3["Android PSM
with Activity Stereotypes"] C -->|CORBA| D4["CORBA PSM
with IDL Stereotypes"] D1 --> E["Code Generator
Acceleo or AndroMDA"] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F["Generated Source Code
Java C Sharp XML"] F --> G["Compilation and Build"] G --> H["Deployable Artifact
JAR WAR APK"] style A fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B9770E,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#F8CBAD,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#F4B183,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#F4B183,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#E06666,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Round-Trip Engineering Loop with PSM ```mermaid flowchart TB PIM1["PIM"] -->|f: forward transform| PSM1["PSM"] PSM1 -->|g: code gen| CODE1["Generated Code"] CODE1 -->|manual edits by developer| CODE2["Modified Code"] CODE2 -->|h: reverse engineer| PSM2["Recovered PSM"] PSM2 -->|k: re-merge into PIM| PIM1 style PIM1 fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F618D,stroke-width:2px style PSM1 fill:#AED6F1,stroke:#1F618D,stroke-width:2px style CODE1 fill:#82E0AA,stroke:#239B56,stroke-width:2px style CODE2 fill:#F9E79F,stroke:#B7950B,stroke-width:2px style PSM2 fill:#AED6F1,stroke:#1F618D,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **Why this matters:** Round-trip engineering is essential in industry because developers always edit generated code. The reverse transformation `h` must reconcile the manual changes back into the PSM and PIM. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define Platform Specific Model (PSM). How does it differ from PIM?** **Model Answer:** A *Platform Specific Model (PSM)* is a model in the OMG MDA framework that represents a system using platform-specific constructs (types, APIs, stereotypes) for a chosen technology such as J2EE or .NET, enabling direct code generation. It differs from a *Platform Independent Model (PIM)* which is purely logical and contains no platform references. The PSM is obtained by transforming a PIM using mapping rules; removing those platform details from a PSM yields the original PIM. --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **List any four common target platforms for which PSMs are typically built.** **Model Answer:** The four common target platforms are: 1. **J2EE / Java EE** — for enterprise web applications (EJB, JSP). 2. **.NET Framework** — for Microsoft-based enterprise systems. 3. **CORBA** — for distributed object systems. 4. **Android** — for mobile applications. *(Other valid answers: ROS for robotics, Cloud Foundry, Salesforce Apex.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students lose marks because they (a) confuse PSM with PIM, (b) omit the *transformation rule* discussion, or (c) fail to mention **QVT** as the OMG standard transformation language. Always structure your answer in the order: **Definition → Diagram → Transformation → Example → Tools**. --- ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the four-layer MDA architecture (CIM, PIM, PSM, Code). Clearly highlight the role of PSM. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the process of transforming a PIM to a PSM using mapping rules. Illustrate with an example showing how a UML `Class` and its `attributes` are mapped to a J2EE EJB. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): The four-layer MDA architecture as defined by the **Object Management Group (OMG)** is: 1. **CIM (Computation Independent Model)** — Captures business requirements and domain vocabulary without any system or platform concerns. Often expressed using BPMN or domain glossaries. 2. **PIM (Platform Independent Model)** — Represents the software's structure and behaviour using UML but without committing to any platform. Uses pure UML types like `String`, `int`. 3. **PSM (Platform Specific Model)** — A platform-specific view obtained by transforming the PIM. It uses platform stereotypes, tagged values, and platform APIs. 4. **Code** — Source code generated from the PSM using a code generator. *(Draw the MDA pyramid diagram — see Section 4.1.)* The **PSM** plays the role of the *bridge* between design and implementation — it absorbs all platform complexity so the source code can be generated almost mechanically. --- #### Model Solution for (b): The PIM-to-PSM transformation is governed by **mapping rules** expressed in standards like **QVT (Query/View/Transformation)** or **MOFScript**. **Steps of Transformation:** 1. Select the target platform (e.g., J2EE). 2. Load the PIM from a repository (XMI files). 3. Apply the platform-specific transformation rule set. 4. Inject stereotypes (e.g., `<>`), tagged values (e.g., `@Column(length=200)`), and platform types. 5. Validate the resulting PSM against the target metamodel. **Illustration — UML Class to EJB:** Consider PIM: ``` Class: Account - balance : double + deposit(amount : double) : void ``` After transformation for **J2EE EJB 3.0**, the PSM becomes: ``` <> Class: Account - balance : double [precision=2] + deposit(double amount) : void throws RemoteException ``` The mapping rules applied are: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Class} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{Bean with stereotype } \texttt{<>} \\ \text{balance} : \text{double} &\;\rightarrow\; \texttt{@Column(precision=2) private double balance;} \\ \text{deposit(amount)} &\;\rightarrow\; \texttt{public void deposit(double amount) throws RemoteException} \end{aligned} $$ A code generator then emits the deployable EJB JAR. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]` **(a)** Discuss in detail the benefits and limitations of using PSM in Model-Driven Development. Mention any three PSM-targeting tools. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With suitable diagrams, explain round-trip engineering using PSM. Why is reverse engineering essential when developers modify generated code? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Benefits of PSM:** - ✅ **Automatic Code Generation:** PSMs enable up to 80 % automation of boilerplate code. - ✅ **Portability:** A single PIM can be re-targeted to multiple platforms by simply switching the transformation rule set. - ✅ **Consistency:** Standardized PSMs eliminate syntactic variations across teams. - ✅ **Traceability:** Every PSM element is traceable to its PIM origin. - ✅ **Quality:** The platform metamodel can enforce constraints (e.g., nullability, length). **Limitations of PSM:** - ❌ **Tooling Cost:** Commercial MDA tools (e.g., IBM Rational, ArcStyler) are expensive. - ❌ **Learning Curve:** Teams must learn QVT, MOF, OCL, and UML 2.0 deeply. - ❌ **Performance Concerns:** Generated code may not match hand-tuned code in performance-critical systems. - ❌ **Round-Trip Complexity:** Reconciling manual changes is non-trivial. - ❌ **Metamodel Dependency:** PSM is only as good as its target metamodel. **Three PSM-targeting Tools:** 1. **AndroMDA** — Open-source MDA tool producing J2EE, .NET, and Spring PSMs. 2. **Acceleo** — Eclipse-based code generator producing PSM and code from EMF/UML models. 3. **ArcStyler** — Industrial-strength MDA tool for J2EE and .NET PSMs. *(Other tools: EMF, OptimalJ, MagicDraw.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Round-Trip Engineering** is the process of forward-engineering a model to code and then reverse-engineering modified code back into the model, while preserving a consistent state. **Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 — the round-trip loop diagram.)* The pipeline is: $$ \text{PIM} \;\xrightarrow{f}\; \text{PSM} \;\xrightarrow{g}\; \text{Code} \;\xrightarrow{h}\; \text{Modified Code} \;\xrightarrow{k}\; \text{Recovered PSM} $$ **Why Reverse Engineering is Essential:** 1. **Developers Always Edit Code:** Generated code rarely stays unchanged; performance tuning, bug fixes, and feature additions all happen in source files. 2. **Avoid Drift:** Without reverse engineering, the model becomes stale and useless for future regenerations. 3. **Preserve Knowledge:** Manual optimizations must be fed back into the PSM so that the next generation cycle inherits them. 4. **Audit & Compliance:** Reverse-engineered PSM lets auditors trace every line of code to its design intent. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **PSM = PIM + platform details** (one step away from code). - ✅ Part of **OMG's MDA** framework — 4 layers: **CIM → PIM → PSM → Code**. - ✅ Built using **platform stereotypes** (e.g., `<>`, `<>`) and **tagged values**. - ✅ Transformation is governed by **mapping rules**, standardized via **QVT** and **OCL**. - ✅ Common target platforms: **J2EE, .NET, CORBA, Android, Cloud**. - ✅ Industrial tools: **AndroMDA, ArcStyler, Acceleo, IBM Rational, EMF**. - ✅ Mathematical model: `f : PIM → PSM` and `g : PSM → Code`; composition `g ∘ f` is the full MDA pipeline. - ✅ Round-trip engineering needs both forward (`f`, `g`) and reverse (`h`) transformations. - ✅ **Preservation property** `sem(PSM) ≡ sem(PIM)` must always hold. - ✅ For KTU answers, **always include a diagram** — examiners reward visual clarity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Mnemonic:** *"**P**latform **S**pecific **M**odel — **P**repares **S**ource for **M**anufacturing"* — i.e., PSM prepares the system for code manufacturing.

topics.title: Introduction to AADL
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to AADL

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AADL (Architecture Analysis and Design Language)** is a standardized, model-based engineering language defined by the **SAE International standard AS5506**. It is specifically designed to analyze and describe the **software and hardware architecture** of **embedded real-time systems**, particularly **safety-critical distributed systems** such as those found in avionics, aerospace, automotive, and medical devices.

AADL provides a formal vocabulary (a meta-model) for representing an embedded system as an architecture composed of clearly identifiable **components** (software, hardware, and system) interacting through well-defined **connections** (ports, parameters, and subprogram calls). It supports early-stage **architecture-level analysis** — including performance, schedulability, reliability, and security — even before a single line of implementation code is written.

> [!NOTE]
> **SAE AS5506** is the formal standard document. Whenever a KTU question references "AADL Standard," the expected answer is **SAE AS5506** (the current revision as of recent updates is **AS5506/3**, with the core being **AS5506/2**). Always quote the standard number for full marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing an aircraft's **autopilot system** as building a house:
- You cannot just start laying bricks. You first need an **architectural blueprint** showing where the rooms, electrical wiring, plumbing, and load-bearing walls go.
- AADL is precisely that **blueprint** for embedded software and hardware systems.
- In a house blueprint, you see *components* (kitchen, bedroom), *connections* (doorways, hallways), and *properties* (room size, wall material).
- In an **AADL model**, you declare *components* (processor, process, thread, memory), *connections* (ports, bus access), and *properties* (execution time, memory footprint, latency).

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary**: AADL = "Universal architectural blueprint language" for embedded systems, ratified as an industry standard so that tools, engineers, and verification suites all "speak the same language" about system architecture.

## 1.3 Key High-Yield Facts (Bold Standard Metrics)

| Standard Item | Value / Identifier |
|---|---|
| **AADL Standard Document** | **SAE AS5506 (AS5506B / AS5506/1, /2, /3)** |
| **Originating Body** | **SAE International (Society of Automotive Engineers)** |
| **Originally Based On** | **MetaH & UML (Hume's modeling approach at Honeywell)** |
| **First AADL Standard Released** | **2004 (AS5506)** |
| **Modeling Paradigm** | **Component-based, Architecture-centric, Textual + Graphical (UML profile)** |
| **Primary Domain** | **Embedded Real-Time Distributed Safety-Critical Systems** |

## 1.4 Scope of AADL

- AADL models three major **execution categories**:
  1. **Software components** — Process, Thread, Thread group, Subprogram, Data
  2. **Hardware/Execution Platform components** — Processor, Memory, Bus, Device
  3. **System (Composite) components** — System, Abstract
- AADL models **interactions** through ports, parameters, subprogram access, and bus access.
- AADL models **properties** describing non-functional attributes (timing, memory, scheduling, security, fault-tolerance).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why AADL? — The "Why" Behind the Language

Before AADL, system architects used **ad-hoc UML diagrams**, free-form documentation, or proprietary modeling tools. These had critical weaknesses:
- **Ambiguity** — UML is a general-purpose language; it has no precise semantics for *timing*, *schedulability*, or *binding to hardware*.
- **No analyzability** — You couldn't run a performance or reliability analysis directly on a UML diagram.
- **Vendor lock-in** — Proprietary tools had their own dialects.

AADL was created to solve these specific problems for **embedded real-time systems**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern**: *"Why was AADL developed when UML already existed?"* — Answer: UML lacks **precise execution semantics**, **timing semantics**, and **hardware-binding constructs** required for embedded real-time systems. AADL provides **analyzable**, **formal**, and **standardized** architectural models.

## 2.2 Goals of AADL (KTU High-Yield Bullets)

1. **Precise modeling of runtime architecture** of embedded software systems.
2. **Unambiguous specification** of timing, performance, and fault-tolerance attributes.
3. **Tool-independent textual and graphical representation** (multiple tool vendors can parse the same model).
4. **Architecture-level analysis** — schedulability, timing, reliability, security — *before* code is written.
5. **Support for incremental development** — refine abstract models into detailed ones.
6. **International standard** (AS5506) ensuring portability of architectural models.

## 2.3 AADL Core Structural Elements

AADL has **three pillars** of modeling:

| Pillar | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Components** | The "boxes" of the architecture | process, thread, processor, memory, bus, system, subprogram, data |
| **Connections** | The "wires" linking components | port connections, parameter connections, bus access, subprogram access |
| **Properties** | The "annotations" giving non-functional detail | execution time, deadline, period, memory size, scheduling protocol |

## 2.4 AADL Component Categories — The Full Taxonomy

AADL classifies all components into **three super-categories**:

1. **Software Components** (logical, not physical)
   - `process` — Protected address space (e.g., a flight-control process).
   - `thread` — Unit of concurrent execution (e.g., sensor-sampling thread).
   - `thread group` — Logical grouping of threads.
   - `subprogram` — Callable code unit (function/procedure).
   - `data` — Data type or data component.

2. **Execution Platform (Hardware) Components** (physical)
   - `processor` — Computing unit (CPU core, microcontroller).
   - `memory` — Storage (RAM, ROM, flash).
   - `bus` — Communication medium (CAN, ARINC 659, Ethernet).
   - `device` — External I/O (sensor, actuator).

3. **System (Composite) Components**
   - `system` — Top-level composite, combines software + hardware.
   - `abstract` — Generic component with no category semantics (used for early-stage modeling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Composition Rule**: Components contain sub-components. For example, a `system` contains `process`es; a `process` contains `thread`s; a `thread` contains `subprogram`s. This recursive composition is what makes AADL a true architectural language.

## 2.5 Connections in AADL

| Connection Type | Between | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Port connection** | Components with ports | Data/event flow |
| **Parameter connection** | Subprogram caller ↔ callee | Subprogram call argument passing |
| **Subprogram access** | Threads ↔ subprograms | Calls a subprogram remotely |
| **Bus access** | Processors/memory ↔ bus | Bind components to communication bus |
| **Data access** | Components ↔ data | Shared data access |

## 2.6 Properties in AADL

Properties attach **non-functional** attributes to components. Key property categories:
- **Timing**: `Period`, `Deadline`, `Execution_Time`, `Compute_Execution_Time`, `Dispatch_Offset`
- **Memory**: `Source_Code_Size`, `Source_Data_Size`, `Memory_Size`
- **Scheduling**: `Scheduling_Protocol` (e.g., RMS, EDF, Sporadic)
- **Reliability/Fault**: `Source_Text`, `SEI_CMMI_Level`
- **Distribution**: `Actual_Connection_Transport`

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Property Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> AADL is descriptive (not formula-driven like physics), but these property *expressions* are frequently tested as 3-mark items.

| Property | Example Syntax | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `Period => 10 ms` | Specifies thread period | The thread dispatches every 10 ms |
| `Deadline => 5 ms` | Thread deadline | Must complete within 5 ms of dispatch |
| `Execution_Time => 2 ms .. 4 ms` | WCET range | Worst-case execution time interval |
| `Memory_Size => 256 KB` | Memory allocation | Component occupies 256 KB |
| `Scheduling_Protocol => (POSIX_1003_HIGH_PRIORITY)` | Scheduling discipline | Real-time scheduling algorithm |

> Critical equation occasionally asked in KTU (throughput / load analysis based on AADL-extracted properties):

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

Where $C_i$ is **Compute_Execution_Time** of thread $i$, and $T_i$ is its **Period**. The system is **schedulable under Rate Monotonic** iff $U \le n(\sqrt[n]{2} - 1)$.

## 2.8 AADL Annexes (KTU Examiner Favourite)

Annexes are **standardized extensions** that add specialized modeling capability:

| Annex | Standard Suffix | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Behavior Annex** | AS5506/2 | Finite-state-machine behavior inside components |
| **Error Model Annex** | AS5506/4 | Fault propagation, error states, reliability analysis |
| **ARINC 653 Annex** | AS5506/5 | Avionics partitioning (ARINC 653) |
| **Data Modeling Annex** | AS5506/7 | Complex data type modeling |
| **Synchronous Annex** | AS5506/6 | Synchronous reactive systems |
| **Security Annex** | (proposed) | Security attributes |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics**: FAA DO-178C compliance verification using AADL-derived models.
- **Automotive**: ISO 26262 functional safety analysis.
- **Aerospace**: NASA, ESA, Boeing, Airbus deploy AADL for safety-critical subsystems.
- **Defense**: US Army, US Navy (e.g., the Joint Multi-Role (JMR) program).
- **Medical devices**: IEC 62304 compliant embedded systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry-Standard AADL Tools**: **OSATE** (Open Source AADL Tool Environment — built on Eclipse), **STOOD**, **Cheddar** (schedulability analysis), **Marzhin**, **AADL Inspector** (Ellidiss Technologies).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 AADL Model Construction — Step-by-Step Derivation of an AADL Package

This section shows the **exhaustive derivation** of a canonical AADL model for a simple **Avionics Attitude Indicator System**, walking through every element.

### Step 1: Create the AADL Package

Every AADL model is wrapped inside a **package**. The package name must match the file name (e.g., `attitude_indicator.aadl`).

```aadl
package Attitude_Indicator
public
  -- declarations go here
end Attitude_Indicator;
```

> **Logic**: A package scopes all component declarations; without it, the model is invalid. This satisfies the AADL meta-model's top-level containment rule.

### Step 2: Declare the Top-Level System (Composite)

```aadl
system Attitude_Indicator_System
end Attitude_Indicator_System;
```

> **Logic**: The `system` is the composite top-level that aggregates software and hardware. It is the **root component** of the architecture.

### Step 3: Declare Sub-Components (Software + Hardware) Inside the System

```aadl
system implementation Attitude_Indicator_System.Impl
  subcomponents
    ctrl_proc   : process Guidance_Process.Impl;
    display_proc: process Display_Process.Impl;
    main_cpu    : processor Main_Cpu.Impl;
    shared_mem  : memory Shared_Memory.Impl;
    avionics_bus: bus Avionics_Bus.Impl;
end Attitude_Indicator_System.Impl;
```

> **Logic**: The `.Impl` suffix denotes a concrete **implementation**, which is the AADL way of separating *type* (interface contract) from *implementation* (internal structure).

### Step 4: Define the Sub-Components

#### Hardware Platform

```aadl
processor Main_Cpu
  properties
    Processor_Speed => 500 MHz;
end Main_Cpu;

processor implementation Main_Cpu.Impl
end Main_Cpu.Impl;

memory Shared_Memory
  properties
    Memory_Size => 4 MB;
end Shared_Memory;

memory implementation Shared_Memory.Impl
end Shared_Memory.Impl;

bus Avionics_Bus
end Avionics_Bus;

bus implementation Avionics_Bus.Impl
end Avionics_Bus.Impl;
```

#### Software Processes

```aadl
process Guidance_Process
  features
    sensor_input : in data port Sensor_Data;
    display_data : out data port Display_Info;
end Guidance_Process;

process implementation Guidance_Process.Impl
  subcomponents
    sampling_t : thread Sampling_Thread.Impl;
    computing_t: thread Computation_Thread.Impl;
end Guidance_Process.Impl;

process Display_Process
  features
    display_data: in data port Display_Info;
end Display_Process;

process implementation Display_Process.Impl
end Display_Process.Impl;
```

### Step 5: Define Data Types and Threads

```aadl
data Sensor_Data
  properties
    Data_Size => 32 Bytes;
end Sensor_Data;

data Display_Info
end Display_Info;

thread Sampling_Thread
  features
    sensor_in: in data port Sensor_Data;
    sample_out: out data port Sensor_Data;
  properties
    Period => 10 ms;
    Deadline => 5 ms;
    Compute_Execution_Time => 1 ms .. 2 ms;
    Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic;
end Sampling_Thread;

thread implementation Sampling_Thread.Impl
end Sampling_Thread.Impl;

thread Computation_Thread
  features
    sample_in: in data port Sensor_Data;
    result_out: out data port Display_Info;
  properties
    Period => 20 ms;
    Deadline => 10 ms;
    Compute_Execution_Time => 3 ms .. 5 ms;
    Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic;
end Computation_Thread;

thread implementation Computation_Thread.Impl
end Computation_Thread.Impl;
```

### Step 6: Add Connections

```aadl
process implementation Guidance_Process.Impl
  subcomponents
    sampling_t : thread Sampling_Thread.Impl;
    computing_t: thread Computation_Thread.Impl;
  connections
    c1: port sampling_t.sample_out -> computing_t.sample_in;
end Guidance_Process.Impl;
```

### Step 7: Add the Property Annex (Properties for Components)

```aadl
system implementation Attitude_Indicator_System.Impl
  subcomponents
    ctrl_proc   : process Guidance_Process.Impl;
    display_proc: process Display_Process.Impl;
    main_cpu    : processor Main_Cpu.Impl;
    shared_mem  : memory Shared_Memory.Impl;
    avionics_bus: bus Avionics_Bus.Impl;
  connections
    c1: port ctrl_proc.display_data -> display_proc.display_data;
    c2: bus access main_cpu -> avionics_bus;
  properties
    Actual_Processor_Binding => (reference (main_cpu)) applies to ctrl_proc;
    Actual_Memory_Binding => (reference (shared_mem)) applies to ctrl_proc;
end Attitude_Indicator_System.Impl;
```

### Step 8: Derivation of Schedulability Using the AADL-Extracted Properties

Given the AADL-extracted properties:

$$
C_1 = 2 \text{ ms}, \quad T_1 = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
C_2 = 5 \text{ ms}, \quad T_2 = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

The **processor utilization** is derived as:

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{5}{20}
$$

$$
U = 0.200 + 0.250
$$

$$
U = 0.450
$$

The **Rate Monotonic Schedulability Bound** for $n = 2$ tasks:

$$
U_{\text{RMS}, n=2} = 2(\sqrt[2]{2} - 1)
$$

$$
U_{\text{RMS}, n=2} = 2(1.4142 - 1)
$$

$$
U_{\text{RMS}, n=2} = 2 \times 0.4142
$$

$$
U_{\text{RMS}, n=2} = 0.8284
$$

**Conclusion**: Since $U = 0.450 \le 0.8284 = U_{\text{RMS}, n=2}$, the system is **schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — AADL vs. Other Languages

| Property | AADL | UML | SysML | Simulink |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Body | **SAE AS5506** | OMG | OMG | MathWorks |
| Real-time semantics | **Yes (precise)** | No | Limited | No |
| Hardware binding | **Yes (native)** | No | Yes | No |
| Tool-independent textual | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | No (proprietary) |
| Schedulability analysis | **Yes (via OSATE + Cheddar)** | No | Limited | No |
| Error/Fault modeling | **Yes (Error Model Annex)** | No | Yes | Limited |
| Analyzability | **High** | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Primary use | **Safety-critical embedded** | General software | General systems | Control simulation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AADL Component Taxonomy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AADL[AADL Components AS5506] --> SW[Software Components]
    AADL --> HW[Execution Platform Components]
    AADL --> SYS[System Composite Components]

    SW --> PROC[process]
    SW --> THR[thread]
    SW --> TG[thread group]
    SW --> SUB[subprogram]
    SW --> DAT[data]

    HW --> CPU[processor]
    HW --> MEM[memory]
    HW --> BUS[bus]
    HW --> DEV[device]

    SYS --> SYST[system]
    SYS --> ABS[abstract]

    PROC -. contains .-> THR
    THR -. calls .-> SUB
    THR -. shares .-> DAT
    PROC -. bound to .-> CPU
    PROC -. uses .-> MEM
    CPU -. connects via .-> BUS
    DEV -. attached to .-> BUS
    SYST -. composes .-> PROC
    SYST -. composes .-> CPU
```

> **Figure Interpretation**: The diagram shows the three top-level AADL component categories and the recursive "contains/bound-to/uses" relationships that define an embedded architecture.

## 4.2 AADL Model Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[System Requirements] --> B[Abstract AADL Model]
    B --> C[Concrete Implementation Model]
    C --> D[Property Annex Addition]
    D --> E[Behavior Annex and Error Model]
    E --> F[OSATE Tooling Import]
    F --> G[Schedulability Analysis via Cheddar]
    F --> H[Reliability Analysis via Stood or AADL Inspector]
    F --> I[Code Generation via OCARINA]
    G --> J[Verification and Validation]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compliant Embedded System]
```

> **Figure Interpretation**: The pipeline shows the iterative refinement path from a textual requirements document to a fully verified AADL-based safety-critical system.

## 4.3 Avionics Attitude Indicator — AADL Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SYS[Attitude_Indicator_System] --> PROC1[Guidance_Process]
    SYS --> PROC2[Display_Process]
    SYS --> CPU[Main_Cpu Processor]
    SYS --> MEM[Shared_Memory]
    SYS --> BUSA[Avionics_Bus]

    PROC1 --> T1[Sampling_Thread]
    PROC1 --> T2[Computation_Thread]
    PROC2 --> D1[Display_Thread]

    T1 -- Sensor_Data --> T2
    T2 -- Display_Info --> PROC1
    PROC1 -- Display_Info --> PROC2
    PROC2 -- Display_Info --> D1

    PROC1 -. bound to .-> CPU
    PROC2 -. bound to .-> CPU
    PROC1 -. uses .-> MEM
    CPU -. bus access .-> BUSA
```

## 4.4 AADL Language Stack Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Domain Engineering: Avionics, Automotive, Medical] --> L2[System Architecture: AADL Core AS5506]
    L2 --> L3[Annex Layers: Behavior, Error Model, ARINC 653, Data]
    L3 --> L4[Tooling Layer: OSATE, STOOD, AADL Inspector]
    L4 --> L5[Analysis Layer: Cheddar, Marzhin, Reliability Tools]
    L5 --> L6[Code Generation: OCARINA to Ada or C]
    L6 --> L7[Target Hardware: Embedded Board]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`**

### Q1. Define AADL. Mention its standard identifier.
**(3 Marks) — Model Answer:**

> AADL stands for **Architecture Analysis and Design Language**. It is a standardized **model-based engineering language** defined by **SAE International under standard AS5506** (currently AS5506/2 and AS5506/3). AADL is used to model the **software and hardware architecture of embedded real-time systems**, especially **safety-critical and distributed** systems. It provides a precise textual and graphical vocabulary for components, connections, and properties, enabling **early architecture-level analysis** (timing, schedulability, reliability) before code implementation.

### Q2. List the three main categories of AADL components with two examples each.
**(3 Marks) — Model Answer:**

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Software Components** | `process`, `thread`, `subprogram`, `data`, `thread group` |
| **Execution Platform (Hardware) Components** | `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device` |
| **System (Composite) Components** | `system`, `abstract` |

> The three categories are: **Software**, **Execution Platform (Hardware)**, and **System (Composite)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the major **goals of AADL**. Why is AADL preferred over UML for embedded real-time systems? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, describe the **AADL component categories** (software, hardware, system) and explain how **connections and properties** are specified in an AADL model. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) AADL Goals and AADL vs. UML

**Goals of AADL**:
1. **Precise modeling** of runtime architecture of embedded software.
2. **Non-functional attribute specification** — timing, memory, fault-tolerance, security.
3. **Tool-independent** representation (textual + graphical).
4. **Standardized** under SAE AS5506.
5. **Supports incremental refinement** — abstract to detailed models.
6. **Analyzable** — schedulability, reliability, timing analysis at architecture level.
7. **Hardware-binding** — explicit binding of software to processors, memory, and buses.

**AADL vs. UML for Embedded Real-Time Systems**:

| Aspect | AADL | UML |
|---|---|---|
| **Real-time semantics** | Precise (period, deadline, execution time) | None |
| **Hardware binding** | First-class construct | Not native |
| **Schedulability analysis** | Direct (via Cheddar/OCARINA) | Not supported |
| **Standardization** | SAE AS5506 | OMG (UML 2.5) |
| **Embedded focus** | Yes | No (general-purpose) |
| **Analyzability** | High | Low |

> **Conclusion**: AADL is preferred over UML for embedded real-time systems because UML lacks **precise timing semantics, hardware-binding, and architectural analysis capabilities**, all of which AADL provides natively.

#### Model Answer — (b) AADL Component Categories, Connections, and Properties

(Refer to **Section 2.4, 2.5, 2.6** for the complete taxonomy and **Section 3.1** for a worked example.)

Key elements to mention:
- **Software components**: process, thread, subprogram, data.
- **Hardware (Execution Platform) components**: processor, memory, bus, device.
- **System components**: system, abstract.
- **Connections**: port, parameter, subprogram access, bus access, data access.
- **Properties**: `Period => 10 ms`, `Deadline => 5 ms`, `Compute_Execution_Time => 1 ms .. 2 ms`, `Scheduling_Protocol => RMS`.

Provide the **Avionics Attitude Indicator example** from Section 3.1 as a concrete demonstration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** What are **AADL Annexes**? Explain any three standard annexes briefly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **AADL model construction pipeline** with the aid of a diagram. Mention at least three AADL-based analysis tools. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) AADL Annexes

**AADL Annexes** are **standardized extensions** of the core AS5506 language that add specialized modeling capabilities without modifying the core meta-model.

Three major annexes:

1. **Behavior Annex (AS5506/2)**:
   - Adds **finite-state-machine** behavioral specifications inside components.
   - Allows AADL components to specify **subprogram call sequences** and **state transitions**.

2. **Error Model Annex (AS5506/4)**:
   - Models **fault occurrence, propagation, and recovery**.
   - Used for **reliability and safety analysis** (e.g., FTA, FMEA).

3. **ARINC 653 Annex (AS5506/5)**:
   - Models **avionics partitioning** per the ARINC 653 standard.
   - Supports **spatial and temporal partitioning** for safety-critical avionics software.

#### Model Answer — (b) AADL Model Construction Pipeline and Tools

(Refer to **Section 4.2** for the diagram and explanation.)

**Pipeline Steps**:
1. **Requirements** — Gather system requirements.
2. **Abstract Model** — Create high-level AADL model (using `abstract` and `system`).
3. **Implementation Model** — Refine into concrete components with sub-components.
4. **Property Addition** — Add timing, memory, scheduling properties.
5. **Annex Extension** — Add Behavior/Error/ARINC 653 annexes.
6. **Tool Import** — Import into OSATE (Open Source AADL Tool Environment).
7. **Analysis** — Run schedulability, reliability, and timing analyses.
8. **Code Generation** — Generate code via OCARINA (for Ada or C targets).

**AADL Tools**:
- **OSATE** (Open Source AADL Tool Environment) — Eclipse-based editor.
- **OCARINA** — AADL code generator (Ada, C, RTSJ).
- **Cheddar** — Real-time schedulability analyzer.
- **STOOD** — Airbus AADL tool (legacy).
- **AADL Inspector** — Ellidiss commercial tool.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse AADL with UML** — UML is general-purpose; AADL is specifically for embedded real-time systems. Lose 2 marks if confused.
> 2. **Always quote the standard number** — "SAE AS5506" is mandatory for full marks. Writing only "AADL" without the standard number is incomplete.
> 3. **Three categories must be named explicitly** — Software, Execution Platform, System (Composite). Students often miss the **System (Composite)** category and list only two.
> 4. **Properties are *not* optional annotations** — They are first-class AADL elements. Always include property examples in your answer.
> 5. **AADL is an architecture language, not a programming language** — AADL is *modeled*, then *analyzed*, and finally *code-generated*. Do not write executable AADL statements like a program.
> 6. **For 14-mark questions, include diagrams** — AADL block diagrams, annex taxonomy, or pipeline flowcharts earn easy 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Introduction to AADL**

- **AADL = Architecture Analysis and Design Language**.
- **Standard**: SAE **AS5506** (AS5506/1, /2, /3) — remember the **SAE** body.
- **Originated from**: MetaH (Honeywell) and influenced by UML.
- **Primary domain**: **Embedded real-time safety-critical systems** (avionics, aerospace, automotive, medical).
- **Three component categories**:
  - **Software** — process, thread, thread group, subprogram, data.
  - **Execution Platform (Hardware)** — processor, memory, bus, device.
  - **System (Composite)** — system, abstract.
- **Connection types**: port, parameter, subprogram access, bus access, data access.
- **Properties** specify non-functional attributes: `Period`, `Deadline`, `Compute_Execution_Time`, `Memory_Size`, `Scheduling_Protocol`, `Dispatch_Protocol`.
- **Type vs. Implementation**: `<Component>.<Impl>` separates interface (type) from internal structure (implementation).
- **AADL Annexes** (must know at least three):
  - **Behavior Annex** (AS5506/2) — state machines.
  - **Error Model Annex** (AS5506/4) — fault modeling.
  - **ARINC 653 Annex** (AS5506/5) — avionics partitioning.
  - **Data Modeling Annex** (AS5506/7) — data types.
  - **Synchronous Annex** (AS5506/6) — synchronous reactive.
- **Major Tools**:
  - **OSATE** — Open source Eclipse-based editor.
  - **OCARINA** — Code generator.
  - **Cheddar** — Schedulability analysis.
  - **AADL Inspector** — Commercial (Ellidiss).
- **Why AADL over UML**: Precise timing semantics, hardware binding, architectural analysis, safety-criticality focus.
- **Schedulability formula (RMS)**: $U = \sum C_i / T_i \le n(\sqrt[n]{2} - 1)$.
- **Hierarchical composition**: System → Process → Thread → Subprogram.
- **AADL is not a programming language** — it is an **architectural modeling language** that supports *analysis* and *code generation*.
- **Incremental development** is a core AADL principle: abstract → concrete → refined.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Comparison of AADL with Other Modeling Languages - Comparison with UML
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Basic Comparison of AADL with Other Modeling Languages - Comparison with UML # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is AADL? **AADL (Architecture Analysis and Design Language)** is a standardized, model-based engineering language standardized by **SAE International** as **AS5506**. It is specifically designed to model the **software and hardware architecture of embedded real-time, safety-critical, and high-integrity systems** (e.g., avionics, automotive, medical devices, spacecraft). AADL provides: - A precise **component model** (process, thread, data, processor, memory, bus, device, system). - **Flow specifications** (data, control, fault, information). - **Properties** for non-functional analysis (timing, memory, power, reliability). - A **formal execution semantics** for verification. > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Alignment):** AADL is a *textual and graphical* modeling language focused on **architecture-level** concerns. Unlike general-purpose languages, AADL is built to support **quantitative analysis** of performance, schedulability, reliability, and security from a single architectural model. ## 1.2 What is UML? **UML (Unified Modeling Language)** is a general-purpose, semi-formal visual modeling language standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. It is widely used in **object-oriented software engineering** to specify, visualize, construct, and document the artifacts of a software-intensive system. UML provides **14 standard diagram types**, divided into: - **Structure diagrams** (Class, Component, Deployment, Composite Structure, etc.) - **Behavior diagrams** (Activity, Sequence, State Machine, Use Case, etc.) - **Interaction diagrams** (Communication, Timing, Interaction Overview) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** UML is a *general-purpose* modeling framework, whereas AADL is a *domain-specific* architecture and analysis language for embedded and real-time systems. ## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint vs. The Universal Sketchpad" | Concept | AADL | UML | |---|---|---| | Analogy | A **specialized architect's blueprint** for a hospital OT (Operation Theatre) with pre-wired sockets, gas lines, sterile zones, and surgical lighting specs. | A **universal sketchpad** that any artist, engineer, or designer can use to draw anything — from a kitchen layout to a car engine. | **Why this analogy works:** - Just as an architect's blueprint for a hospital OT is **legally binding, dimensionally accurate, and analyzable** (you can check oxygen flow, electrical load, sterile circulation paths), **AADL models are analyzable** (you can check schedulability, memory usage, fault propagation). - Just as a universal sketchpad is **flexible and expressive** but lacks the precision needed for safety-critical construction, **UML is expressive but requires extensions** (like MARTE, SysML) to do the kind of analysis AADL does natively. > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway:** If your system must **fly, brake, pump, or pace a heart** → use AADL. If your system is **business software, web apps, or general enterprise IT** → UML is sufficient. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix ## 2.1 Why Do We Need a Comparison? — The Engineering "Why" Modern safety-critical systems (aircraft, autonomous vehicles, pacemakers) cannot rely solely on traditional UML class/sequence diagrams because: 1. **UML lacks a direct notion of hardware components** (processor, bus, memory, sensor) that the software binds to. 2. **UML has weak support for timing and resource analysis** — sequence diagrams give an *order*, not a *time bound*. 3. **UML execution semantics are not formally defined** — different tools may interpret the same diagram differently. AADL was invented by **SAE in 2004** (after UML had been widely adopted but was found insufficient for avionics software) to bridge this exact gap. ## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Comparison Axis ### 2.2.1 Standardization Body and Domain - **AADL** → **SAE AS5506** → Designed for **embedded real-time, safety-critical, distributed, hybrid (HW+SW) systems**. - **UML** → **OMG** (Object Management Group) → Designed for **general software-intensive systems**, with extensions (profiles) for real-time and embedded. ### 2.2.2 Semantics and Formality - **AADL** → Has a **formal execution semantics** (a tool like OSATE can mathematically analyze a model). The semantics are defined in terms of **state-transition logic** for each component category. - **UML** → Has **semi-formal** semantics. The OMG specification is precise but the *meaning* of an interaction or activity is interpretable — making formal verification harder. ### 2.2.3 Component Model - **AADL** provides a **rich, fixed component taxonomy**: `system`, `process`, `thread`, `thread group`, `data`, `subprogram`, `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`, `abstract`, `feature group`. - **UML** provides **only three basic classifiers**: `Class`, `Component`, and `Artifact`. Hardware must be represented as a stereotyped class, which loses semantic precision. ### 2.2.4 Non-Functional / Quality Attribute Modeling - **AADL** has **built-in properties** (e.g., `Timing_Properties::Period`, `Memory_Properties::Source_Code_Size`, `Reliability_Properties::MTTF`). Analysis tools plug directly into these. - **UML** requires **MARTE (Modeling and Analysis of Real-Time Embedded systems)** or **SysML** profiles to express the same. The semantics are not standardized across tools. ### 2.2.5 Extensibility Mechanism - **AADL** → Uses **Annexes** (AADL annex sublanguage, e.g., Behavior Annex for state machines, Error Model Annex for fault propagation). - **UML** → Uses **Profiles, Stereotypes, and Tagged Values** (e.g., `<>`, `<>`). ### 2.2.6 Verification and Analysis Support - **AADL** has first-class tools: **OSATE, Cheddar, AADL Inspector, FLAVOR, ADePT** for schedulability, latency, memory, fault tree analysis. - **UML** relies on model transformations, e.g., from UML/MARTE to analysis tools (mostly academic). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Comparison Table | # | Comparison Axis | **AADL** (AS5506) | **UML** (OMG 2.5.1) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Standardization Body** | SAE International | OMG | | 2 | **Primary Domain** | Embedded, real-time, safety-critical | General software engineering | | 3 | **Hardware Awareness** | First-class (processor, memory, bus, device) | Only via stereotypes/profiles | | 4 | **Software Components** | Process, Thread, Thread Group, Subprogram, Data | Class, Component, Artifact | | 5 | **Semantics** | Formal execution semantics | Semi-formal | | 6 | **Non-Functional Properties** | Built-in (timing, memory, power, reliability) | Requires MARTE / SysML profiles | | 7 | **Extensibility** | Annexes (Behavior, Error Model, ARINC653) | Profiles, Stereotypes, Tagged Values | | 8 | **Flow Modeling** | Native (Data, Control, Information, Fault flows) | Not native; must be modeled in activity/sequence | | 9 | **Analysis Tools** | OSATE, AADL Inspector, Cheddar, FLAVOR | Papyrus, Rhapsody, MagicDraw (mostly design only) | | 10 | **Industry Use** | Avionics (DO-178C), Automotive (ISO 26262), Space (ECSS) | Banking, ERP, Web, Telecom, Embedded (less) | | 11 | **Notation Style** | Graphical + Textual (AADL-text) | Primarily graphical | | 12 | **Schedule Analysis** | Native (via period, deadline, compute execution time) | Not native; MARTE gives it partially | | 13 | **Fault / Reliability Analysis** | Error Model Annex (state machines + probabilities) | Not native; requires external tools | | 14 | **Deployment Modeling** | Direct binding of software to processor | Deployment diagram (less precise) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule for KTU Exam:** "AADL is a *domain-specific* language for *embedded architecture and analysis*; UML is a *general-purpose* language for *software design*." Memorize this one-liner. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Comparison (Detailed Walkthrough) ## 3.1 The Three Pillars of Comparison To make this analysis rigorous, we group the differences into **three analytical pillars**: ### Pillar 1 — Origin, Purpose, and Standardization **Step 1 — Trace the origin.** UML was created in **1996** by **Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, and Ivar Jacobson** (the "Three Amigos") under OMG. It unified the Booch, OMT, and OOSE methods. AADL was created in **2004** by the **SAE Avionics Systems Division (AS-2C)** after UML failed to provide the precision needed for **DO-178B/C** avionics certification. **Step 2 — Identify the driving need.** UML's driving need was *unified notation for OO software*. AADL's driving need was *analyzable architecture models for safety-critical embedded systems*. **Step 3 — Compare scope of standardization.** - UML is the most widely used modeling standard in the world — endorsed by ISO (ISO/IEC 19505). - AADL is a smaller but deeply specialized standard (AS5506B, AS5506/2) with strong adoption in defense, aerospace, and automotive. --- ### Pillar 2 — Expressiveness of Component Models **Step 1 — Count component categories in AADL.** AADL has **15 component categories** split into three families: $$ \text{AADL Categories} = \underbrace{\{ \text{system, process, thread, thread group, data, subprogram, abstract} \}}_{\text{Software/Application}} \cup \underbrace{\{ \text{processor, memory, bus, device} \}}_{\text{Hardware/Execution Platform}} \cup \{ \text{feature group}, \text{port group}, \text{parameter}, \text{subprogram call} \} $$ **Step 2 — Compare to UML.** UML has **2 primary component types** (Class, Component). Hardware has no native representation. One must write: ``` <> FlightComputer <> ARINC_659 ``` This relies on the **stereotype** mechanism, which is essentially a label, not a semantic guarantee. **Step 3 — Conclude.** AADL gives **analyzable, tool-checked component types**; UML gives **flexible but untyped stereotypes**. --- ### Pillar 3 — Analytic Capability **Step 1 — List AADL's first-class analyses.** Using AADL's property-driven model, the following analyses are native: $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{AADL Native Analyses} = \{ &\text{Schedulability analysis (Rate Monotonic, EDF)}, \\ &\text{End-to-end latency analysis}, \\ &\text{Memory footprint analysis}, \\ &\text{Fault tree / FMEA analysis (via Error Annex)}, \\ &\text{Security analysis (via Security Annex)} \} \end{aligned} } $$ **Step 2 — List what UML natively offers.** UML by itself offers **none of these analyses**. It requires the **MARTE profile** (for time/scheduling) or **SysML** (for systems engineering) or **SafeRe** (for safety). Even then, no formal execution semantics is given. **Step 3 — Verdict.** AADL is **analysis-centric by design**; UML is **documentation-centric by design**. --- ## 3.2 Side-by-Side Example Walkthrough ### Scenario: Model a Flight Control System **Using AADL (analyzable):** ``` system FlightControlSystem features pilot_input: in data port PilotCommand; actuator_cmd: out data port ActuatorSignal; end FlightControlSystem; process ControlLoop features pilot: in data port PilotCommand; out_cmd: out data port ActuatorSignal; properties Timing_Properties::Period => 20 ms; Timing_Properties::Deadline => 15 ms; Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic; end ControlLoop; processor FlightComputer properties Processor_Properties::Clock_Period => 1 ms; Memory_Properties::RAM_Size => 2_048_KB; end FlightComputer; system implementation FlightControlSystem.impl subcomponents sw: process ControlLoop; hw: processor FlightComputer; connections c1: port pilot_input -> sw.pilot; c2: port sw.out_cmd -> actuator_cmd; properties Actual_Processor_Binding => reference (hw) applies to sw; end FlightControlSystem.impl; ``` This model is **directly analyzable**: a tool can check that the 20 ms period, 15 ms deadline, and 1 ms clock satisfy the schedulability equation: $$ R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_j}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j \quad \leq \quad D_i $$ where $R_i$ is the worst-case response time, $C_i$ is the compute time, $T_j$ is the period of higher-priority task $j$, and $D_i$ is the deadline. **Using UML (descriptive, not directly analyzable):** - A **class diagram** would model `ControlLoop` as a class. - A **sequence diagram** would show pilot → loop → actuator (no timing bounds). - A **deployment diagram** would show `ControlLoop <>` deployed on `FlightComputer <>` (stereotypes, not types). - To get the schedulability check, you must **export** to a third-party tool via MARTE. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** AADL gives one **complete, analyzable** model. UML gives **multiple coordinated views** that must be hand-correlated and rarely provide quantitative guarantees. --- ## 3.3 When to Use Each — Engineering Decision Matrix | System Characteristic | Recommended Language | |---|---| | Avionics DO-178C software | **AADL** | | AUTOSAR automotive | **AADL** (with AUTOSAR annex) | | Pacemaker / Medical device | **AADL** | | Banking application | **UML** | | Web application | **UML** | | Enterprise ERP | **UML** | | Distributed cloud microservices | **UML** (with C4 / ArchiMate) | | CubeSat / Spacecraft | **AADL** | | Smartphone app | **UML** | | Industrial robot | **AADL** (with MARTE bridge) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — AADL vs UML Architecture Below is a Mermaid diagram showing the **architectural scope** of AADL versus UML and where they overlap (via MARTE/SysML). ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SYS["System Engineering Domain"] AADL["AADL Core
AS5506"] AADL_ANN["AADL Annexes
Behavior, Error, ARINC653"] UML["UML Core
OMG 2.5.1"] MARTE["UML MARTE Profile
Real-Time Extension"] SYSML["SysML
Systems Extension"] end subgraph SW["Software Components"] THREAD["Thread / Process"] CLASS["Class / Component"] end subgraph HW["Hardware Components"] PROC["Processor / Bus / Memory / Device"] STEREO["Stereotyped Class
processor artifact"] end subgraph ANAL["Analysis Capability"] SCHED["Schedulability & Latency"] FAULT["Fault Tree Analysis"] DOC["Documentation Only"] end AADL --> THREAD AADL --> PROC AADL --> SCHED AADL_ANN --> FAULT AADL --> AADL_ANN UML --> CLASS UML --> STEREO UML --> DOC MARTE --> SCHED SYSML --> FAULT UML --> MARTE UML --> SYSML classDef aadlBox fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef umlBox fill:#9333ea,stroke:#9333ea,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef commonBox fill:#059669,stroke:#059669,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px class AADL,AADL_ANN aadlBox class UML,MARTE,SYSML umlBox class THREAD,PROC,CLASS,STEREO,SCHED,FAULT,DOC commonBox ``` ## 4.2 Layered View — Coverage Overlap Between AADL and UML ```mermaid graph LR L0["Application / Mission Logic"] L1["Software Architecture"] L2["Execution Platform / Hardware"] L3["Non-Functional Properties
Timing, Memory, Power"] L4["Analysis & Verification"] A1["AADL
Native"] -.covers.-> L0 A1 -.covers.-> L1 A1 -.covers.-> L2 A1 -.covers.-> L3 A1 -.covers.-> L4 U1["UML
Native"] -.covers.-> L0 U1 -.covers.-> L1 U2["UML + MARTE/SysML"] -.covers.-> L2 U2 -.covers.-> L3 U2 -.partial.-> L4 classDef aadlStyle fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px classDef umlStyle fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px class A1 aadlStyle class U1,U2 umlStyle ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — "Which Language Should I Use?" ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is the system safety-critical
or real-time embedded?"} Q2{"Do you need schedulability,
fault, or latency analysis?"} Q3{"Is it a hardware/software
co-design problem?"} R1["Use AADL"] R2["Use UML"] R3["Use UML + MARTE"] R4["Use SysML"] R5["Use AADL + Annexes"] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2 Q1 -- No --> Q3 Q2 -- Yes --> R5 Q2 -- No --> R1 Q3 -- Yes --> R4 Q3 -- No --> R2 classDef yesBox fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#064e3b,stroke-width:2px classDef noBox fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px classDef outBox fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px class Q1,Q2,Q3 yesBox class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 outBox ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between AADL and UML with respect to their standardization bodies and primary application domains. (3 marks)** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | AADL | UML | |---|---|---| | Standardization Body | SAE International (AS5506) | OMG (Object Management Group) | | Primary Application Domain | Embedded real-time, safety-critical, and high-integrity systems (e.g., avionics, automotive, medical) | General-purpose software engineering (e.g., banking, web, ERP, enterprise) | | Hardware Support | First-class (processor, bus, memory, device) | Not native; requires stereotypes | > **[Valuation Tip]** A 3-mark answer must contain **three valid points** of comparison. A table is the cleanest, highest-scoring format. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Why is AADL preferred over UML for modeling safety-critical embedded systems? (3 marks)** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. AADL has **formal execution semantics**, enabling mathematical verification — UML's semantics are semi-formal. 2. AADL supports **native non-functional property modeling** (timing, memory, power) suitable for certification (DO-178C, ISO 26262). 3. AADL provides **built-in analysis support** for schedulability, end-to-end latency, and fault propagation through its annexes — UML requires external profiles like MARTE. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Sample Paper]** #### **(a) Compare AADL and UML along any SEVEN dimensions. (7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **(i) Standardization Body** *(1 mark)* - AADL → SAE AS5506; UML → OMG. **(ii) Primary Domain** *(1 mark)* - AADL → embedded, real-time, safety-critical; UML → general software engineering. **(iii) Semantics** *(1 mark)* - AADL → formal execution semantics; UML → semi-formal. **(iv) Component Model** *(1 mark)* - AADL → 15 typed component categories (process, thread, processor, bus, memory, device, etc.); UML → Class, Component, Artifact (hardware is a stereotype). **(v) Non-Functional Properties** *(1 mark)* - AADL → built-in property sets (Timing, Memory, Reliability, Power); UML → requires MARTE/SysML profiles. **(vi) Extensibility** *(1 mark)* - AADL → Annexes (Behavior, Error, ARINC653); UML → Profiles, Stereotypes, Tagged Values. **(vii) Analysis Capability** *(1 mark)* - AADL → native schedulability, latency, fault analysis; UML → requires model transformations to external tools. > **[Valuation Tip]** Examiners expect **a table OR a list of 7+ differences**. Do not write a paragraph — tabular format scores full marks. --- #### **(b) With a suitable scenario, explain how AADL models a flight control system and what analyses become possible that UML does not natively support. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the scenario (1 mark).** Consider an avionics flight control system with a periodic control loop running every **20 ms** on a dedicated flight computer. The system reads pilot commands and produces actuator outputs. **Step 2 — Show the AADL model structure (2 marks).** The model contains: - A `system` FlightControlSystem. - A `process` ControlLoop with `Period => 20 ms` and `Deadline => 15 ms`. - A `processor` FlightComputer with `Clock_Period => 1 ms` and `RAM_Size => 2_048 KB`. - A `bus` ARINC_659 connecting the processor to a sensor `device`. - Binding property: `Actual_Processor_Binding => reference (hw) applies to sw`. **Step 3 — List the analyses AADL enables (3 marks).** 1. **Schedulability Analysis** — Using the **Rate Monotonic / Response Time Analysis** equation, OSATE checks if the deadline is met: $$ \boxed{R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j \leq D_i} $$ 2. **End-to-End Latency** — AADL's *flow specifications* allow a tool to compute the worst-case time from `pilot_input` port to `actuator_cmd` port. 3. **Memory Footprint** — The `Source_Code_Size`, `Data_Size`, and `RAM_Size` properties are summed to verify the binary fits on the target. 4. **Fault Tree Analysis** — The **Error Model Annex** attaches probabilistic error states to components and computes top-event probability for the system. **Step 4 — State UML's limitation (1 mark).** UML has no native concept of `Period`, `Deadline`, or `Clock_Period`. To achieve the same, a developer must use MARTE and a third-party model transformation tool, with no guarantee of standardized semantics. --- ### **Question B — Alternative Choice (KTU Internal Choice Pattern) #### **(a) Explain the component model of AADL. How does it differ from UML's component modeling? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — AADL's Component Model (3 marks).** AADL provides three **component categories** with 15 sub-categories: $$ \text{AADL Components} = \underbrace{ \{\text{system, process, thread, thread group, data, subprogram, abstract}\} }_{\text{Application/Software}} \cup \underbrace{ \{\text{processor, memory, bus, device}\} }_{\text{Execution Platform}} $$ Each component has: - A **type** (interface signature, features, properties). - An **implementation** (internal subcomponents, connections, modes, flows). - **Features** for interaction: ports (data, event, event data), parameters, subprogram calls, feature groups. - **Properties** for non-functional analysis. **Step 2 — UML's Component Model (2 marks).** UML provides: - A `Component` (a replaceable software part). - A `Class` (the universal classifier). - An `Artifact` (a physical file). - Hardware is represented as a **stereotyped class**, e.g., `<>`, `<>`. Stereotypes are essentially labels, not semantic types. **Step 3 — Key Differences (2 marks).** | Aspect | AADL | UML | |---|---|---| | Component Categories | 15 typed, semantically precise | 3 base types, hardware via stereotypes | | Interface Specification | Ports, feature groups, parameters | Ports, interfaces (lollipop notation) | | Hardware Modeling | Native (processor, bus, memory) | Not native | | Modality | Modes of operation natively | Not in base UML | --- #### **(b) Discuss the role of AADL annexes and UML profiles in extending the languages. Compare their power and limitations. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — AADL Annexes (3 marks).** AADL annexes are **sublanguages** defined in separate normative documents. Examples: - **Behavior Annex** — embeds state machines and transition logic inside AADL components. - **Error Model Annex** — adds probabilistic error states and transitions for fault tree analysis. - **ARINC653 Annex** — maps AADL to the ARINC 653 avionics standard. - **Security Annex** — supports security-level classification. Annexes are **first-class citizens** of AADL — the AADL toolset parses them as part of the model. **Step 2 — UML Profiles (2 marks).** UML's extensibility uses the **profile mechanism**: - **Stereotypes** (e.g., `<>`). - **Tagged values** (e.g., `{period = 20ms}`). - **Constraints** (OCL). Profiles are tool-specific, though standards like **MARTE, SysML, SafeRe** exist. **Step 3 — Comparative Verdict (2 marks).** | Aspect | AADL Annex | UML Profile | |---|---|---| | Standardization | Per annex, formal | Per profile, less formal | | Tool Support | Tight (OSATE) | Variable (depends on tool vendor) | | Semantics | Semantically integrated | Often not fully integrated | | Portability | High | Low (stereotypes may not transfer) | > **[Valuation Tip]** This question tests whether you understand that *both languages are extensible, but AADL annexes are semantically richer* — a common KTU-expected comparison. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Writing "AADL is just like UML"** — WRONG. AADL is **domain-specific**; UML is **general-purpose**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion. > 2. **Forgetting to mention the standardization body** (SAE vs OMG). This is a **compulsory point** worth 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing AADL annexes with UML profiles.** Annexes are *integrated sublanguages*; profiles are *metamodel extensions*. The distinction is worth 2 marks. > 4. **Writing only 3–4 differences when 7 are asked.** Always over-prepare — list at least **one more** difference than asked. > 5. **Failing to give a real-world example** (e.g., flight control, automotive braking). Generic answers score low. > 6. **Mixing up "MARTE" with "AADL."** MARTE is a UML *profile*, not part of AADL. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **AADL = SAE AS5506**, **UML = OMG** — memorize the standardization bodies. - **AADL = embedded + real-time + safety-critical.** **UML = general software.** - AADL has **formal execution semantics**; UML is **semi-formal.** - AADL has **15 component categories** (software + hardware); UML has **3** (Class, Component, Artifact). - AADL has **built-in properties** for timing, memory, reliability, power; UML needs **MARTE/SysML** profiles. - AADL extends via **annexes** (Behavior, Error, ARINC653, Security); UML extends via **profiles, stereotypes, tagged values.** - AADL supports **native analysis** (schedulability, latency, fault tree); UML does not. - AADL is widely used in **avionics, automotive, space, medical**; UML in **banking, web, ERP, enterprise IT.** - AADL is **analysis-centric by design**; UML is **documentation-centric by design.** - Hybrid approach: AADL with **Behavior Annex** ≈ UML with **State Machine**; both are valid for state-based logic. - The **schedulability equation** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j \leq D_i$ is the **most-tested formula** in AADL-related KTU questions. - Tools: **OSATE, AADL Inspector, Cheddar, FLAVOR** (AADL) vs **Papyrus, Rhapsody, MagicDraw, StarUML** (UML). - Key phrase for 7-mark answers: *"AADL provides a typed, semantically formal, hardware-aware component model with built-in non-functional properties, while UML provides a flexible but untyped general-purpose notation requiring profiles for analysis."*

topics.title: Developing a Simple Model - Define the Components - Explain with Example (Powerboat Autopilot System)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Developing a Simple Model - Define the Components - Explain with Example (Powerboat Autopilot System)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)
