In **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, a *model* is a **formal, abstract, and executable representation** of a system that captures its structure, behaviour, and communication patterns using a well-defined modelling language (e.g., **SysML, UML, Simulink/Stateflow, or SCADE**). When we speak of "**Developing a Simple Model**", we refer to the disciplined engineering activity of *decomposing* a real-world control problem into a small set of interconnected modelling primitives — namely, **entities (blocks), interfaces (ports), behaviours (state machines / equations), and data flows (signals)** — and assembling them so that the resulting model is *complete*, *consistent*, and *simulatable* before any hand-written code is produced.

The fundamental **components of a model** in MBD are:

1. **Blocks (or Classes/Objects)** — the basic structural units that encapsulate data and behaviour.
2. **Ports & Interfaces** — typed communication channels through which blocks exchange signals (discrete events, continuous values, or messages).
3. **Connectors / Flow Lines** — wiring elements that bind outputs of one block to inputs of another.
4. **State Machines** — finite-state behavioural descriptions (states, transitions, guards, actions).
5. **Equations / Continuous Blocks** — mathematical relationships defining the dynamic evolution of variables.
6. **Parameters & Constants** — tunable design values that calibrate the system.
7. **Environment / Boundary** — sensors (inputs from the physical world) and actuators (outputs to the physical world).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The examiner expects students to be able to *identify* and *justify* the choice of each component. Memorising names is insufficient — you must explain the **role** of every block in the system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cruise Control of a Boat"

Imagine you are steering a powerboat on a lake. Instead of holding the wheel, you set a compass heading (say, **North, 90°**), press the *Engage* button, and the boat steers itself. The autopilot is essentially a **closed-loop digital brain** built from a handful of plug-and-play parts. 

Think of it like a **kitchen blender with labelled jars**:
- The **jars** = blocks (each holds one ingredient, e.g., compass, error-calculator, rudder-controller).
- The **lids with specific shapes** = ports (only compatible jars can be joined).
- The **pipes between jars** = connectors (carry the data/signal).
- The **recipe card** = state machine (says *what to do next* based on conditions).
- The **dial and timer** = parameters (allow calibration without rebuilding the blender).
- The **wall socket and the spout** = environment (power input & fluid output).

Each "jar" is a self-contained component you can replace, test, and reuse — that is the essence of **model-based thinking**.

### 1.3 Physical / Engineering Constants for Marine Autopilots

| Constant / Metric | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Compass update rate | **10 Hz** | Heading sample frequency |
| Rudder slew rate | **\leq 5°/s** | Maximum rudder movement speed |
| Heading dead-band | **\pm 2°** | No correction zone around setpoint |
| Proportional gain (K_p) | **0.8 – 1.2** | Reduces steady-state heading error |
| Derivative gain (K_d) | **0.1 – 0.4** | Damps oscillation / overshoot |
| Maximum rudder angle | **\pm 35°** | Physical actuator limit |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logic — The "Define-the-Components" Methodology

When an MBD engineer is asked to **"develop a simple model"**, the canonical 6-step logic is:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Purpose (The "Why")**
  Ask: *What problem are we solving?* For the powerboat autopilot → maintain a user-set compass heading automatically.

- **Step 2 — Identify the Boundary (The "Where does it end?")**
  Separate the controller from the *plant*. The plant is the boat (hull, propeller, rudder); the controller is the autopilot brain.

- **Step 3 — Enumerate the Inputs (Sensors)**
  - Compass heading $\theta_{actual}$
  - Set heading $\theta_{set}$
  - Boat speed $v$ (used to scale rudder authority)
  - Mode switch (MANUAL / STANDBY / AUTO)

- **Step 4 — Enumerate the Outputs (Actuators)**
  - Rudder command $\delta_{cmd}$

- **Step 5 — Decide the Behaviour (Equations + State Machine)**
  The model is a hybrid: **continuous** for the heading error equation, **discrete** for the operating mode.

- **Step 6 — Compose the Model (Wiring)**
  Connect blocks: Sensor → Error Calculator → PID / Bang-Bang Controller → Rudder Actuator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters:** KTU examiners reward answers that show *systematic decomposition*. Randomly listing components without a boundary first is the most common cause of losing 2–3 marks.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. Memorise the **heading-error law** and the **PD control law** — they appear in nearly every Part B question on this module.

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heading error | $e(t) = \theta_{set} - \theta_{actual}(t)$ | Deviation from desired course | Input to controller |
| 2 | Rate of error | $\dot{e}(t) = \dfrac{d\,e(t)}{dt}$ | How fast error is changing | Predicts future error |
| 3 | PD Control law | $\delta(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_d\,\dot{e}(t)$ | Rudder deflection command | Most common autopilot control law |
| 4 | Bang-Bang control | $\delta(t) = \begin{cases} +\delta_{max}, & e(t) > \theta_{db} \\ 0, & \vert e(t) \vert \leq \theta_{db} \\ -\delta_{max}, & e(t) < -\theta_{db} \end{cases}$ | On–off rudder | Simple, cheap autopilots |
| 5 | Angular wrap | $e(t) = \operatorname{atan2}\!\big(\sin(\theta_{set}-\theta_{actual}),\,\cos(\theta_{set}-\theta_{actual})\big)$ | Keeps error in $(-\pi,\pi]$ | Avoids 359° → 1° jumps |
| 6 | Rudder rate limit | $\dot{\delta}(t) = \begin{cases} \dot{\delta}_{max}, & \dot{\delta}_{cmd} > \dot{\delta}_{max} \\ \dot{\delta}_{cmd}, & \vert \dot{\delta}_{cmd} \vert \leq \dot{\delta}_{max} \\ -\dot{\delta}_{max}, & \dot{\delta}_{cmd} < -\dot{\delta}_{max} \end{cases}$ | Slew limiter | Protects mechanical rudder |
| 7 | Yaw dynamics (linearised) | $T\,\ddot{\psi} + \dot{\psi} = K\,\delta$ | First-order Nomoto model | Plant model of boat |
| 8 | Settling time estimate | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\,\omega_n}$ | 2% band criterion | Tuning metric |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The powerboat autopilot is not an academic toy — it is a **canonical case study** in:

- **Marine Electronics** (Raymarine, Garmin autopilots — all MBD-developed).
- **Autonomous Surface Vehicles (ASVs)** used in oceanographic survey and naval mine-countermeasures.
- **Aerospace autopilot precursors** — the same PD structure underpins attitude-hold in quadcopters and small UAVs.
- **Automotive lane-keeping systems** — same block topology, different plant.

Studying this small example teaches the **scaling principle**: a model with 7 components can become a model with 7,000 components using the *same composition rules*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Heading-Error PD Law

We begin with the **physical objective**: drive the heading error to zero. Deriving the control law step-by-step (as the KTU valuation key expects).

**Step 1 — Define the error signal.**

The user sets a desired heading $\theta_{set}$ and the compass returns the actual heading $\theta_{actual}(t)$. The instantaneous heading error is the angular difference.

$$
e(t) \;=\; \theta_{set} \;-\; \theta_{actual}(t)
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is a simple subtraction; the unit is **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)**. Always keep the unit consistent — the controller gains depend on it.

**Step 2 — Avoid the wrap-around problem.**

If $\theta_{set} = 5°$ and $\theta_{actual} = 355°$, the raw error is $-350°$, which is wrong (the boat is only $10°$ off). We normalise using the $\operatorname{atan2}$ function:

$$
e(t) \;=\; \operatorname{atan2}\!\Big(\sin\!\big(\theta_{set}-\theta_{actual}(t)\big),\;\cos\!\big(\theta_{set}-\theta_{actual}(t)\big)\Big)
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$ returns the principal angle in $(-\pi, \pi]$, automatically wrapping the difference. This is the standard engineering fix for circular quantities.

**Step 3 — Apply the dead-band.**

If the boat is within a small tolerance, we do not want the rudder to constantly twitch. We apply a dead-band of $\pm \theta_{db}$:

$$
e_{db}(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} e(t) - \theta_{db}, & e(t) > \theta_{db} \\ 0, & \vert e(t) \vert \leq \theta_{db} \\ e(t) + \theta_{db}, & e(t) < -\theta_{db} \end{cases}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Subtracting $\theta_{db}$ after the comparison preserves continuity at the boundary (avoids a jump).

**Step 4 — Compute the derivative of error.**

We approximate the derivative using a finite difference over sample period $T_s$:

$$
\dot{e}[k] \;=\; \dfrac{e[k] \;-\; e[k-1]}{T_s}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $T_s$ is the sampling period (e.g., $T_s = 0.1$ s for **10 Hz**). The discrete form is what actually runs on the embedded controller.

**Step 5 — Combine proportional and derivative terms (PD law).**

$$
\delta_{cmd}(t) \;=\; K_p\,e_{db}(t) \;+\; K_d\,\dot{e}(t)
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The proportional term $K_p e$ pulls the boat toward the heading; the derivative term $K_d \dot{e}$ damps overshoot. Together they form a stable, well-tuned autopilot.

**Step 6 — Apply rudder limits and slew rate.**

Physical rudders cannot move instantly and cannot exceed mechanical limits. We *saturate* the command:

$$
\delta_{cmd}^{clipped}(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} +\delta_{max}, & \delta_{cmd}(t) > \delta_{max} \\ \delta_{cmd}(t), & \vert \delta_{cmd}(t) \vert \leq \delta_{max} \\ -\delta_{max}, & \delta_{cmd}(t) < -\delta_{max} \end{cases}
$$

Then slew-rate limit:

$$
\dot{\delta}(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} +\dot{\delta}_{max}, & \delta_{cmd}^{clipped}(t) - \delta(t) > \dot{\delta}_{max}\,T_s \\ \delta_{cmd}^{clipped}(t) - \delta(t), & \text{otherwise (within limit)} \end{cases}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The slew rate limit $\dot{\delta}_{max}$ is typically **5°/s** in real autopilots to prevent hydraulic shock.

**Step 7 — Update the rudder state.**

$$
\delta(t + T_s) \;=\; \delta(t) \;+\; \dot{\delta}(t)\cdot T_s
$$

**Step 8 — Plant update (Nomoto model, first-order approximation).**

The boat's yaw rate responds to the rudder:

$$
T\,\ddot{\psi}(t) \;+\; \dot{\psi}(t) \;=\; K\,\delta(t)
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $T$ is the yaw time constant (≈ 1–3 s for a small powerboat), $K$ is the rudder gain. The first-order Nomoto model is the *plant equation* — the autopilot engineer needs it to tune $K_p$ and $K_d$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Components of the Powerboat Autopilot Model

| # | Component Name | Type | Inputs (Ports) | Outputs (Ports) | Behaviour / Equation | Role in System |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Compass Sensor** | Continuous block | — | $\theta_{actual}$ | Samples magnetic heading | Provides feedback |
| 2 | **Set-Heading Memory** | Parameter block | User dial | $\theta_{set}$ | Holds desired value | Stores goal |
| 3 | **Mode Switch** | Discrete input | Pilot button | `mode` enum | MANUAL/STAND-BY/AUTO | Safety state selector |
| 4 | **Error Calculator** | Continuous block | $\theta_{set}$, $\theta_{actual}$ | $e(t)$ | $\operatorname{atan2}$ formulation | Compares goal vs reality |
| 5 | **Dead-Band Filter** | Continuous block | $e(t)$ | $e_{db}(t)$ | Piecewise as Step 3 | Avoids rudder chatter |
| 6 | **PD Controller** | Continuous block | $e_{db}(t)$, $\dot{e}(t)$ | $\delta_{cmd}$ | $K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ | Generates rudder command |
| 7 | **Rudder Saturator** | Continuous block | $\delta_{cmd}$ | $\delta_{cmd}^{clipped}$ | Piecewise as Step 6 | Enforces physical limit |
| 8 | **Slew-Rate Limiter** | Continuous block | $\delta_{cmd}^{clipped}$, $\delta$ | $\dot{\delta}$ | Rate-limit law | Protects hydraulics |
| 9 | **Plant (Boat)** | Continuous block | $\delta$ | $\theta_{actual}$ (to compass), $\psi, \dot{\psi}$ | Nomoto first-order | The world being controlled |
| 10 | **Operating-Mode State Machine** | Discrete block | `mode` button, $e_{db}$ | `engaged` flag | States: OFF → STBY → AUTO → ALARM | Coordinates top-level flow |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Powerboat Autopilot Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pilot: Set Heading Dial] --> B[Set Heading Memory]
    C[Compass Sensor on Boat] --> D[Compass Reading]
    D --> E[Error Calculator]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Dead Band Filter]
    F --> G[PD Controller Kp Kd]
    H[Mode Switch: Manual Standby Auto] --> I[Mode State Machine]
    I --> J[Engagement Gate]
    G --> J
    J --> K[Rudder Saturation Clamp]
    K --> L[Slew Rate Limiter]
    L --> M[Rudder Actuator]
    M --> N[Boat Plant Nomoto]
    N --> O[Rudder Angle Sensor]
    N --> C
    O --> G2[Feedback to PD Derivative]
    G2 --> G
    I --> P[Alarm Panel]
    P --> Q[Audible Buzzer]
```

> *How to read this diagram (intuition):* Follow the arrow from the **Pilot** at the top-left downward to the **Boat Plant**, then back up from the **Boat Plant** to the **Compass**, completing the *closed loop*. The **PD Controller** is the brain sitting in the middle.

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Operating Modes of the Autopilot

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> STANDBY: pilot presses POWER
    STANDBY --> AUTO: pilot presses ENGAGE
    AUTO --> STANDBY: pilot presses DISENGAGE
    AUTO --> ALARM: heading error greater than 30 deg for 10 s
    ALARM --> STANDBY: pilot presses ACK
    STANDBY --> OFF: pilot presses POWER
    OFF --> [*]
```

> *Reading aid:* **OFF** consumes no power. **STANDBY** powers the sensors but the rudder is un-driven. **AUTO** is the active control loop. **ALARM** is a fail-safe state that releases the rudder to manual and rings a buzzer if the system cannot maintain heading.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Signal Flow Summary

The model is a **single-input single-output (SISO) closed-loop control system** with one supervisory discrete layer. The signal flow is:

- **Reference path:** Pilot → $\theta_{set}$ → Error Calculator.
- **Feedback path:** Boat → Compass → $\theta_{actual}$ → Error Calculator.
- **Forward path:** Error → Dead-band → PD → Saturation → Slew limit → Rudder → Plant.
- **Supervisory path:** Mode switch → State machine → Engagement gate (which *enables* or *disables* the forward path).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a "model" in the context of Model-Based Software Development. List any FOUR essential components of a simple model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *model* in MBSD is a **formal, abstract, and executable representation** of a system that captures its structure, behaviour, and interfaces using a precise notation (e.g., SysML, Simulink, SCADE) **before code is generated**.

Four essential components:

1. **Blocks / Classes** — structural units encapsulating data and behaviour.
2. **Ports & Interfaces** — typed communication channels.
3. **Connectors** — wires that bind output ports to input ports.
4. **State Machines / Equations** — behavioural descriptions (discrete events and/or continuous dynamics).

*(Allocate ½ mark for definition, ½ mark for each of the four components.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of a "port" in a model? Differentiate between an input port and an output port with one example each from a powerboat autopilot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **port** is a typed interface on a block that defines the *kind* of data a block can send or receive. Ports enforce type-safety and enable plug-and-play composition of blocks.

- **Input port** — *receives* data from outside the block.
  *Example:* The `thetaSet` input port of the **Error Calculator** block receives the desired heading from the pilot's dial.
- **Output port** — *emits* data produced by the block to outside consumers.
  *Example:* The `rudderCmd` output port of the **PD Controller** block sends the commanded rudder angle to the **Rudder Saturator**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for input example, 1 mark for output example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a simple model** for a powerboat autopilot system. Clearly identify inputs, outputs, and the boundary between controller and plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **state machine** that governs the operating modes of the autopilot (OFF, STANDBY, AUTO, ALARM). For each state, list the **entry action**, **exit action**, and **guarding condition** for at least one transition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The powerboat autopilot is a **closed-loop SISO control system**. The boundary between the *controller* and the *plant* must be drawn explicitly.

**Controller (the model we develop):**

- **Input ports** (sensors / pilot commands):
  - `compassHeading` (from physical compass) — actual heading $\theta_{actual}$.
  - `setHeading` (from pilot dial) — desired heading $\theta_{set}$.
  - `modeSwitch` (pilot button) — discrete state `mode`.
  - `rudderAngle` (from rudder sensor) — current $\delta$.

- **Internal blocks** (the model):
  1. **Error Calculator** — produces $e(t) = \operatorname{atan2}(\sin(\theta_{set}-\theta_{actual}), \cos(\theta_{set}-\theta_{actual}))$.
  2. **Dead-Band Filter** — applies $\pm \theta_{db}$ tolerance.
  3. **PD Controller** — produces $\delta_{cmd} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$.
  4. **Rudder Saturation & Slew-Rate Limiter** — clamps to $\pm \delta_{max}$ and rate $\pm \dot{\delta}_{max}$.
  5. **Mode State Machine** — gates the engagement of the forward path.

- **Output ports** (to the plant):
  - `rudderCommand` → sent to the **rudder actuator**.

**Plant (outside the model, the physical world):**

- **Boat dynamics** — modelled by the Nomoto first-order equation $T\ddot{\psi} + \dot{\psi} = K\delta$.
- **Disturbances** — waves, wind, current (not modelled explicitly but felt as $\theta_{actual}$ noise).

The block diagram (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1) shows:

- The reference path enters from the top-left (pilot dial).
- The feedback path enters from the bottom (compass reading from the boat).
- The forward path passes through PD → Saturation → Slew → Rudder.
- A supervisory **engagement gate** (controlled by the mode state machine) sits between the PD output and the saturation block; when the mode is `MANUAL`, the gate holds $\delta_{cmd} = 0$.

**Awarding guide (7 marks):**

- 2 marks — correctly identifying inputs (sensors + pilot).
- 2 marks — correctly identifying outputs (rudder command).
- 2 marks — clearly drawing controller vs plant boundary.
- 1 mark — naming the internal blocks.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

The state machine has **four states**: `OFF`, `STANDBY`, `AUTO`, `ALARM`.

| State | Entry Action | Exit Action | Guarded Transition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OFF** | Initialise all variables to zero; beep once | Power down compass; lock rudder | `modeSwitch = POWER_PRESSED` → `STANDBY` |
| **STANDBY** | Power up compass; sample $\theta_{actual}$ at 10 Hz; illuminate green LED | Disable forward path; rudder returns to amidships | `modeSwitch = ENGAGE_PRESSED` and `speed > 3 knots` → `AUTO` |
| **AUTO** | Enable PD controller; engage rudder actuator; log start time | Disengage rudder; record end time | `modeSwitch = DISENGAGE_PRESSED` → `STANDBY` |
| **ALARM** | Sound buzzer; set $\delta_{cmd} = 0$; flash red LED | Silence buzzer; clear error log | `modeSwitch = ACK_PRESSED` → `STANDBY` |

**Key transitions and their guards:**

- `OFF → STANDBY`: guard = `modeSwitch == POWER_ON`.
- `STANDBY → AUTO`: guard = `modeSwitch == ENGAGE` **AND** `v > 3 knots` (boat must be moving for rudder to be effective).
- `AUTO → ALARM`: guard = `e(t) > 30°` for **10 seconds** continuously (autopilot is unable to correct).
- `ALARM → STANDBY`: guard = `modeSwitch == ACK` (pilot acknowledges alarm).

**Awarding guide (7 marks):**

- 1 mark — listing all four states.
- 3 marks — entry/exit actions and transitions.
- 2 marks — guards (especially the speed guard and the time-based alarm guard).
- 1 mark — neatness / correct use of notation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** omit the **speed guard** on `STANDBY → AUTO`. Many students forget that a rudder has no authority when the boat is stationary — losing 1 mark.
> - Do **NOT** forget the **time condition** in the alarm guard. `e > 30°` alone is insufficient; the *duration* of the error matters. This costs another 1 mark.
> - State diagrams must show **entry/exit actions explicitly** — writing only the state names is a 2-mark deduction.

---

**Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024]`
**(a)** List and explain the **components of a model** in Model-Based Development. For each component, state whether it is *structural* or *behavioural*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the powerboat autopilot example, derive the **PD control law** for the rudder command. Show the step-by-step derivation from the heading error to the final saturated rudder command. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

| # | Component | Structural / Behavioural | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Block** | Structural | A modular unit that encapsulates related data and behaviour. *Example:* `PDController` block. |
| 2 | **Port** | Structural | A typed interface on a block. *Example:* `thetaSet` input port. |
| 3 | **Connector** | Structural | A wiring element that joins an output port of one block to an input port of another. *Example:* wire from `Compass.out` to `ErrorCalc.in1`. |
| 4 | **Parameter** | Structural (data) | A tunable design constant. *Example:* `Kp = 1.0`, `Kd = 0.2`. |
| 5 | **State Machine** | Behavioural | Defines discrete states and transitions. *Example:* OFF → STANDBY → AUTO. |
| 6 | **Equation / Continuous Block** | Behavioural | Defines dynamic evolution of continuous variables. *Example:* Nomoto plant equation. |
| 7 | **Constraint / Invariant** | Behavioural (declarative) | A condition that must always hold. *Example:* `-35° ≤ δ ≤ +35°`. |

**Awarding guide (7 marks):**

- 1 mark for each correctly identified component (up to 6 components = 6 marks).
- 1 mark for correctly classifying each as structural vs behavioural (full credit only if all are correct).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Define error:** $e(t) = \theta_{set} - \theta_{actual}(t)$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Wrap to principal angle:** use $\operatorname{atan2}$ to keep $e \in (-\pi, \pi]$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Apply dead-band:** $e_{db}(t) = 0$ if $\vert e \vert \leq \theta_{db}$, else $e_{db} = e \mp \theta_{db}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Compute derivative:** $\dot{e}[k] = (e[k] - e[k-1]) / T_s$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — PD law:** $\delta_{cmd} = K_p e_{db} + K_d \dot{e}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Saturate:** clip to $\pm \delta_{max} = \pm 35°$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 7 — Slew-limit:** enforce $\vert \dot{\delta} \vert \leq \dot{\delta}_{max} = 5°/s$. *(1 mark)*

**Awarding guide:** 1 mark per step; full credit requires both the equation and a one-line justification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write $\delta = K_p e + K_i \int e$ (PID) — but the question asked for **PD**; including the integral term *without* justification is a 1-mark deduction.
> - Forgetting the **dead-band** step loses 1 mark; the examiner treats it as a distinct engineering decision.
> - Always state the **units** of $K_p$ and $K_d$ (e.g., deg/deg and deg·s/deg) — at least one such remark earns a bonus impression.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall).**

- **Model =** abstract, formal, executable representation of a system.
- **Components of a simple model:** *Blocks, Ports, Connectors, State Machines, Equations, Parameters, Boundary/Environment*.
- **Powerboat Autopilot =** closed-loop SISO controller with a discrete supervisory layer.
- **Plant boundary:** controller = PD + saturation + slew + state machine; plant = boat dynamics (Nomoto).
- **Heading error law:** $e(t) = \operatorname{atan2}(\sin(\theta_{set}-\theta_{actual}),\cos(\theta_{set}-\theta_{actual}))$ — *always wrap to principal angle*.
- **Dead-band** $\pm \theta_{db}$ prevents rudder chatter.
- **PD control law:** $\delta_{cmd} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$.
- **Saturation:** $\delta_{cmd}^{clipped} \in [-\delta_{max}, +\delta_{max}]$, where $\delta_{max} = 35°$.
- **Slew-rate limit:** $\vert \dot{\delta} \vert \leq 5°/s$.
- **State machine states:** OFF → STANDBY → AUTO → ALARM → STANDBY.
- **Critical guards:** `STANDBY → AUTO` requires `speed > 3 knots`; `AUTO → ALARM` requires error `> 30°` for `> 10 s`.
- **Typical parameters:** $K_p \in [0.8, 1.2]$, $K_d \in [0.1, 0.4]$, $\theta_{db} = 2°$, sample rate **10 Hz**.
- **Engineering utility:** same structure scales from marine autopilots to UAVs and lane-keeping systems.
- **Common exam pitfalls:** (i) omitting the wrap-around; (ii) forgetting the speed guard; (iii) writing PID when asked for PD; (iv) confusing *structural* vs *behavioural* components.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Develop a Top-Level Model - Use Example Powerboat Autopilot (PBA) System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Develop a Top-Level Model - Use Example Powerboat Autopilot (PBA) System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Top-Level Model** (also called a *System Context Model* or *Architectural Model*) in Model-Based Software Development (MBSD) is the highest-level abstraction of a system that captures its **external actors**, **boundary**, **primary use cases**, **operational modes**, and **external interfaces** before any detailed internal structure is defined. In the context of the **Powerboat Autopilot (PBA) System**, the top-level model represents the system-under-development (SuD) as a single black box interacting with its environment (helmsman, GPS, compass, rudder, powerboat, weather, etc.) and lists the high-level services the system must provide to satisfy the stakeholder needs.

According to **SysML / UML 2.5** and the **Harmony/INCOSE Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodology**, the top-level model comprises:
- A **System Context Diagram** (Block Definition Diagram showing the SuD and its external entities)
- A **Top-Level Use Case Diagram** (identifying actors and primary use cases)
- An **Operational Concept / Modes of Operation** specification
- **External Interface Definitions**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Powerboat Autopilot (PBA) system is a *canonical pedagogical example* used globally in MBSE textbooks (notably Bruce Powel Douglass, *Doing Hard Time*, and Holt & Perry, *SysML for Systems Engineering*) to teach the systematic construction of a top-level executable model using executable UML/state machines. The KTU examiner expects you to trace the **systems engineering workflow** from requirements → context → use cases → modes → states.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a new cruise ship**:
- Before drawing the cabins, hallways, and engine rooms, you first draw a **simple sketch** showing the ship as a single rectangle, the ocean (environment) around it, the ports it visits (external actors), and the high-level activities it performs — *sail, dock, refuel, anchor*.
- That sketch is your **top-level model**. It does not yet show any internal machinery.
- Only after this "big picture" is approved do you zoom in to design subsystems.

Similarly, the **PBA top-level model** treats the autopilot as a single black box that:
- **Receives** course commands from the helmsman
- **Senses** the boat's heading (compass) and position (GPS)
- **Drives** the rudder actuator
- **Responds** to environmental disturbances (wind, waves, current)

The key insight: *you cannot design the inside of a system until you have agreed on what the outside looks like and what it must do.*

### 1.3 The Powerboat Autopilot (PBA) — System Overview

The PBA is a **closed-loop, real-time, embedded control system** that automatically steers a powerboat to maintain or change heading. It replaces (or assists) a human helmsman. Key characteristics:

- **Hard real-time** constraints (rudder corrections must occur within milliseconds).
- **Safety-critical** (failure can cause collision, grounding, or loss of life).
- **Reactive** (continuously responds to disturbances and operator commands).
- **Mode-rich** (has several distinct operational modes: Off, Standby, Engaged, Auto, Manual, Track, etc.).

### 1.4 Why Develop a Top-Level Model First?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Engineering Reasons:**
> 1. **Stakeholder Alignment** — All engineers, customers, and regulators see the *same picture* of the system's external behavior.
> 2. **Scope Management** — Explicitly defines what is *inside* the system boundary vs. *outside* (the environment).
> 3. **Traceability** — Each low-level requirement can be traced back to a top-level use case, enabling impact analysis.

### 1.5 Key Physical / Engineering Constants for the PBA

- Typical boat heading sampling rate: **10 Hz** (every **100 ms**)
- Rudder actuator response time: **< 200 ms**
- Heading accuracy target: **± 2°**
- GPS update rate: **1 Hz**
- Compass (fluxgate) update rate: **10 Hz**
- Maximum rudder deflection: **± 35°**
- Safe operating speed: **5–40 knots**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Methodology to Develop the PBA Top-Level Model

A standard MBSE workflow (per Douglass, *Doing Hard Time*) for the PBA proceeds as follows:

**Stage 1 — Identify the System-Under-Development (SuD) and Its Boundary**

Clearly mark the *single black box* representing the autopilot. Everything inside the box is to be designed; everything outside is the environment (not designed by us).

**Stage 2 — Identify External Actors**

Actors are *anything outside the system* that interacts with it. For the PBA:

- **Helmsman (Human Operator)** — sets mode, course, and gains authority
- **Compass** — provides heading feedback
- **GPS Receiver** — provides position and waypoint data
- **Rudder Actuator** — receives control signals (output)
- **Powerboat (Plant/Dynamics)** — the physical vessel being controlled
- **Environment** — wind, waves, current (disturbances)
- **Power Supply** — electrical source
- **Display / HMI** — shows mode, heading, errors
- **Off-Board System (Maintenance / Diagnostics)** — for service mode

**Stage 3 — Identify Top-Level Use Cases**

A **use case** is a discrete piece of externally-visible functionality the system provides. For the PBA the canonical top-level use cases are:

- `Engage Autopilot` — switch from manual to automatic steering
- `Disengage Autopilot` — return to manual control
- `Steer to Heading` — maintain a commanded compass heading
- `Steer to Waypoint` — steer along a path to a GPS waypoint
- `Handle Disturbance` — compensate for wind/wave/current
- `Configure System` — set gains, calibrate sensors
- **and the always-running implicit use case** `Monitor System Health`

**Stage 4 — Identify Modes of Operation**

Modes partition the system's behavior. For the PBA:

| Mode | Description | Key Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Off** | No power | System inactive |
| **Manual** | Helmsman in full control | Autopilot observes but does not actuate |
| **Standby** | Powered, ready to engage | Listens for engage command |
| **Auto (Heading Hold)** | Holds compass heading | Engages rudder control loop |
| **Nav (Track / Waypoint)** | Follows GPS track | Engages cross-track error correction |
| **Fault** | Sensor/actuator failure detected | Disengages, alarms |
| **Test / Calibration** | Maintenance mode | Accepts test inputs |

**Stage 5 — Define External Interfaces**

Each actor connection has an *interface contract* specifying data type, units, range, and timing:

- `compass → PBA` → **Heading** (0–359°, updated @ 10 Hz, ± 1° resolution)
- `GPS → PBA` → **Position, Waypoint, SOG/COG** (updated @ 1 Hz)
- `Helmsman → PBA` → **Engage/Disengage, Mode Select, Commanded Heading** (event-driven)
- `PBA → Rudder Actuator` → **Rudder Command** (–35° to +35°, PWM or analog, 50 Hz update)
- `PBA → HMI` → **Mode, Heading, Error, Alarms** (10 Hz)
- `Environment → PBA` (via plant dynamics) → **Disturbance Torque** (modeled, not measured)

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Top-Level Model Element | PBA Instance | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | System Boundary | Single block `PBA : Autopilot` | Scope control |
| 2 | Primary External Actors | Helmsman, Compass, GPS, Rudder Actuator, HMI, Power | Identify interfaces |
| 3 | Top-Level Use Cases | Steer-To-Heading, Steer-To-Waypoint, Engage, Disengage | Functional scope |
| 4 | Operational Modes | Off / Manual / Standby / Auto / Nav / Fault | Behavior partitioning |
| 5 | Interfaces | Heading, Waypoint, Rudder Cmd, Engage | Data contracts |
| 6 | Implicit Use Case | Monitor Health | Non-functional / safety |
| 7 | Use-Case Realization | Later mapped to capsules & state machines | Architectural bridge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This top-level model is the *contract* between the systems engineers and the software/hardware developers. It is what gets reviewed at System Requirements Review (SRR) and Preliminary Design Review (PDR).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Order

You develop a top-level model **before** designing internals because:

1. **The system's external behavior is the only thing the customer can observe and judge.** Internal elegance is irrelevant if external behavior is wrong.
2. **Many design errors are interface errors** — wrong units, wrong timing, wrong protocol. Catching them at the top level costs almost nothing; catching them after detailed design costs a fortune.
3. **Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA)** must begin at the top level because hazards exist at the *system-environment boundary*.

### 2.4 Connection to Executable UML and State Machines

Once the top-level model is approved, the PBA's **modes** become a **top-level state machine** (one state per mode, with guarded transitions triggered by events such as `engage()`, `headingReached()`, `sensorFault()`). The **use cases** become **operations on the top-level capsule** (e.g., `engage()`, `disengage()`, `steerToHeading(h)`). This is the bridge to detailed design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Development Walkthrough — PBA Top-Level Model

This section gives the **exhaustive, exam-ready derivation** of the PBA top-level model that the KTU examiner expects you to be able to reproduce on paper.

### 3.1 Step 1 — State the Mission Need / System Purpose

Begin with a one-sentence mission statement:

> *"The Powerboat Autopilot shall automatically steer a single-engine recreational powerboat (5–40 knots) to maintain a commanded compass heading or follow a sequence of GPS waypoints, while ensuring the human helmsman can override at any time."*

This sentence fixes the **purpose** — the *why*.

### 3.2 Step 2 — Draw the System Context

Represent the PBA as a single block in a **Block Definition Diagram (BDD)** or a SysML **IBD (Internal Block Diagram)** in context form. The block has *ports* on its boundary, each connected to exactly one external entity.

The structural arrangement is:

$$
\text{External Environment} = \{\text{Helmsman, Compass, GPS, Rudder Actuator, HMI, Power, Environment}\}
$$

$$
\text{SuD} = \{\text{PBA} \mid \text{PBA} \cap \text{External Environment} = \emptyset\}
$$

That is: the system and its environment are *disjoint* sets; they communicate *only* through the named interfaces.

### 3.3 Step 3 — Enumerate Actors (Exhaustive List)

For each actor, the development team must answer four questions:

1. **Who/what is it?** (identity)
2. **Why does it interact with the PBA?** (stake)
3. **What information does it provide or consume?** (interface)
4. **Is it human, hardware, or software?** (classification)

| Actor | Type | Stake | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Helmsman | Human | Commands & overrides | Bi-directional |
| Compass | Hardware | Heading feedback | Provides to PBA |
| GPS | Hardware | Position / waypoint | Provides to PBA |
| Rudder Actuator | Hardware | Physical control | Receives from PBA |
| HMI / Display | Hardware | Status visualization | Receives from PBA |
| Power Supply | Hardware | Energy | Provides to PBA |
| Environment (wind/waves) | Physical | Disturbance | Affects plant only |
| Maintenance Console | Human/Software | Diagnostics | Bi-directional (rare) |

### 3.4 Step 4 — Derive the Top-Level Use Cases

A use case is *derived* by asking: **"What goal does each actor want the PBA to achieve?"**

- Helmsman wants the boat to hold heading → `SteerToHeading`
- Helmsman wants the boat to reach a destination → `SteerToWaypoint`
- Helmsman wants to take control → `Disengage`
- Helmsman wants to delegate control → `Engage`
- System itself wants to remain safe → `MonitorHealth` (implicit, cross-cutting)
- Regulator/operator wants the system to fail safely → `HandleFault` (implicit)

**Resulting Use Case Set:**

$$
U_{PBA} = \{ UC_1\text{:Engage},\; UC_2\text{:Disengage},\; UC_3\text{:SteerToHeading},\; UC_4\text{:SteerToWaypoint},\; UC_5\text{:MonitorHealth},\; UC_6\text{:HandleFault} \}
$$

### 3.5 Step 5 — Derive the Modes of Operation

Each use case must be *realizable* in some mode. The mapping is:

$$
\text{Use Case} \rightarrow \text{Mode in which it is active}
$$

- `Engage` causes transition: **Standby → Auto** (or **Standby → Nav**)
- `Disengage` causes transition: **Auto/Nav → Manual**
- `SteerToHeading` is the body behavior of mode **Auto**
- `SteerToWaypoint` is the body behavior of mode **Nav**
- `MonitorHealth` runs in **all** powered modes
- `HandleFault` is triggered from any mode → transition to **Fault** mode

This gives the modes set:

$$
M_{PBA} = \{ \text{Off},\; \text{Manual},\; \text{Standby},\; \text{Auto},\; \text{Nav},\; \text{Fault},\; \text{Calibration} \}
$$

### 3.6 Step 6 — Specify the External Interfaces

For each interface port on the PBA boundary, the developer writes a small *interface control document* (ICD) entry. Standard form:

> **Port:** `rudderCmd`
> **Type:** Analog output, –10 V to +10 V
> **Range:** corresponds to –35° to +35° rudder deflection
> **Latency:** < 50 ms from compute to output
> **Update Rate:** 50 Hz

This level of detail is required even at the *top level* because interface errors are the most common source of system-level failure.

### 3.7 Step 7 — Verify the Top-Level Model

A top-level model is *complete* if and only if:

1. Every stakeholder need is satisfied by at least one use case.
2. Every use case is reachable from at least one mode.
3. Every mode is reachable from the initial mode via some transition.
4. Every interface is associated with a real actor.
5. No internal component is mentioned (that belongs to the next level of decomposition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logical Test:** The most common exam question asks *"Given this set of use cases and modes, identify the missing transition or the missing use case."* The answer is always found by applying the **reachability** and **completeness** rules above.

### 3.8 Step 8 — Bridge to Detailed Design

The top-level model is *executable* in modern MBSE tools (e.g., Cameo, Rhapsody, Enterprise Architect). At this point each use case becomes an **operation** on a single top-level capsule `PBA`, and the modes become the **states of its classifier state machine**. This is the *bridge* to Module 4 / Module 5 where detailed capsules, ports, and state machines are designed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All diagrams use the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards: purely alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords as IDs, raw uppercase alphanumeric labels (no `**` or HTML inside quotes).

### 4.1 PBA System Context Diagram (External View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[HELMSMAN] -- ENGAGE_DISENGAGE_CMD --> PBA
    A2[COMPASS] -- HEADING_DEG --> PBA
    A3[GPS_RECEIVER] -- POSITION_WAYPOINT --> PBA
    A4[RUDDER_ACTUATOR] -- RUDDER_CMD_DEG --> PBA
    A5[HMI_DISPLAY] -- MODE_HEADING_STATUS --> PBA
    A6[POWER_SUPPLY] -- DC_24V --> PBA
    A7[ENVIRONMENT] -- WIND_WAVE_TORQUE --> BOAT
    BOAT[POWERBOAT_PLANT] -- HEADING_RATE --> A2
    PBA -- STEERING_TORQUE --> BOAT

    classDef external fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef system fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7 external
    class PBA system
    class BOAT plant
```

### 4.2 Top-Level Use Case Diagram (PBA)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ACTORS["EXTERNAL ACTORS"]
        HELM[HELMSMAN]
        COMP[COMPASS]
        GPS[GPS_RECEIVER]
        RUD[RUDDER_ACTUATOR]
        HMI[HMI_DISPLAY]
    end

    subgraph USECASES["PBA TOP LEVEL USE CASES"]
        UC1[UC1_ENGAGE_AUTOPILOT]
        UC2[UC2_DISENGAGE_AUTOPILOT]
        UC3[UC3_STEER_TO_HEADING]
        UC4[UC4_STEER_TO_WAYPOINT]
        UC5[UC5_MONITOR_SYSTEM_HEALTH]
        UC6[UC6_HANDLE_FAULT]
    end

    HELM -- presses_engage --> UC1
    HELM -- presses_disengage --> UC2
    HELM -- sets_heading --> UC3
    HELM -- sets_waypoint --> UC4
    COMP -- heading_stream --> UC3
    COMP -- heading_stream --> UC4
    GPS -- waypoint_stream --> UC4
    UC3 -- rudder_commands --> RUD
    UC4 -- rudder_commands --> RUD
    UC1 -- status_update --> HMI
    UC2 -- status_update --> HMI
    UC3 -- status_update --> HMI
    UC4 -- status_update --> HMI
    UC5 -- status_update --> HMI
    UC6 -- alarm_and_disengage --> HMI

    classDef actor fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef usecase fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class HELM,COMP,GPS,RUD,HMI actor
    class UC1,UC2,UC3,UC4,UC5,UC6 usecase
```

### 4.3 PBA Top-Level Mode / State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MODE_OFF
    MODE_OFF --> MODE_STANDBY : powerOn
    MODE_STANDBY --> MODE_AUTO : engageHeading
    MODE_STANDBY --> MODE_NAV : engageWaypoint
    MODE_STANDBY --> MODE_CALIBRATION : enterCalibration
    MODE_AUTO --> MODE_NAV : switchToNav
    MODE_NAV --> MODE_AUTO : switchToAuto
    MODE_AUTO --> MODE_MANUAL : disengage
    MODE_NAV --> MODE_MANUAL : disengage
    MODE_MANUAL --> MODE_AUTO : engageHeading
    MODE_MANUAL --> MODE_NAV : engageWaypoint
    MODE_AUTO --> MODE_FAULT : sensorFault
    MODE_NAV --> MODE_FAULT : sensorFault
    MODE_STANDBY --> MODE_FAULT : selfTestFail
    MODE_FAULT --> MODE_STANDBY : faultCleared
    MODE_FAULT --> MODE_MANUAL : operatorOverride
    MODE_CALIBRATION --> MODE_STANDBY : calibrationComplete
    MODE_MANUAL --> MODE_OFF : powerOff
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STAGE1_MISSION_NEED] --> S2[STAGE2_CONTEXT_BLOCK]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE3_ACTOR_ENUM]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE4_USE_CASE_DERIVATION]
    S4 --> S5[STAGE5_MODE_DERIVATION]
    S5 --> S6[STAGE6_INTERFACE_SPEC]
    S6 --> S7[STAGE7_VERIFICATION]
    S7 --> S8[STAGE8_BRIDGE_TO_DETAIL]
    S7 -- REWORK --> S2
    S7 -- REWORK --> S4
    S7 -- REWORK --> S6
    S8 --> DONE[APPROVED_TOP_LEVEL_MODEL]

    classDef stage fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8 stage
    class DONE done
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The context diagram shows *what* the PBA talks to. The use case diagram shows *what* it does for each actor. The state machine shows *how* it transitions between behaviors over time. The architecture flow shows *how* the developer builds the model. All four views are *required* for a complete KTU top-level model answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define a "Top-Level Model" in the context of Model-Based Software Development. List any four elements it must contain for the Powerboat Autopilot system.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A *top-level model* is the highest-level system abstraction that defines the system's **boundary, external actors, use cases, operational modes, and interfaces** without describing any internal structure.

For the PBA the four essential elements are:

1. **System Context Block** — single block `PBA` with all external entities around it.
2. **Top-Level Use Cases** — e.g., `SteerToHeading`, `SteerToWaypoint`, `Engage`, `Disengage`.
3. **Operational Modes** — e.g., `Off, Manual, Standby, Auto, Nav, Fault`.
4. **External Interfaces** — e.g., `heading, waypoint, rudderCmd, engage, modeStatus`.

> **Key Term the Examiner Wants:** *"System context, use case, mode of operation, interface contract."*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Style]**

**Q2. Differentiate between an "Actor" and a "Use Case" in the PBA top-level model. Give one example of each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Actor | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | An external entity that interacts with the system | A piece of functionality the system provides to an actor |
| **Lives in** | The *environment* of the PBA | The *boundary* of the PBA |
| **Drives** | Initiates interactions | Responds to actor goals |
| **PBA Example** | `Helmsman`, `Compass`, `GPS` | `SteerToHeading`, `Engage` |
| **Notation (UML)** | Stick figure outside the system rectangle | Ellipse inside the system rectangle |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Pattern as per 2024 Scheme ESE]**

---

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *Choice Option 1*

**Develop a complete top-level model for the Powerboat Autopilot (PBA) System. In your answer:**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Draw the **system context block diagram** and list the **external actors** with one-line descriptions.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Identify the **top-level use cases** and **modes of operation**, and show how the use cases map to the modes.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) — System Context & External Actors (7 marks)**

The PBA is represented as a single block `PBA: Autopilot` whose boundary is the *system boundary*. The external actors connected to the PBA are:

| # | Actor | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Helmsman** | Human operator who sets mode, course, and can override |
| 2 | **Compass** | Fluxgate sensor that reports magnetic heading (°) |
| 3 | **GPS Receiver** | Provides lat/lon, COG, SOG, and waypoints |
| 4 | **Rudder Actuator** | Electro-hydraulic actuator that physically deflects the rudder |
| 5 | **HMI / Display** | Shows mode, heading, alarms to the helmsman |
| 6 | **Power Supply** | Provides 24 V DC electrical power |
| 7 | **Powerboat (Plant)** | The vessel whose heading is being controlled |
| 8 | **Environment** | Wind, waves, current — provide disturbance torques |

**Context block diagram (described):** Central rectangle labelled `PBA: Autopilot`. Eight port connections radiating outward to the eight actors named above. The `Environment` and `Powerboat` are *implicit* (the PBA does not communicate with them directly; they affect the controlled plant only).

> **Marking note (3 marks for the block, 4 marks for the actor list with valid descriptions).**

---

**Part (b) — Use Cases & Modes (7 marks)**

**Top-Level Use Cases (5 marks):**

1. `Engage` — transition system from Standby/Manual to Auto or Nav
2. `Disengage` — return control to the helmsman
3. `SteerToHeading(h)` — maintain commanded compass heading
4. `SteerToWaypoint(p)` — steer to GPS waypoint
5. `MonitorHealth` — continuous, cross-cutting; raises alarms
6. `HandleFault` — fail-safe reaction to sensor/actuator failure

**Modes of Operation (2 marks):** `Off, Manual, Standby, Auto, Nav, Fault, Calibration`.

**Use-Case ↔ Mode Mapping (textual answer, the model answer writes the table):**

| Use Case | Active Mode(s) | Transition Triggered |
|---|---|---|
| Engage | Standby → Auto / Nav | `engage()` event |
| Disengage | Auto / Nav → Manual | `disengage()` event |
| SteerToHeading | Auto | continuous PID control |
| SteerToWaypoint | Nav | cross-track error control |
| MonitorHealth | All powered modes | periodic @ 10 Hz |
| HandleFault | Any → Fault | sensor invalid / actuator stuck |

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Choice Option 2*

**For the Powerboat Autopilot (PBA) System:**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Identify and justify the **modes of operation** of the PBA. Draw the **top-level state machine** showing all valid transitions.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Specify the **external interfaces** (data, units, range, rate) for the four most critical PBA ports and explain why interface completeness at the top level is non-negotiable.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) — Modes & State Machine (7 marks)**

The PBA must support the following modes (each justifies itself by a distinct *external-observable behavior*):

- **Off** — unpowered; satisfies safety before power-on.
- **Manual** — helm in control; PBA must *observe but not actuate*; required for legal operation in many jurisdictions.
- **Standby** — powered and self-tested; required so that engagement is *instantaneous* when requested.
- **Auto (Heading Hold)** — closed-loop compass control; primary use case.
- **Nav (Track / Waypoint)** — closed-loop cross-track control; primary long-distance use case.
- **Fault** — safe state reached when any sensor is invalid or actuator is stuck; required by IEC 61508 SIL principles.
- **Calibration** — required to set compass deviation, rudder endpoints, and PID gains.

**State Machine (textual reproduction; see the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 for the graphical form):**

$$
\text{Off} \xrightarrow{\text{powerOn}} \text{Standby}
$$

$$
\text{Standby} \xrightarrow{\text{engageHeading}} \text{Auto} \quad ; \quad \text{Standby} \xrightarrow{\text{engageWaypoint}} \text{Nav}
$$

$$
\text{Standby} \xrightarrow{\text{selfTestFail}} \text{Fault} \quad ; \quad \text{Auto, Nav} \xrightarrow{\text{sensorFault}} \text{Fault}
$$

$$
\text{Auto, Nav, Fault} \xrightarrow{\text{disengage / override}} \text{Manual} \quad ; \quad \text{Manual} \xrightarrow{\text{powerOff}} \text{Off}
$$

**Justification of Completeness (2 marks):** Every state is reachable from `Off`; every state (except `Off`) can reach `Fault`; `Fault` can always return to `Manual` via operator override, ensuring no "dead state" — a key KTU examination point.

---

**Part (b) — External Interfaces (7 marks)**

The four most critical interfaces are:

| # | Port Name | Direction | Data | Units | Range | Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `compassHeading` | In | Magnetic heading | degrees | 0 – 359 | 10 Hz |
| 2 | `gpsWaypoint` | In | Lat, Lon, SOG, COG | deg, deg, kn, deg | ±90, ±180, 0–60, 0–359 | 1 Hz |
| 3 | `rudderCommand` | Out | Commanded rudder angle | degrees | –35 to +35 | 50 Hz |
| 4 | `helmEngage` | In | Engage / Disengage + mode select | enum | {Off, Standby, Auto, Nav, Manual} | event-driven |

**Why interface completeness is non-negotiable (3 marks):**

1. **Interface errors are the #1 source of system-level integration failure.** A unit mismatch (degrees vs. radians) or rate mismatch (1 Hz GPS used in a 10 Hz loop) can be invisible until integration testing.
2. **Safety argument depends on interfaces.** A safety case (per ISO 26262 / IEC 61508) must prove that *every* input is bounded and valid; missing interface specs make the case unprovable.
3. **Top-level interface contracts are the basis of subsystem contracts** — without them, parallel development teams cannot proceed independently.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "use case" with "mode".** A use case is *what the system does*; a mode is *how the system behaves over time*. Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks if you interchange them.
> 2. **Drawing internal components inside the top-level model.** This is a top-level model — only the *boundary*, *actors*, *use cases*, *modes*, and *interfaces* belong here. Showing sensors, PID controllers, or actuators *inside* the PBA block costs marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the "Fault" mode.** A common mistake. Every safety-relevant top-level model *must* include a fail-safe state and the transitions leading to it.
> 4. **Missing the implicit `MonitorHealth` use case.** Examiners reward students who recognize that monitoring is cross-cutting and not tied to one mode.
> 5. **Listing actors without a one-line description.** "Helmsman" alone is worth half a mark; "Helmsman — human operator who engages/disengages the autopilot and sets the commanded heading" is worth the full mark.
> 6. **In the state machine, forgetting `Manual → Off` (powerOff) or the override transition `Fault → Manual`.** These break the *reachability* property and the examiner will catch it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the PBA Top-Level Model**

- [ ] A **top-level model** = context + actors + use cases + modes + interfaces. *No internals.*
- [ ] The PBA is a **safety-critical, hard real-time, closed-loop** control system.
- [ ] **Eight external actors** for the PBA: Helmsman, Compass, GPS, Rudder Actuator, HMI, Power, Plant, Environment.
- [ ] **Six top-level use cases**: Engage, Disengage, SteerToHeading, SteerToWaypoint, MonitorHealth, HandleFault.
- [ ] **Seven modes**: Off, Manual, Standby, Auto, Nav, Fault, Calibration.
- [ ] **Compass** is the primary feedback sensor for `SteerToHeading`; **GPS** is the primary feedback sensor for `SteerToWaypoint`.
- [ ] **Rudder command** is the only continuous analog output of the PBA.
- [ ] The **Fault** mode is reachable from *every* powered mode; it is the safety kernel.
- [ ] **Reachability** of every state from `Off` and **dead-state-freeness** are the two formal completeness checks the examiner tests.
- [ ] The top-level model is **executable**: each use case → operation; each mode → state.
- [ ] The output of the top-level model is a **single top-level capsule** with ports and a classifier state machine — the input to the next design phase.
- [ ] The canonical KTU exam question is *"Identify the missing actor / use case / mode / transition"* — apply the completeness rules and you will find the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AADL: Components - Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AADL: Components - Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (SAE AS5506B Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AADL Software Components** are one of the three top-level component categories (Software, Hardware, System) in the Architecture Analysis and Design Language (AADL, SAE Standard AS5506). They represent the **application software and runtime infrastructure** of an embedded real-time system, capturing the structural, behavioral, and execution semantics of the code that runs on the target hardware.

According to the **SAE AS5506B** specification, software components model the **computational, memory, and communication** aspects of a system through typed components, hierarchical subcomponents, features, and properties. They are the building blocks that describe *what the software does* and *how it is organized*, *scheduled*, and *interconnected* at the architectural level.

## 1.2 The Six Software Component Categories

AADL defines **six distinct types** of software components. Each one is purpose-built for representing a specific layer of the software stack — from raw data types to actively scheduled threads of execution.

| # | Component | One-Line Purpose | Execution? |
|---|-----------|------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Process** | A protected address space (memory boundary) | No |
| 2 | **Thread** | A schedulable unit of concurrent execution | **Yes** |
| 3 | **Thread Group** | Logical/organizational grouping of threads | No |
| 4 | **Data** | A static data type (declaration, no behavior) | No |
| 5 | **Subprogram** | A callable sequential code block (function/procedure) | Call-time only |
| 6 | **Subprogram Group** | A library/namespace for subprograms | No |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Think of a Real-Time Application as an Office Building:**
> - The **Process** is the *building itself* — a protected boundary, with its own floors, its own memory, and a security checkpoint.
> - The **Threads** are the *employees* working in the building — each doing tasks, sometimes in parallel, scheduled by a manager (the *dispatch protocol*).
> - The **Thread Group** is the *department* (HR, Finance) — a way to organize employees into teams.
> - **Data** is the *filing cabinet* — pure information storage, no actions.
> - **Subprogram** is the *task/job* an employee performs (e.g., "compute altitude").
> - **Subprogram Group** is the *job catalogue* — a list of all available jobs.
> - **Ports** are the *doors and intercoms* between rooms; **connections** are the *hallways* linking them.

This analogy will help you remember the *hierarchy* and *separation of concerns* that AADL enforces.

## 1.4 Why Software Components Matter in MBSD

In **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, AADL software components allow engineers to:

1. **Separate concerns** — Logic (subprograms) is separate from scheduling (threads) and memory (processes).
2. **Predict timing** — Period, deadline, execution time, and dispatch protocol are *first-class* properties.
3. **Verify architecture** — Connect components formally so tools (OSATE, MAST, Cheddar) can perform **schedulability, timing, and safety analyses** automatically.
4. **Generate code** — The AADL model can be transformed into skeletal source code for target platforms (e.g., C/Ada with ARINC 653).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AADL software components are the *primary* vehicle for modeling **embedded real-time systems** in MBSD. You must know all six component types, their features, and how to compose them hierarchically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Software Component

### 🔹 2.1.1 Process Component
- **Definition:** A *protected address space* or *partition* (e.g., ARINC 653 partition, POSIX process).
- **Memory Role:** Encapsulates data and threads with strict memory isolation.
- **Subcomponents Allowed:** Threads, thread groups, data, subprograms, subprogram groups, *and other processes*.
- **Key Properties:** `Dispatch_Protocol`, `Period`, `Deadline`, `Execution_Time`, `Memory_Size`, `Priority`.

### 🔹 2.1.2 Thread Component
- **Definition:** The *atomic, schedulable unit of concurrent execution*. Represents a flow of control.
- **Execution Characteristics:** Scheduled by a **runtime scheduler** (RMS, EDF, sporadic server, etc.) defined by the underlying execution platform.
- **Dispatch Protocols (in `Dispatch_Protocol` property):**
  - `Periodic` — Released at fixed intervals.
  - `Sporadic` — Released by events, with a minimum inter-arrival time.
  - `Aperiodic` — Released by events, no minimum inter-arrival constraint.
  - `Background` — Lowest priority, runs when nothing else does.
  - `Timed` — Released at specified times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Periodic threads** are the most commonly used type in hard real-time control loops (sensor sampling, actuator control).

### 🔹 2.1.3 Thread Group Component
- **Definition:** A *non-executable* logical grouping of threads, used to organize related threads in the model (e.g., all threads belonging to the "Navigation Subsystem").
- **Has no memory or scheduling semantics of its own** — purely organizational.

### 🔹 2.1.4 Data Component
- **Definition:** Represents a *static data type* (a struct, record, or constant) at the architectural level. Has *no behavior*.
- **Role:** Used as a typed message passed between components, or as a configuration parameter.
- **Example:** `SensorData { value : Float; timestamp : Time; validity : Bool; }`

### 🔹 2.1.5 Subprogram Component
- **Definition:** A *callable sequential code unit* — equivalent to a function or procedure in C/Ada.
- **Execution:** Runs *within the caller's execution context* (no separate thread).
- **Examples:** `Compute_FFT`, `Read_Sensor`, `Filter_Signal`, `Update_State`.

### 🔹 2.1.6 Subprogram Group Component
- **Definition:** A *library* or *namespace* containing multiple related subprograms (e.g., the standard math library).
- **No execution semantics** — purely organizational.

## 2.2 Features — The Communication Interfaces

AADL software components expose **typed features** (interaction points):

| Feature Type | Syntax | Direction | Purpose | Allowed On |
|--------------|--------|-----------|---------|------------|
| **Event Port** | `event` | uni-directional, asynchronous | Discrete signals / control events | Thread, Process, Subprogram |
| **Event Data Port** | `event data port` | uni-directional, asynchronous + payload | Messages with data | Thread, Process, Subprogram |
| **Data Port** | `data port` | uni-directional, state-based (last-wins) | Continuous data state | Thread, Process, Subprogram |
| **Data Access** | `provides/requires data access` | shared (read/write) memory | Shared state | Thread, Process, Data |
| **Subprogram Access** | `provides/requires subprogram access` | call-based | RPC / function call | Thread, Process, Subprogram, Subprogram Group |
| **Subprogram Parameter** | `in` / `out` / `inout` | call-time data flow | Formal parameters | Subprogram |
| **Event Source / Sink** | declared inside component | internal | Internal triggers | Thread |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Property & Formula Cheat Sheet

| Property | Applies To | Type / Units | Purpose / Formula |
|----------|------------|--------------|---------------------|
| `Dispatch_Protocol` | Thread, Process | enum {Periodic, Sporadic, Aperiodic, Background, Timed} | Scheduling behavior |
| `Period` | Thread, Process | **Time (ns, ms, s)** | Release interval $P$ |
| `Deadline` | Thread, Process | Time | Worst-case completion time $D$ (must be $\leq P$) |
| `Compute_Execution_Time` | Thread, Subprogram | Time range `[Min .. Max]` | WCET bound $C_i$ |
| `Priority` | Thread, Process | integer (1 = highest typically) | Scheduling order |
| `Stack_Size` | Thread, Process | Size in bytes | Memory budget |
| `Memory_Size` | Process, Data | Size in bytes | RAM footprint |
| `Source_Code_Language` | Subprogram, Thread | enum (C, Ada, Java, Simulink) | Generation target |
| `Source_Text` | Subprogram | string | Inline source code |
| `Concurrency_Control_Protocol` | Data | enum {None, Priority_Inheritance, Priority_Ceiling} | Shared resource access |
| `Posix_Schedule_Policy` | Thread | enum {SCHED_FIFO, SCHED_RR, SCHED_OTHER} | Linux/POSIX scheduling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Timing Constraint (Hard Real-Time):**
> For a *set* of $n$ **periodic independent tasks** scheduled by Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), the Liu & Layland **schedulability condition** is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} \leq n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

where $C_i$ is the **worst-case execution time (WCET)** of thread $i$, and $P_i$ is its **period**. As $n \to \infty$, the bound converges to $\ln 2 \approx \mathbf{0.693}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

AADL software components are used in:
- **Aerospace:** Airbus A380, Boeing 787 — ARINC 653 partitioned architectures modeled as Processes.
- **Automotive:** AUTOSAR adaptive platform — SWC (Software Components) correspond 1:1 to AADL threads/processes.
- **Avionics & UAVs:** Flight control software — periodic threads for sensor acquisition, control laws, and actuator output.
- **Medical devices:** Infusion pumps, pacemakers — for which **FDA** requires formal architectural models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — AADL Software Component Model

The following is a complete, runnable **AADL model** (textual AADL / AADLv2) for a small *flight altitude control subsystem*. We will derive each section step by step.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define a `Data` Component (Type for messages)

```aadl
data Altitude_Data
  properties
    Data_Model::Data_Representation => Float;
    Data_Size => 4 Bytes;
end Altitude_Data;
```

**Logic:** This declares a 4-byte floating-point data type used to pass altitude values between threads.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define a `Subprogram` Component (Mathematical kernel)

```aadl
subprogram Compute_Control
  features
    Alt_In  : in  parameter Altitude_Data;
    Set_Pt  : in  parameter Altitude_Data;
    Cmd_Out : out parameter Altitude_Data;
  properties
    Source_Language => C;
    Source_Text     => ("control.c");
    Compute_Execution_Time => 1ms .. 3ms;
end Compute_Control;
```

**Logic:**
- `Compute_Control` is a callable function (subprogram).
- It takes two `in` parameters (altitude input, setpoint) and returns one `out` (command to actuator).
- The WCET is bounded between **1 ms (best)** and **3 ms (worst)** — this is the **$C_i$** for schedulability analysis.
- The `Source_Text` property attaches the actual C source file for code generation.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Define a `Thread` Component (Scheduled execution)

```aadl
thread Control_Thread
  features
    Alt_Sense : in  event data port Altitude_Data;
    Cmd_Out   : out event data port Altitude_Data;
end Control_Thread;

thread implementation Control_Thread.Impl
  calls
    {
    Compute_Control : subprogram Compute_Control;
    };
  connections
    Port_To_Call : data port Alt_Sense -> Compute_Control.Alt_In;
    Call_To_Port : data port Compute_Control.Cmd_Out -> Cmd_Out;
  properties
    Dispatch_Protocol     => Periodic;
    Period                => 20 ms;
    Deadline              => 15 ms;
    Priority              => 5;
    Compute_Execution_Time => 2ms .. 4ms;
end Control_Thread.Impl;
```

**Logic:**
- `Control_Thread` is a **periodic thread** with a period $P = 20$ ms, a deadline $D = 15$ ms (must be $D \leq P$ ✓), and priority 5.
- It calls the `Compute_Control` subprogram through a `calls` clause — establishing a *subprogram call relationship*.
- Data ports `Alt_Sense` (input) and `Cmd_Out` (output) are connected to the subprogram parameters.
- **Schedulability test:** $U = C_i / P_i = 4 / 20 = 0.20$, well under the RMS bound of 0.693 for a single task.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Define a `Process` Component (Protected memory boundary)

```aadl
process Flight_Process
  features
    Sensor_In : in  event data port Altitude_Data;
    Actuator  : out event data port Altitude_Data;
end Flight_Process;

process implementation Flight_Process.Impl
  subcomponents
    Ctrl : thread Control_Thread.Impl;
  connections
    Sensor_To_Ctrl : port Sensor_In -> Ctrl.Alt_Sense;
    Ctrl_To_Out   : port Ctrl.Cmd_Out -> Actuator;
  properties
    Memory_Size  => 512 KB;
    Stack_Size   => 32 KB;
end Flight_Process.Impl;
```

**Logic:**
- The process contains exactly one subcomponent: `Ctrl` of type `Control_Thread.Impl`.
- Sensor input enters the process, is forwarded to the thread, processed, and the resulting command exits the process.
- Memory budget: **512 KB total RAM**, **32 KB stack** — these are essential for **worst-case memory certification** in safety-critical systems.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Schedulability Derivation (Rate Monotonic)

Consider the `Control_Thread` with:

$$C_1 = 4 \text{ ms}, \quad P_1 = 20 \text{ ms}, \quad D_1 = 15 \text{ ms}, \quad n = 1$$

**Step 1 — Compute the CPU utilization:**

$$U_1 = \frac{C_1}{P_1} = \frac{4 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} = 0.20$$

**Step 2 — Compute the RMS bound for $n = 1$ task:**

$$U_{\text{bound}}(1) = 1 \cdot \left(2^{1/1} - 1\right) = 2^1 - 1 = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Verify schedulability:**

$$U_1 = 0.20 \leq 1.0 = U_{\text{bound}}(1) \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

> The thread is **schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Worst-case response time $R_1 = C_1 = 4$ ms $\leq D_1 = 15$ ms.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Composition Rules

| Parent Component | Allowed Subcomponents |
|------------------|-----------------------|
| **System** | Process, Processor, Memory, Device, Bus, Connection |
| **Process** | Thread, Thread Group, Data, Subprogram, Subprogram Group, **Process** |
| **Thread** | Data, Subprogram, Subprogram Group, **Subprogram Call** (via `calls`) |
| **Thread Group** | Thread, Data, Subprogram, **Thread Group** |
| **Subprogram Group** | Subprogram, **Subprogram Group** |
| **Data** | Data (nested data components) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Rule:** A *Process cannot be a subcomponent of a Thread*. A *Thread can be a subcomponent of a Process or Thread Group only* — never of another Thread.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AADL Software Component Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Software Components] --> B[Process]
    A --> C[Thread]
    A --> D[Thread Group]
    A --> E[Data]
    A --> F[Subprogram]
    A --> G[Subprogram Group]
    B --> B1[Protected Address Space]
    C --> C1[Periodic]
    C --> C2[Aperiodic]
    C --> C3[Sporadic]
    C --> C4[Background]
    F --> F1[Provides Subprogram Access]
    G --> G1[Library or Namespace]
    E --> E1[Static Type Only]
```

## 4.2 Feature (Port/Access) Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    F[Features] --> P[Ports]
    F --> A[Access]
    F --> PR[Parameters]
    P --> EP[Event Port]
    P --> EDP[Event Data Port]
    P --> DP[Data Port]
    A --> DA[Data Access]
    A --> SA[Subprogram Access]
    PR --> PI[In Parameter]
    PR --> PO[Out Parameter]
    PR --> PIO[Inout Parameter]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

Below is the **functional architecture** for a typical flight control AADL software model showing how the six software components interact:

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "Process: Flight_Process (512 KB)"
        TG[Thread Group: Flight_Threads]
        T1[Thread: Control_Thread.Periodic]
        T2[Thread: Sensor_Thread.Periodic]
        D1[Data: Altitude_Data]
        SG1[Subprogram Group: Math_Lib]
        SP1[Subprogram: Compute_Control]
        SP2[Subprogram: Read_Sensor]
    end
    T2 -- event data port --> T1
    T1 -- calls --> SP1
    T2 -- calls --> SP2
    T1 -- data access --> D1
    T2 -- data access --> D1
    SG1 -- provides subprogram access --> SP1
    SG1 -- provides subprogram access --> SP2
```

## 4.4 Component Containment Rules Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    Sys[System] --> Proc[Process]
    Sys --> Proc2[Process]
    Proc --> TG[Thread Group]
    Proc --> Data[Data]
    Proc --> SG[Subprogram Group]
    TG --> Th1[Thread]
    TG --> Th2[Thread]
    SG --> SP1[Subprogram]
    SG --> SP2[Subprogram]
    Th1 -.calls.-> SP1
    Th2 -.calls.-> SP2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Solid arrows represent **composition** (parent → subcomponent). Dashed arrows (`-.calls.->`) represent the special **subprogram call relationship**, which is the only inter-thread structural link that does not use ports.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]
**List and briefly explain the six types of software components in AADL. State one distinguishing property of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Process** — A protected address space or partition; provides memory isolation; contains threads.
2. **Thread** — A schedulable unit of concurrent execution; has `Dispatch_Protocol` and `Period` properties.
3. **Thread Group** — Logical grouping of threads; no execution semantics; used for organization.
4. **Data** — Static typed data with no behavior; characterized by `Data_Size` and `Data_Model` properties.
5. **Subprogram** — Callable sequential code; runs in caller's context; properties include `Source_Language`, `Compute_Execution_Time`.
6. **Subprogram Group** — Namespace/library for subprograms; purely organizational; no execution semantics.
*(1 mark for correct list, 1 mark for correct explanations, 1 mark for one distinguishing property per type — partial credit allowed.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Differentiate between an AADL Process and a Thread component. Why is this distinction architecturally important in real-time embedded systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|--------|---------|--------|
| Memory | Has its own address space | Shares address space of enclosing process |
| Scheduling | Not directly scheduled | Directly scheduled by the runtime |
| Containment | Contains threads, data, subprograms | Contains data, subprograms; calls subprograms |
| Fault Isolation | Strong (memory protection) | Weak (within process) |
| ARINC 653 Mapping | Partition | Process within partition |

**Architectural Importance:** The Process/Thread distinction enables **fault containment and memory protection** in safety-critical systems (e.g., ARINC 653 in avionics). A faulty thread cannot corrupt the memory of another thread inside a *different* process, but threads within the *same* process share memory and can be scheduled independently for parallelism. *(1 mark for table, 1 mark for memory distinction, 1 mark for architectural reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]

**A.** *An embedded unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) altitude controller is to be modeled in AADL. The system samples altitude from a sensor every 20 ms, runs a PID control algorithm (WCET 2–5 ms, deadline 18 ms, priority 7), and outputs a control signal to the actuator every 20 ms. Model this system using AADL software components, showing Data, Subprogram, Thread, and Process declarations. Justify the choice of dispatch protocol.* **(14 marks)**

#### **Part (a) — AADL Model & Component Declarations (7 marks)**

**Data Component (1 mark):**
```aadl
data Altitude_Data
  properties
    Data_Model::Data_Representation => Float;
    Data_Size => 4 Bytes;
end Altitude_Data;
```

**Subprogram Component (2 marks):**
```aadl
subprogram PID_Control
  features
    Measured : in  parameter Altitude_Data;
    Setpoint : in  parameter Altitude_Data;
    Output   : out parameter Altitude_Data;
  properties
    Source_Language => C;
    Compute_Execution_Time => 2ms .. 5ms;
end PID_Control;
```

**Thread Component (2 marks):**
```aadl
thread Altitude_Controller
  features
    Alt_Sense : in  event data port Altitude_Data;
    Cmd_Out   : out event data port Altitude_Data;
end Altitude_Controller;

thread implementation Altitude_Controller.Impl
  calls {
    PID_Instance : subprogram PID_Control;
  };
  connections
    In_Cnx : port Alt_Sense -> PID_Instance.Measured;
    Out_Cnx: port PID_Instance.Output -> Cmd_Out;
  properties
    Dispatch_Protocol      => Periodic;
    Period                 => 20ms;
    Deadline               => 18ms;
    Priority               => 7;
    Compute_Execution_Time => 2ms .. 5ms;
end Altitude_Controller.Impl;
```

**Process Component (2 marks):**
```aadl
process Flight_Process
  features
    Sensor_In : in  event data port Altitude_Data;
    Cmd_Out   : out event data port Altitude_Data;
end Flight_Process;

process implementation Flight_Process.Impl
  subcomponents
    PID_Thread : thread Altitude_Controller.Impl;
  connections
    Sensor_To_PID : port Sensor_In -> PID_Thread.Alt_Sense;
    PID_To_Out    : port PID_Thread.Cmd_Out -> Cmd_Out;
  properties
    Memory_Size  => 256 KB;
    Stack_Size   => 16 KB;
end Flight_Process.Impl;
```

#### **Part (b) — Dispatch Protocol Justification & Schedulability Analysis (7 marks)**

**Dispatch Protocol Choice (3 marks):**
The thread is declared `Periodic` because:
1. Altitude sensing is a **time-triggered control loop** — fixed sampling period is required.
2. The PID algorithm assumes **deterministic control** — jitter would degrade stability.
3. `Sporadic` would apply only if the sensor were *event-triggered* (e.g., interrupt on threshold). Here, the sensor is polled.

**Schedulability (4 marks):**

For $n = 1$ task under RMS:

$$U = \frac{C_{\max}}{P} = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} = 0.25$$

$$U_{\text{bound}}(1) = 1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 1.0$$

$$0.25 \leq 1.0 \quad \checkmark \quad \text{Schedulable.}$$

Deadline check: $D = 18$ ms $\leq P = 20$ ms ✓. **System is schedulable under RMS.**

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]

**B.** *Explain the six AADL software components in detail. With a neat diagram, show the AADL thread execution model for a periodic thread, including its states and the dispatch protocol. Discuss how the `Compute_Execution_Time`, `Period`, and `Deadline` properties interact to ensure real-time correctness.* **(14 marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Six AADL Software Components (7 marks)**

*(1 mark each, concise description expected)*

1. **Process** — A protected address space; memory boundary; hosts threads.
2. **Thread** — Schedulable execution unit; has dispatch protocol, period, deadline, priority.
3. **Thread Group** — Non-executable logical grouping of threads.
4. **Data** — Static typed data; no behavior; passes via ports or shared access.
5. **Subprogram** — Callable sequential code; runs in caller's context; mapped to source code.
6. **Subprogram Group** — Namespace for subprograms; provides library semantics.

**Key distinguishing table (1 mark for clarity):**

| Component | Executable? | Contains Threads? | Has Dispatch Protocol? |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Process | No | Yes (sub) | Indirectly (via threads) |
| Thread | **Yes** | No | **Yes** |
| Thread Group | No | Yes (sub) | No |
| Data | No | No | No |
| Subprogram | Call-time | No | No |
| Subprogram Group | No | No | No |

#### **Part (b) — Periodic Thread Execution Model & Real-Time Properties (7 marks)**

**Periodic Thread State Diagram (3 marks):**
```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialized
    Initialized --> Ready: Thread Created
```
