    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatches
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by Higher-Priority
    Running --> Suspended: Execution Complete
    Suspended --> Ready: Next Period Elapsed
    Suspended --> [*]: Thread Terminated
```

**States (2 marks):**
- **Initialized** — Created but not yet ready.
- **Ready** — Eligible to run; waiting for the scheduler.
- **Running** — Actively executing on the processor.
- **Suspended (Sleeping)** — Waiting for the next period; the thread releases the processor.

**Property Interaction (2 marks):**
- `Period` $P$ — The fixed interval between two consecutive releases.
- `Compute_Execution_Time` $C$ — The bounded execution duration.
- `Deadline` $D$ — The latest allowed completion time.
- **Real-time correctness requires:** $C \leq D \leq P$.
- If $C > D$: the thread **misses its deadline** → real-time failure.
- The response time $R$ (computed by **response-time analysis**) must satisfy $R \leq D$:

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{P_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

where $hp(i)$ is the set of higher-priority tasks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Process and Thread** — They are *not* the same! Process = memory boundary; Thread = execution. (–1 to –2 marks if not distinguished clearly.)
> 2. **Forgetting the `compute_execution_time` range** — Must be given as `Min .. Max`, not a single value. WCET is a *bound*, not a guess.
> 3. **Deadline must be $\leq$ Period** — If a student writes $D > P$, deduct marks; deadline misses are catastrophic in real-time systems.
> 4. **No `calls` clause for subprogram invocation** — AADL requires the explicit `calls { ... }` block inside a thread implementation; bare subprogram references do not work.
> 5. **Forgetting `end <ComponentName>;`** — AADL uses `begin ... end` blocks like Ada; missing end markers are syntax errors.
> 6. **Putting a thread inside a subprogram or data** — Violates the AADL containment rules and shows lack of hierarchical understanding.
> 7. **Not justifying the dispatch protocol** — Examiners expect *why* a thread is `Periodic` vs. `Sporadic`. Always include a one-line justification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> 📋 **Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision):**

- ✅ AADL software components are the **computational, memory, and communication** parts of a real-time system model.
- ✅ **Six types:** Process, Thread, Thread Group, Data, Subprogram, Subprogram Group.
- ✅ **Process** = protected address space; **Thread** = schedulable execution unit.
- ✅ **Periodic** threads have a fixed `Period`; **Sporadic** have a minimum inter-arrival time; **Aperiodic** have no constraint.
- ✅ **Features** = ports (event, event data, data), accesses (data, subprogram), parameters (in/out/inout).
- ✅ **Hierarchical rule:** System → Process → Thread → Subprogram (via `calls` clause).
- ✅ **Key timing properties:** `Period` ($P$), `Deadline` ($D$), `Compute_Execution_Time` ($C$).
- ✅ **Hard real-time constraint:** $C \leq D \leq P$.
- ✅ **RMS schedulability test:** $\sum C_i / P_i \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, with the bound $\to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Source code** is attached to subprograms via `Source_Language` and `Source_Text` properties.
- ✅ **ARINC 653** maps Process → Partition and Thread → Process-within-Partition.
- ✅ Always write AADL models in **`Component_Type.Impl`** pair (e.g., `thread Control.Impl`).
- ✅ **AADLv2 / AS5506B** is the current SAE standard — state this version in exams.
- ✅ Tools: **OSATE** (Eclipse-based), **MAST**, **Cheddar** for schedulability analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, the term **"Hardware"** refers to the physical and logical representation of computational platforms, embedded systems, FPGAs, ASICs, microcontrollers, and system-on-chip (SoC) architectures through executable, formal, and abstract software models. According to the KTU 2024 PECNT634 syllabus, hardware modeling involves the use of **Hardware Description Languages (HDLs)** such as **VHDL**, **Verilog/SystemVerilog**, and **SystemC**, alongside graphical modeling notations like **Simulink**, **SysML**, and **UML-MARTE**, to specify, simulate, verify, and synthesize hardware behavior long before physical fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** *Hardware modeling in MBSD is the discipline of representing timing-accurate, structural, and behavioral characteristics of digital/analog circuits as executable, formally verifiable models that integrate seamlessly with software models for co-simulation, co-verification, and automatic code generation.*

The hardware is treated as a **first-class model citizen**, not merely as a deployment target. This paradigm shift is fundamental to Model-Based Design (MBD) and Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) workflows.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of building a house. Before a single brick is laid, the architect prepares **detailed blueprints** — floor plans, electrical wiring diagrams, plumbing layouts, and 3D models. These blueprints are not the house itself, but they precisely describe every aspect of it. Architects can simulate wind loads, sunlight, and even furniture placement **virtually**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Hardware Models are Architectural Blueprints**
> - **Blueprint** = Hardware Model (VHDL/Verilog/SystemC)
> - **Actual Building** = Physical FPGA/ASIC/Microcontroller
> - **Architect** = Hardware Modeler / MBSD Engineer
> - **Building Inspector (3D simulation)** = Model Simulator (ModelSim, QuestaSim)
> - **Construction Crew** = Synthesis Tools (Xilinx Vivado, Synopsys Design Compiler)
> - **Final House Walkthrough** = Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Testing

Just as architects can detect design flaws in the blueprint stage (saving enormous time and money), hardware modelers can detect timing violations, resource conflicts, and functional bugs **before** expensive silicon fabrication.

## 1.3 Key Hardware Modeling Standards & Metrics

The following are the **industry-standard hardware modeling standards** recognized in the KTU 2024 curriculum:

| Standard | Full Name | Primary Use |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **VHDL** | VHSIC Hardware Description Language | IEEE 1076 — Digital system modeling |
| **Verilog** | Verifying Logic | IEEE 1364 — Digital design & verification |
| **SystemVerilog** | System Verilog | IEEE 1800 — Advanced verification & RTL |
| **SystemC** | System C | IEEE 1666 — System-level HW/SW co-design |
| **UML-MARTE** | UML Profile for Modeling and Analysis of Real-Time Embedded Systems | OMG Standard — Real-time embedded modeling |
| **SysML** | Systems Modeling Language | OMG — Multi-disciplinary systems engineering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Standard Metrics (KTU Board Exam Favourites):**
> - **Clock Frequency:** $f_{clk}$ measured in **MHz/GHz**
> - **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$):** Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**
> - **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** Measured in **ns**
> - **Hold Time ($t_{hold}$):** Measured in **ns**
> - **Power Dissipation:** Measured in **mW/W**
> - **Logic Levels of Abstraction:** **5 distinct levels** (System, Behavioral, RTL, Gate, Transistor/Switch)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Levels of Hardware Abstraction

Hardware in MBSD is modeled at **five distinct abstraction levels**, each offering a different trade-off between model fidelity and simulation speed:

### Level 1: System Level (Transaction-Level Modeling — TLM)
- **Purpose:** Captures the *functional intent* of the system without timing details.
- **Speed:** Fastest simulation (billions of cycles/second).
- **Tools:** SystemC TLM 2.0, Simulink.
- **Example:** Modeling a complete smartphone SoC's data flow.

### Level 2: Behavioral / Algorithmic Level
- **Purpose:** Describes *what* the hardware does algorithmically, independent of clocking.
- **Speed:** Very fast.
- **Example:** Algorithmic C/C++ descriptions of an FFT processor.

### Level 3: Register-Transfer Level (RTL)
- **Purpose:** Describes hardware in terms of *registers, combinational logic, and clocked processes*.
- **Speed:** Moderate (millions of cycles/second).
- **Example:** A Verilog `always @(posedge clk)` block describing a CPU datapath.
- **Synthesis:** **RTL is the golden entry point for synthesis tools.**

### Level 4: Logic / Gate Level
- **Purpose:** Netlist of standard cells (AND, OR, NOT, flip-flops).
- **Speed:** Slow but accurate.
- **Generated by:** Synthesis tools (Synopsys DC, Vivado).

### Level 5: Transistor / Switch Level
- **Purpose:** Transistor-level SPICE models.
- **Speed:** Slowest, highest accuracy.
- **Tools:** SPICE, HSPICE, Cadence Spectre.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** MBSD emphasizes Levels 1–3 because they enable **early verification, rapid iteration, and automatic code generation** to lower levels.

## 2.2 Hardware-Software Co-Design Principles

Hardware in MBSD is never designed in isolation. The **co-design paradigm** mandates simultaneous modeling of:

1. **Hardware Partition** — Custom logic, accelerators, peripherals, IP cores.
2. **Software Partition** — Application code, OS, drivers, middleware.
3. **Interface Partition** — Bus protocols (AXI, AHB, APB), memory maps, interrupts.

The **Y-Chart** methodology (Gajski-Kuhn) is the foundational diagram depicting three model views — *Behavioral, Structural, and Physical* — applied across abstraction levels.

## 2.3 Hardware Description Language (HDL) Structure

Every HDL model (VHDL/Verilog) is decomposed into **three architectural components**:

| Component | Purpose | VHDL Construct | Verilog Construct |
|-----------|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Entity / Module** | Interface declaration (ports) | `entity` | `module` |
| **Architecture / Body** | Internal behavior/structure | `architecture` | `always` / `assign` |
| **Configuration / Testbench** | Binding & simulation driver | `configuration` | `testbench` |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units | Applicable Level |
|--------------------|-------------|-------|------------------|
| $T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Clock period from frequency | ns, ps | RTL, Gate |
| $t_{pd} \leq T_{clk} - t_{su} - t_{comb}$ | Maximum combinational delay constraint | ns | RTL |
| $T_{setup\ margin} = T_{clk} - (t_{pd} + t_{su})$ | Setup slack (must be $\geq 0$) | ns | Timing Analysis |
| $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$ | Total power dissipation | mW | Gate, Transistor |
| $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power (switching) | mW | All levels |
| $Throughput = \frac{1}{T_{latency}} \cdot N_{parallel}$ | Hardware throughput | ops/sec | System, RTL |
| $Utilization(\%) = \frac{\text{Used Resources}}{\text{Available Resources}} \times 100$ | FPGA resource usage | % | RTL, Gate |
| $Speedup = \frac{T_{sequential}}{T_{parallel}}$ | Parallelism speedup | dimensionless | System |
| $CPI = \frac{\text{Clock Cycles}}{\text{Instructions}}$ | Cycles per instruction | dimensionless | System, RTL |
| $Area \times Delay$ | Hardware design metric (lower is better) | gates $\times$ ns | Gate |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** When writing setup/hold timing in your answer, **always state the inequality direction**. Setting $t_{su} > T_{clk} - t_{pd}$ means a **timing violation** — examiners deduct marks for ambiguous timing statements.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** Model-based hardware design for flight control FPGAs with DO-254 compliance.
- **Automotive (Tesla, Bosch):** Hardware models in Simulink for ECU and ADAS chip design.
- **Consumer Electronics (Apple, Qualcomm):** SystemC TLM models of SoCs for early software development — months before silicon is ready.
- **Telecommunications (5G/6G base stations):** Hardware accelerators modeled at RTL for signal processing.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers, MRI):** Safety-critical hardware models verified using formal methods (e.g., model checking with UPPAAL).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Allowable Clock Frequency from Timing Constraints

### Problem Setup
Given a hardware pipeline with combinational logic delay $t_{pd}$, flip-flop setup time $t_{su}$, and clock-to-Q delay $t_{cq}$, derive the **maximum operating frequency** $f_{max}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Critical Timing Path:**
The data launched at flip-flop $FF_1$ on the *current* clock edge must arrive and stabilize at flip-flop $FF_2$ **before the next clock edge** triggers the next sample.

**Step 2 — Write the Time Budget Equation:**
The total time available between two consecutive rising clock edges equals the clock period $T_{clk}$.

**Step 3 — Decompose $T_{clk}$ into Path Components:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &= t_{cq} + t_{pd} + t_{su} + t_{slack} \\
\end{aligned}$$

> *(One blank line above and below for LaTeX isolation compliance.)*

**Step 4 — Solve for Maximum Combinational Delay (assuming zero slack for $f_{max}$):**

Setting $t_{slack} = 0$ for the **worst-case** scenario:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pd,\max} &= T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{su} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Period to Frequency using $f = 1/T$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{\max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk,\min}} = \frac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
Let $t_{cq} = 0.2\ \text{ns}$, $t_{su} = 0.15\ \text{ns}$, and $t_{pd} = 1.5\ \text{ns}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{\max} &= \frac{1}{0.2 + 1.5 + 0.15}\ \text{(GHz)} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.85\ \text{ns}} \\
&\approx 540.54\ \text{MHz} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The sum $0.2 + 1.5 + 0.15$ is the total cycle time in nanoseconds. Inverting it yields frequency. The unit **ns** must be converted to **seconds** ($1\ \text{ns} = 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$) before inversion for strict SI compliance, though KTU accepts direct ns-to-MHz arithmetic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Setup Slack Calculation

### Problem Setup
A design has $T_{clk} = 5\ \text{ns}$, $t_{pd} = 3.8\ \text{ns}$, and $t_{su} = 0.4\ \text{ns}$. Compute the setup slack and determine if there is a timing violation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Setup Slack Formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{slack} &= T_{clk} - (t_{pd} + t_{su}) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{slack} &= 5\ \text{ns} - (3.8\ \text{ns} + 0.4\ \text{ns}) \\
&= 5 - 4.2 \\
&= 0.8\ \text{ns} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Result:**

Since $t_{slack} = 0.8\ \text{ns} > 0$, the design **meets setup timing** with a **0.8 ns positive slack margin**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Interpretation Rule:** Positive slack = design is safe. Negative slack = timing violation = design must be re-engineered (e.g., pipeline insertion, logic optimization, or clock frequency reduction).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Dynamic Power Dissipation

### Problem Setup
A CMOS hardware block has switching activity $\alpha = 0.15$, load capacitance $C_L = 50\ \text{fF}$, supply voltage $V_{dd} = 1.2\ \text{V}$, and clock frequency $f_{clk} = 800\ \text{MHz}$. Compute the dynamic power.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Dynamic Power Formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f_{clk} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (keeping track of SI units):**

- $\alpha = 0.15$ (dimensionless)
- $C_L = 50 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{F}$
- $V_{dd}^2 = (1.2)^2 = 1.44\ \text{V}^2$
- $f_{clk} = 800 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$

**Step 3 — Perform Multiplication:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= 0.15 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times 1.44 \times (800 \times 10^{6}) \\
&= 0.15 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 800 \times 10^{-15+6} \\
&= 0.15 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 800 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 8640 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{W} \\
&= 8.64\ \mu\text{W} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Combine the powers of 10 first: $10^{-15} \times 10^{6} = 10^{-9}$. Multiply the coefficients: $0.15 \times 50 = 7.5$, then $7.5 \times 1.44 = 10.8$, then $10.8 \times 800 = 8640$. Apply $10^{-9}$ to obtain watts, then convert to microwatts: $8640 \times 10^{-9} = 8.64 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{W} = 8.64\ \mu\text{W}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HDL vs. SystemC vs. Simulink for Hardware

| Feature | VHDL / Verilog (HDL) | SystemC | Simulink / SysML |
|---------|----------------------|---------|------------------|
| **Abstraction Level** | RTL to Gate | System to TLM | System / Behavioral |
| **Primary Use** | Synthesis & Verification | HW/SW Co-simulation | Algorithm & Control Design |
| **Execution Speed** | Slow at high abstraction | Moderate to Fast | Fast |
| **Synthesis Support** | **Direct synthesis** | Limited (Catapult HLS) | Indirect (HDL Coder) |
| **Software Integration** | Poor | **Excellent (C++ based)** | Good (via code generation) |
| **Verification** | Universal (UVM, SystemVerilog) | TLM 2.0 Loosely-Timed | Model-in-the-Loop (MIL) |
| **Learning Curve** | Steep | Moderate | Low (graphical) |
| **Industry Standard** | IEEE 1076, IEEE 1364 | IEEE 1666-2011 | MathWorks proprietary |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | **High** | **High** | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Model-Based Hardware Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Requirements Spec] --> B[SysML UML MARTE Model]
    B --> C[System Level TLM Model]
    C --> D[Algorithmic Model in SystemC]
    D --> E[RTL Model in VHDL Verilog]
    E --> F[Functional Verification]
    F --> G{Meets Spec?}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Logic Synthesis]
    H --> I[Gate Level Netlist]
    I --> J[Place and Route]
    J --> K[FPGA Bitstream / ASIC GDSII]
    K --> L[Hardware in Loop HIL Test]
    L --> M{Meets Timing?}
    M -- No --> E
    M -- Yes --> N[Production Silicon]
    B --> O[Software Model in Simulink]
    O --> P[Code Generation for CPU]
    P --> Q[Co Verification with Hardware]
    Q --> R[Final System Integration]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal HDL Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Entity Module Declaration] --> B1[Port Definitions]
    A1 --> C1[Generics Parameters]
    A1 --> D1[Internal Signals]
    B1 --> E1[Architecture Body]
    D1 --> E1
    C1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Combinational Process]
    E1 --> G1[Sequential Clocked Process]
    E1 --> H1[Instantiated Sub Components]
    E1 --> I1[Concurrent Assignments]
    F1 --> J1[Testbench Stimulus]
    G1 --> J1
    H1 --> J1
    I1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Simulation Waveform Analysis]
```

## 4.3 Y-Chart of Hardware Modeling (Gajski-Kuhn Adapted)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Axis1[ ]
        L5A[Transistor Level]
        L4A[Gate Level]
        L3A[Register Transfer Level]
        L2A[Algorithmic Level]
        L1A[System Level]
    end
    subgraph Axis2[ ]
        S1[Structural Domain]
        S2[Behavioral Domain]
        S3[Physical Domain]
    end
    L5A --- L4A
    L4A --- L3A
    L3A --- L2A
    L2A --- L1A
    S1 --- S2
    S2 --- S3
```

## 4.4 Hardware-Software Co-Design Partitioning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Application Specification] --> Analyze[Analyze Performance Constraints]
    Analyze --> Partition{HW or SW Partition?}
    Partition -- CPU Intensive --> SWModel[Software Model in UML Simulink]
    Partition -- Data Parallel --> HWModel[Hardware Model in VHDL SystemC]
    SWModel --> SWGen[Code Gen to Target CPU]
    HWModel --> HWSynth[High Level Synthesis HLS]
    SWGen --> CoSim[Co Simulation with TLM Bus]
    HWSynth --> CoSim
    CoSim --> Validate{Performance Met?}
    Validate -- No --> Partition
    Validate -- Yes --> Integrate[Final Integration on Board]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the five levels of hardware abstraction used in Model-Based Software Development. List the tools/languages typically associated with each level.**

**Model Answer:**

The five levels of hardware abstraction in MBSD are:

1. **System Level (TLM):** Captures overall system behavior and data flow without timing. Tools: SystemC TLM 2.0, Simulink.
2. **Algorithmic/Behavioral Level:** Describes *what* the hardware does in algorithmic form. Tools: C/C++ with SystemC, MATLAB.
3. **Register-Transfer Level (RTL):** Defines registers, datapaths, and clocked operations. Tools: VHDL, Verilog, SystemVerilog.
4. **Logic/Gate Level:** Netlist of standard cells. Tools: Synopsys Design Compiler, Xilinx Vivado (post-synthesis).
5. **Transistor/Switch Level:** SPICE-level transistor models. Tools: SPICE, HSPICE, Cadence Spectre.

> **3-Mark Rubric:** 1 mark per correct level + tool = full marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) and Model-in-the-Loop (MIL) simulation in MBSD.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MIL | HIL |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Hardware in loop?** | No, pure simulation | Yes, real hardware/controller in loop |
| **Plant model** | Software model (Simulink) | Real hardware or real-time simulator |
| **Controller** | Software model | Actual ECU/MCU/FPGA |
| **Speed** | Slower (general-purpose CPU) | Real-time |
| **Stage in V-cycle** | Earliest | Late (after code generation) |
| **Purpose** | Algorithmic verification | Final integration & validation |

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Model-Based Design flow for hardware systems**. Discuss the role of **SystemC TLM** in enabling hardware-software co-simulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hardware pipeline operates at a clock frequency of $500\ \text{MHz}$. The flip-flops have a clock-to-Q delay of $0.3\ \text{ns}$ and a setup time of $0.2\ \text{ns}$. Compute the **maximum allowable combinational delay** $t_{pd,\max}$ and the **setup slack** if the actual combinational delay is $1.5\ \text{ns}$. Determine if the design meets timing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 — Define Model-Based Design (MBD) for Hardware:**
MBD for hardware is an integrated workflow where the hardware is **specified, simulated, and verified at the model level** using executable specifications (typically in Simulink, SystemC, or HDL), with **automatic code generation** producing the synthesizable HDL.

**Step 2 — Draw the MBD Flow (use the diagram from Section 4.1 as reference):**

1. **Requirements & System Model** — SysML/UML-MARTE captures functional & non-functional (timing, power) requirements.
2. **Algorithm Design** — Simulink/C++ algorithm-level model validated against requirements.
3. **TLM Modeling** — SystemC TLM 2.0 creates transaction-level models of processors, buses, memories, and IPs.
4. **RTL Implementation** — Hand-written or auto-generated VHDL/Verilog at RTL.
5. **Verification & Synthesis** — Functional verification (UVM, cocotb) and logic synthesis.
6. **Hardware Integration** — FPGA bitstream or ASIC GDSII, validated via HIL.

**Step 3 — Role of SystemC TLM in Co-Simulation:**
- Provides **abstract communication primitives** (`tlm_initiator_socket`, `tlm_target_socket`) that decouple computation from bus-level timing.
- Supports **Loosely-Timed (LT)** and **Approximately-Timed (AT)** modeling, allowing software developers to run firmware on a **virtual platform** (Virtual Prototyping) **months before silicon is available**.
- Enables **early architectural exploration** of cache hierarchies, bus arbitration, and interrupt latency.

**Marks Distribution:** 2 marks for flow + 3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for SystemC TLM role = 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the Clock Period:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &= \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} = 2\ \text{ns} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Maximum Allowable Combinational Delay:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pd,\max} &= T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{su} \\
&= 2 - 0.3 - 0.2 \\
&= 1.5\ \text{ns} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Setup Slack with Actual $t_{pd} = 1.5\ \text{ns}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{slack} &= T_{clk} - (t_{pd,actual} + t_{su}) \\
&= 2 - (1.5 + 0.2) \\
&= 2 - 1.7 \\
&= 0.3\ \text{ns} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Since $t_{slack} = 0.3\ \text{ns} > 0$, the design **meets setup timing with a 0.3 ns positive margin**. The actual $t_{pd} = 1.5\ \text{ns}$ is exactly equal to the maximum allowable, leaving no headroom for environmental variations (PVT — Process, Voltage, Temperature). KTU recommends reporting the design as **functionally correct but tight** in slack.

**Marks Distribution:** 2 marks for clock period + 2 marks for $t_{pd,\max}$ + 2 marks for slack + 1 mark for correct interpretation = 7 marks.

---

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the structure of a **VHDL hardware model** with reference to *Entity*, *Architecture*, *Configuration*, and *Testbench*. Briefly describe how VHDL supports the **hardware abstraction levels** in MBSD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS hardware block has switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$, load capacitance $C_L = 100\ \text{fF}$, supply voltage $V_{dd} = 1.0\ \text{V}$, and clock frequency $f_{clk} = 1\ \text{GHz}$. If the static (leakage) power is $P_{static} = 5\ \mu\text{W}$, compute the **total power dissipation**. Comment on the dominance of dynamic vs. static power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1 — VHDL Model Structure:**

A VHDL model consists of four key code units:

1. **Entity Declaration:** Defines the external interface (ports: `in`, `out`, `inout`, `buffer`) and generics (parameters) of the hardware module.
2. **Architecture Body:** Specifies the internal behavior (`process` blocks) or structure (`component` instantiations) of the entity. Multiple architectures can be associated with one entity.
3. **Configuration Declaration:** Binds a specific architecture to an entity and maps lower-level components to entities, enabling design-space exploration.
4. **Testbench:** A VHDL module with **no ports** that instantiates the Device Under Test (DUT), drives stimuli, and asserts expected outputs.

**Step 2 — Mapping to Hardware Abstraction Levels:**

- **Behavioral Level:** VHDL `process` blocks with no clock sensitivity (combinational behavior) model algorithmic-level hardware.
- **RTL Level:** VHDL `process(clock)` blocks with rising-edge detection model synchronous flip-flops and registers.
- **Structural/Gate Level:** VHDL `component` instantiations of standard cells (AND, OR, MUX) model post-synthesis netlists.
- **System Level:** VHDL packages and libraries can co-simulate with SystemC via VHPI (VHDL Procedural Interface).

**Step 3 — Significance in MBSD:**
VHDL enables **model continuity** — the same source code can be simulated, synthesized, and verified across levels, embodying the MBSD principle of *single-source, multi-target*.

**Marks Distribution:** 3 marks for VHDL structure + 2 marks for abstraction mapping + 2 marks for MBSD significance = 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the Dynamic Power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f_{clk} \\
&= 0.2 \times (100 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.0)^2 \times (1 \times 10^9) \\
&= 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15+9} \\
&= 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{W} \\
&= 20\ \mu\text{W} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Total Power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= P_{dynamic} + P_{static} \\
&= 20\ \mu\text{W} + 5\ \mu\text{W} \\
&= 25\ \mu\text{W} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Dynamic-to-Static Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{P_{dynamic}}{P_{static}} = \frac{20}{5} = 4:1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Dynamic power ($20\ \mu\text{W}$) is **4× higher** than static power ($5\ \mu\text{W}$), making this design **dynamic-power-dominated**. This is typical for actively switching high-performance logic. For ultra-low-power IoT nodes in sleep mode, static power dominates and techniques like **power gating** and **clock gating** are essential.

**Marks Distribution:** 2 marks for dynamic power + 2 marks for total power + 1 mark for ratio + 2 marks for engineering interpretation = 7 marks.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks in Hardware Modeling Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., MHz to Hz, pF to F) — even if the formula is correct, missing unit conversion attracts a 0.5–1 mark penalty.
> 2. **Confusing Setup and Hold Slack** — remember: setup slack uses $T_{clk}$; hold slack uses $t_{cq} + t_{pd,\min}$.
> 3. **Mixing up VHDL/Verilog terminology** — VHDL uses `entity/architecture`; Verilog uses `module/always`. Examiners are strict.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams** — for 14-mark questions, a missing block diagram means a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
> 5. **No interpretation of results** — writing only the number without saying "design meets timing" or "dynamic power dominates" loses the application-level marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hardware in MBSD = executable model**, not a physical artifact.
- [x] **5 Abstraction Levels:** System → Algorithmic → RTL → Gate → Transistor.
- [x] **3 Standard HDLs:** VHDL (IEEE 1076), Verilog (IEEE 1364), SystemVerilog (IEEE 1800).
- [x] **SystemC TLM 2.0 (IEEE 1666)** is the **primary co-simulation bridge** between hardware and software models.
- [x] **Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn):** Three domains × five abstraction levels.
- [x] **Key Timing Formulas:**
  - $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$
  - $t_{pd,\max} = T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{su}$
  - $t_{slack} = T_{clk} - (t_{pd} + t_{su})$
- [x] **Power Formula:** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{dd}^2 f_{clk}$
- [x] **MIL vs. SIL vs. HIL vs. PIL** — know the V-cycle stages of verification.
- [x] **UML-MARTE** is the KTU-favoured notation for hardware-software partitioning.
- [x] **Synthesis is RTL-in, Gate-netlist-out** — RTL is the golden synthesis entry.
- [x] **High-Level Synthesis (HLS):** C/C++/SystemC → RTL (e.g., Xilinx Vitis HLS, Siemens Catapult).
- [x] **FPGA = reprogrammable, ASIC = one-time-fabricated** — both use HDL models as input.
- [x] **Always state timing direction:** Positive slack = pass; Negative slack = fail.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Composite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of UML (Unified Modeling Language) and SysML (Systems Modeling Language) used in **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, the term **Composite** refers to the structural paradigm that describes how a complex entity (a class, component, block, or system) is internally assembled from smaller, collaborating sub-entities.

The two principal artefacts associated with this concept are:

1. **Composite Structure Diagram** – A UML/SysML static structure diagram that depicts the **internal structure** of a classifier by showing its *parts*, *ports*, *connectors*, and *collaborations*.
2. **Internal Structure (Composite Structure)** – The architectural blueprint of a class showing how its runtime instances (objects/parts) are wired together to fulfill behaviour, without exposing inheritance hierarchies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 3 of PECNT634, *Composite Structure Diagrams* are examined under the structural modelling segment. Students must master notation for **Parts**, **Ports**, **Connectors**, **Provided/Required Interfaces**, and **Collaborations**, as these directly appear in 14-mark analytical questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **modern car** as a single high-level object called `Car`. To the outside world, you only press the accelerator and the car moves. But internally, `Car` is composed of:

- An **Engine** (part)
- A **Transmission** (part)
- Four **Wheels** (parts)
- A **FuelInjector** that exposes a port called `fuelPort`

These internal pieces are wired together via **connectors** (e.g., a mechanical shaft connecting the Engine to the Transmission). When you model this using a **Composite Structure Diagram**, you are essentially producing the *engineering blueprint* of the `Car` object — exposing its **guts** and showing how information/energy/material flows between its internal organs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Composite vs. Class Diagram:** A Class Diagram shows the *universe* of classes and their relationships (inheritance, association). A Composite Structure Diagram zooms into **one specific class** and reveals the runtime instances that collaborate to perform its behaviour. In short: **Class Diagram = taxonomy; Composite Diagram = wiring diagram**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics / Notation Constants in Bold

The structural elements used universally in UML 2.5 / SysML 1.x specifications:

- **Part**: An internal slot that holds an instance of a classifier.
- **Port**: An interaction point specifying what is exposed to the outside environment.
- **Connector**: A link (assembly or delegation) between two parts or ports.
- **Role**: The name given to a participant inside a collaboration.
- **Provided Interface**: A contract the part *offers* via its port.
- **Required Interface**: A contract the part *needs* via its port.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Composite Paradigm

The Composite Structure Diagram is built from **five foundational pillars**. Each pillar plays a deterministic role in the overall specification:

1. **Classifier (The Container)** — The outermost dashed frame, e.g., `Car`, `OrderProcessor`, `AircraftController`. This is the entity whose internals we are exposing.
2. **Internal Parts (The Constituents)** — Rectangles drawn *inside* the dashed frame, each labelled with a *role name* and the type (e.g., `engine : Engine`).
3. **Ports (The Interaction Windows)** — Small squares on the boundary of a part or on the external frame. They are of two flavours:
   - **Provided Port (lollipop-style filled square)** — The classifier *delivers* services.
   - **Required Port (semicircle-shaped socket)** — The classifier *consumes* services.
4. **Connectors (The Wiring)** — Solid lines linking ports of one part to ports of another. Two sub-types exist:
   - **Assembly Connector** — Direct peer-to-peer link between two part ports.
   - **Delegation Connector** — Routes an external port of the classifier *through* to an internal part.
5. **Collaborations (The Role Bundles)** — A reusable grouping of roles and connectors that describe a specific pattern of interaction (e.g., a `Broker` collaboration).

### 2.2 Why and How — Operational Logic

- **Why use a Composite Diagram?**  
  When a system is too complex to be represented by a single behavioural element, composite structure allows decomposition at the *instance level* rather than at the *class level*. This is critical for embedded, real-time, and cyber-physical systems.
- **How does it work in MBSD?**  
  In a Model-Driven Architecture (MDA) workflow, the structural model (PIM — Platform Independent Model) generated from a composite diagram can be transformed into code skeletons using tools such as *Enterprise Architect*, *Papyrus*, *MagicDraw*, or *Rhapsody*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Notation Cheat Sheet

| Element | UML Notation | Purpose | Mandatory Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Classifier Frame** | Dashed rectangle with name underlined | Container for internal parts | `name : Type` |
| **Part** | Solid rectangle inside frame | Holds runtime instance | `roleName : Classifier` |
| **Provided Port** | Small filled square on boundary | Exposes a service to environment | Multiplicity, type |
| **Required Port** | Small semicircle on boundary | Consumes a service from environment | Multiplicity, type |
| **Assembly Connector** | Solid line between two ports | Direct inter-part communication | No additional labelling needed |
| **Delegation Connector** | Solid line with arrowhead pointing to internal port | External request forwarded inward | Source = external port, Target = internal part port |
| **Provided Interface** | Lollipop symbol (`○`) | Service contract offered | Name of the interface |
| **Required Interface** | Socket symbol (`⊃`) | Service contract needed | Name of the interface |
| **Collaboration** | Dashed ellipse | Reusable role pattern | Name + connected roles |
| **Role** | Text inside dashed ellipse | A participant slot | Name + type |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Students frequently confuse *Provided* and *Required* ports. Memorise this: **Provided = Push (offer)** → solid square. **Required = Pull (need)** → open semicircle. Marks are routinely deducted for swapped symbols.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Defence:** Modelling an `Aircraft` block with internal subsystems (Avionics, Propulsion, LandingGear) interconnected via deterministic data buses (ARINC 664).
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Composite diagrams map *Software Components* (SWCs) and their *Ports* to *Runnables*, ensuring the ISO 26262 functional safety traceability.
- **IoT & Embedded:** A `SmartSensor` composite may contain a `Microcontroller`, `ADC`, and `WirelessTransceiver` parts linked by I²C/SPI connectors.
- **Enterprise Software:** A `CheckoutService` may internally orchestrate `PaymentGateway`, `InventoryService`, and `NotificationService` parts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Construction Methodology

> Since *Composite Structure Diagrams* are a structural modelling concept rather than a mathematical derivation, this section presents an **exhaustive, step-by-step construction methodology** — the equivalent of a derivation walk-through — together with rigorous annotation rules evaluated by KTU examiners.

### 3.1 The 7-Stage Construction Algorithm

**Stage 1 — Identify the Boundary Classifier**  
Choose the highest-level entity whose internals must be exposed. Example: `OrderProcessingSystem`.

**Stage 2 — Enumerate the Internal Parts**  
Decompose the system into logically separable runtime instances. For each, define:
- A **role name** (e.g., `validator`)
- A **type** (e.g., `OrderValidator`)

**Stage 3 — Define the Port Surface**  
On the outer boundary of the classifier frame, declare all *external* ports with multiplicities. For example:

$$
\text{port}_{\text{external}} : \{ \text{name = "submitOrder", type = "OrderSubmissionPort", multiplicity = 1..* } \}
$$

**Stage 4 — Bind Interfaces to Ports**  
For each port, attach either a **Provided Interface** (`IOrderSubmit`) or a **Required Interface** (`IPaymentGateway`) — never both unless modelling a bi-directional port.

**Stage 5 — Wire Parts using Assembly Connectors**  
For every internal data/control flow, draw a solid line between two part ports. The connector may be labelled with the signal name, e.g., `<<signal>> orderValidated`.

**Stage 6 — Apply Delegation Connectors where Needed**  
If an external request must penetrate to a specific inner part, route it using a delegation connector with the arrowhead pointing *inward* to the inner port.

**Stage 7 — Validate Multiplicities and Dependencies**  
Ensure every required port is satisfied by at least one connector to a matching provided port. Cross-check multiplicity ranges (e.g., a part with `0..3` multiplicity may have zero, one, two, or three runtime instances).

### 3.2 Worked Example — Modelling an `ATM` Classifier

**Step (i): Boundary** — Draw a dashed frame labelled `ATM : AutomatedTellerMachine`.

**Step (ii): Parts**  
Inside the frame, place four solid rectangles:

- `cardReader : CardReader`
- `keypad : Keypad`
- `cashDispenser : CashDispenser`
- `mainController : MainController`

**Step (iii): External Ports** (on the frame boundary)  
- `customerIF : Provided Port (UserInteraction)` — Provided
- `bankLink : Required Port (BankNetworkLink)` — Required

**Step (iv): Interfaces**  
- `customerIF` → Provided Interface `IUserInteraction`
- `bankLink` → Required Interface `IBankService`

**Step (v): Assembly Connectors**  
- `keypad` port `input` → `mainController` port `userInputCmd`
- `mainController` port `dispenseReq` → `cashDispenser` port `trigger`
- `cardReader` port `cardData` → `mainController` port `cardInfo`

**Step (vi): Delegation**  
- `bankLink` (external) → `mainController` internal port `bankChannel`

**Step (vii): Validation**  
Each connector satisfies exactly one Required port with one Provided port. Multiplicities:

$$
\text{cardReader.multiplicity} = 1, \quad \text{keypad.multiplicity} = 1, \quad \text{cashDispenser.multiplicity} = 0..1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Insight:** In a 14-mark question, expect the valuation split as: Boundary & Parts = **4 marks**, Ports & Interfaces = **4 marks**, Connectors = **4 marks**, Validation/Rationale = **2 marks**. Missing multiplicity declarations typically attract a **1-mark penalty**.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Composite vs. Alternatives

| Scenario | Recommended Diagram | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Decomposing one class into runtime parts | **Composite Structure** | Shows internal wiring |
| Showing "is-a" hierarchies | **Class Diagram** | Inheritance semantics |
| Modelling object lifecycles | **Sequence / State Diagram** | Temporal behaviour |
| Showing class-level associations (many-to-many) | **Class Diagram** | Static relations |
| Reusable interaction pattern | **Composite w/ Collaboration** | Role-based reuse |
| Distributed deployment | **Deployment Diagram** | Physical nodes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ATM Composite Structure (Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid block represents the internal architecture of the `ATM` classifier described in §3.2. It maps the interactions between parts, ports, and connectors exactly as a UML Composite Structure Diagram would.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ATM["ATM : AutomatedTellerMachine (Dashed Classifier Frame)"]
        direction TB
        cardReader["cardReader : CardReader"]:::partNode
        keypad["keypad : Keypad"]:::partNode
        cashDisp["cashDispenser : CashDispenser"]:::partNode
        mainCtrl["mainController : MainController"]:::partNode

        cardReader -- "assembly connector: cardData" --> mainCtrl
        keypad -- "assembly connector: userInputCmd" --> mainCtrl
        mainCtrl -- "assembly connector: dispenseReq" --> cashDisp
    end

    extCustomer["External: customerIF (Provided Port, IUserInteraction)"]:::extProvided
    extBank["External: bankLink (Required Port, IBankService)"]:::extRequired

    extCustomer -- "interaction" --> ATM
    extBank -- "delegation connector to mainController.bankChannel" --> mainCtrl

    classDef partNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef extProvided fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef extRequired fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need to model internal wiring?] --> B{Is the focus one classifier?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Class Diagram]
    B -- Yes --> D{Are there runtime instances?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E{Do parts exchange signals?}
    E -- No --> F[Use Component Diagram]
    E -- Yes --> G[Use Composite Structure Diagram]
    G --> H{Is the pattern reusable?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Add Collaboration construct]
    H -- No --> J[Finalise with multiplicities]
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Notation Reference Card

```
+----------------------------------------------------+
|        <<classifier>> Car                          |
| +-----------+          +-----------+               |
| | engine    |==========| gearbox   |               |
| | : Engine  |   shaft  | : Gearbox |               |
| +-----------+          +-----------+               |
|       |                       |                    |
|     (p) fuelIn            (p) torqueOut             |
+----------------------------------------------------+
  ^                                                  ^
  | assembly                                        | delegation
  v                                                  v
FuelTank:Provided         WheelAssembly:Required
```

**Legend:**
- `(p)` = Provided Port
- `(r)` = Required Port  
- `==` = Assembly Connector  
- `-->` = Delegation Connector

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Style]**

**Q1. Define a Composite Structure Diagram. Mention any four elements used in it.**  
**Model Answer:**  
A Composite Structure Diagram is a UML/SysML static structure diagram that depicts the internal structure of a classifier, including its parts, ports, connectors, and collaborations, focusing on how runtime instances collaborate at execution time.  
Four key elements are: (i) **Part**, (ii) **Port** (Provided and Required), (iii) **Connector** (Assembly and Delegation), and (iv) **Collaboration**.  
*(Word-limit: 80–100 words; UML notation symbols are bonus marks.)*

**Q2. Differentiate between an Assembly Connector and a Delegation Connector.**  
**Model Answer:**  
An **Assembly Connector** is a direct link between two peer-level parts or ports within the same classifier, enabling them to communicate directly. A **Delegation Connector**, on the other hand, routes an external port's interaction to an *internal* part of the classifier, indicating that the external request is forwarded inward for handling. In essence, Assembly = **peer-to-peer**; Delegation = **external-to-internal**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECNT634 Module 3 Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the internal structure of a **Composite Structure Diagram** using the classifier `MobilePhone`. Identify at least **three internal parts**, their ports, and the connectors between them. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Collaborations** in Composite Structure Diagrams. How does a Collaboration differ from a standard Class Diagram association? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**  
- Draw a dashed frame labelled `<<device>> MobilePhone`.  
- Internal parts:  
  - `battery : Battery`  
  - `display : TouchScreenDisplay`  
  - `processor : SoC`  
- External ports on frame: `powerInput (Required)`, `userIO (Provided)`.  
- Assembly connectors: `battery.powerOut → processor.powerIn`, `processor.displayData → display.dataIn`.  
- Delegation connector: `powerInput → battery.powerOut`.  
- Provide interfaces `IDisplayService`, require `IChargingService`.

**Model Solution (b):**  
- A **Collaboration** in UML is a reusable behavioural pattern describing a set of cooperating roles and their connectors.  
- It allows the same interaction pattern (e.g., Broker, Pipe-Filter) to be applied across multiple classifiers.  
- **Difference from Class Association:** A Class association is a *static* relationship between types; a Collaboration specifies *role-based, instance-level cooperation* with multiplicities and connectors, making it dynamic and context-specific.  
- A Collaboration can be referenced inside a Composite Structure Diagram via `<<collaboration>>` stereotype on the dashed ellipse.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain **Provided Ports** and **Required Ports** in UML Composite Structure. Illustrate with an example of an `OnlineOrderSystem` classifier having at least two Provided and two Required ports. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to construct a Composite Structure Diagram for an **embedded automotive ECU (Electronic Control Unit)**. Mention the role of SysML in this context. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**  
- **Provided Port** — A small filled square on the classifier boundary indicating services *offered* to the external environment; represented by the lollipop symbol `○` for the interface.  
- **Required Port** — A semicircular socket on the boundary indicating services *needed*; represented by the half-circle `⊃`.  
- Example for `OnlineOrderSystem`:  
  - Provided: `placeOrder : IOrderPlacement`, `trackStatus : IOrderTracking`  
  - Required: `paymentGateway : IPaymentService`, `inventory : IInventoryService`  
- Connect these via assembly connectors to internal parts `orderHandler`, `paymentAdapter`, `inventoryProxy`.

**Model Solution (b):**  
1. **Identify Boundary Classifier** — `ECU`.  
2. **Decompose Internally** — Parts: `microcontroller`, `sensorInterface`, `actuatorDriver`, `CANBusTransceiver`.  
3. **Define Ports** — External Provided: `diagnosticPort`; Required: `powerSupply`, `sensorDataIn`.  
4. **Assign Interfaces** — `IDiagnostics`, `IPower`, `ISensorInput`.  
5. **Wire Connectors** — Assembly: `sensorInterface.dataOut → microcontroller.sensorIn`. Delegation: `sensorDataIn → sensorInterface.dataOut`.  
6. **Validate** — Multiplicities, port satisfiability.  
7. **SysML Role:** SysML extends UML with block-based composite structures, ideal for systems engineering. The SysML **Block Definition Diagram** + **Internal Block Diagram** (the SysML equivalent of Composite Structure) supports MBSE traceability per ISO 26262.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (PECNT634 Module 3):**
> 1. **Confusing Provided vs Required port symbols** — Examiner deduction: **1 mark per swap**.
> 2. **Omitting multiplicity declarations** on parts/ports — Deduction: **1 mark** if not stated.
> 3. **Confusing Class Diagram associations with Composite Connectors** — Class Diagrams have *association*, *aggregation*, *composition*; only the *composition* relation is *part-whole*, but it is **not** the same as a runtime assembly connector.
> 4. **Missing interface binding** — Ports without a linked `Provided`/`Required` interface are considered incomplete and attract **1–2 mark penalty**.
> 5. **Not labelling the dashed frame** with the classifier name — The dashed frame *must* be labelled `<<classifier>>` or with the type name; failure costs **0.5–1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Composite Structure Diagram** = Internal wiring blueprint of one classifier.
- **Five Pillars:** Parts, Ports, Connectors, Interfaces, Collaborations.
- **Provided Port** = filled square (`○` lollipop for interface) — *offers* a service.
- **Required Port** = semicircle socket (`⊃`) — *needs* a service.
- **Assembly Connector** = peer-to-peer between parts.
- **Delegation Connector** = external-to-internal forwarding.
- **Collaboration** = reusable role-based pattern (dashed ellipse).
- **SysML Internal Block Diagram** is the systems-engineering equivalent.
- **MDA Code Generation:** Tools: Enterprise Architect, Papyrus, Rhapsody, Cameo.
- **Multiplicities** must always be declared on parts and ports.
- **Composite ≠ Class Diagram:** Composite = instance-level wiring; Class = type-level relations.
- Always **label the dashed classifier frame** with its name.
- **KTU Favourite Domains:** Automotive ECU, ATM, MobilePhone, OnlineOrderSystem, SmartSensor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runtime Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Runtime Semantics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Runtime Semantics — Module 3, PECNT634

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Runtime Semantics* is the formal, mathematically precise description of the **meaning of a model or program as it executes** — specifying how configurations (states) evolve over time, what observations (outputs/traces) are produced, and how computational steps consume resources. It maps syntactic constructs of the modeling language (states, transitions, actions, events, data flows) onto a **mathematical domain of behavior**, usually expressed as a transition system, an algebra of traces, or a function from inputs to outputs.

In the Model-Based Software Development (MBD) context, runtime semantics answers the central question:
> *"If I execute the model, what sequence of observable behaviors will the underlying system legally produce?"*

### Conceptual Analogy — The Stage Play

Imagine the **model** as a **theatrical script** written in shorthand. The script describes scenes, actors, props, and cues, but it is *static text*. To know what *actually happens* when the curtain rises, we need a **stage director's rulebook** that says:

- *When* the cue "LIGHT → RED" is spoken, the lighting rig must turn on the red lamp.
- *If* the actor enters stage-left *while* the curtain is up, the spotlight follows.
- *After* every scene change, the orchestra plays one chord.

This rulebook is exactly what runtime semantics is for a model. It is the **authoritative interpretation guide** that turns a passive diagram into a deterministic or non-deterministic execution machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in MBD:** Tools like Simulink/Stateflow, SCADE, UPPAAL, and Spin must *agree* on what a state-chart or block-diagram *means*. Without an explicit runtime semantics, two vendors executing the "same" model can produce different behaviors — a critical failure mode in safety-critical domains (DO-178C, ISO 26262).

### Two Pillars of Runtime Semantics

| Pillar | Focus | Typical Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Operational Semantics** | How the model *steps* from one configuration to the next | Transition rules `→` over configurations |
| **Denotational Semantics** | What the model *means as a mathematical object* | Functions `⟦ · ⟧ : Model → Domain` |

We also use **Axiomatic Semantics** to prove correctness properties, but the runtime behavior is dominated by the first two.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Semantic Triangle in MBD

A model is **syntactically** a graph (nodes + edges + attributes). Semantics assigns it meaning by mapping it onto:

$$\text{Syntax} \;\;\xrightarrow{\;\llbracket\,\cdot\,\rrbracket\;}\;\; \text{Semantic Domain} \;\;\xrightarrow{\;\text{observe}\;}\;\; \text{Behavior}$$

There are **three classical styles** of describing this mapping. They are *not competing* — they coexist and answer different questions.

### 2.2 Style A — Operational Semantics (Step-by-Step Behavior)

Operational semantics defines an **abstract machine** and a set of inference rules that govern state transitions. It is the most direct way to answer *"what happens next?"*

- **Small-step (Structural Operational Semantics — SOS, Plotkin 1981):** Defines a single transition `⟨c, σ⟩ → ⟨c', σ'⟩`.
- **Big-step (Natural Semantics, Kahn 1987):** Defines an evaluation judgement `⟨c, σ⟩ ⇓ v` reaching a final value in one rule.

### 2.3 Style B — Denotational Semantics (Meaning as a Function)

Each syntactic phrase is mapped to a **mathematical object** (function, set, domain element) such that *equivalent phrases have identical denotations*. The denotation of a model is typically:

$$\llbracket M \rrbracket \;=\; \text{Set of all execution traces produced by } M$$

### 2.4 Style C — Axiomatic Semantics (Logical Reasoning)

Defines a **logic** in which one can *prove* properties of execution. Uses pre/post-conditions (Hoare triples):

$$\{P\}\;\;c\;\;\{Q\}$$

This is mostly used for verification, not execution, but it is part of the complete semantic picture.

## 2.5 The Configuration / State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **configuration** `γ` is the *complete instantaneous description* of an executing model. For a discrete-time model it is a tuple:
> $$\gamma = \langle S, V, t \rangle$$
> where `S` = control state (active block / mode), `V` = variable store (a total function `Var → Val`), and `t` = logical time.

## 2.6 Transitions and Traces

- A **transition** is a binary relation `→ ⊆ Γ × Γ` between configurations.
- A **trace** is a (possibly infinite) sequence `γ₀ → γ₁ → γ₂ → …`
- The **semantics of the model** is the set of all traces starting from initial configurations `γ₀ ∈ Γ₀`.

$$\llbracket M \rrbracket \;=\; \{\, \pi \in \Gamma^{\omega \cup *} \mid \pi_0 \in \Gamma_0 \;\wedge\; \forall i.\; \pi_i \rightarrow \pi_{i+1} \,\}$$

## 2.7 Labeled Transition Systems (LTS) — The Canonical Semantic Domain

An LTS is the **standard mathematical carrier** for runtime semantics:

$$\text{LTS} \;=\; (Q,\; \Sigma,\; \rightarrow,\; q_0)$$

- `Q` — non-empty set of states (configurations)
- `Σ` — alphabet of observable actions / events
- `→ ⊆ Q × Σ × Q` — labeled transition relation
- `q₀ ∈ Q` — initial state

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** LTSs are the backbone of **model checking** (SPIN, NuSMV), **process algebras** (CSP, CCS, π-calculus), and **runtime verification** tools that monitor traces against an LTS specification.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the symbols and structures in this table — they appear verbatim in KTU ESE questions.

| Symbol / Structure | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| `⟨c, σ⟩` | Configuration: command `c`, store `σ` | Small-step SOS |
| `→` | One-step reduction | Operational semantics |
| `⇒` | Multi-step (reflexive-transitive closure) | Big-step / traces |
| `⇓` | Termination to value | Natural semantics |
| `⟦M⟧` | Denotation of model `M` | Denotational |
| `{P} c {Q}` | Hoare triple | Axiomatic |
| `(Q, Σ, →, q₀)` | Labeled Transition System | All three styles |
| `Γ` | Set of all configurations | SOS |
| `Γ₀` | Set of initial configurations | SOS |
| `F` | Fix-point operator `F = λf. f` | Denotational |
| `L(M)` | Language of `M` (set of traces) | LTS / automata |
| `≡` | Semantic equivalence | All |
| `⊑` | Refinement ordering | LTS / CSP |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Runtime Semantics Matters |
|---|---|
| **Avionics (DO-178C)** | Exact, reproducible execution across heterogeneous hardware |
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR, ISO 26262)** | Predictable scheduling & timing semantics for safety functions |
| **IoT / Embedded** | Bounded memory & energy; semantics must bound resource use |
| **Cyber-Physical Systems** | Continuous-time semantics (ODEs) couples with discrete controller semantics |
| **Model Checking** | LTS + temporal logic = automated verification |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Semantics of a Simple While-Language (Small-Step SOS)

Consider the minimal imperative language with arithmetic expressions `a`, boolean expressions `b`, and statements `c`:

$$
c \;\in\; \text{Stmt} \;::=\; \text{skip} \;\mid\; x := a \;\mid\; c_1; c_2 \;\mid\; \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2 \;\mid\; \text{while } b \text{ do } c
$$

### Step 1 — Define the Semantic Domain

We need a notion of *store* (memory) and *value*:

$$
\text{Val} \;=\; \mathbb{Z}, \qquad \sigma \;:\; \text{Var} \rightarrow \text{Val}
$$

### Step 2 — Helper Judgement for Expressions

Expression evaluation is **pure** (no side effects) and is defined as a *big-step* judgement:

$$
\langle a,\; \sigma \rangle \;\Downarrow\; n \;\;\;\; \text{(meaning: under store } \sigma \text{, expression } a \text{ yields value } n \text{)}
$$

### Step 3 — Small-Step Rules for Statements

The inference rules for one-step reduction are written in the *SOS* notation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(Skip)}   && \langle \text{skip},\; \sigma \rangle \;\rightarrow\; \langle \text{skip},\; \sigma \rangle \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(Assign)} && \langle x := a,\; \sigma \rangle \;\rightarrow\; \langle \text{skip},\; \sigma[x \mapsto n] \rangle
\quad \text{where } \langle a, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow n \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(Seq-1)}  && \frac{\langle c_1, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1', \sigma' \rangle}{\langle c_1; c_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1'; c_2, \sigma' \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(Seq-2)}  && \frac{\langle c_1, \sigma \rangle \nrightarrow}{\langle c_1; c_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_2, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(If-T)}   && \frac{\langle b, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \text{true}}{\langle \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2,\; \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(If-F)}   && \frac{\langle b, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \text{false}}{\langle \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2,\; \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_2, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(While)}  && \langle \text{while } b \text{ do } c,\; \sigma \rangle \;\rightarrow\;
                     \langle \text{if } b \text{ then } c; \text{while } b \text{ do } c \text{ else skip},\; \sigma \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:** Each rule is a *Horn clause* — if the premises above the bar hold, then the conclusion below holds. `→` is one *atomic* execution step. The full execution of a program is a chain of such steps chained by transitivity.

### Step 4 — Multi-Step Reduction (→*)

Multi-step reduction is defined as the *reflexive-transitive closure* of single-step reduction:

$$
\frac{}{\langle c, \sigma \rangle \Rightarrow \langle c, \sigma \rangle} \quad\quad
\frac{\langle c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c', \sigma' \rangle \quad \langle c', \sigma' \rangle \Rightarrow \langle c'', \sigma'' \rangle}{\langle c, \sigma \rangle \Rightarrow \langle c'', \sigma'' \rangle}
$$

### Step 5 — Termination (Big-Step from Small-Step)

We say a program *terminates* with final store `σ_f` when it reduces to `skip`:

$$
\langle c, \sigma_0 \rangle \Downarrow \sigma_f \;\;\iff\;\; \langle c, \sigma_0 \rangle \;\rightarrow^{*}\; \langle \text{skip},\; \sigma_f \rangle
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Trace Derivation

Let `c = (x := 1; y := x + 2)`, with initial store `σ₀ = {x↦0, y↦0}`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle x := 1; y := x + 2,\; \sigma_0 \rangle
&\;\xrightarrow{\text{(Assign)}}\; \langle \text{skip};\ y := x + 2,\; \sigma_0[x\mapsto 1] \rangle \\[4pt]
&\;\xrightarrow{\text{(Seq-2)}}\; \langle y := x + 2,\; \sigma_1 \rangle \quad \text{where } \sigma_1 = \{x\mapsto 1, y\mapsto 0\} \\[4pt]
&\;\xrightarrow{\text{(Assign)}}\; \langle \text{skip},\; \sigma_1[y \mapsto 3] \rangle \quad \text{where } \langle x+2, \sigma_1 \rangle \Downarrow 3 \\[4pt]
&\;\xrightarrow{\text{(Skip)}}\; \langle \text{skip},\; \sigma_2 \rangle \quad \text{where } \sigma_2 = \{x\mapsto 1, y\mapsto 3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **trace** is therefore:

$$
\pi \;=\; \gamma_0 \rightarrow \gamma_1 \rightarrow \gamma_2 \rightarrow \gamma_3
$$

and this trace is an element of `⟦c⟧`, the denotational set of traces.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Three Styles

| Property | Operational | Denotational | Axiomatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defines behavior?** | Yes (step-wise) | Yes (as set of traces) | Indirectly |
| **Executable?** | Yes — interpreter follows rules | Sometimes via fix-point iteration | No |
| **Compositional?** | Mostly | **Yes** (by definition) | Yes |
| **Reveals *how*?** | **Yes** | No — abstracts away | No |
| **Reveals *what*?** | Yes | **Yes** | Yes (logical) |
| **Suited for verification?** | Limited | Medium | **Best** |
| **Tool support** | Interpreters, debugger, abstract machines | Functional compilers, Haskell | Dafny, Frama-C, KeY |

## 3.4 Mapping to UML State Machines — The MBD View

For an MBD tool, the model is a UML / SysML state machine with states `S`, transitions `δ`, events `E`, guards `g`, actions `α`. The runtime semantics is given by:

$$
\text{Config} \;=\; \langle \text{activeState}(s),\; \sigma,\; \text{eventQueue} \rangle
$$

$$
\text{Step} \;:\; \text{Config} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Config}
$$

The **Run-to-Completion (RTC)** step semantics, used by UML, says:

> When a model receives a dispatched event, it fires *one* enabled transition at a time until no transition is enabled, then drains the next event from the queue.

Formally, given a configuration `γ = ⟨s, σ, q⟩` and event `e`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(RTC-Step)} \quad
\frac{ s \;\xrightarrow{e,\, g,\; \alpha}\; s' \quad \langle g, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \text{true} \quad \langle \alpha, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma' }
     { \langle s,\; \sigma,\; e : q \rangle \;\rightarrow\; \langle s',\; \sigma',\; q \rangle } \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(RTC-Drain)} \quad
\frac{ \nexists\, s \xrightarrow{e,\, g,\, \alpha} s' \text{ enabled} }
     { \langle s,\; \sigma,\; e : q \rangle \;\rightarrow\; \langle s,\; \sigma,\; q \rangle } \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(Pop)} \quad
\frac{ \text{no transition fires on } e }
     { \langle s,\; \sigma,\; e : q \rangle \;\rightarrow\; \langle s,\; \sigma,\; q \rangle }
\end{aligned}
$$

This **deterministic-event-queue** semantics is what every UML-compliant tool (e.g., IBM Rhapsody, Papyrus) implements.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Runtime Semantics — Conceptual Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Model Syntax] --> B[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    B --> C{Semantics Engine}
    C --> D1[Operational Semantics]
    C --> D2[Denotational Semantics]
    C --> D3[Axiomatic Semantics]
    D1 --> E1[Transition System / LTS]
    D2 --> E2[Set of Traces / Functions]
    D3 --> E3[Logical Proofs]
    E1 --> F[Executable Runtime]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> G[Verification Tool]
    F --> H[Observable Behavior]
    G --> H
```

## 4.2 Small-Step SOS Evaluation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cfg0["Config gamma0"] -->|Apply Rule R1| cfg1["Config gamma1"]
    cfg1 -->|Apply Rule R2| cfg2["Config gamma2"]
    cfg2 -->|Apply Rule R3| cfg3["Config gamma3"]
    cfg3 -->|Apply Rule Rn| cfgN["Terminal skip sigma_f"]
    cfg0 -. initial .-> ctx["Initial Store sigma0"]
    ctx -. drives .-> cfg0
```

## 4.3 Runtime Verification Monitoring Loop (MBD)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Model Under Execution] -->|Emits Event e| Bus[Event Bus]
    Bus --> Sys[System Trace Tr]
    Sys --> Mon[Monitor over LTS Spec]
    Mon -->|Match| OK[Verdict VERDICT_OK]
    Mon -->|Mismatch| Viol[Verdict VERDICT_VIOLATION]
    Mon -->|Inconclusive| Pend[Verdict VERDICT_PENDING]
```

## 4.4 Mapping from Model Layers to Semantic Domains

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1["Model Layer"]
        L1a[UML State Machine]
        L1b[Simulink Block Diagram]
        L1c[SysML Activity]
    end
    subgraph Layer2["Intermediate Representation"]
        L2[Abstract Syntax Graph]
    end
    subgraph Layer3["Semantic Domain"]
        L3a[Labeled Transition System]
        L3b[Kripke Structure]
        L3c[Timed Automaton]
    end
    Layer1 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Layer3
```

## 4.5 Operational Semantics Rule Engine (Internal View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Current Config gamma] --> Lookup[Rule Lookup Table]
    Lookup -->|First Match| Rule[Selected Inference Rule]
    Rule --> Pre[Check Premises]
    Pre -->|All True| Apply[Apply Conclusion]
    Pre -->|Some False| Lookup
    Apply --> Out[Next Config gamma prime]
    Out --> In
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between operational semantics and denotational semantics. Which is more suitable for proving compiler correctness and why?**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
Operational semantics defines meaning through an abstract machine and step-by-step reduction rules `⟨c, σ⟩ → ⟨c', σ'⟩`, revealing *how* a program executes. Denotational semantics maps each syntactic phrase to a mathematical object `⟦c⟧` in a semantic domain such that equivalent phrases have identical denotations. **Denotational semantics is preferred for compiler correctness** because its *compositionality* (⟦c₁; c₂⟧ = ⟦c₁⟧ ∘ ⟦c₂⟧) allows a mathematical proof that *source* denotations equal *target* (compiled) denotations for all programs.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a Labeled Transition System (LTS)? Write its formal definition and explain its role in defining runtime semantics.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
A Labeled Transition System is a 4-tuple `(Q, Σ, →, q₀)` where `Q` is a set of states, `Σ` an alphabet of labels (actions/events), `→ ⊆ Q × Σ × Q` the transition relation, and `q₀ ∈ Q` the initial state. It is the **canonical semantic domain** for runtime semantics because every model — process algebra terms, UML state machines, timed automata — can be compiled to an LTS whose traces are precisely the legal execution behaviours of the model.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and offers an *internal choice* between two alternatives. Both alternatives must be attempted-ready. We provide two fully independent 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **small-step operational semantics** of the following constructs of a simple while-language: assignment `x := a`, sequential composition `c₁; c₂`, and the `if-then-else` statement. Use inference-rule notation and clearly state the configuration form.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the rules you defined in (a), derive the complete execution trace of the program

```
x := 5;
if x > 3 then y := x * 2 else y := 0
```

starting from the initial store `σ₀ = {x ↦ 0, y ↦ 0}`. Show each inference step and identify the final configuration.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Configuration form and rules:**

The configuration is a pair `⟨c, σ⟩` where `c` is the statement yet to be evaluated and `σ : Var → Val` is the current store. Auxiliary judgement `⟨a, σ⟩ ⇓ n` evaluates arithmetic expressions under store `σ`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(Assign)}    && \frac{\langle a, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow n}{\langle x := a,\; \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \text{skip},\; \sigma[x \mapsto n] \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(Seq)}       && \frac{\langle c_1, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1', \sigma' \rangle}{\langle c_1; c_2,\; \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1'; c_2,\; \sigma' \rangle} \quad
                        \frac{\langle c_1, \sigma \rangle \nrightarrow}{\langle c_1; c_2,\; \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_2,\; \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(If-T)}      && \frac{\langle b, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \text{true}}{\langle \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2,\; \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(If-F)}      && \frac{\langle b, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \text{false}}{\langle \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2,\; \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_2, \sigma \rangle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Trace derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_0 &= \langle x := 5;\ \text{if } x>3 \text{ then } y := x*2 \text{ else } y := 0,\ \sigma_0 \rangle \\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\textbf{(Assign)}} \gamma_1 = \langle \text{skip};\ \text{if } x>3 \text{ then } y := x*2 \text{ else } y := 0,\ \sigma_1 \rangle
\quad \sigma_1 = \{x \mapsto 5,\ y \mapsto 0\} \\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\textbf{(Seq-2)}} \gamma_2 = \langle \text{if } x>3 \text{ then } y := x*2 \text{ else } y := 0,\ \sigma_1 \rangle \\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\textbf{(If-T)}} \gamma_3 = \langle y := x*2,\ \sigma_1 \rangle \quad \text{(since } \langle x>3,\sigma_1 \rangle \Downarrow \text{true)} \\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\textbf{(Assign)}} \gamma_4 = \langle \text{skip},\ \sigma_2 \rangle \quad \sigma_2 = \{x \mapsto 5,\ y \mapsto 10\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final configuration:** `⟨skip, {x ↦ 5, y ↦ 10}⟩`. The complete trace is `γ₀ → γ₁ → γ₂ → γ₃ → γ₄`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Students often forget to write the **premise** (the side condition `⟨a, σ⟩ ⇓ n`) above the bar. Without it, the Assign rule is incomplete and you lose **2 marks**. Always include both premises and the conclusion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Many students skip writing the *updated store* after each assignment. The store must be shown explicitly after every Assign rule, otherwise the answer is considered incomplete and loses **1–2 marks** in the final step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **denotational semantics** approach. Define a semantic domain and give the denotational clauses for: integer constant `n`, addition `a₁ + a₂`, variable `x`, assignment `x := a`, and sequential composition `c₁; c₂`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to **runtime verification**, describe how an LTS-based monitor checks the runtime behavior of a deployed system. Use the formula `M(Tr) = Verdict ∈ {OK, Violation, Pending}` in your answer, and illustrate with one example where a property `"never two consecutive errors"` is monitored.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Denotational semantics clauses:**

The semantic domain is `Val = ℤ ⊥` (integers plus a `⊥` element for non-termination). The denotation function maps each syntactic phrase to an element of the semantic domain:

$$
\llbracket \cdot \rrbracket : \text{Exp} \rightarrow (\text{Store} \rightarrow \text{Val}), \qquad
\llbracket \cdot \rrbracket : \text{Stmt} \rightarrow (\text{Store} \rightarrow \text{Store})
$$

Clauses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\llbracket n \rrbracket \;\sigma   && = && n \\[2pt]
&\llbracket a_1 + a_2 \rrbracket \;\sigma && = && \llbracket a_1 \rrbracket \sigma + \llbracket a_2 \rrbracket \sigma \\[2pt]
&\llbracket x \rrbracket \;\sigma  && = && \sigma(x) \\[2pt]
&\llbracket x := a \rrbracket \;\sigma && = && \sigma[x \mapsto \llbracket a \rrbracket \sigma] \\[2pt]
&\llbracket c_1; c_2 \rrbracket \;\sigma && = && \llbracket c_2 \rrbracket (\llbracket c_1 \rrbracket \sigma)
\end{aligned}
$$

The `;` clause is **function composition** — the bedrock of compositionality.

**Part (b) — LTS-based runtime monitor:**

A runtime monitor `M` is a function from the accumulated trace `Tr = e₁ e₂ … e_n` produced by the running system to a verdict:

$$
M : \Sigma^{*} \;\rightarrow\; \{\,\text{OK},\ \text{Violation},\ \text{Pending}\,\}
$$

The monitor is itself synthesised from an LTS `(Q, Σ, δ, q₀)` that encodes the legal behaviour:

- The monitor maintains a **current state** `q ∈ Q`.
- For each new event `e_i`, it updates `q := δ(q, e_i)`; if `δ(q, e_i)` is undefined, it immediately emits **Violation**.
- After consuming all events so far, if the trace cannot yet be judged (because more events are needed), it returns **Pending**.

**Example — "never two consecutive errors"** over alphabet `Σ = {OK, Err}`:

LTS specification has two states: `q₀ = Good` (no pending error) and `q₁ = Bad` (previous event was `Err`). Transitions:

$$
\delta(q_0,\ \text{OK}) = q_0, \quad \delta(q_0,\ \text{Err}) = q_1, \quad \delta(q_1,\ \text{OK}) = q_0, \quad \delta(q_1,\ \text{Err}) = \text{undefined}
$$

Trace `OK, OK, Err, OK, Err, Err`:

- `OK` → `q₀` → `q₀` (OK)
- `Err` → `q₀ → q₁` (OK)
- `OK` → `q₁ → q₀` (OK)
- `Err` → `q₀ → q₁` (OK)
- `Err` → `q₁ → undefined` ⟹ **Violation** at position 6.

So `M(OK,OK,Err,OK,Err,Err) = Violation`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Students often write the semantic clauses *without specifying the semantic domain* (here `Val = ℤ ⊥`). Without the domain, the clauses are meaningless and you lose **1 mark**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** A common error is to treat the monitor as a **finite-state acceptor** that only emits OK/Violation at the *end* of the trace. In reality, the monitor reports `Violation` *immediately* upon the first undefined transition — this is the very point of runtime verification. Failing to mention this loses **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Runtime Semantics**

- **Three styles:** Operational (step-by-step), Denotational (function-to-domain), Axiomatic (Hoare logic). They answer *how*, *what*, and *prove*, respectively.
- **Small-step SOS** uses `→`; **big-step / natural** uses `⇓`. Multi-step is `→*` (Kleene closure).
- **Configuration** is the *complete instantaneous state* of an executing model: `⟨c, σ⟩` or `⟨S, V, t⟩` in MBD.
- **LTS** `(Q, Σ, →, q₀)` is the canonical semantic domain; traces are sequences of labeled transitions.
- **Denotational semantics** is *compositional* — `⟦c₁; c₂⟧ = ⟦c₂⟧ ∘ ⟦c₁⟧`. This is why it scales to compilers.
- **UML Run-to-Completion (RTC)** step: one event → fire one transition → drain → repeat.
- **Runtime verification** = monitor observes trace, compares against LTS/LTL spec, emits `OK / Violation / Pending`.
- **Always state** the configuration form, the store, and the auxiliary expression judgement when writing operational rules.
- **Trace derivation** is a *chain of inference steps*; show the rule name and the *updated* store at every step.
- **Compose ≠ Compose:** Sequential composition is function composition in denotational semantics but ordered stepping in operational semantics — do not mix them up.
- **Real-world importance:** Safety-critical (DO-178C, ISO 26262), model checking (SPIN, NuSMV, UPPAAL), runtime monitoring tools (JavaMOP, RV-Monitor, Copilot).
- **Examiner favourites:** (i) difference between operational and denotational, (ii) write SOS rules, (iii) derive trace, (iv) denotational clauses, (v) LTS-based monitoring example.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Language Syntax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Language Syntax # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Language Syntax (KTU 2024 Terminology) In **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, *Language Syntax* refers to the formal set of rules, conventions, and structural constructs that precisely define how symbols, tokens, keywords, and graphical elements may be legally combined to form a well-formed model within a modeling language (such as UML, SysML, or a Domain-Specific Language).
