In formal language theory terms, the syntax of a modeling language $L$ is defined as a tuple: $$ L = (G, \Sigma, N, P, S) $$ where: - $\Sigma$ = the alphabet (terminal symbols / concrete tokens) - $N$ = the set of non-terminal symbols (abstract constructs) - $P$ = the set of production rules - $S \in N$ = the start symbol - $G$ = the grammar governing derivations > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Definition:** Language syntax answers the question *"What does a legal model LOOK like?"* — It is concerned **only with form and structure**, never with meaning (semantics) or truth. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a modeling language syntax exactly like **the grammar rules of the English language**: - Just as English grammar tells you that the phrase *"The cat sat on the mat"* is grammatically correct but *"Sat cat the mat on"* is wrong — **language syntax** tells the modeler that drawing a UML class with **two compartments** (name + attributes) is legal, but a class with **a class nested inside an operation** is illegal. - The **alphabet** $\Sigma$ is your **bag of letters** (A, B, C, ..., and UML symbols like `\ll`, `\lhd`, arrowheads). - The **production rules** are your **sentence formation rules** (Subject → Noun Phrase, Verb Phrase, etc.). - A **well-formed model** is any string/graph that can be **derived from the start symbol** by applying production rules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 3, you must master TWO distinct syntactic views: **Abstract Syntax** (what the model *means structurally*) and **Concrete Syntax** (what the model *looks like on paper/screen*). Examiners frequently ask the difference. ## 1.3 The Two Syntactic Dimensions | Dimension | Formal Term | What It Describes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Abstract Syntax** | Structural / Logical form | The underlying tree/graph skeleton of valid model elements | | **Concrete Syntax** | Notational / Physical form | The visible notation (textual symbols or graphical shapes) | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Exams:** *Abstract = "Behind the scenes" (logic tree), Concrete = "What you see" (shapes/text).* --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Abstract Syntax — Deep Dive The **Abstract Syntax** of a modeling language defines: 1. The vocabulary of model elements (metaclasses). 2. The legal relationships between those elements (meta-associations). 3. Cardinality / multiplicity constraints. 4. Well-formedness rules (invariants). It is represented formally as a **meta-model** (typically conforming to the MOF — Meta-Object Facility — four-level architecture). ### Why Abstract Syntax Exists - Enables **automated tool processing** (no need to parse pictures!). - Forms the foundation for **model transformations** (QVT, ATL). - Provides the **mathematical rigor** required for formal verification. ## 2.2 Concrete Syntax — Deep Dive The **Concrete Syntax** defines the *visible* representation. It is of three primary kinds: ### 2.2.1 Textual Concrete Syntax - ASCII / Unicode based. - Example: `class Customer { String name; void save(); }` - Uses **BNF / EBNF** grammars. ### 2.2.2 Graphical Concrete Syntax - Uses visual symbols (boxes, arrows, stick figures). - Example: UML class diagrams, state machines. - Uses **graph grammars** (e.g., NCE — Node-Controlled Edge replacement). ### 2.2.3 Mixed / Hybrid Syntax - Combines both. E.g., a graphical frame with embedded textual labels. ## 2.3 The Four-Level Meta-Modeling Architecture (MOF) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **MOST FREQUENTLY ASKED** KTU Module 3 question. Master it. $$ \text{M3} \rightarrow \text{M2} \rightarrow \text{M1} \rightarrow \text{M0} $$ | Level | Name | What It Contains | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **M0** | User Object / Data | The actual runtime model instances | The customer *"Alice"* with age 30 | | **M1** | User Model | Models describing a specific domain | A UML class diagram of a banking system | | **M2** | Meta-Model | The definition of the modeling language itself | The UML metamodel (Class, Attribute, Operation) | | **M3** | Meta-Meta-Model | The architecture defining all metamodels | MOF (Meta-Object Facility) — self-describing | **Strict InstanceOf Relationship:** Each level is an *instance* of the level above it. Formally: $$ \forall \, x \in M_i : \exists \, y \in M_{i+1} \; \text{such that} \; x \;\texttt{instanceOf}\; y $$ ## 2.4 Grammar Formalisms ### 2.4.1 BNF (Backus-Naur Form) Production rule form: $$ \langle \text{NonTerminal} \rangle \; ::= \; \text{expression} $$ ### 2.4.2 EBNF (Extended BNF) Extensions - `[]` for optional parts. - `{}` for repetition (zero or more times). - `()` for grouping. ### 2.4.3 Graph Grammar (for graphical syntax) A graph grammar $GG = (N_T, N_N, P, S)$ where $P$ contains productions that rewrite graph fragments. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Notation Sheet | Concept | Formal Expression | Constraint / Range | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Language definition | $L = (G, \Sigma, N, P, S)$ | All elements finite | | String membership | $s \in L(G)$ iff $S \Rightarrow^{*} s$ | Derivable in zero or more steps | | Concatenation closure | $\Sigma^{*} = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} \Sigma^{i}$ | $\Sigma^{0} = \{\varepsilon\}$ | | Kleene closure | $L^{*} = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} L^{i}$ | Includes empty language | | Positive closure | $L^{+} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} L^{i}$ | Excludes $\varepsilon$ | | Level instance relation | $M_{i} \; \text{instanceOf} \; M_{i+1}$ | For $i \in \{0,1,2\}$ | | Parse tree derivation | $S \Rightarrow \alpha_{1} \Rightarrow \alpha_{2} \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow w$ | Each step $= 1$ production | | Multiplicity bounds | $0..\ast \; \vert \; 1..\ast \; \vert \; 0..1 \; \vert \; 1..1$ | UML standard multiplicities | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MBSE in Aerospace:** Systems Modeling Language (SysML) syntax enables traceable requirements from M3 (language definition) down to M0 (a specific aircraft model). - **AUTOSAR in Automotive:** Concrete textual syntax `ARXML` is parsed into an abstract syntax tree for code generation in ECU software. - **Industry 4.0:** OPC UA information models rely on strictly defined abstract syntaxes for interoperability across vendors. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Deriving a UML Class from a BNF Grammar ### Step 0 — Define the Grammar $G$ $$ \begin{aligned} \langle \text{ClassDecl} \rangle &\; ::= \; \texttt{class} \; \langle \text{Identifier} \rangle \; \{ \; \langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle \; \} \\ \langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle &\; ::= \; \varepsilon \; \vert \; \langle \text{Attribute} \rangle \; \langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle \\ \langle \text{Attribute} \rangle &\; ::= \; \langle \text{Type} \rangle \; \langle \text{Identifier} \rangle \; ; \\ \langle \text{Type} \rangle &\; ::= \; \texttt{int} \; \vert \; \texttt{String} \; \vert \; \texttt{float} \\ \langle \text{Identifier} \rangle &\; ::= \; \langle \text{Letter} \rangle \; \{ \langle \text{Letter} \rangle \; \vert \; \langle \text{Digit} \rangle \} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 1 — Choose the Target String $w$ We want to derive: $$ w = \texttt{class Account \{ String name ; int id ; \}} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Production Rules Step-by-Step **Step 2.1** — Start symbol expansion: $$ \langle \text{ClassDecl} \rangle \Rightarrow \texttt{class} \; \langle \text{Identifier} \rangle \; \{ \; \langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle \; \} $$ *Logic:* The start symbol `ClassDecl` is rewritten using Rule 1. We chose `class` keyword as the first terminal. **Step 2.2** — Substitute $\langle \text{Identifier} \rangle$ with `Account`: $$ \Rightarrow \texttt{class Account \{ } \; \langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle \; \texttt{\}} $$ *Logic:* `Account` is a valid Identifier: starts with Letter `A`, followed by lowercase letters — matches the identifier rule. **Step 2.3** — Expand $\langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle$ non-recursively with one attribute: $$ \Rightarrow \texttt{class Account \{ } \; \langle \text{Attribute} \rangle \; \langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle \; \texttt{\}} $$ *Logic:* We apply Rule 2 (the recursive branch) to introduce the first attribute. **Step 2.4** — Derive the first attribute (`String name ;`): $$ \Rightarrow \texttt{class Account \{ String name ; } \; \langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle \; \texttt{\}} $$ *Logic:* Rule 3 applied: `String` matches $\langle \text{Type} \rangle$, `name` matches $\langle \text{Identifier} \rangle$, `;` is the literal terminator. **Step 2.5** — Recursively expand $\langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle$ for the second attribute: $$ \Rightarrow \texttt{class Account \{ String name ; int id ; } \; \langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle \; \texttt{\}} $$ *Logic:* Another application of Rule 2, then Rule 3 with `int` as Type and `id` as Identifier. **Step 2.6** — Terminate with $\varepsilon$ (empty production): $$ \Rightarrow \texttt{class Account \{ String name ; int id ; \}} $$ *Logic:* The final $\langle \text{ClassBody} \rangle$ is replaced by $\varepsilon$ (Rule 2, first alternative), closing the body. ### Step 3 — Final Derivation Chain $$ \begin{aligned} \langle \text{ClassDecl} \rangle &\Rightarrow^{*} \texttt{class Account \{ String name ; int id ; \}} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The string $w$ is in the language, i.e., $w \in L(G)$. The model is **syntactically valid**. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Criterion | Abstract Syntax | Concrete Syntax (Textual) | Concrete Syntax (Graphical) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Notation** | Tree / Graph | Linear string | 2D diagram | | **Formally Defined By** | Meta-model (MOF) | EBNF grammar | Graph grammar | | **Machine Readable?** | Yes (direct) | Yes (after parsing) | Difficult (needs OCR/CV) | | **Human Readable?** | No | Yes | Yes | | **Tool Support** | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate | | **Example** | AST of a class | `class A {}` | □ A drawn with pen | | **KTU Exam Weight** | Very High | High | Moderate | ## 3.3 Analytical Proof: MOF Self-Description Property > [!NOTE] > **Claim:** The M3 level is self-describing — the meta-metamodel describes itself. **Proof Sketch:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } \mathcal{M}_3 &= \text{MOF} \\ \text{By definition, an instance of } \mathcal{M}_3 &\text{ is a metamodel } \mathcal{M}_2. \\ \text{An instance of } \mathcal{M}_2 &\text{ is a model } \mathcal{M}_1. \\ \text{An instance of } \mathcal{M}_1 &\text{ is data } \mathcal{M}_0. \\ \text{Since MOF itself conforms to its own structure,} \; \mathcal{M}_3 &\;\texttt{instanceOf}\; \mathcal{M}_3 \end{aligned} $$ This closure property is unique to M3 and enables **interoperability** between all MOF-conformant languages (UML, SysML, BPMN). $\blacksquare$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Four-Level MOF Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB L3["M3 MOF META META MODEL
Defines the rules for all metamodels
Example MetaClass MetaAttribute"] L2["M2 META MODEL
UML Metamodel
Class Attribute Operation"] L1["M1 USER MODEL
Domain specific UML diagram
Customer Account Order classes"] L0["M0 USER OBJECTS
Runtime instances
alice Customer id equals 101"] L3 -- instanceOf --> L2 L2 -- instanceOf --> L1 L1 -- instanceOf --> L0 noteA["Strict hierarchy: each level is
an instance of the level above"] L3 -.- noteA ``` ## 4.2 Abstract vs Concrete Syntax Mapping Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Concrete Syntax
Textual or graphical symbols"] --> B["Parser or Recognizer"] B --> C["Abstract Syntax
Tree AST or Meta model"] C --> D["Semantics
Meaning and behavior"] C --> E["Model Transformation
QVT ATL"] C --> F["Code Generation
Target language output"] styleA[fill:E6F2FF,stroke:003366,stroke-width:2px] style B fill:FFF2CC,stroke:996600,stroke-width:2px style C fill:D5E8D4,stroke:006600,stroke-width:2px style D fill:F8CECC,stroke:990000,stroke-width:2px style E fill:E1D5E7,stroke:660066,stroke-width:2px style F fill:DAE8FC,stroke:000099,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Grammar Derivation Tree (Parse Tree for Section 3 Example) ```mermaid flowchart TD root["ClassDecl"] n1["Keyword: class"] n2["Identifier: Account"] n3["Symbol: left brace"] n4["ClassBody"] n5["Symbol: right brace"] n6["Attribute 1"] n7["ClassBody tail"] n8["Type: String"] n9["Identifier: name"] n10["Symbol: semicolon"] n11["Attribute 2"] n12["epsilon (empty)"] n13["Type: int"] n14["Identifier: id"] n15["Symbol: semicolon 2"] root --> n1 root --> n2 root --> n3 root --> n4 root --> n5 n4 --> n6 n4 --> n7 n6 --> n8 n6 --> n9 n6 --> n10 n7 --> n11 n7 --> n12 n11 --> n13 n11 --> n14 n11 --> n15 ``` ## 4.4 Syntactic Processing Pipeline (Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Source Model
concrete form"] P2["Lexer or Scanner"] P3["Token Stream"] P4["Parser"] P5["Parse Tree"] P6["AST Builder"] P7["Abstract Syntax Tree"] P8["Well formedness
Validator"] P9["Valid Model"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Abstract Syntax and Concrete Syntax in the context of a modeling language.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Abstract Syntax | Concrete Syntax | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Describes the *logical structure* of a model (its underlying tree/graph of elements). | Describes the *physical representation* (textual symbols or graphical shapes). | | **Audience** | Designed for tools and automated processing. | Designed for human consumption. | | **Example** | An AST showing a `Class` node linked to two `Attribute` nodes. | A UML class box with name *"Customer"* and attributes listed inside. | | **Formalism** | Defined via meta-model (e.g., MOF). | Defined via BNF/EBNF (textual) or graph grammar (graphical). | *(1 mark per clear distinction point; total 3 marks.)* ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the role of a Meta-Object Facility (MOF) in defining modeling language syntax?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. MOF is the **standardized M3-level architecture** that defines how metamodels themselves are constructed. **(1 mark)** 2. It provides a **self-describing set of meta-classes** (e.g., `MOF.Class`, `MOF.Attribute`, `MOF.Association`) used to build any domain-specific metamodel. **(1 mark)** 3. By conforming to MOF, languages like UML, SysML, and BPMN ensure **interoperability and tool integration** across the four modeling levels M0 through M3. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question 3A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Set 1]` **(a)** Explain the four-level meta-modeling architecture of MOF with a suitable example. Describe how each level is an instance of the level above it. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define BNF and EBNF. Given the grammar below, derive the string `int x ; int y ;` and draw the corresponding parse tree. **(7 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \langle \text{Block} \rangle &\; ::= \; \{ \; \langle \text{StmtList} \rangle \; \} \\ \langle \text{StmtList} \rangle &\; ::= \; \langle \text{Decl} \rangle \; \langle \text{StmtList} \rangle \; \vert \; \varepsilon \\ \langle \text{Decl} \rangle &\; ::= \; \langle \text{Type} \rangle \; \langle \text{Id} \rangle \; ; \\ \langle \text{Type} \rangle &\; ::= \; \texttt{int} \; \vert \; \texttt{float} \\ \langle \text{Id} \rangle &\; ::= \; x \; \vert \; y \; \vert \; z \end{aligned} $$ #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Introduction (1 mark):** The MOF four-level architecture provides a layered hierarchy that defines modeling languages and their instances. Each level conforms to the one above it. 2. **Level M0 — User Objects (1 mark):** Contains the actual runtime data instances. *Example:* A specific customer object named *"Alice"* with age 30. 3. **Level M1 — User Models (1 mark):** Contains domain-specific models built from a metamodel. *Example:* A UML class diagram showing the *Customer* and *Order* classes for a banking system. 4. **Level M2 — Meta-Model (2 marks):** Defines the abstract syntax of the modeling language itself. *Example:* The UML metamodel contains `Class`, `Attribute`, `Operation` metaclasses and their relationships. 5. **Level M3 — Meta-Meta-Model (1 mark):** The architecture used to define metamodels. MOF itself is the canonical example — it is self-describing (MOF describes MOF). 6. **instanceOf Chain (1 mark):** $$ M_0 \;\texttt{instanceOf}\; M_1 \;\texttt{instanceOf}\; M_2 \;\texttt{instanceOf}\; M_3 $$ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Define BNF/EBNF (2 marks):** - BNF (Backus-Naur Form) uses production rules of the form `::=`. EBNF extends BNF with regex-like operators: `[]` for optional, `{}` for repetition, `()` for grouping. **Step 2 — Leftmost Derivation of `int x ; int y ;` (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \langle \text{Block} \rangle &\Rightarrow \{ \; \langle \text{StmtList} \rangle \; \} \\ &\Rightarrow \{ \; \langle \text{Decl} \rangle \; \langle \text{StmtList} \rangle \; \} \\ &\Rightarrow \{ \; \texttt{int} \; x \; ; \; \langle \text{StmtList} \rangle \; \} \\ &\Rightarrow \{ \; \texttt{int} \; x \; ; \; \langle \text{Decl} \rangle \; \langle \text{StmtList} \rangle \; \} \\ &\Rightarrow \{ \; \texttt{int} \; x \; ; \; \texttt{int} \; y \; ; \; \langle \text{StmtList} \rangle \; \} \\ &\Rightarrow \{ \; \texttt{int} \; x \; ; \; \texttt{int} \; y \; ; \; \varepsilon \; \} \\ &\Rightarrow \texttt{\{ int x ; int y ; \}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Parse Tree (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TD pRoot["Block"] pLB["Symbol: left brace"] pSL["StmtList"] pRB["Symbol: right brace"] pD1["Decl 1"] pSL2["StmtList tail"] pT1["Type: int"] pI1["Id: x"] pSC1["Symbol: semicolon"] pD2["Decl 2"] pEps["epsilon"] pT2["Type: int"] pI2["Id: y"] pSC2["Symbol: semicolon 2"] pRoot --> pLB pRoot --> pSL pRoot --> pRB pSL --> pD1 pSL --> pSL2 pD1 --> pT1 pD1 --> pI1 pD1 --> pSC1 pSL2 --> pD2 pSL2 --> pEps pD2 --> pT2 pD2 --> pI2 pD2 --> pSC2 ``` --- ### **Question 3B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Set 2]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the four-level MOF meta-modeling architecture. Discuss why the M3 level is said to be *self-describing*. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the difference between *Textual Concrete Syntax* and *Graphical Concrete Syntax*. Provide one modeling-language example for each and the grammar formalism used to formally define each. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Diagram of MOF Levels (3 marks):** Use the Mermaid block from Section 4.1 of these notes. Clearly label M0, M1, M2, M3 with examples. 2. **Self-Describing Explanation (3 marks):** The M3 layer (MOF) defines abstract constructs like *Class*, *Attribute*, *Association*. Since MOF is itself constructed using these very constructs, it conforms to itself. Therefore, the rule $\mathcal{M}_3 \;\texttt{instanceOf}\; \mathcal{M}_3$ holds — a unique closure property. 3. **Practical Benefit (1 mark):** Any tool that understands MOF automatically understands any MOF-conformant metamodel — enabling universal tool support. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks 1. **Textual Concrete Syntax (3.5 marks):** - Uses ASCII/Unicode linear sequences. - *Example:* The Java/C++ class declaration `class Customer { String name; }`. - Formally defined by **BNF / EBNF** context-free grammars. - Advantages: easy to parse, version-control friendly, fast text-based processing. 2. **Graphical Concrete Syntax (3.5 marks):** - Uses 2D visual symbols, lines, arrows. - *Example:* A UML class diagram with boxes, arrows, and multiplicity labels. - Formally defined by **Graph Grammars** (e.g., NCE — Node-Controlled Edge replacement grammar) or by the meta-model alone (in practice). - Advantages: intuitive, spatial reasoning, easier stakeholder communication. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Students commonly lose **2 marks** by confusing *Abstract Syntax* with *Concrete Syntax* — remember: **Abstract = tree, Concrete = picture/text**. > - In derivation questions, students often skip steps — KTU examiners award **partial marks per production rule application**. Show every $\Rightarrow$ step explicitly. > - When drawing parse trees, **every leaf must be a terminal symbol** (no non-terminals left unexpanded). Otherwise expect a **1-mark deduction**. > - Do NOT confuse **MOF levels** with **UML diagram types** — they are entirely different categorization schemes. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Language Syntax":** - **Abstract Syntax** = the logical/structural form (tree/graph). Defined by the **meta-model**. - **Concrete Syntax** = the visible form. **Textual** uses EBNF; **Graphical** uses graph grammars. - **MOF Four Levels:** **M0** (data), **M1** (model), **M2** (meta-model), **M3** (MOF). - The **instanceOf relation** links each level to the one directly above it. - **MOF is self-describing** at M3 — unique closure property. - **BNF rule form:** `NonTerminal ::= expression` using `|` for alternatives. - **EBNF extensions:** `[]` optional, `{}` zero-or-more repetition, `()` grouping. - A string $w$ belongs to language $L(G)$ iff $S \Rightarrow^{*} w$ (derivable from start symbol). - **Parse tree leaves are always terminals**; internal nodes are non-terminals. - **Well-formedness rules** are syntactic constraints (e.g., multiplicities, naming) — distinct from semantic rules. - **UML** is the canonical example of a MOF-conformant graphical modeling language. - **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs)** can have their own abstract + concrete syntax defined via meta-modeling tooling (e.g., Eclipse Xtext, JetBrains MPS). - **Syntactic validity** $\neq$ **Semantic correctness** — a syntactically legal model can still be meaningless. ---

topics.title: AADL Declarations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** AADL Declarations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **AADL (Architecture Analysis and Design Language)** is a SAE International standard (**AS5506B / AS5506C**) designed for model-based engineering of **embedded real-time, safety-critical, and distributed systems**. It is a textual + graphical modeling language with formal semantics that supports analysis of performance, schedulability, reliability, and security *before* code generation. An **AADL Declaration** is the formal textual construct used to specify the architectural elements of a system within an AADL model. Declarations describe **what exists** in the architecture (components), **how they behave** (features, flows, modes), and **how they are configured** (subcomponents, connections, properties). | Term | Meaning in KTU Syllabus | |---|---| | **Package** | A logical grouping/namespacing unit for AADL declarations | | **Component Type** | The *interface* or *blueprint* of a component | | **Component Implementation** | The *internal structure* of a component (subcomponents + connections) | | **Feature** | An interaction point of a component (port, parameter, access) | | **Subcomponent** | A nested component inside an implementation | | **Property Association** | Attaches typed values (timing, memory, etc.) to elements | > [!NOTE] > **AADL is NOT a programming language.** It is a **modeling + analysis language** — its primary output is analyzable architectural models, not executable binaries. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Blueprint" Imagine you are an **architect** designing a hospital building: - **AADL Package** = The project folder containing all your drawings. - **Component Type Declaration** = The **outer view** of the "Operation Theatre" — it says: "An OT has 1 main door, 1 emergency door, requires oxygen supply, and has a power socket." It defines the *interface* only. - **Component Implementation Declaration** = The **inner view** of the same OT — it says: "Inside the OT, there is 1 surgical light, 1 ventilator, 1 anesthesia machine, all connected to a central power bus." It defines the *internals*. - **Features** = The doors, windows, sockets (interaction points). - **Subcomponents** = The light, ventilator, machine (internal parts). - **Connections** = Cables/pipes joining those subcomponents. - **Properties** = Specifications like "surgical light must produce **50,000 lux**" or "OT must maintain positive pressure **> 5 Pa**". - **Modes** = "Normal Mode" (everything on) vs. "Emergency Mode" (only ventilator on). In AADL, you write textual declarations (or model them graphically in **OSATE** — the Open Source AADL Tool Environment) that perform exactly this architectural specification for software + hardware systems. ## 1.3 Why AADL Declarations Matter in Industry AADL is the **de facto standard** for: - **Avionics** (DO-178C compliance support) — Boeing, Airbus - **Automotive** (ISO 26262) — automotive embedded ECUs - **Aerospace** (NASA uses AADL for crew systems) - **Medical devices** (FDA IEC 62304 alignment) - **Industrial control** systems Without proper declarations, you cannot run **schedulability analysis, latency analysis, reliability analysis, or code generation** — all of which are core deliverables of model-based development (MBD). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of AADL Declarations AADL follows a **strict 3-level hierarchical structure** of declarations, which is a high-yield KTU exam point. ``` Level 1 : Package Declaration (namespacing) Level 2 : Component Type Declaration (interface / signature) Level 3 : Component Implementation Declaration (internal structure) ``` ### 2.1.1 Package Declaration ```ada package Flight_Control public -- component type and implementation declarations go here end Flight_Control; ``` - Provides **namespacing** to avoid identifier conflicts. - Has `public` and `private` sections (private declarations hidden from importers). - Imported using `with` clause: `with Flight_Control;` ### 2.1.2 Component Type Declaration A component type defines the **external interface** of a component. ```ada system FlightController features SensorInput : in data port SensorData; ActuatorCmd : out data port CmdSignal; Power : requires bus access PowerBus; MemoryPort : requires data access MemoryBlock; properties Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic; Period => 20 ms; end FlightController; ``` Key elements: **features**, **flows**, **modes**, **properties**, **prototypes**, **requires/extends**. ### 2.1.3 Component Implementation Declaration A component implementation defines the **internal structure** — what subcomponents exist and how they connect. ```ada system implementation FlightController.Impl subcomponents NavThread : thread Navigation::NavFilter.Impl; CtrlThread : thread Control::CtrlLogic.Impl; SharedMem : memory Memory::RAM.Impl; connections conn1 : port SensorInput -> NavThread.SensorIn; conn2 : port NavThread.Out -> CtrlThread.In; flows main_flow : end to end flow SensorInput -> conn1 -> NavThread.flow1 -> conn2 -> CtrlThread.flow2 -> ActuatorCmd; properties Actual_Processor_Binding => reference (Processor1); end FlightController.Impl; ``` ## 2.2 The 10 AADL Component Categories — Critical KTU Topic > [!IMPORTANT] > This is one of the most asked KTU questions. You **must** memorize all 10 categories and their classifications. | # | Category | Class | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Thread** | Software (active) | Concurrent unit of execution; has its own dispatch | | 2 | **Thread Group** | Software (composite) | A logical grouping of threads (no execution) | | 3 | **Process** | Software (composite) | Protected address space containing threads | | 4 | **Data** | Software (passive) | Static data component (e.g., a struct, record) | | 5 | **Subprogram** | Software (passive) | Callable sequential code (function/procedure) | | 6 | **Subprogram Group** | Software (composite) | Collection of related subprograms | | 7 | **Processor** | Execution Platform | Schedules and executes threads; represents a CPU | | 8 | **Memory** | Execution Platform | Stores code/data; represents RAM/ROM/Flash | | 9 | **Bus** | Execution Platform | Communication medium between processors/memory | | 10 | **Device** | Execution Platform | External I/O — sensors, actuators | | 11 | **System** | Composite (top-level) | Represents the entire system; topmost component | | 12 | **Abstract** | Generic | Generic component (no specific category assumed) | > [!NOTE] > **Abstract** is the 12th one — it's the most general. KTU sometimes gives 10 or 11, but the standard has **12** categories per AS5506B. ## 2.3 Feature Declarations — The Interaction Interface Features describe **how a component interacts with the outside world**. | Feature | Direction | Used By | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Data Port** | in / out / inout | Thread, Process, System, Abstract | Asynchronous data exchange | | **Event Port** | in / out | Thread, Process, System | Asynchronous event signaling (no payload) | | **Event Data Port** | in / out | Thread, Process, System | Event + data together (queue of messages) | | **Subprogram Parameter** | in / out / inout | Subprogram, Subprogram Group | Function call argument | | **Subprogram Access** | requires / provides | Thread, Process, System | Calls a remote subprogram | | **Bus Access** | requires / provides | Processor, Memory, System, Device | Connection to a bus | | **Data Access** | requires / provides | Thread, Process, Data | Shared data access (read-only or read-write) | | **Abstract Feature** | — | Abstract, System | For components that don't fit standard features | ## 2.4 Property Associations Properties attach **typed metadata** to AADL elements (components, features, connections, subcomponents). Syntax: ```ada PropertyName => Value [ (applies to (which elements)) ]; ``` Categories of properties: - **Timing properties**: `Period`, `Deadline`, `Compute_Execution_Time`, `Dispatch_Protocol` - **Memory properties**: `Source_Code_Size`, `Data_Size`, `Memory_Size` - **Binding properties**: `Actual_Processor_Binding`, `Actual_Memory_Binding` - **Thread properties**: `Priority`, `Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic/Aperiodic/Sporadic` > [!TIP] > **Dispatch_Protocol values**: `Periodic`, `Sporadic`, `Aperiodic`, `Background`, `Timed`. This is a frequent KTU fill-in-the-blank. ## 2.5 Subcomponent, Connection, and Flow Declarations ### Subcomponent ```ada subcomponents MySubComp : thread MyPackage::MyThread.Impl {Classifier}; ``` ### Connection ```ada connections c1 : port Src.Out_Port -> Dst.In_Port; c2 : bus access Proc1.Power -> Bus1.Power; ``` ### Flow Declarations (4 types) - **Source flow** — flow that originates at the component boundary. - **Sink flow** — flow that ends at the component boundary. - **End-to-end flow** — flow that goes from a source to a sink across the system. - **Path flow** — flow that traverses through subcomponents. ## 2.6 Modes and Mode Transitions Modes capture **discrete operating states** of a system (e.g., Init, Run, Error, Standby, Recovery). ```ada modes Init : initial mode; Running : mode; Error : mode; ErrorRecovery : mode; mode transitions t1 : Init -[ StartSwitch ]-> Running; t2 : Running -[ Failure ]-> Error; t3 : Error -[ Reset ]-> ErrorRecovery; ``` ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table | KTU Question Pattern | Quick Answer | |---|---| | AADL full form | Architecture Analysis and Design Language | | AADL standard number | SAE AS5506B / AS5506C | | Number of component categories | **12** (including Abstract) | | Software components | Thread, Thread Group, Process, Data, Subprogram, Subprogram Group | | Execution platform components | Processor, Memory, Bus, Device | | Composite components | System, Abstract | | Active vs Passive | Thread & Process are **active**; Data, Subprogram, Memory are **passive** | | Port types | Data, Event, Event Data | | `with` vs `extends` | `with` imports a package; `extends` inherits from a type | | `requires` vs `provides` | requires = needs the resource; provides = offers the resource | | `port` vs `access` | port = message-passing; access = shared resource | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Walkthrough: Building a Complete AADL Declaration Let us build, step-by-step, a complete AADL declaration for a **simple "Smart Light" embedded system** to understand the syntax flow. ### Step 1: Define the Package We need a namespace to hold our declarations. ```ada package SmartLight public -- declarations go here end SmartLight; ``` **Logic**: The `package` keyword introduces a new scope. The `public` section is visible to importers; the optional `private` section is hidden. ### Step 2: Declare Component Types (Interfaces) We need three types: a controller thread, a sensor data component, and the top-level system. ```ada -- Data type for sensor reading data SensorReading properties Data_Size => 4 Bytes; end SensorReading; -- Thread that processes sensor data thread Controller features LdrInput : in event data port SensorReading; LedOutput : out data port LightCmd; properties Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic; Period => 50 ms; Compute_Execution_Time => 0 ms .. 5 ms; Deadline => 50 ms; end Controller; -- Top-level system system SmartLight features LdrSensor : in event data port SensorReading; LedCtrl : out data port LightCmd; end SmartLight; ``` **Logic Explanation**: - `data SensorReading` declares a passive data type with 4-byte size. - `thread Controller` defines an *active* component with two ports and a 50ms periodic schedule. - `event data port` = a port that carries both an event signal *and* data. - The `system SmartLight` is the top-level type that exposes two external ports. ### Step 3: Declare Component Implementations (Internals) The implementation declares *which* subcomponents exist and *how* they are wired. ```ada thread implementation Controller.Impl end Controller.Impl; system implementation SmartLight.Impl subcomponents CtrlThread : thread SmartLight::Controller.Impl; Mcu : processor ARMCortexM3; Flash : memory NORFlash.Impl; connections c1 : port LdrSensor -> CtrlThread.LdrInput; c2 : port CtrlThread.LedOutput -> LedCtrl; properties Actual_Processor_Binding => reference (Mcu) applies to CtrlThread; Actual_Memory_Binding => reference (Flash) applies to CtrlThread; end SmartLight.Impl; ``` **Logic Explanation**: - `subcomponents` block instantiates 3 sub-elements: the controller thread, an MCU processor, and Flash memory. - `connections` block defines two wire-like links: incoming sensor data → thread input; thread output → external LED control port. - `Actual_Processor_Binding` binds the `CtrlThread` to the `Mcu` processor. - `applies to (CtrlThread)` scopes the property to that specific subcomponent only. ### Step 4: Verify Declaration Semantics For a valid AADL model, the following must hold: 1. **Type-implementation pairing**: Every implementation must reference a defined type (`Type.Impl`). 2. **Port direction consistency**: An `in` port can only be a destination of a connection; an `out` port can only be a source. 3. **Category constraints**: A `process` cannot be a subcomponent of a `thread`. 4. **Property type matching**: `Period => 50 ms` — value `50 ms` must match the `Time` type defined for `Period`. ### Step 5: Analyze the Model Once the declaration is complete, tools like **OSATE** (Eclipse-based) perform: - **Type checking** — does every reference resolve? - **Well-formedness analysis** — does the model violate any AADL rules? - **Schedulability analysis** — using the Cheddar or MAST plugins. - **Latency analysis** — end-to-end flow latency. - **Code generation** — produces Ada, C, or Simulink skeleton code. ## 3.2 Walkthrough: Resolving a KTU-Type "Which is NOT a Component Category" Question Suppose the KTU question asks: *"Which of the following is NOT a valid AADL component category?"* Options: (A) Thread (B) Process (C) Function (D) Processor (E) Bus **Step 1**: Recall the **12 official AADL component categories**. The 12 are: **system, abstract, process, thread group, thread, data, subprogram group, subprogram, processor, memory, bus, device**. **Step 2**: Check each option: - (A) Thread ✓ valid - (B) Process ✓ valid - (C) Function ✗ **NOT valid** — AADL has `subprogram`, not `function` - (D) Processor ✓ valid - (E) Bus ✓ valid **Step 3**: Answer: **(C) Function** This is the typical **logical derivation** expected in KTU 14-mark answers where students must justify each choice. ## 3.3 Walkthrough: Distinguishing Type vs Implementation | Aspect | Type Declaration | Implementation Declaration | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | External interface | Internal structure | | **Contains** | Features, flows (out), properties, modes | Subcomponents, connections, flows (in) | | **Naming** | `thread Controller` | `thread implementation Controller.Impl` | | **Number** | A type can have **multiple** implementations | Each impl must be linked to a type | | **Example** | Like a **class declaration** in C++ | Like a **.cpp file** defining the class body | | **Reusability** | High — you can use the same type in many impls | One impl can be referenced by many subcomponents | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: AADL Declaration Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD AADLFile["AADL Source File"] --> Pkg["Package Declaration
(namespace)"] Pkg --> Type1["Component Type
Declaration"] Pkg --> Type2["Component Type
Declaration"] Pkg --> Impl1["Component Implementation
Declaration"] Pkg --> Impl2["Component Implementation
Declaration"] Type1 --> F1["Features
(ports, parameters, access)"] Type1 --> F2["Flows (source/sink)"] Type1 --> F3["Properties
(timing, memory)"] Type1 --> F4["Modes"] Impl1 --> S1["Subcomponents"] Impl1 --> S2["Connections"] Impl1 --> S3["Flows (end-to-end, path)"] Impl1 --> S4["Mode Transitions"] Impl1 --> S5["Property Associations"] classDef pkg fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef type fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef impl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef detail fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px class Pkg pkg class Type1,Type2 type class Impl1,Impl2 impl class F1,F2,F3,F4,S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 detail ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: AADL Component Category Taxonomy ```mermaid graph TD Root["AADL Component Categories
(12 total)"] --> Software["Software Components"] Root --> ExecPlat["Execution Platform Components"] Root --> Composite["Composite/Generic Components"] Software --> SWActive["Active (runnable)"] Software --> SWPassive["Passive (data/code)"] Software --> SWGroup["Grouping (composite)"] SWActive --> Thread["Thread"] SWActive --> Process["Process"] SWPassive --> Data["Data"] SWPassive --> Subprogram["Subprogram"] SWGroup --> ThreadGroup["Thread Group"] SWGroup --> SubprogramGroup["Subprogram Group"] ExecPlat --> Processor["Processor"] ExecPlat --> Memory["Memory"] ExecPlat --> Bus["Bus"] ExecPlat --> Device["Device"] Composite --> System["System (top-level)"] Composite --> Abstract["Abstract (generic)"] classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef category fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef component fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px class Root root class Software,ExecPlat,Composite,SWActive,SWPassive,SWGroup category class Thread,Process,Data,Subprogram,ThreadGroup,SubprogramGroup,Processor,Memory,Bus,Device,System,Abstract component ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Feature Classification ```mermaid graph TD Feature["AADL Features
(interaction points)"] --> PortKind["Ports"] Feature --> ParamKind["Parameters"] Feature --> AccessKind["Access Features"] Feature --> AbstractFeat["Abstract Features"] PortKind --> DataPort["Data Port
(in/out/inout)"] PortKind --> EventPort["Event Port
(asynchronous, no payload)"] PortKind --> EventDataPort["Event Data Port
(asynchronous + payload)"] ParamKind --> ParamIn["Parameter In"] ParamKind --> ParamOut["Parameter Out"] ParamKind --> ParamInOut["Parameter Inout"] AccessKind --> SubAcc["Subprogram Access
(provides/requires)"] AccessKind --> BusAcc["Bus Access
(provides/requires)"] AccessKind --> DataAcc["Data Access
(read-only / read-write)"] classDef feat fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef sub fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px class Feature feat class PortKind,ParamKind,AccessKind,AbstractFeat,DataPort,EventPort,EventDataPort,ParamIn,ParamOut,ParamInOut,SubAcc,BusAcc,DataAcc sub ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: AADL Analysis Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR Decl["AADL Declarations
(.aadl files)"] --> OSATE["OSATE Tool
(Eclipse IDE)"] OSATE --> Check["Well-Formedness
Checking"] OSATE --> Sched["Schedulability
Analysis"] OSATE --> Lat["Latency
Analysis"] OSATE --> Rel["Reliability
Analysis"] OSATE --> Codegen["Code Generation
(Ada / C / Simulink)"] OSATE --> Annex["Annex Libraries
(EMV2, ARINC653, BA)"] classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef tool fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px class Decl input class OSATE tool class Check,Sched,Lat,Rel,Codegen,Annex output ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** > **Q1.** Define AADL. What is the standard number associated with it? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > AADL stands for **Architecture Analysis and Design Language**. It is a SAE International standard with number **AS5506B** (or AS5506C) used for model-based specification, analysis, and code generation of **embedded real-time, distributed, and safety-critical systems**. It supports both **software** and **execution platform** components, allowing engineers to model, analyze (timing, reliability, performance), and auto-generate code from the same architectural model. > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q2.** List the **12 component categories** in AADL and classify them into software, execution platform, and composite components. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **12 AADL component categories** are: > > 1. **Software (Active)**: Thread, Process > 2. **Software (Passive)**: Data, Subprogram > 3. **Software (Grouping)**: Thread Group, Subprogram Group > 4. **Execution Platform**: Processor, Memory, Bus, Device > 5. **Composite/Generic**: System, Abstract > > (2 marks for correct listing + 1 mark for correct classification) --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Apr/May 2024, Model Question]** ### Question A (14 Marks) > **(a)** Explain the **three-level declaration structure** of AADL with a suitable example. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Differentiate between **Component Type Declaration** and **Component Implementation Declaration** in AADL. *(7 marks)* ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) > **(a)** Describe the various **feature declarations** in AADL with examples. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Explain the **port types** in AADL. How are data ports different from event data ports? *(7 marks)* --- ### Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Three-Level Declaration Structure The AADL declaration model follows a **three-level hierarchy**: 1. **Package Declaration** — Provides namespacing and visibility control. 2. **Component Type Declaration** — Defines the external interface of a component. 3. **Component Implementation Declaration** — Defines the internal structure of a component. **Example for a "Cruise Control" system:** ```ada -- LEVEL 1: Package Declaration package CruiseControl public -- LEVEL 2: Component Types system CruiseCtrl features speedSense : in data port SpeedData; throttleCmd : out data port ThrottleCmd; end CruiseCtrl; data SpeedData properties Data_Size => 4 Bytes; end SpeedData; data ThrottleCmd properties Data_Size => 2 Bytes; end ThrottleCmd; -- LEVEL 3: Component Implementation system implementation CruiseCtrl.Impl subcomponents SenseThread : thread CruiseControl::SpeedSensor.Impl; ActThread : thread CruiseControl::ThrottleActuator.Impl; connections c1 : port speedSense -> SenseThread.inSensor; c2 : port SenseThread.outSpeed -> ActThread.inSpeed; c3 : port ActThread.outCmd -> throttleCmd; end CruiseCtrl.Impl; end CruiseControl; ``` **Explanation of levels:** - **Package** `CruiseControl` — Groups all related declarations in one namespace. (2 marks) - **Component Type** `CruiseCtrl` — Lists the *external* features the system exposes. No internal structure is visible. (2 marks) - **Component Implementation** `CruiseCtrl.Impl` — Lists *subcomponents* (the sensor thread and actuator thread) and the *connections* (c1, c2, c3) that wire them. (3 marks) #### Part (b) — Type vs Implementation Declaration | Aspect | Component Type | Component Implementation | |---|---|---| | **Keyword** | `system`, `thread`, etc. | ` implementation` | | **Defines** | External interface | Internal structure | | **Contains** | features, flows, properties, modes | subcomponents, connections, calls | | **Naming** | `thread Controller` | `thread implementation Controller.Impl` | | **Count** | One type can have **multiple** impls | Each impl must reference a type | | **Analogous to** | C++ class declaration (header) | C++ class body (.cpp) | | **Reuse** | High | Specific to one structural variant | **Example showing the difference:** ```ada -- Type: declares the interface only thread Controller features input : in data port CmdData; output : out data port StatusData; end Controller; -- Implementation 1: single-threaded design thread implementation Controller.Single subcomponents Parser : subprogram ParseCmd.Impl; connections c1 : port input -> Parser.param1; end Controller.Single; -- Implementation 2: alternative multi-threaded design thread implementation Controller.Multi subcomponents Parser : thread SubParser.Impl; Logger : thread SubLogger.Impl; connections c1 : port input -> Parser.cmd; c2 : port Parser.result -> Logger.msg; c3 : port Logger.log -> output; end Controller.Multi; ``` This shows **one type, two implementations** — a powerful feature of AADL for **variant modeling and architectural trade-off analysis**. (7 marks for table + example) --- ### Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Feature Declarations **Features** are the interaction points between a component and its environment. AADL supports **8 categories** of features: 1. **Data Port** — Asynchronous data exchange (no event signal). 2. **Event Port** — Asynchronous event signal (no payload data). 3. **Event Data Port** — Both an event *and* a data payload (queued). 4. **Subprogram Parameter** — Function-call style argument. 5. **Subprogram Access** — Remote subprogram call. 6. **Bus Access** — Connection to a hardware bus. 7. **Data Access** — Shared data segment access. 8. **Abstract Feature** — Generic feature (untyped). **Example illustrating all major feature types:** ```ada abstract component EngineCtrl features -- Data ports ThrottlePos : in data port Float; RpmReading : out data port Integer; -- Event port Ignition : in event port; -- Event data port Diagnostic : in event data port DiagMsg; -- Subprogram parameter (only on subprogram) -- Subprogram access FlashAPI : requires subprogram access FirmwareUpdater.Impl; -- Bus access CanBusConn : requires bus access VehicleCANBus.Impl; -- Data access LookupTable : requires data access FuelMap.Impl; -- Abstract feature OptionalIn : in feature; end EngineCtrl; ``` (7 marks: 4 for listing + 3 for the example) #### Part (b) — Port Types and Data vs Event Data Ports **Three port types in AADL:** | Port Type | Payload? | Event? | Queueing? | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Data Port** | Yes | No | No (overwrites) | Sensor readings, control signals | | **Event Port** | No | Yes | N/A (signal only) | Triggers, interrupts, alarms | | **Event Data Port** | Yes | Yes | Yes (FIFO) | Asynchronous messages, requests | **Differences between Data Port and Event Data Port:** 1. **Data Port** — Sends the *current value* of data. The receiver always gets the *latest* snapshot. If the receiver is slow, intermediate values are **dropped** (or **overwritten**). Suitable for **periodic** signals like temperature, velocity. 2. **Event Data Port** — Sends a *discrete message* tagged with an event. The receiver consumes messages in **FIFO order** from a queue. **No messages are dropped** (provided the queue does not overflow). Suitable for **asynchronous commands**, requests, or notifications. **Example contrasting them:** ```ada thread SensorProcessor features -- Data port: latest temperature always available Temperature : in data port Float; -- Event data port: each alarm is a discrete message AlarmMsg : in event data port AlarmPayload; end SensorProcessor; ``` If the temperature is updated 10 times but the consumer only reads once, it gets the **10th value** with a data port. With an event data port, it gets **all 10 messages queued**, dispatched one at a time. (7 marks) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU 2024 marking penalties — AADL Declarations:** > > - **−2 marks** if you confuse **Component Type** with **Component Implementation** (very common; remember: *type = interface, implementation = internals*). > - **−1 mark** for spelling *"Implimentation"* instead of *"Implementation"* — AADL compilers are case-insensitive, but KTU evaluators are strict. > - **−1 mark** if you write only **10** component categories when the answer requires **12** — always include `System` and `Abstract`. > - **−2 marks** if you confuse the `with` keyword with `extends`. `with` imports; `extends` inherits. Writing `system Ctrl extends MyCtrl` inside a `with` clause is a frequent error. > - **−2 marks** if you forget the `applies to` clause on properties meant for subcomponents — without it, the property will apply to the *parent* implementation, not the subcomponent. > - **−1 mark** for using `Function` instead of `Subprogram` — AADL does not have a `Function` component category. > - **−3 marks** for failing to mention `Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic/Aperiodic/Sporadic` when asked about real-time thread properties. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for AADL Declarations:** > > - **AADL = Architecture Analysis and Design Language (SAE AS5506B)** > - **Three declaration levels**: Package → Component Type → Component Implementation > - **12 component categories**: System, Abstract, Process, Thread Group, Thread, Subprogram Group, Subprogram, Data, Processor, Memory, Bus, Device > - **Active software components** (have their own thread of execution): Thread, Process > - **Passive software components**: Data, Subprogram > - **Execution platform**: Processor, Memory, Bus, Device > - **Three port types**: Data Port, Event Port, Event Data Port > - **Access features**: Subprogram Access, Bus Access, Data Access (each can be `requires` or `provides`) > - **`with`** = import a package; **`extends`** = inherit from a type > - **Dispatch protocols**: Periodic, Sporadic, Aperiodic, Background, Timed > - **Common timing properties**: Period, Deadline, Compute_Execution_Time, Priority > - **Common binding properties**: Actual_Processor_Binding, Actual_Memory_Binding > - **Flows**: source, sink, end-to-end, path > - **Modes** capture discrete operating states; mode transitions connect them > - **OSATE** is the reference open-source AADL tool (Eclipse-based) > - **Annexes** extend AADL: EMV2 (error modeling), ARINC653, BA (behavior), Behavior_Annex > - AADL is **declarative**, not procedural — it describes *what* and *how it's connected*, not *how it executes*

topics.title: AADL Classifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** AADL Classifiers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of AADL Classifiers ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **Architecture Analysis & Design Language (AADL)** — standardized as **SAE AS5506** — a **Classifier** is the fundamental *type-level* construct used to describe the structural and behavioral blueprint of an embedded, real-time, or distributed system. A classifier acts as a **reusable template (declarative type)** from which concrete component **instances** are later instantiated during system composition and deployment. AADL classifiers are categorized into five primary families: 1. **Component Classifiers** – the main architectural building blocks (e.g., `system`, `process`, `thread`, `data`, `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`, `abstract`, `subprogram`). 2. **Feature Classifiers** – classify interface elements such as ports, parameters, and data access. 3. **Connection Classifiers** – represent inter-component communication paths. 4. **Flow Classifiers** – define end-to-end information propagation specifications. 5. **Property Classifiers** – attach typed attributes (timing, memory, power) to elements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A classifier in AADL is always declared using the keyword `classifier` or via a hierarchical naming path. It is **declarative, not executable** — meaning it describes *what* exists, not *how* the code runs. This aligns with the **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** philosophy mandated by the NEP 2020 syllabus. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blueprint of a Building Imagine constructing a **multi-storey hospital building**: - The **architectural blueprint** defines how many floors, where the ICU, operation theatre, and lifts are, and how corridors connect them. This blueprint is a **classifier**. - When the building is actually constructed, the concrete hospital is the **instance** of that blueprint. - Different wings (Cardiology, Neurology, Pediatrics) may be built using the same *generic* ward blueprint — this is **classifier reuse**. - A blueprint can also specify *properties* such as fire-resistance rating of walls (**AADL properties**) and *flows* such as the patient path from ER → ICU → Recovery Room (**AADL flows**). In AADL: - `thread` = the blueprint of a single patient-care unit - `process` = the blueprint of an entire floor - `system` = the blueprint of the entire hospital - `processor` = the blueprint of the central HVAC/electrical infrastructure - `bus` = the blueprint of the elevator/communication backbone --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Constant / Metric | Value | Purpose in AADL Classifiers | |---|---|---| | **SAE Standard** | **AS5506 (AADL v1, 2004) / AS5506/2 (Annex E)** | Normative language reference | | **Current Standard** | **SAE AS5506 Rev C (2017)** | Latest published revision | | **Tool Support** | **OSATE (Open Source AADL Tool Environment)** | Eclipse-based IDE by CMU SEI | | **Target Domains** | **Avionics, Automotive, Aerospace, Medical, Industrial Control** | Industrial application scope | > [!NOTE] > AADL classifiers operate on **discrete-event architectural abstraction**, not continuous-time dynamics. Hence classical physical constants like Planck's constant or Boltzmann's constant do **not** appear in AADL specifications. --- ## 1.4 Why AADL Classifiers Matter in Engineering Embedded systems — such as the **flight control computer of an aircraft** or the **brake-by-wire ECU of a car** — must satisfy **hard real-time constraints**. AADL classifiers allow engineers to: - Capture **separation of concerns** (hardware vs. software vs. communication). - Reason about **timing budgets, scheduling, and memory** *before* code is written. - Perform **formal analyses** such as schedulability, safety, and security evaluation. - Enable **model-based code generation** (C, Ada, Simulink) automatically. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Level Classifier Architecture: Type vs. Implementation AADL is unique because it **mandates a two-level classification** for every component. This is one of the most heavily tested KTU concepts. ### 2.1.1 Type Classifier A **type classifier** describes the **external interface** of a component — its *what*, not *how*. ``` classifier → declares external view component category → system | process | thread | ... features → ports, subprogram access, data access flows → flow specifications properties → non-behavioral attributes modes → operational mode declarations ``` ### 2.1.2 Implementation Classifier An **implementation classifier** describes the **internal structure** — its *how* (subcomponents + connections). ``` classifier.implementation → declares internal composition subcomponents → child component instances connections → inter-feature wiring flows → end-to-end refined flows properties → implementation-specific attributes modes → mode transition refinements ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Standard:** A *type* may have *many* implementations (e.g., `Sensor_Impl_Cheap`, `Sensor_Impl_Premium` for the same `Sensor` type). A *type* is analogous to a Java **interface**, while an *implementation* is analogous to a **concrete class**. --- ## 2.2 The 13 Component Classifiers — A Systematic Taxonomy AADL defines **13 standard component categories**. The following table is the **canonical KTU reference**: | # | Category | Spatial Domain | Execution Domain | Real-Time Support | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `system` | Logical Composite | Composite | N/A | Top-level integration | | 2 | `process` | Address Space | Software Boundary | N/A | Process boundary | | 3 | `thread group` | Within Process | Inside Process | N/A | Logical grouping | | 4 | `thread` | Within Process | Schedulable Unit | **Yes** | Concurrent execution | | 5 | `subprogram` | Within Thread | Callable Code | N/A | Function/procedure | | 6 | `data` | N/A | N/A | N/A | Static data structure | | 7 | `subprogram group` | Within Process | Grouping | N/A | Library/category | | 8 | `processor` | Hardware | Execution Platform | N/A | CPU / ECU / board | | 9 | `virtual processor` | Logical | Logical partition | N/A | Virtualization | | 10 | `memory` | Hardware | Storage | N/A | RAM / ROM / Flash | | 11 | `bus` | Hardware | Communication | N/A | CAN, ARINC 429, Ethernet | | 12 | `virtual bus` | Logical | Logical Comms | N/A | Virtual channels | | 13 | `device` | Hardware | External Sensor/Actuator | N/A | Sensors, actuators | | 14 | `abstract` | Generic | Generic | N/A | Catch-all | > [!NOTE] > The AADL v2 specification consolidates these into standardized categories. The **`abstract`** category is the most flexible — it can be used when the exact category is not yet decided, or to represent cross-cutting concerns (e.g., a software-hardware hybrid). --- ## 2.3 Hierarchical Composition Rules (The Containment Matrix) AADL enforces **strict containment rules** for subcomponents. Violating these is a **frequent cause of KTU exam deductions**. | Child → | system | process | thread | thread group | subprogram | data | processor | memory | bus | device | abstract | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **system** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | **process** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | **thread group** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | **thread** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | **subprogram** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | **abstract** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | **processor** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | **memory** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | **bus** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | **device** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | **virtual processor** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | **virtual bus** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** A `process` CANNOT contain another `process` directly. A `thread` CANNOT contain a `process`. Always check the containment matrix before declaring subcomponents. The KTU examiner will deduct marks for invalid hierarchies. --- ## 2.4 Feature Classification Every component classifier exposes its **interface** through *features*. AADL classifies features as follows: | Feature Type | Direction | Use | |---|---|---| | `event data port` | Bidirectional | Async data with event semantics | | `event port` | Bidirectional | Async signal only | | `data port` | Outgoing (read by others) | Data sample | | `data event port` | Outgoing | Data + event trigger | | `in out data port` | Bidirectional | Data in & out | | `in event port` | Incoming | Event in | | `out event port` | Outgoing | Event out | | `requires subprogram access` | Caller side | Calls a subprogram | | `provides subprogram access` | Callee side | Offers a subprogram | | `requires data access` | Shared data | Read-only access | | `provides data access` | Shared data | Provides shared memory | | `in out data port` | Read/Write | Full data exchange | | `parameter` | Subprogram only | Pass value/reference | | `abstract feature` | Generic | Custom extension | --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Classifier Properties (Timing & Sizing) | Property | Applies To | Units | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | `Compute_Execution_Time` | thread, subprogram | **ns, us, ms, s** | Worst-case execution time (WCET) | | `Compute_Deadline` | thread | **ns, us, ms, s** | Hard deadline | | `Compute_Period` | thread | **ns, us, ms, s** | Period for periodic threads | | `Compute_Priority` | thread | **integer (1–255)** | Scheduling priority | | `Dispatch_Protocol` | thread | enum | Periodic, Sporadic, Aperiodic, Timed, Hybrid, Background | | `Memory_Size` | data, subprogram, thread | **bytes** | Static memory footprint | | `Stack_Size` | thread, process | **bytes** | Runtime stack | | `Period` | processor | **ns, us, ms, s** | CPU clock period | | `Throughput` | bus | **bits/sec, MBps** | Communication bandwidth | | `Latency` | bus, connection | **ns, us, ms, s** | Wire delay | | `Transmission_Time` | connection | **ns, us, ms, s** | End-to-end transmission | ### Core Real-Time Constraint Equation For every **thread classifier**, the schedulability condition (Rate Monotonic Analysis — RMA) must hold: $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq U_{bound} $$ Where: - $C_i$ = `Compute_Execution_Time` of thread *i* (WCET) - $T_i$ = `Compute_Period` of thread *i* - $U_{bound}$ = Liu & Layland bound for $n$ periodic tasks: $$ U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula:** Memorize the schedulability inequality. It is a **guaranteed KTU board question** for 7 or 14 marks. The bound is **$0.693$ for $n \to \infty$** and **$1.0$ for $n=1$**. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility | Industry | Classifier Use Case | |---|---| | **Avionics (Boeing 787, Airbus A350)** | AADL threads for flight control laws; memory & latency properties verified against DO-178C | | **Automotive (Adaptive AUTOSAR)** | `system` classifiers for ECUs; `bus` for CAN/FlexRay/Ethernet | | **Medical (Pacemakers, Insulin Pumps)** | `process`/`thread` classifiers for control loops; safety properties for FDA certification | | **Space (NASA Curiosity Rover)** | `device` for sensors; `abstract` for redundant fault-tolerance patterns | | **Industrial (Siemens PLCs)** | `processor`/`memory` mapping for IEC 61131-3 systems | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example: AADL Classifier Pair (Type + Implementation) The following example demonstrates the **canonical AADL pattern** expected in KTU board answers. ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement > Design AADL classifiers for a **simple temperature monitoring subsystem** of a smart building. The system has: > - 1 main process called `Building_Monitor` running on a Raspberry Pi 4 > - 2 threads inside: a `Temp_Sensor_Reader` (reads a temperature sensor every 100 ms) and an `Alarm_Controller` (activates an alarm if temperature > 40°C) > - The sensor thread sends data to the alarm thread via an `event data port` > - The alarm thread calls a subprogram `Trigger_Buzzer` > - The process executes on a `RPi4_Processor` with 1 GB memory --- ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Classifier Construction #### Step 1 — Declare the Type Classifier for the Process ``` process Building_Monitor features Temp_Input : in event data port Temp_Data; flows temp_sink : flow sink Temp_Input; properties Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic; Period => 100 ms; Compute_Deadline => 100 ms; end Building_Monitor; ``` > **Explanation:** The *type* declares only the **external interface** (`Temp_Input` port) and a **flow sink** that consumes the incoming data. No internal composition is shown here. --- #### Step 2 — Declare the Type Classifier for Each Thread ``` thread Temp_Sensor_Reader features Out_Temp : out event data port Temp_Data; flows temp_source : flow source Out_Temp; properties Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic; Period => 100 ms; Compute_Execution_Time => 5 ms; Compute_Deadline => 10 ms; end Temp_Sensor_Reader; thread Alarm_Controller features In_Temp : in event data port Temp_Data; Buzzer : requires subprogram access Buzzer_Sub; flows alarm_flow : flow path In_Temp -> Buzzer; properties Dispatch_Protocol => Sporadic; Compute_Execution_Time => 3 ms; Compute_Deadline => 8 ms; end Alarm_Controller; ``` --- #### Step 3 — Declare the Subprogram Classifier ``` subprogram Buzzer_Sub features Level : in parameter; properties Source_Stack_Size => 1 KByte; end Buzzer_Sub; ``` --- #### Step 4 — Declare the Data Classifier ``` data Temp_Data properties Data_Size => 4 Bytes; end Temp_Data; ``` --- #### Step 5 — Implement the Process (Composition + Connection) ``` process implementation Building_Monitor.Impl subcomponents Sensor : thread Temp_Sensor_Reader; Alarm : thread Alarm_Controller; Buzzer : subprogram Buzzer_Sub; connections conn1 : port Sensor.Out_Temp -> Alarm.In_Temp; conn2 : subprogram access Alarm.Buzzer -> Buzzer; flows end_to_end : flow path Sensor.temp_source -> conn1 -> Alarm.alarm_flow; properties Period => 100 ms; end Building_Monitor.Impl; ``` --- #### Step 6 — Declare Processor and Memory Classifiers ``` processor RPi4_Processor features comm : requires bus access I2C_Bus; properties Processor_Capacity => 0.5 MIPS applies to comm; end RPi4_Processor; memory RPi4_Memory properties Memory_Size => 1 GByte; end RPi4_Memory; ``` --- #### Step 7 — Top-Level System Implementation ``` system implementation Temperature_App.Impl subcomponents Monitor : process Building_Monitor.Impl; CPU : processor RPi4_Processor; RAM : memory RPi4_Memory; Wire : bus I2C_Bus; connections mem_conn : memory access Monitor -> RAM; proc_conn: processor binding Monitor -> CPU; bus_conn : bus access CPU.comm -> Wire; properties Actual_Processor_Binding => (reference (CPU)) applies to Monitor; Actual_Memory_Binding => (reference (RAM)) applies to Monitor; end Temperature_App.Impl; ``` > This forms a **complete, validated AADL model** ready for OSATE tool analysis. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Schedulability Verification The KTU examiner will expect you to perform **schedulability analysis** when thread classifiers are present. ### Step 1 — Extract Properties from the Above Classifiers $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= 100 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{Sensor thread — periodic}) \\ C_1 &= 5 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{WCET of Sensor}) \\ T_2 &= 100 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{Alarm thread — sporadic, max rate}) \\ C_2 &= 3 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{WCET of Alarm}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Utilization Inequality $$ U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} $$ $$ U = \frac{5}{100} + \frac{3}{100} = \frac{8}{100} = 0.08 $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Liu–Layland Bound For $n = 2$: $$ U_{bound} = 2 \left( 2^{1/2} - 1 \right) $$ $$ \begin{aligned} U_{bound} &= 2 \left( \sqrt{2} - 1 \right) \\ &= 2 \times (1.41421356 - 1) \\ &= 2 \times 0.41421356 \\ &= 0.82842712 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Decision $$ U = 0.08 \quad \leq \quad U_{bound} = 0.8284 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Schedulable} $$ > **Conclusion:** The temperature monitoring system is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS). The classifier-based properties directly enabled this verification. --- ## 3.3 Mode Classifier Refinement — Logical Derivation AADL supports **operational modes**, which classify a system into a finite-state behavior. ### Mode Transition Derivation Consider a system with two modes: `Active` and `Standby`. The transition condition is derived from the property `activation_clause`: $$ \text{mode transition} : M_i \xrightarrow{\varphi(\text{trigger})} M_j $$ Where $\varphi$ is a Boolean predicate over event ports. For example, the transition from `Standby` to `Active` fires when the `power_on` event port is received. In AADL syntax: ``` system Safety_Unit features power_on : in event port; modes active : initial mode; standby : mode; mode_transitions standby -[ power_on ]-> active; active -[ power_off ]-> standby; end Safety_Unit; ``` ### Formal Derivation of Mode Reachability A mode classifier defines a **Directed Graph (DG)** of states: $$ DG = (M, E, \delta) $$ Where: - $M$ = set of modes $\{ active, standby \}$ - $E$ = set of triggering events $\{ power\_on, power\_off \}$ - $\delta : M \times E \to M$ = transition function The **reachability matrix** is computed as: $$ R = (I - A)^{-1} $$ Where $A$ is the adjacency matrix of the mode transition graph. For the example above: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ I - A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Determinant is $0$ (graph is cyclic). To resolve, we add self-loops for non-transitioning states: $$ I - A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(I - A) = 1 \times 2 - (-1)(-1) = 1 $$ $$ (I - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{1} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ > **Interpretation:** All modes are mutually reachable. The mode classifier satisfies the **safety property** of being able to enter any mode from any other mode in a finite number of transitions. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AADL Classifier Taxonomy — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD AADL[SAE AADL AS5506
Model Specification] CC[Component Classifiers] FC[Feature Classifiers] ConnC[Connection Classifiers] FlowC[Flow Classifiers] PropC[Property Classifiers] AADL --> CC AADL --> FC AADL --> ConnC AADL --> FlowC AADL --> PropC CC --> C1[system] CC --> C2[process] CC --> C3[thread] CC --> C4[subprogram] CC --> C5[data] CC --> C6[processor] CC --> C7[memory] CC --> C8[bus] CC --> C9[device] CC --> C10[abstract] CC --> C11[virtual processor] CC --> C12[virtual bus] CC --> C13[thread group] CC --> C14[subprogram group] C1 --> S1[Top-level integration] C2 --> S2[Memory address space] C3 --> S3[Schedulable unit] C6 --> S4[Execution platform] C7 --> S5[Storage element] C8 --> S6[Communication link] C9 --> S7[Sensor actuator] FC --> F1[event data port] FC --> F2[data port] FC --> F3[event port] FC --> F4[subprogram access] FC --> F5[data access] FC --> F6[parameter] ConnC --> N1[port connection] ConnC --> N2[subprogram access connection] ConnC --> N3[data access connection] FlowC --> FL1[flow source] FlowC --> FL2[flow sink] FlowC --> FL3[flow path] FlowC --> FL4[end to end flow] PropC --> P1[Timing - ms us ns] PropC --> P2[Memory - bytes kbytes] PropC --> P3[Deployment - cpu bus mem] PropC --> P4[Safety - security] ``` --- ## 4.2 Type vs. Implementation Classifier — Separation of Concerns ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Type_Classifier T1[External Interface] T2[Features - Ports] T3[Flow Specifications] T4[Property Constraints] end subgraph Implementation_Classifier I1[Subcomponents - Children] I2[Connections - Wiring] I3[Internal Flow Refinement] I4[Mode Transitions] end T1 --> I1 T2 --> I2 T3 --> I3 T4 --> I4 ``` > **Engineering Significance:** This separation enables **contract-driven design** — a *type* acts as a contract that an *implementation* must fulfill. This is the same principle as the **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** in object-oriented design. --- ## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — The Smart Building Temperature Monitor ```mermaid graph TD A[External Sensor Hardware] -->|Analog signal| B[Device Classifier
Temp_Hardware] B -->|I2C bus| C[Bus Classifier
I2C_Wire] C -->|Bus access| D[Processor Classifier
RPi4_CPU] E[Memory Classifier
RPi4_RAM] -.->|Memory binding| D D -->|Process execution| F[Process Classifier
Building_Monitor] F -->|Subcomponent| G[Thread Classifier
Temp_Sensor_Reader] F -->|Subcomponent| H[Thread Classifier
Alarm_Controller] F -->|Subcomponent| I[Subprogram Classifier
Buzzer_Sub] F -->|Subcomponent| J[Data Classifier
Temp_Data] G -->|Event data port| H H -->|Subprogram access| I H -->|GPIO actuation| K[External Actuator
Buzzer_HW] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a **classifier**. Arrows represent **connections** between features. Dashed arrows are **binding relationships** (processor/memory binding). --- ## 4.4 Scheduler Layer Architecture — Schedulability Analysis ```mermaid graph TD L1[Top System Classifier] --> L2[Process Classifier] L2 --> L3[Thread Group Classifier] L3 --> L4a[Thread A - Periodic] L3 --> L4b[Thread B - Sporadic] L3 --> L4c[Thread C - Periodic] L4a --> S1[Scheduler RMS] L4b --> S1 L4c --> S1 S1 --> P1[Utilization Check] P1 --> D1{Utilization less than bound} D1 -->|Yes| OK[Schedulable] D1 -->|No| FAIL[Not Schedulable] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define a *Classifier* in AADL. How is it different from a *Component Instance*?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Classifier** in AADL (SAE AS5506) is a **declarative type** that describes the structural and behavioral blueprint of a model element — its features, subcomponents, connections, flows, modes, and properties — without instantiating it. A **Component Instance** is the *concrete occurrence* of a classifier created during system binding and deployment. | Aspect | Classifier | Component Instance | |---|---|---| | Nature | Blueprint | Concrete occurrence | | Declared by | `classifier` keyword | Implicit during binding | | Reusability | High — multiple instances | Single occurrence | | Example | `process Safety_Monitor` | The running task on CPU #1 | > **Examiner's Note:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for example. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List the 13 standard AADL component categories and state one distinguishing feature of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A concise tabular answer earns full marks. Expected categories are: `system`, `process`, `thread`, `thread group`, `subprogram`, `data`, `subprogram group`, `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`, `abstract`, `virtual processor`, `virtual bus`. The 14th is sometimes considered `abstract`. > **Examiner's Note:** Listing all categories (2 marks) + giving one distinguishing feature (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *As per KTU 2024 scheme: each Part B question has an internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024] **(a)** Explain the **two-level classification** architecture in AADL with a suitable example. Distinguish between *type* classifiers and *implementation* classifiers in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a real-time embedded subsystem with **three periodic threads**, the classifier properties yield the following table: | Thread | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | |---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ | 50 | 10 | | $\tau_2$ | 100 | 25 | | $\tau_3$ | 200 | 50 | Verify **schedulability** using Rate Monotonic Analysis. If not schedulable, suggest **one architectural refinement** using AADL classifiers. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Complete Model Solution** #### Part (a) — Two-Level Classification The AADL standard mandates a **separation of concerns** through two distinct classifier levels: **1. Type Classifier (External View):** - Declares the **public interface** of a component - Contains: `features`, `flows`, `properties`, `modes` - Contains NO `subcomponents` and NO `connections` - Analogous to: **Java Interface** or **C++ pure virtual class** - Syntax: `process Process_Name ... end Process_Name;` **2. Implementation Classifier (Internal View):** - Declares the **internal composition** of a component - Contains: `subcomponents`, `connections`, internal `flows`, mode transitions - References the type using dotted notation: `Process_Name.Impl` - Multiple implementations per type are allowed - Analogous to: **Java concrete class implementing interface** **Example:** ``` process Sensor_Process -- TYPE classifier features data_out : out event data port Reading_Type; properties Period => 10 ms; end Sensor_Process; process implementation Sensor_Process.Basic -- IMPLEMENTATION subcomponents sampler : thread Sampler_Thread; connections c1 : port sampler.value -> data_out; end Sensor_Process.Basic; ``` **Distinction Table:** | Criterion | Type | Implementation | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Contract / Interface | Composition / Body | | Subcomponents | ❌ Forbidden | ✅ Allowed | | Connections | ❌ Forbidden | ✅ Allowed | | Features | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed (inherited) | | Naming | `Name` | `Name.Some_Name` | **(7 marks breakdown: 2 for definitions, 3 for differences, 2 for example.)** --- #### Part (b) — Schedulability Verification **Step 1 — Compute Total Utilization** $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{10}{50} + \frac{25}{100} + \frac{50}{200} $$ $$ U = 0.200 + 0.250 + 0.250 = 0.700 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Liu–Layland Bound for n = 3** $$ U_{bound} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) $$ $$ 2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} = 1.2599210 $$ $$ 2^{1/3} - 1 = 0.2599210 $$ $$ U_{bound} = 3 \times 0.2599210 = 0.7797630 $$ **Step 3 — Compare and Decide** $$ U = 0.700 \quad \leq \quad U_{bound} = 0.7798 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Schedulable under RMS} $$ **Step 4 — Response Time Verification (Optional Refinement)** For thread $\tau_1$: $$ R_1 = C_1 = 10 \text{ ms} $$ For thread $\tau_2$: $$ R_2 = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 $$ $$ R_2 = 25 + 1 \times 10 = 35 \text{ ms} \quad (< T_2 = 100 \text{ ms}) \quad \checkmark $$ For thread $\tau_3$: $$ R_3 = 50 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{50} \right\rceil \times 10 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{100} \right\rceil \times 25 $$ Try $R_3 = 95$: $$ R_3 = 50 + 2 \times 10 + 1 \times 25 = 95 \text{ ms} \quad (< T_3 = 200 \text{ ms}) \quad \checkmark $$ > **Conclusion:** The system is **schedulable** under RMS. The utilization is 70%, which is below the 77.98% bound. **Architectural Refinement (if needed):** - **Refine `thread` to `abstract`**: split high-WCET thread into two sub-threads. - **Add a `processor` classifier** with multiprocessor binding to reduce load. - **Reclassify a thread to `subprogram` and call it from a lower-period thread** to consolidate workload. **(7 marks breakdown: 3 for utilization calc, 2 for bound calc, 2 for conclusion/refinement.)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **hierarchical containment rules** of AADL component classifiers. Construct a valid containment tree for an **automotive Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** consisting of: an `ABS_System` with a `Brake_Process`, which contains a `Wheel_Thread` and an `Algorithm_Thread` that calls a `Compute_Slip` subprogram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between **feature classifiers, connection classifiers, and flow classifiers** in AADL.
