Give one example of each from a real-time safety-critical system. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Complete Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) — Containment Tree **Hierarchical Containment Rules:** AADL enforces that certain components may contain only certain other components. The relevant rules for software components are: - `system` may contain `process`, `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`, `abstract` - `process` may contain `thread`, `thread group`, `data`, `subprogram group`, `abstract` - `thread group` may contain `thread`, `data`, `abstract` - `thread` may contain `subprogram`, `data`, `abstract` - `subprogram` may contain `subprogram`, `data`, `abstract` - `processor` may contain `virtual processor`, `abstract` - `memory` may contain `data`, `abstract` - `bus` may contain `virtual bus`, `abstract` **Containment Tree for ABS:** ``` ABS_System (system) ├── Brake_Process (process) │ ├── Wheel_Thread (thread) │ │ ├── Compute_Slip (subprogram) │ │ └── Sensor_Data (data) │ └── Algorithm_Thread (thread) │ ├── Compute_Slip (subprogram, shared via access) │ └── Slip_Record (data) ├── ECU_Processor (processor) ├── Flash_Memory (memory) ├── CAN_Bus (bus) └── Wheel_Sensor (device) ``` **AADL Implementation Snippet:** ``` system implementation ABS_System.Impl subcomponents brake : process Brake_Process.Impl; ecu : processor ECU_Processor; flash : memory Flash_Memory; can : bus CAN_Bus; sensor_l : device Wheel_Sensor; sensor_r : device Wheel_Sensor; end ABS_System.Impl; process implementation Brake_Process.Impl subcomponents wheel_t : thread Wheel_Thread.Impl; algo_t : thread Algorithm_Thread.Impl; cs_sub : subprogram Compute_Slip; end Brake_Process.Impl; ``` **(7 marks breakdown: 2 for rules, 3 for tree, 2 for AADL snippet.)** --- #### Part (b) — Feature, Connection, and Flow Classifiers | Aspect | Feature Classifier | Connection Classifier | Flow Classifier | |---|---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Declares a port / interface element | Wires features between components | Describes end-to-end information propagation | | **Where declared** | Inside `features` clause | Inside `connections` clause | Inside `flows` clause | | **Direction** | `in`, `out`, `in out` | Source → Sink (directional) | `source`, `sink`, `path` | | **Granularity** | Single component | Across components | Across hierarchy | **Example — Aircraft Autopilot:** 1. **Feature Classifier:** ``` thread Autopilot_Controller features altitude_in : in event data port Altitude_Type; pitch_out : out event data port Pitch_Command; end Autopilot_Controller; ``` 2. **Connection Classifier:** ``` system implementation Airplane.Impl connections c1 : port sensor.altitude -> autopilot.altitude_in; c2 : port autopilot.pitch_out -> actuator.pitch; end Airplane.Impl; ``` 3. **Flow Classifier:** ``` process implementation Flight_Control.Impl flows e2e_pitch : flow path sensor.altitude -> c1 -> autopilot.pitch_out -> c2 -> actuator.pitch; end Flight_Control.Impl; ``` **(7 marks breakdown: 2 for definitions, 3 for tabular comparison, 2 for examples.)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls That Cause Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Confusing type vs. implementation** — a `type` cannot contain subcomponents; students often write both inside the type. > 2. **Invalid containment** — putting a `process` inside a `thread` is **illegal** and will cost 2–3 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the `end` keyword** — AADL is a fully named, block-structured language; every block needs `end BlockName;`. > 4. **Using `()` instead of dotted notation** — implementations are `Name.Impl`, not `Name(Impl)`. > 5. **Schematic dep** — The Liu–Layland bound is the **most-likely** 7-mark question. Memorize: $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. > 6. **Mixing logical and physical** — `abstract` is logical; `processor` and `memory` are physical. AADL does not allow a `thread` to be bound directly to a `memory` — binding must be `process → memory` or `system → memory`. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **Classifier** is the *type-level* blueprint in AADL; an **instance** is the concrete occurrence. - ✅ AADL has **13 standard component categories** plus `abstract`. - ✅ **Type classifier** = external interface; **Implementation classifier** = internal composition. - ✅ Multiple implementations can exist for a single type (a key AADL feature). - ✅ **Containment is hierarchical and strict** — a process cannot be inside a thread, etc. - ✅ **Features** are ports and access declarations; classified as `port`, `subprogram access`, `data access`, `parameter`, `abstract feature`. - ✅ **Connections** wire features; classified as `port connection`, `subprogram access connection`, `data access connection`, `parameter connection`. - ✅ **Flows** describe end-to-end semantics: `flow source`, `flow sink`, `flow path`, `end to end flow`. - ✅ **Properties** classify typed attributes: timing, memory, deployment, safety. - ✅ **Modes** classify operational states; transitions are triggered by event ports. - ✅ The **Liu–Layland schedulability bound** is $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — must know for KTU. - ✅ AADL analyses supported by **OSATE** (CMU SEI): schedulability, latency, safety, security, reliability. - ✅ AADL v1 is **SAE AS5506** (2004); current is **AS5506 Rev C (2017)**. - ✅ AADL supports **code generation** to C, Ada, Simulink — a major industry reason for adoption. - ✅ AADL is **declarative, not executable** — it models architecture, not algorithmic logic. > **Final Word:** Master the **type vs. implementation distinction**, the **13 categories**, and the **containment matrix**. These three alone cover **~60% of all KTU 2024 questions** on AADL Classifiers.

topics.title: AADL System Models and Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AADL System Models and Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AADL (Architecture Analysis and Design Language)** is a standardized, model-based engineering language standardized by the **SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers) under the AS5506 standard**, designed for the specification, analysis, automated code generation, and integration of real-time, embedded, distributed, and high-integrity software-intensive systems. AADL is a core **MBSE (Model-Based Systems Engineering)** notation adopted by avionics (DO-178C), automotive (AUTOSAR), and aerospace industries.

AADL provides a precise textual and graphical notation for describing both the **software architecture** (functional application) and the **execution platform** (hardware) of an embedded real-time system, together with a precise mapping between them (binding). It supports **analyzable specifications** through formal property declarations (timing, memory, fault, security) and supports extension through standardized **Annex sublanguages**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: AADL as the "Architect's Blueprint for a Smart Building"**

Imagine you are an architect designing a high-tech, real-time hospital building. You wouldn't just sketch a rough drawing — you would create a **precise, layered blueprint**:

- **Rooms & People** (Software components like `Process`, `Thread`, `Data`) → tell us *what* logic runs.
- **Electrical Wiring, HVAC, Servers** (Execution platform: `Processor`, `Memory`, `Bus`, `Device`) → tell us *where* and *on what* it runs.
- **Doors, Windows, Sensors** (`Ports` — `in`, `out`, `event`, `event data`) → tell us *how* information flows in and out.
- **Owner Manual / Specifications** (`Properties` like `Deadline`, `Period`, `Execution_Time`) → guarantee that elevators respond in 2 seconds, oxygen supply is uninterrupted, etc.
- **Safety Inspection Annexes** (Behaviour Annex, Error Model Annex) → extra rigorous documents for failure analysis and certification.

Just like an architect's blueprint is used to construct, verify, simulate, and certify a building, an AADL model is used to **simulate, analyze, generate code, verify schedulability, and certify** the embedded system. The plan is "the system" before the actual concrete system exists.

## 1.3 Standardization and Industry Relevance

| Standard / Industry Domain | AADL Usage | Verification Standard |
|---|---|---|
| SAE AS5506 (Core) | Base language definition | SAE International |
| Avionics | Flight control systems, DO-178C mapping | DO-178C / DO-331 |
| Aerospace | NASA cFS, unmanned aerial vehicles | DO-254, ECSS |
| Automotive | AUTOSAR integration | ISO 26262 |
| Defense | Mission-critical real-time systems | MIL-STD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAE AS5506** is the official standard identifier. AADL v2 was released in 2004, with continuous evolution including v3 drafts focusing on enhanced modularity, **behavioral semantics**, and **formal verification** (co-simulation, theorem proving).

## 1.4 Why AADL? The MBSE Justification

Traditional embedded systems are specified via **unstructured natural language** (e.g., an SRS document) and built using hand-written C/Ada. This leads to:
- Ambiguity at the requirements–design boundary.
- Late discovery of timing/scheduling errors.
- Manual, error-prone hardware–software binding.

AADL solves this by:
1. **Separation of concerns** (software vs. platform vs. binding).
2. **Explicit, analyzable properties** (deadlines, budgets).
3. **Tool-supported analysis** (schedulability, latency, fault tree, security).
4. **Code generation** (Ada, C, Simulink) for rapid prototyping.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Component Categories of AADL

AADL organizes all system elements into **three primary categories**, with a fourth "composite" category for hierarchy:

> [!NOTE]
> The language is **typed** — every component belongs to exactly one *category* (a sub-language type), with a corresponding *implementation* and *subcomponent instance*.

### A. Software Components (Application / Functional Side)

These model the **executable, data, and computational** aspects of the application. They are typically *bound* to execution platform components.

| Component | Symbol | Purpose | Key Property Anchors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process** | Oval | Address space, memory protection boundary; contains threads | `Allowed_Processor_Binding` |
| **Thread** | Pill/Capsule | Unit of concurrent execution / schedulable activity | `Deadline`, `Period`, `Dispatch_Protocol` |
| **Thread Group** | Capsule cluster | Logical grouping of threads for analysis | Grouping only |
| **Data** | Cylinder | Static data type / data component instance | `Data_Size`, `Data_Representation` |
| **Subprogram** | Rectangle (rounded) | Callable sequential code unit | `Source_Code_Size`, `Execution_Time` |
| **Subprogram Group** | Cluster | Library of subprograms (e.g., math library) | Grouping |
| **Subprogram Call / Parameter** | implicit | Invocation/argument features of subprograms | — |

### B. Execution Platform (Hardware) Components

These model the **physical / virtual hardware** on which software executes.

| Component | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Processor** | Rectangle with chip | Schedules and executes threads (CPU, MCU, FPGA soft-core) |
| **Memory** | Rectangle with stripes | Stores code/data; bound to subprograms/data |
| **Bus** | Thick arrow / line | Communication medium (CAN, AFDX, Ethernet, PCIe) |
| **Virtual Processor / Virtual Bus** | same | Hierarchical composition of processors/buses |
| **Device** | Trapezoid | Sensors, actuators, I/O peripherals |

### C. System Component

A `system` is the **top-level composite** that aggregates both software and hardware components. It is the *root* of an AADL model.

### D. Abstract Component

A `data` or `system` placeholder used early in design when concrete classification is not yet known.

## 2.2 Features — The "Interfaces" of Components

Features are **typed interaction points** of an AADL component. They are the typed ports/doors by which components exchange data, events, and call services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AADL features are **strongly typed** (must declare a feature classifier, usually a `data` component). They are *not* generic C-style function signatures — they have execution semantics (event vs. data).

### 2.2.1 Port Features

Ports are the **primary data/event exchange points** in AADL. They come in several flavors:

| Port Type | Direction | Semantic | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `data port` | in, out, in out | Atomic data transfer on dispatch | Sensor value, control set-point |
| `event port` | in, out | Discrete event signal (no payload) | Trigger, alarm, interrupt |
| `event data port` | in, out, in out | Queued event with data payload | Async message with payload |
| `event data port` (in) with queue | in | Buffered message queue | Logging input, network packets |

The declared port direction must match the connection direction in the parent component.

### 2.2.2 Access Features (Service Calls & Data Sharing)

| Feature | Purpose | Bound to |
|---|---|---|
| `provides subprogram access` | Component offers a callable service | Subprogram component |
| `requires subprogram access` | Component needs to call a service | Subprogram (via `subprogram call`) |
| `provides data access` | Exposes shared data | Data component |
| `requires data access` | Reads shared data | Data component |
| `provides bus access` | Exposes a bus | Bus component |
| `requires bus access` | Needs a bus | Bus component |
| `feature group` | Bundle of related features | Composite feature classifier |

### 2.2.3 Abstract Features

For untyped/classifier-less interaction (used in early design).

## 2.3 Properties — The "Non-Functional Contract"

Properties are **typed, name-value pairs** that declare non-functional attributes. They are the foundation of AADL's *analyzability*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every property has a **type** (e.g., `Time`, `Range`, `Integer`, `Classifier`, `Reference`, `List of …`, `Boolean`, `String`), a **name**, an optional **applies-to** clause, and a **value**.

### 2.3.1 Predefined Standard Property Sets

| Property Set | Example Properties | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `Timing_Properties` | `Period`, `Deadline`, `Execution_Time`, `Compute_Execution_Time` | Real-time scheduling analysis |
| `Memory_Properties` | `Source_Code_Size`, `Data_Size`, `Stack_Size`, `Heap_Size` | Memory budget analysis |
| `Communication_Properties` | `Queue_Size`, `Required_Connection`, `Latency` | Communication/queue analysis |
| `Deployment_Properties` | `Actual_Processor_Binding`, `Actual_Memory_Binding`, `Allowed_Connection` | HW/SW binding |
| `Thread_Properties` | `Dispatch_Protocol`, `Priority`, `POSIX_Scheduling_Policy` | Thread semantics |
| `Process_Properties` | `Execution_Platform`, `Allowed_Processor_Binding` | Process containment |
| `AADL_Project` | `Name`, `Version`, `Description` | Documentation |

### 2.3.2 Property Type System

AADL properties are statically type-checked. Common types include:
- `aadlboolean`, `aadlinteger`, `aadlreal`, `aadlstring`
- `time` (units: ps, ns, us, ms, sec, min, hr)
- `range` (value range with units)
- `classifier` (reference to a component category)
- `list of <type>`
- `record` (`[Field1 => val1; Field2 => val2;]`)

## 2.4 Connections — Wiring the Architecture

AADL connections are **explicit, named wires** between compatible features. They are typed by their source and destination feature categories.

> [!NOTE]
> A connection is a **first-class modeling element** (not just a graphical line). It has its own properties (latency, security level) and is analyzable.

Connection types:
1. **Port connection** (`port x -> y;`) — data/event/data-event port to port.
2. **Parameter connection** (`x: in parameter "param";`) — subprogram argument.
3. **Access connection** (`x: requires data access "shared";`) — data access delegation.
4. **Feature group connection** — bundles connections across composition boundaries.
5. **Bus access connection** — connects a bus-required component to a bus.

Connection patterns:
- **One-to-one** (1 → 1)
- **One-to-many** (broadcast: 1 → N)
- **Many-to-one** (multiplexed)
- **Many-to-many** (bus-implicit)

## 2.5 Flows — End-to-End Logical Information Streams

Flows model **logical data and control streams** that traverse multiple components. They are *analyzable* for end-to-end latency, jittter, and security.

```
flow Src -> A.in_port -> A.out_port -> B.in_port -> B.out_port -> Snk;
```

Two kinds:
- **Flow source**: starting point (typically a sensor input or event input).
- **Flow sink**: terminating point (typically an actuator output or display).

Tools compute **flow latency** by summing `Latency` properties on connections along the flow path.

## 2.6 Modes and Mode Transitions

Real-time systems operate in distinct **operational states** (e.g., INIT, NORMAL, DEGRADED, EMERGENCY). AADL `modes` capture this.

```
modes
  init   : initial mode;
  normal : mode;
  emerg  : mode;
  init -[ switch_on ]-> normal;
  normal -[ fault ]-> emerg;
```

- Modes can have **mode-specific subcomponents, features, connections, flows, properties**.
- Mode transitions may have **transition triggers** (events, port values, timeouts).
- Modes are essential for **fault-tolerant and reconfigurable systems**.

## 2.7 Bindings — Deployment & Allocation

AADL distinguishes three binding relationships:

| Binding | Syntax Concept | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Process → Processor | `Actual_Processor_Binding` | Where processes run |
| Data/Subprogram → Memory | `Actual_Memory_Binding` | Code/data placement |
| Connection → Bus | `Actual_Connection_Binding` | Communication channel |

This **explicit binding** is critical for **performance, partitioning, and safety** certification (e.g., ARINC 653 partitioning in avionics).

## 2.8 Annexes — Extending AADL

The base AADL core is intentionally minimal. Power comes from **annexes** (sub-languages attached to AADL).

| Annex | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Behavior Annex** | Sequential and state-based behavior (state machines) |
| **Error Model Annex** | Fault propagation, error states, FMEA |
| **ARINC653 Annex** | Avionics partitioning (IMA) |
| **BA (Behavior) Annex** | Algorithm-level behavior |
| **Security Annex** | MLS (multi-level security) annotations |
| **Data Modeling Annex** | Refined data type modeling |
| **Code Generation Annex** | Custom code-gen mapping |

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Summary Table (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Definition | KTU 2024 Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AADL Standard | SAE AS5506 | **High** |
| 2 | Software Categories | `process`, `thread`, `thread group`, `data`, `subprogram` | **High** |
| 3 | Hardware Categories | `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`, `virtual processor` | **High** |
| 4 | System Category | `system` (top-level composite) | **High** |
| 5 | Port types | `event`, `data`, `event data` (in/out/in out) | **High** |
| 6 | Access features | `subprogram access`, `data access`, `bus access` | Medium |
| 7 | Properties | `Timing_Properties`, `Memory_Properties`, `Deployment_Properties` | **High** |
| 8 | Connections | `port connection`, `access connection`, `parameter connection` | **High** |
| 9 | Flows | End-to-end logical data streams with latency | Medium |
| 10 | Modes | Operational state machines with mode-specific elements | Medium |
| 11 | Bindings | `Actual_Processor_Binding`, `Actual_Memory_Binding` | **High** |
| 12 | Annexes | `Behavior`, `Error Model`, `ARINC653`, `Security` | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Constructing an AADL System Model

This section walks through the **systematic construction of a canonical AADL system model** — a real-time temperature monitoring system with a sensor, controller, and display. The objective is to demonstrate *how* AADL's category system, features, properties, connections, flows, and bindings are assembled into a complete, analyzable specification.

> [!NOTE]
> Each analytical step corresponds to a KTU exam scenario. Understand **why** each element exists, not just memorize syntax.

## Step 1 — Identify Component Categories

The system has:
- A **temperature sensor** (hardware device) → `device` category.
- A **microcontroller** (hardware) → `processor`.
- A **control application** (software) → `process` containing `thread` and `data` components.
- A **display** (hardware device) → `device`.
- A **wiring bus** (e.g., SPI/I2C) → `bus`.

```
System constituents:
  Hardware : 2 devices + 1 processor + 1 bus
  Software : 1 process → 1 thread + 1 data
  System   : top-level composite
```

## Step 2 — Declare Type and Implementation for Each Component

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AADL distinguishes **type** (interface declaration) from **implementation** (internal structure + properties). This two-level declaration is a core exam point.

### Step 2.1 — Hardware Component Declarations

```
-- (1) Temperature sensor device type
device Temperature_Sensor
  features
    raw_data : out data port Temperature_Reading;
  flows
    src : flow source raw_data;
end Temperature_Sensor;

device implementation Temperature_Sensor.impl
  properties
    Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic;
    Period => 10 ms;
    Latency => 1 ms .. 2 ms;
end Temperature_Sensor.impl;

-- (2) Display device
device Display
  features
    display_input : in event data port Display_Message;
  flows
    sink : flow sink display_input;
end Display;

device implementation Display.impl
  properties
    Latency => 5 ms .. 10 ms;
end Display.impl;

-- (3) Microcontroller processor
processor MicroController
  features
    spi_bus : requires bus access SPI_Bus;
end MicroController;

processor implementation MicroController.arm_cortex_m4
  properties
    Scheduling_Protocol => (Rate_Monotonic_Protocol);
    Clock_Period => 1 ms;
    Processor_Capacity => 1.0;
end MicroController.arm_cortex_m4;

-- (4) SPI Bus
bus SPI_Bus
end SPI_Bus;

bus implementation SPI_Bus.standard
  properties
    Bus_Properties::BandWidth => 1.0 Mbps;
    Transmission_Time => [Fixed => 0.5 ms..1 ms;];
end SPI_Bus.standard;
```

### Step 2.2 — Software Component Declarations

> [!NOTE]
> The `data` type `Temperature_Reading` is referenced as the port classifier. This enforces **strong typing** of data across the model.

```
-- Data type
data Temperature_Reading
  properties
    Data_Size => 4 Bytes;
end Temperature_Reading;

data Display_Message
  properties
    Data_Size => 32 Bytes;
end Display_Message;

-- Subprogram for control logic
subprogram Compute_Control
  features
    reading  : in parameter Temperature_Reading;
    command  : out parameter Display_Message;
end Compute_Control;

subprogram implementation Compute_Control.simple
  properties
    Source_Code_Size => 2 KBytes;
    Execution_Time   => 50 us .. 200 us;
end Compute_Control.simple;

-- Thread that periodically runs the control subprogram
thread Controller_Thread
  features
    sensor_in   : in event data port Temperature_Reading;
    display_out : out event data port Display_Message;
  flows
    flow_path   : flow path sensor_in -> display_out;
end Controller_Thread;

thread implementation Controller_Thread.periodic
  calls
    call_seq : {
      compute : subprogram Compute_Control.simple;
    };
  connections
    param1 : parameter sensor_in -> compute.reading;
    param2 : parameter compute.command -> display_out;
  properties
    Dispatch_Protocol  => Periodic;
    Period             => 50 ms;
    Deadline           => 40 ms;
    Priority           => 5;
    Compute_Execution_Time => 50 us .. 200 us;
    Stack_Size         => 4 KByte;
end Controller_Thread.periodic;

-- Process (address space) containing the thread
process Control_Process
  features
    sensor_if   : in event data port Temperature_Reading;
    display_if  : out event data port Display_Message;
end Control_Process;

process implementation Control_Process.simple
  subcomponents
    ctrl_thread : thread Controller_Thread.periodic;
  connections
    p1 : port sensor_if  -> ctrl_thread.sensor_in;
    p2 : port ctrl_thread.display_out -> display_if;
  properties
    Allowed_Processor_Binding => (reference (MicroController));
end Control_Process.simple;
```

## Step 3 — Declare the System Type and Implementation (Composite)

The system composite integrates software and hardware and declares **flows** and **connections** across the system boundary.

```
system Temperature_Monitor
end Temperature_Monitor;

system implementation Temperature_Monitor.final
  subcomponents
    sensor   : device Temperature_Sensor.impl;
    display  : device Display.impl;
    cpu      : processor MicroController.arm_cortex_m4;
    bus1     : bus SPI_Bus.standard;
    app      : process Control_Process.simple;
  connections
    c1 : port sensor.raw_data    -> app.sensor_if;
    c2 : port app.display_if     -> display.display_input;
    c3 : bus access bus1  <-> cpu.spi_bus;
  flows
    sensor_to_display : flow path sensor.raw_data ->
                        app.sensor_if -> app.display_if ->
                        display.display_input;
  properties
    Actual_Processor_Binding => (reference (cpu)) applies to app;
end Temperature_Monitor.final;
```

## Step 4 — Analytical Interpretation of the Constructed Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Once the model is complete, **analyzers** can perform several mathematically rigorous analyses without further user input.

### 4.1 End-to-End Latency Computation

For the flow `sensor_to_display`:

$$
L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{conn,c1} + L_{process} + L_{conn,c2} + L_{display}
$$

$$
L_{total} = (1 \text{ ms} .. 2 \text{ ms}) + 0 + (50 \,\mu\text{s} .. 200 \,\mu\text{s}) + 0 + (5 \text{ ms} .. 10 \text{ ms})
$$

$$
L_{total} = 6.05 \text{ ms} .. 12.2 \text{ ms}
$$

This latency is then **compared with the `Deadline` property** to verify real-time correctness.

### 4.2 Schedulability (Rate Monotonic Analysis)

Utilization check for the single thread:

$$
U = \frac{C}{T} = \frac{200 \,\mu\text{s}}{50 \text{ ms}} = 0.004 = 0.4\%
$$

For $n$ threads under Rate-Monotonic scheduling, sufficient condition is:

$$
U \le n(\sqrt[n]{2} - 1)
$$

For $n = 1$: $U \le 1.0$ — satisfied.

### 4.3 Memory Footprint

$$
M_{total} = M_{subprogram} + M_{stack} + M_{data}
$$

$$
M_{total} = 2 \text{ KB} + 4 \text{ KB} + (4 \text{ B} + 32 \text{ B}) \approx 6.04 \text{ KB}
$$

### 4.4 Schedulability Constraint on End-to-End Latency vs. Deadline

A real-time assertion: the end-to-end latency must be strictly less than the period (or the explicit deadline of the system's worst-case flow).

$$
L_{total,max} < T_{controller} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
12.2 \text{ ms} \ll 50 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Schedulable and meets the deadline.}
$$

## Step 5 — Verification Summary

| Analysis | Result | Tool Support |
|---|---|---|
| Type checking | ✅ | OSATE (Open Source AADL Tool Environment) |
| Flow latency | 6.05 – 12.2 ms ✅ | OSATE Flow Latency Analysis |
| Schedulability (RMA) | 0.4% utilization ✅ | OSATE / MAST / Cheddar |
| Memory budget | 6.04 KB ✅ | OSATE Memory Analysis |
| Binding completeness | ✅ All processes bound | OSATE Resolute |
| Fault model | Requires Error Model Annex | OSATE EMV2 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AADL Component Category Hierarchy

graph TD

    P2 --> P3["Phase 3 AADL\nImplementation\nSubcomponents and connections"]
    P3 --> P4["Phase 4 Property\nPopulation\nTiming memory properties"]
    P4 --> P5["Phase 5 Binding\nHW SW allocation"]
    P5 --> P6["Phase 6 Analysis\nLatency schedulability memory"]
    P6 --> P7["Phase 7 Code\nGeneration\nAda C C++"]
    P7 --> P8["Phase 8 Verification\nand Certification\nDO-178C ISO 26262"]
    P6 -->|model refinement| P2

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test direct definitional and conceptual recall. Answers must be **concise, precise, and use standard AADL terminology** (SAE AS5506).

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define AADL. List the three main categories of AADL components with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**AADL (Architecture Analysis and Design Language)** is a SAE-standardized (AS5506) model-based language used for the specification, analysis, and code generation of real-time, embedded, distributed systems.

The three main categories are:

1. **Software Components** — e.g., `process`, `thread`, `data`, `subprogram`.
2. **Execution Platform (Hardware) Components** — e.g., `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`.
3. **System Component** — `system` (top-level composite integrating software and hardware).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Differentiate between `data port`, `event port`, and `event data port` in AADL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Port Type | Payload | Transmission Trigger | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `data port` | Yes (atomic) | Synchronous with dispatch | Sensor value, control variable |
| `event port` | No | Asynchronous (discrete signal) | Triggers, alarms, interrupts |
| `event data port` | Yes (queued) | Asynchronous with data | Async message with payload, e.g., log messages |

`event data port` (incoming) typically carries a queue of messages, whereas `data port` carries the latest value.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **module-internal choice** in Part B. Two alternatives (A and B) are provided; the student answers **one**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — AADL System Model Construction

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the major AADL component categories and the distinction between AADL *type* declaration and *implementation* declaration with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a real-time **fire detection and alarm system** with a smoke sensor, a fire controller thread (subprogram: `Evaluate_Threshold`), a buzzer device, and an LED display. Construct a complete AADL system model specification with:
- All hardware and software component types and implementations
- A data type `Alarm_State` and `Smoke_Concentration`
- A `Periodic` thread with `Period = 100 ms` and `Deadline = 50 ms`
- A flow path from sensor to buzzer
- Proper bindings

State two analyses that the constructed model enables. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Component Categories & Type/Implementation Distinction [7 marks]:**

AADL classifies components into three primary categories:

1. **Software components** model the *application* (functional) side:
   - `process`: address space, memory boundary.
   - `thread`: concurrent, schedulable execution unit.
   - `data`: typed data.
   - `subprogram`: callable code unit.
   - `thread group` / `subprogram group`: logical aggregations.

2. **Execution Platform (Hardware) components** model the *physical* infrastructure:
   - `processor`: CPU/MCU executing threads.
   - `memory`: code/data storage.
   - `bus`: communication medium (CAN, AFDX, SPI).
   - `device`: sensor/actuator.

3. **System component**: top-level composite aggregating software and hardware.

**Type vs. Implementation:**

- The **type** declares the *external interface* (features, flows, modes) without revealing internal structure.
- The **implementation** declares the *internal structure*: subcomponents, connections, modes, properties, flows.

This two-level approach supports **information hiding, multiple implementations, and substitutability** — analogous to header vs. implementation in C/C++.

**Example:**

```
-- Type (interface)
thread Sensor_Thread
  features
    trig : in event port;
end Sensor_Thread;

-- Implementation (internals)
thread implementation Sensor_Thread.periodic
  properties
    Dispatch_Protocol => Periodic;
    Period            => 20 ms;
end Sensor_Thread.periodic;
```

A single `Sensor_Thread` type can have multiple implementations (`.periodic`, `.sporadic`, `.aperiodic`) — supporting design alternatives and reuse.

---

**(b) Fire Detection AADL System Model [7 marks]:**

```
package Fire_Alarm
public
  with Base_Types;
  with Timing_Properties;
  with Deployment_Properties;
  with Communication_Properties;

  -- (i) Data types
  data Smoke_Concentration
    properties
      Data_Size => 4 Bytes;
  end Smoke_Concentration;

  data Alarm_State
    properties
      Data_Size => 2 Bytes;
  end Alarm_State;

  -- (ii) Subprogram: threshold evaluation
  subprogram Evaluate_Threshold
    features
      smoke   : in  parameter Smoke_Concentration;
      alarm   : out parameter Alarm_State;
  end Evaluate_Threshold;

  subprogram implementation Evaluate_Threshold.simple
    properties
      Source_Code_Size => 3 KBytes;
      Execution_Time   => 100 us .. 500 us;
  end Evaluate_Threshold.simple;

  -- (iii) Smoke sensor (device)
  device Smoke_Sensor
    features
      reading : out data port Smoke_Concentration;
    flows
      src     : flow source reading;
  end Smoke_Sensor;

  device implementation Smoke_Sensor.analog
    properties
      Period            => 50 ms;
      Latency           => 0 ms .. 2 ms;
  end Smoke_Sensor.analog;

  -- (iv) Buzzer actuator (device)
  device Buzzer
    features
      alarm_in : in event data port Alarm_State;
    flows
      sink     : flow sink alarm_in;
  end Buzzer;

  device implementation Buzzer.impl
    properties
      Latency => 1 ms .. 3 ms;
  end Buzzer.impl;

  -- (v) LED Display (device)
  device LED_Display
    features
      state_in : in event data port Alarm_State;
  end LED_Display;

  -- (vi) Processor
  processor ARM_Cortex
  end ARM_Cortex;

  processor implementation ARM_Cortex.m4
    properties
      Scheduling_Protocol => (Rate_Monotonic_Protocol);
      Clock_Period        => 1 ms;
  end ARM_Cortex.m4;

  -- (vii) Thread (control logic)
  thread Fire_Controller
    features
      sensor_in   : in  data port Smoke_Concentration;
      buzzer_out  : out event data port Alarm_State;
      display_out : out event data port Alarm_State;
    flows
      to_buzzer   : flow path sensor_in -> buzzer_out;
  end Fire_Controller;

  thread implementation Fire_Controller.periodic
    calls
      call_seq : {
        eval : subprogram Evaluate_Threshold.simple;
      };
    connections
      c1 : parameter sensor_in -> eval.smoke;
      c2 : parameter eval.alarm -> buzzer_out;
      c3 : parameter eval.alarm -> display_out;
    properties
      Dispatch_Protocol     => Periodic;
      Period                => 100 ms;
      Deadline              => 50 ms;
      Priority              => 10;
      Compute_Execution_Time => 100 us .. 500 us;
      Stack_Size            => 4 KByte;
  end Fire_Controller.periodic;

  -- (viii) Process
  process Fire_App
    features
      smoke_in  : in  data port Smoke_Concentration;
      buzzer_if : out event data port Alarm_State;
      led_if    : out event data port Alarm_State;
  end Fire_App;

  process implementation Fire_App.simple
    subcomponents
      ctrl : thread Fire_Controller.periodic;
    connections
      p1 : port smoke_in        -> ctrl.sensor_in;
      p2 : port ctrl.buzzer_out  -> buzzer_if;
      p3 : port ctrl.display_out -> led_if;
    properties
      Allowed_Processor_Binding => (reference (ARM_Cortex));
  end Fire_App.simple;

  -- (ix) System composite
  system Fire_Alarm_System
  end Fire_Alarm_System;

  system implementation Fire_Alarm_System.deployed
    subcomponents
      sensor    : device Smoke_Sensor.analog;
      buzzer    : device Buzzer.impl;
      led       : device LED_Display;
      cpu       : processor ARM_Cortex.m4;
      fire_app  : process Fire_App.simple;
    connections
      c1 : port sensor.reading     -> fire_app.smoke_in;
      c2 : port fire_app.buzzer_if -> buzzer.alarm_in;
      c3 : port fire_app.led_if    -> led.state_in;
    flows
      smoke_to_buzzer : flow path sensor.reading ->
                        fire_app.smoke_in ->
                        fire_app.buzzer_if ->
                        buzzer.alarm_in;
    properties
      Actual_Processor_Binding => (reference (cpu)) applies to fire_app;
  end Fire_Alarm_System.deployed;

end Fire_Alarm;
```

**Two Enabled Analyses (must mention any 2):**

1. **End-to-End Flow Latency Analysis** — total latency from sensor to buzzer = 2 ms + (100 – 500 µs) + 3 ms = 5.1 ms – 5.5 ms, compared against `Deadline = 50 ms` (satisfies the real-time constraint).
2. **Schedulability Analysis (Rate-Monotonic)** — utilization $U = 500 \mu s / 100 ms = 0.5\% \ll 0.693$ (Liu & Layland bound for $n = 1$), so the system is schedulable.
3. **Memory Budget Analysis** — `Source_Code_Size = 3 KB + Stack_Size = 4 KB = 7 KB` — verifies the microcontroller's flash/RAM capacity.
4. **Binding Consistency Analysis** — verifies that every process has an `Actual_Processor_Binding` to `ARM_Cortex`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — AADL Features, Properties & Connections

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various types of **port features** in AADL with syntax examples. Differentiate between **port features** and **access features** with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following scenario: a **vehicle anti-lock braking system (ABS)** has an ECU (processor), a wheel speed sensor, a brake actuator, and a CAN bus. The ECU runs a periodic thread (Period = 5 ms, Deadline = 4 ms) that reads wheel speed, computes brake pressure, and applies it via the actuator. Specify the **timing properties, memory properties, deployment properties, and the flow path from sensor to actuator** using AADL property syntax. Justify the choice of `Periodic Dispatch_Protocol` for this critical control loop. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Port Features and Access Features [7 marks]:**

**Port Features** are typed interaction points of AADL components that exchange *data* and *events*. There are three primary kinds:

1. **Data Port** — atomic data transfer synchronized with thread dispatch.
   ```
   features
     setpoint : in  data port Float;
     measured : out data port Float;
   ```

2. **Event Port** — discrete asynchronous event signal with no payload.
   ```
   features
     trigger : in  event port;
     done    : out event port;
   ```

3. **Event Data Port** — asynchronous event with a queued data payload.
   ```
   features
     msg : in  event data port Network_Packet;
   ```
   (When incoming, an `event data port` is typically queued using `Queue_Size`.)

**Direction qualifiers:** `in`, `out`, `in out` (bidirectional).

**Access Features** model *service* and *shared data* relationships:

1. **Subprogram access** — exposes a callable service:
   ```
   features
     log_service : provides subprogram access Log;
     log_user    : requires subprogram access Log;
   ```

2. **Data access** — exposes shared data:
   ```
   features
     shared_state : requires data access Global_State;
   ```

3. **Bus access** — exposes a bus for communication:
   ```
   features
     can : requires bus access CAN;
   ```

**Key Differences [Table]:**

| Aspect | Port Feature | Access Feature |
|---|---|---|
| Communication | Discrete message passing | Service / shared resource |
| Data flow | One-way or bidirectional, atomic | Call-and-return or shared access |
| Examples | `data port`, `event port` | `subprogram access`, `data access` |
| Latency | Bounded by `Latency` property | Bounded by call/return time |
| Used in | Real-time inter-component I/O | Library access, shared state |

---

**(b) ABS AADL Property Specification [7 marks]:**

```
package ABS_System
public
  with Timing_Properties;
  with Memory_Properties;
  with Deployment_Properties;
  with Communication_Properties;

  data Wheel_Speed
    properties
      Data_Size => 2 Bytes;
  end Wheel_Speed;

  data Brake_Pressure
    properties
      Data_Size => 2 Bytes;
  end Brake_Pressure;

  thread ABS_Controller
    features
      ws_in   : in  data port Wheel_Speed;
      bp_out  : out data port Brake_Pressure;
  end ABS_Controller;

  thread implementation ABS_Controller.periodic
    properties
      Timing_Properties::Dispatch_Protocol     => Periodic;
      Timing_Properties::Period                => 5 ms;
      Timing_Properties::Deadline              => 4 ms;
      Timing_Properties::Compute_Execution_Time => 100 us .. 800 us;
      Thread_Properties::Priority              => 100;
      Memory_Properties::Stack_Size            => 8 KByte;
      Memory_Properties::Heap_Size             => 2 KByte;
      Memory_Properties::Data_Size             => 4 KByte;
      Communication_Properties::Queue_Size     => 1;
  end ABS_Controller.periodic;

  process ABS_App
    features
      ws_in  : in  data port Wheel_Speed;
      bp_out : out data port Brake_Pressure;
  end ABS_App;

  process implementation ABS_App.simple
    subcomponents
      ctrl : thread ABS_Controller.periodic;
    connections
      c1 : port ws_in     -> ctrl.ws_in;
      c2 : port ctrl.bp_out -> bp_out;
  end ABS_App.simple;

  processor ECU
  end ECU;
  processor implementation ECU.mpc5xxx
    properties
      Deployment_Properties::Scheduling_Protocol => (Rate_Monotonic_Protocol);
      Deployment_Properties::Clock_Period         => 1 ms;
  end ECU.mpc5xxx;

  device Wheel_Sensor
    features
      ws : out data port Wheel_Speed;
    flows
      src : flow source ws;
  end Wheel_Sensor;

  device Brake_Actuator
    features
      bp : in data port Brake_Pressure;
    flows
      sink : flow sink bp;
  end Brake_Actuator;

  bus CAN
  end CAN;

  system ABS_System
  end ABS_System;

  system implementation ABS_System.final
    subcomponents
      ws_dev   : device Wheel_Sensor;
      ba_dev   : device Brake_Actuator;
      ecu      : processor ECU.mpc5xxx;
      can_bus  : bus CAN;
      app      : process ABS_App.simple;
    connections
      c1 : port ws_dev.ws    -> app.ws_in;
      c2 : port app.bp_out   -> ba_dev.bp;
      c3 : bus access can_bus <-> ecu.can;
    flows
      ws_to_bp : flow path ws_dev.ws -> app.ws_in ->
                  app.bp_out -> ba_dev.bp;
    properties
      Deployment_Properties::Actual_Processor_Binding =>
        (reference (ecu)) applies to app;
  end ABS_System.final;

end ABS_System;
```

**Justification of `Periodic Dispatch_Protocol`:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Periodic dispatch** is chosen because the ABS loop is a **time-triggered control system** where:
> 1. Wheel-speed sampling and brake-pressure adjustment occur at a **fixed sampling rate** ($f = 1/T = 200$ Hz).
> 2. **Determinism** is critical: the period (5 ms) and deadline (4 ms) are derived from the Nyquist sampling requirement for wheel-speed variations during hard braking.
> 3. Rate-Monotonic scheduling (with `Priority = 100`) ensures the ABS thread is **never preempted by lower-criticality tasks** (e.g., infotainment).
> 4. Periodic dispatch enables **flow latency analysis** with a guaranteed worst-case bound:
>
> $$
> L_{worst} = T_{sensor} + T_{execute,max} + T_{actuator} = 2 + 0.8 + 2 = 4.8 \text{ ms} < 5 \text{ ms (Period)}
> $$

The flow `ws_to_bp` therefore is *always* completed within one period — a hard real-time correctness condition.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in AADL Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "type" and "implementation" declarations** — AADL requires `package_name.type` and `package_name.implementation` declarations to be **paired** and use **distinctive naming**. Many students write properties in the *type* declaration (which is wrong — properties belong in the *implementation*). **(−2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Wrong port directionality** — A `port` connection `x -> y` requires compatible directions (an `out` port cannot connect to another `out` port). Students often write `port connection app.x -> y.z` without considering that `y.z` must be `in` or `in out`. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Mixing up port types** — Using `event port` where data must be passed (no payload!) or using `data port` for asynchronous triggering (synchronous-only with dispatch!). **(−1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Forgetting `applies to` clause in properties** — A `property X => value;` without `applies to (subcomponent);` in a system-wide context causes *binding ambiguity*. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Wrong category classification** — Calling a `subprogram` a "thread" or a `device` a "processor". Always memorize the official AADL category names from SAE AS5506. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Skipping unit suffixes** — Writing `Period => 5;` instead of `Period => 5 ms;`. AADL type system requires explicit units for `time` properties. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 7. **Missing flow declarations** — KTU questions often ask for **flow path** — students forget that flows must be declared in *both* the type and implementation, with matching identifiers. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 8. **Incomplete binding** — A complete AADL model needs `Actual_Processor_Binding` for every process. Students often forget to bind processes to processors. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist** — Memorize these bullets before the KTU exam.

### Core Knowledge Bullets

- ✅ AADL = **SAE AS5506** standard for embedded real-time system modeling.
- ✅ AADL is part of the **MBSE** paradigm; used in avionics, automotive, aerospace.
- ✅ **Three primary categories**: Software (`process`, `thread`, `data`, `subprogram`), Execution Platform (`processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`), and `system` composite.
- ✅ **Type** declares the *interface*; **Implementation** declares *internals* + properties.
- ✅ **Port features**: `data port`, `event port`, `event data port` (with directions `in`, `out`, `in out`).
- ✅ **Access features**: `subprogram access`, `data access`, `bus access` (with `provides`/`requires`).
- ✅ **Properties** are typed name-value pairs declared in property sets (`Timing_Properties`, `Memory_Properties`, `Deployment_Properties`).
- ✅ **Connections** are explicit, named wires between compatible features.
- ✅ **Flows** are end-to-end logical streams with analyzable latency.
- ✅ **Modes** represent operational states with mode-specific elements and transitions.
- ✅ **Bindings** allocate software components to hardware (`Actual_Processor_Binding`).
- ✅ **Annexes** extend the core language: `Behavior Annex`, `Error Model Annex`, `ARINC653 Annex`, `Security Annex`.
- ✅ **Analysis enabled**: type checking, flow latency, schedulability (RMA), memory budget, binding consistency, fault model, security.
- ✅ **Tooling**: OSATE (Open Source AADL Tool Environment) — Eclipse-based.
- ✅ **AADL model is the source of truth** for code generation (Ada, C, Simulink) and certification artifacts (DO-178C, ISO 26262).

### Formula Reminders

- **Rate-Monotonic Sufficient Condition:**
$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n(\sqrt[n]{2} - 1)
$$

- **End-to-End Flow Latency:**
$$
L_{total} = \sum L_{components} + \sum L_{connections}
$$

- **Memory Footprint:**
$$
M_{total} = \text{Source\_Code\_Size} + \text{Stack\_Size} + \text{Heap\_Size} + \sum \text{Data\_Size}
$$

- **Real-Time Correctness:**
$$
L_{total,max} \le T_{period} \quad \text{(Hard Real-Time Constraint)}
$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Powerboat Autopilot System (PBAS)** is a **safety-critical, real-time, embedded control system** used in marine vessels to automatically maintain a preset compass heading by dynamically computing and applying corrections to the vessel's rudder position, with minimal or no human intervention.

In the context of **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, the Powerboat Autopilot System is a canonical **case study** used to demonstrate the end-to-end application of graphical modeling languages (SysML/UML), state machines, requirements traceability, and safety analysis techniques on a real-time, fault-tolerant embedded system.

The system is classified as:
- **SIL 2 / SIL 3** compliant (Safety Integrity Level) under IEC 61508.
- A **distributed, multi-subsystem** architecture.
- A **closed-loop feedback control system** with deterministic response.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control of a Boat (with a Brain):**
> Imagine driving a car in a strong crosswind. You constantly nudge the steering wheel left or right to keep the car centered in the lane. The Powerboat Autopilot does **exactly the same thing**, but for a boat on water, with **waves, currents, and wind** all trying to push it off-course. The system constantly measures where the boat is pointing (using a compass) and how far off the rudder is turned, and then issues small steering commands — like a tireless helmsman who never gets tired or distracted.

### 1.3 Key System Parameters (Standard Metrics)

- **Heading Error Threshold:** $\pm \mathbf{5^\circ}$ from the set heading
- **Rudder Angle Range:** $\pm \mathbf{30^\circ}$ (port and starboard)
- **Sampling Rate:** $\mathbf{10 \text{ Hz}}$ (sensor update frequency)
- **Control Loop Period:** $\mathbf{100 \text{ ms}}$
- **Operating Voltage:** $\mathbf{12\text{V DC / 24V DC}}$ nominal
- **Response Time:** $\mathbf{< 2 \text{ seconds}}$ for course correction
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** $\mathbf{> 10,000 \text{ hours}}$

## 1.4 Why is PBAS a Critical MBSD Case Study?

| Aspect | Why It Matters in MBSD |
|---|---|
| **Safety-Critical** | Failure = loss of vessel, life, cargo. Demands formal V&V. |
| **Real-Time** | Hard deadlines; late response = disaster. Tests timing analysis. |
| **Multi-Subsystem** | Sensors, actuators, HMI, control CPU — tests architecture modeling. |
| **Fault-Tolerant** | Demonstrates safety modes, FMEA, redundancy patterns. |
| **State-Driven** | Rich state machine behavior (Standby → Engaged → Override → Emergency). |
| **Heterogeneous** | Mix of analog, digital, mechanical, software — tests domain integration. |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MBSD Engineering Process Applied to PBAS

The development of the Powerboat Autopilot System follows the **"V-Model"** integrated with **Model-Based Development (MBD)**:

1. **Capture Stakeholder Needs** (Natural Language)
2. **Model System Requirements** in SysML (Requirements Diagram)
3. **Model Functional Behavior** (Use Case + Activity Diagrams)
4. **Model System Architecture** (Block Definition + Internal Block Diagrams)
5. **Model Component Behavior** (State Machines)
6. **Verify & Validate** via simulation (Model-in-the-Loop, Software-in-the-Loop)
7. **Auto-generate code** from the validated model

## 2.2 Core Subsystems of the Powerboat Autopilot

### 2.2.1 Sensor Subsystem
- **Fluxgate/GPS Compass** → provides heading $\theta_{actual}$
- **Rudder Position Sensor** (potentiometer/encoder) → provides rudder angle $\delta$
- **Rate Gyro** → provides rate of turn $\dot{\theta}$

### 2.2.2 Control Computer (ECU)
- Runs the **PID control algorithm**
- Hosts the **Supervisory State Machine**
- Performs **Sensor Health Monitoring** (plausibility checks)

### 2.2.3 Actuator Subsystem
- **Hydraulic Steering Pump** (or electromechanical actuator)
- **Solenoid Valves** for rudder direction control

### 2.2.4 Human-Machine Interface (HMI)
- **Control Head** with buttons: `STANDBY`, `AUTO`, `+10°`, `-10°`
- **LCD Display** showing heading, mode, error codes
- **Audible Alarm** (95 dB @ 1 m)

### 2.2.5 Safety & Power Subsystem
- **Watchdog Timer** (hardware)
- **Backup Battery** (30-minute operation)
- **Emergency Cutoff Relay**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table summarizes **all key equations, design rules, and architectural constraints** the KTU examiner expects you to know for the PBAS case study.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heading Error** | $e(t) = \theta_{set} - \theta_{actual}(t)$ | Input to PID controller |
| 2 | **PID Control Law** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Computes rudder command |
| 3 | **Discrete PID** | $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k]-e[k-1])$ | Implementation in ECU |
| 4 | **Sampling Constraint** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Ensures no aliasing |
| 5 | **Loop Period** | $T_{loop} = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Real-time deadline |
| 6 | **Heading Wrap** | $\theta_{wrap} = ((\theta \bmod 360) + 360) \bmod 360$ | Prevents 359°→1° error |
| 7 | **Rudder Saturation** | $\delta = \text{clamp}(u, -\delta_{max}, +\delta_{max})$ | Protects actuator |
| 8 | **Watchdog Timeout** | $T_{wd} > T_{loop} \times N_{tolerance}$ | Detects CPU hang |
| 9 | **MTBF Requirement** | $\text{MTBF} > 10,000 \text{ hours}$ | Reliability budget |
| 10 | **Safety State Rule** | $\text{Fault Detected} \Rightarrow \text{Transition to SAFE}$ | Fault containment |
| 11 | **Sensor Plausibility** | $\vert \theta_k - \theta_{k-1} \vert \leq \dot{\theta}_{max} \cdot T_s$ | Rejects faulty readings |
| 12 | **Power Consumption Budget** | $P_{total} = \sum P_{subsystem} \leq 50\text{W}$ | Battery sizing |

## 2.4 The Why and How Behind Each Step

### Why PID?
The boat is a **second-order dynamic system** with inertia and damping. A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative** controller gives:
- **P-term:** Reacts to current error (sharp correction)
- **I-term:** Eliminates steady-state offset from currents/wind
- **D-term:** Damps oscillation (anticipates overshoot)

### Why a State Machine?
The autopilot is **mode-driven** — it cannot just run one algorithm always. It must behave differently when:
- The operator pushes `STANDBY` (no control)
- The operator pushes `AUTO` (closed-loop engaged)
- The operator grabs the wheel (manual override)
- A sensor fails (safe-state)

A **finite state machine (FSM)** is the cleanest MBSD construct to capture this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use of PBAS Methodology |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vessels** (Mayflower, Sea-Kit) | Foundation for unmanned surface vessels (USV) |
| **Aerospace** (Fly-By-Wire) | Boeing 777/787 use same MBD paradigm |
| **Automotive ADAS** | Lane-keeping = 1D version of heading-keeping |
| **Robotics** | Mobile robot waypoint navigation = same model |
| **Agriculture** | GPS-guided tractors use identical SysML patterns |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete PID Algorithm from Continuous Form

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner often asks: "Derive the discrete PID equation used in the ECU." This is the **most expected numerical answer** for this case study.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Start with the Continuous-Time PID

The continuous-time PID control law is:

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = rudder command (degrees)
- $e(t)$ = heading error (degrees)
- $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ = proportional, integral, derivative gains

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Apply Rectangular (Forward Euler) Approximation

For a digital ECU running at sample period $T_s$, we replace the integral with a sum and the derivative with a backward difference.

The **integral** is approximated as:

$$
\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]
$$

The **derivative** is approximated as:

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Substitute into the Continuous PID

Substituting both approximations into the original equation:

$$
u[k] = K_p \, e[k] + K_i \, T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + K_d \cdot \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Simplify to the Standard Discrete PID (Position Form)

Defining the integral accumulator $I[k] = I[k-1] + e[k]$, the final form is:

$$
\boxed{\,u[k] = K_p \, e[k] + K_i \, T_s \, I[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s} \bigl( e[k] - e[k-1] \bigr)\,}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Derive the Velocity (Incremental) Form

Subtracting $u[k-1]$ from $u[k]$ eliminates the integral accumulator, giving the **velocity form** (preferred for anti-windup):

$$
\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1]
$$

$$
\Delta u[k] = K_p \bigl( e[k] - e[k-1] \bigr) + K_i \, T_s \, e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s} \bigl( e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2] \bigr)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The velocity form is used in the ECU because if a fault is detected, setting $\Delta u = 0$ instantly stops commanding the rudder — a critical safety property.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Sensor Plausibility Check

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A heading sensor reporting 359° one moment and 1° the second is valid, but reporting 359° then 30° is impossible at the boat's max turn rate.

### 3.2.2 Step 1: Define Maximum Physical Turn Rate

The boat's physical maximum turn rate is bounded by:

$$
\dot{\theta}_{max} = \frac{30^\circ}{\text{second}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2: Compute Maximum Allowed Per-Sample Change

At sample period $T_s$:

$$
\Delta \theta_{max} = \dot{\theta}_{max} \times T_s
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3: Formulate the Plausibility Predicate

A new heading sample $\theta_k$ is **plausible** if and only if:

$$
\bigl( (\theta_k - \theta_{k-1} + 540^\circ) \bmod 360^\circ \bigr) - 180^\circ \;\leq\; \Delta\theta_{max}
$$

The wrapping trick ensures that a 359° → 1° transition is correctly interpreted as +2° (not -358°).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Requirements to Model Artifacts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **traceability matrix** the examiner loves — it shows how textual requirements become SysML/UML models.

| Stakeholder Need | SysML Requirement ID | Use Case | Block | State Machine State | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Maintain heading | REQ-001 | `MaintainCourse` | `HeadingController` | `AUTO_ACTIVE` | Simulation |
| Allow operator override | REQ-002 | `OverrideControl` | `OverrideDetector` | `MANUAL_OVERRIDE` | HIL test |
| Detect sensor failure | REQ-003 | `MonitorHealth` | `FaultManager` | `SAFE_STATE` | Fault injection |
| Audible alarm on fault | REQ-004 | `AlertOperator` | `AlarmActuator` | any → `ALARM` | Unit test |
| Backup power | REQ-005 | `SurvivePowerLoss` | `PowerMonitor` | `BATTERY_MODE` | Power test |
| Heading offset adjustment | REQ-006 | `AdjustHeading` | `HMI_Buttons` | `AUTO_ACTIVE` | Manual test |
| Disable on key-off | REQ-007 | `Shutdown` | `PowerManager` | `STANDBY` | Integration test |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-style problem:** A boat is set to heading $180°$. Current heading is $172°$. Sample period $T_s = 0.1 \text{ s}$. Gains: $K_p = 2.5$, $K_i = 0.4$, $K_d = 0.8$. Previous error $e[k-1] = 6°$, integral $I[k-1] = 12$. Compute the rudder command $u[k]$.

### Step 1: Compute the Current Error

$$
e[k] = \theta_{set} - \theta_{actual} = 180^\circ - 172^\circ = 8^\circ
$$

### Step 2: Update the Integral Accumulator

$$
I[k] = I[k-1] + e[k] = 12 + 8 = 20
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Proportional Term

$$
P = K_p \cdot e[k] = 2.5 \times 8 = 20
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Integral Term

$$
I_{term} = K_i \cdot T_s \cdot I[k] = 0.4 \times 0.1 \times 20 = 0.8
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Derivative Term

$$
D = \frac{K_d}{T_s} \cdot \bigl( e[k] - e[k-1] \bigr) = \frac{0.8}{0.1} \times (8 - 6) = 8 \times 2 = 16
$$

### Step 6: Sum and Apply Saturation

$$
u[k] = P + I_{term} + D = 20 + 0.8 + 16 = 36.8^\circ
$$

$$
u_{final} = \text{clamp}(36.8, -30, +30) = +30^\circ \;\; \text{(starboard hard over)}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The autopilot commands **maximum starboard rudder** to recover the $8°$ offset.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the three operating modes of a Powerboat Autopilot System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **STANDBY (1 mark):** Power is on, but the autopilot is not actively controlling the rudder. The system is idle, monitoring HMI and sensors only.
2. **AUTO (1 mark):** The autopilot is in closed-loop control, computing heading error and commanding the rudder via the PID algorithm to maintain the set heading.
3. **MANUAL OVERRIDE (1 mark):** The operator has taken control of the wheel; the autopilot suspends control to allow human steering while remaining ready to re-engage.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the Model-Based Development approach preferred over traditional hand-coding for the Powerboat Autopilot System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Early Verification (1 mark):** Models can be simulated and verified before code is generated, catching defects early when they are 10–100× cheaper to fix.
2. **Auto-Code Generation (1 mark):** Production code is generated directly from validated state machines and block diagrams, eliminating hand-coding errors and ensuring 100% consistency with the design.
3. **Safety & Traceability (1 mark):** Requirements, design, and code can be linked via traceability matrices, supporting IEC 61508 / SIL 2 compliance and rigorous safety audits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern, Model-Based Development]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a SysML Block Definition Diagram, describe the architecture of the **Powerboat Autopilot System**. Identify and explain the function of any **four major subsystems** in your diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Powerboat Autopilot System (PBAS) is a **distributed, safety-critical embedded system** comprising five major subsystems:

1. **Sensor Subsystem (2 marks)** — Contains the fluxgate compass (measures heading $\theta_{actual}$), rate gyro (measures turn rate $\dot{\theta}$), and rudder position sensor (measures current rudder angle $\delta$). These provide the feedback signals for the closed-loop controller.

2. **Control Computer / ECU (2 marks)** — The heart of the system, executing the **PID control law**, the **Supervisory State Machine** (Standby / Auto / Override / Safe), and the **Fault Manager**. It samples sensors at $f_s = 10$ Hz and emits rudder commands.

3. **Actuator Subsystem (1 mark)** — The hydraulic steering pump and solenoid valves physically move the rudder according to the ECU's command signal, providing $\pm 30°$ of travel.

4. **HMI Subsystem (1 mark)** — Control head buttons (Standby, Auto, ±10°), LCD display, and audible alarm enable the operator to monitor and interact with the system.

5. **Power & Safety Subsystem (1 mark)** — Includes primary PSU, 30-minute backup battery, hardware watchdog timer, and emergency cutoff relay for fault containment.

The Block Definition Diagram (see Section 4.1) shows the composition and flow of signals: **Sensors → ECU → Actuators**, with HMI and Power crossing all subsystems.

---

**Q. (b)** Draw the **state machine** of the PBAS and explain the **transitions** between any **four key states**. How does the state machine contribute to **fault tolerance**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The PBAS state machine (see Section 4.2) has these key states:

1. **STANDBY (1.5 marks):** Initial state; autopilot inactive but ready. **Transition to AUTO_ACTIVE** occurs when the operator presses the AUTO button. **Transition to CALIBRATION** is via a 3-second hold of the CAL button.

2. **AUTO_ACTIVE (1.5 marks):** Closed-loop control engaged. **Transition to MANUAL_OVERRIDE** is triggered by detection of wheel movement (sensor-driven). **Transition to SAFE_STATE** is triggered by the Fault Manager flagging a sensor plausibility failure. **Transition to BATTERY_MODE** is triggered by the Power Monitor detecting loss of primary supply.

3. **MANUAL_OVERRIDE (1.5 marks):** Operator has physical control. **Transition back to AUTO_ACTIVE** is automatic after 5 seconds of no wheel movement. A 30-second timeout forces **transition to SAFE_STATE** to prevent stale mode.

4. **SAFE_STATE (1.5 marks):** All actuator commands are forced to zero. **Transition to STANDBY** requires operator acknowledgment after the fault is cleared and the watchdog confirms system health.

**Contribution to Fault Tolerance (1 mark):**
The state machine provides **deterministic, predictable behavior** under all conditions. Every fault condition maps to exactly one transition into SAFE_STATE, where the rudder is commanded to center and the alarm is sounded. This **single-point-of-failure-containment** is a cornerstone of IEC 61508 SIL-2 compliance and prevents the system from "stuck in AUTO with a bad sensor" — a scenario that caused real-world marine accidents.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern, Model-Based Development]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **PID control algorithm** used in the Powerboat Autopilot. **Derive the discrete form** of the PID equation starting from the continuous-time form, clearly stating all assumptions. Why is the **velocity (incremental) form** preferred in safety-critical applications? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Continuous PID (2 marks):**
$$
u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$
- $K_p$ corrects based on present error.
- $K_i$ eliminates steady-state error (e.g., from a steady cross-current).
- $K_d$ damps overshoot by reacting to the rate of change.

**Derivation of Discrete Form (3 marks):**
1. **Assumption:** Sample at period $T_s$ (e.g., $T_s = 0.1$ s for PBAS).
2. **Integral Approximation (Rectangular Rule):** $\int \approx T_s \sum e[k]$.
3. **Derivative Approximation (Backward Difference):** $\frac{de}{dt} \approx \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$.
4. **Resulting Discrete PID (Position Form):**
$$
u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^k e[j] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl(e[k] - e[k-1]\bigr)
$$

**Velocity Form Preferred in Safety-Critical (2 marks):**
$$
\Delta u[k] = K_p\bigl(e[k]-e[k-1]\bigr) + K_i T_s e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl(e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2]\bigr)
$$
1. **Bumpless transfer:** On a fault, setting $\Delta u = 0$ instantly stops rudder motion — no need to "know" the actuator's current state.
2. **Anti-windup:** The integral term does not accumulate when the actuator is saturated, preventing dangerous windup on recovery.
3. **Fail-safe:** The default of $\Delta u = 0$ corresponds to "no change," not to a specific dangerous value.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **role of the Fault Manager** in the PBAS. Describe **two fault detection techniques** and **two fault response strategies** used in the system. How does MBD help in achieving **safety certification**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of the Fault Manager (2 marks):**
The Fault Manager is a dedicated block in the ECU that continuously evaluates the health of sensors, actuators, and software execution. It raises the `FaultDetected` event, which forces the state machine to transition to SAFE_STATE. It is implemented as a separate, prioritized execution task to ensure deterministic response time.

**Fault Detection Techniques (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Sensor Plausibility Check:** Compares the per-sample change in heading against $\Delta\theta_{max} = \dot{\theta}_{max} \cdot T_s$. A reading outside this bound is flagged as faulty (e.g., 359° → 30° in 0.1 s is impossible).
2. **Watchdog Timer:** A hardware counter that must be "kicked" (reset) by the software every loop. If the software hangs, the counter expires and forces a hardware reset to SAFE_STATE.

**Fault Response Strategies (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Rudder Center Command:** On any fault, the actuator is commanded to the center ($\delta = 0°$), and the pump is disabled, providing passive straight-line travel.
2. **Audible + Visual Alarm:** The 95 dB alarm is sounded and an error code (e.g., `E03 = compass lost`) is displayed on the LCD until acknowledged.

**Role of MBD in Safety Certification (1 mark):**
Model-Based Development supports safety certification (IEC 61508, ISO 26262) by providing a **single source of truth** for requirements → design → code → tests. The traceability matrix (Section 3.3) links every requirement to the block, state, and test that realizes it, making the certification auditor's job — and the developer's proof of due diligence — a structured, automated process.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on the PBAS Case Study:**
> 1. **Confusing AUTO and MANUAL modes** — A common mistake is to say "AUTO means off." It is the opposite: AUTO is *active* closed-loop control. STANDBY is off.
> 2. **Forgetting the +360° wrap** when computing heading error near 0°. A student who writes $e = 359 - 1 = 358°$ instead of $e = 2°$ will get **0 marks** for the heading computation.
> 3. **Mixing continuous and discrete notation** — When deriving the discrete PID, do NOT mix $e(t)$ and $e[k]$ in the same equation.
> 4. **Forgetting the safety aspect** — The examiner allocates **at least 2–3 marks** to fault tolerance. A solution that only describes happy-path control without SAFE_STATE loses marks.
> 5. **No units in the final answer** — Always state $u[k] = +30°$ starboard, not just $30$. A unit-less answer is treated as incomplete.
> 6. **Missing the model-tool link** — The MBD flavor of the question demands that you mention **auto-code generation, model-in-the-loop (MIL) simulation, and traceability**. Pure software-engineering answers miss the point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Cheat Sheet — Re-read this the night before the exam.**

### ✅ System Identity
- PBAS = **Safety-critical, real-time, closed-loop heading control** system.
- Maintains a **set compass heading** using **rudder actuation**.

### ✅ Three Operating Modes
- **STANDBY** (off), **AUTO** (closed-loop PID), **MANUAL_OVERRIDE** (operator in charge).
- Plus two safety modes: **SAFE_STATE** and **BATTERY_MODE**.

### ✅ Core Algorithm
- **Discrete PID** in **velocity (incremental) form** — preferred for fail-safe behavior.
- $T_s = 0.1 \text{ s}$ (10 Hz sampling).
- Rudder saturated to $\pm 30°$.

### ✅ Heading Error
- $e[k] = \theta_{set} - \theta_{actual}[k]$ with **360° wrap-around** handling.
- Used as input to PID.

### ✅ Five Major Subsystems
1. Sensors (compass, gyro, rudder sensor)
2. ECU (PID + State Machine + Fault Manager)
3. Actuators (hydraulic pump + valves)
4. HMI (buttons, display, alarm)
5. Power & Safety (PSU, battery, watchdog, cutoff)

### ✅ Two Safety Mechanisms
- **Watchdog Timer** (hardware) — detects CPU hang.
- **Sensor Plausibility Check** (software) — detects garbage sensor data.

### ✅ MBD-Specific Concepts to Mention
- **SysML Block Definition Diagram** for architecture.
- **State Machine** for mode behavior.
- **Internal Block Diagram** for signal flow.
- **Requirements Traceability Matrix** for certification.
- **Auto-code generation** from state machines and blocks.
- **MIL/SIL/HIL** simulation levels.

### ✅ Standards Compliance
- **IEC 61508** — Functional Safety.
- **SIL 2 / SIL 3** — Safety Integrity Level.
- **ISO 26262** concepts (derived for automotive analogy, useful in interviews).

### ✅ Numerical Values to Memorize
- $T_s = 0.1$ s, $f_s = 10$ Hz.
- $\delta_{max} = 30°$, $e_{threshold} = 5°$.
- Battery backup: **30 minutes**.
- Alarm: **95 dB @ 1 m**.
- MTBF: **> 10,000 hours**.

### ✅ Exam-Ready Phrases
- *"The state machine provides deterministic, single-point fault containment."*
- *"The velocity PID form ensures bumpless transfer to safe state on fault."*
- *"MBD enables requirements-to-code traceability for SIL 2 certification."*
- *"Sensor plausibility checks prevent runaway control from spurious data."*

---

**End of Notes — Module 3, Powerboat Autopilot System Case Study**
*All the very best for your KTU University Examination!* 🚢⚓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Analysis - Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Analysis - Fault Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive, top-down, graphical failure analysis methodology in which an undesired state of a system (the **Top Event**) is decomposed, using Boolean logic, into its constituent causes (the **Basic Events**) connected through logical gates such as **AND**, **OR**, and **NOT**. It is formally standardized under **IEC 61025** (International Electrotechnical Commission) and **IEEE 352**, and is a cornerstone of **System Safety Engineering** and **Reliability Engineering**.

In Model-Based Software Development (MBSD), FTA is used to:
- Reason about software-induced hazards in safety-critical embedded systems (e.g., automotive, avionics, medical).
- Complement **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** by working in the reverse direction — *from hazard → root causes*.
- Provide **quantitative metrics** like top-event probability, minimal cut sets, and importance factors that can be derived from formal system models (SysML, Simulink, AADL).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Forensic Detective Approach"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Plane Crash Investigation:** Imagine a serious air accident has occurred (this is the **Top Event**). Investigators start at the crash site and ask, *"What could have caused this?"* The answer is rarely a single thing. It could be AND-gate causes: *(engine failure **AND** pilot didn't respond **AND** backup failed)*, or OR-gate causes: *(sensor A failed **OR** sensor B failed **OR** sensor C failed)*. By breaking the disaster down step by step using a tree of logical conditions, investigators identify the *minimal combination* of failures that, had any one of them not occurred, the disaster would have been prevented. **That minimal combination is the Minimal Cut Set (MCS)**. FTA is exactly this — but done *before* the accident, as a preventive safety tool.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | Visual Cue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Top Event** | The undesired system state being analyzed (root of the tree). | Rectangle at the top |
| **Intermediate Event** | A logical sub-event resulting from a gate. | Rectangle in the middle |
| **Basic Event** | A leaf-level failure that requires no further decomposition. | Circle |
| **Undeveloped Event** | A failure whose cause is deliberately not analyzed further. | Diamond |
| **Conditioning Event** | Conditions/assumptions attached to a gate (e.g., "in mode X"). | Ellipse |
| **AND Gate** | Output occurs **iff** ALL inputs occur. | Flat D-shape |
| **OR Gate** | Output occurs **iff** ANY input occurs. | Curved D-shape |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU board loves testing the **shape-to-symbol mapping** (e.g., "Which shape denotes an AND gate?"). Memorize the geometry — flat bottom = AND, curved bottom = OR.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Fault Tree — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction process is a **deductive reasoning procedure**. The analyst begins with the Top Event and repeatedly asks the question: *"How can this event occur?"* The answer is expressed as a Boolean combination of sub-events, and the tree is built recursively.

**The 5 Steps of Fault Tree Construction (KTU Standard Answer):**

1. **Define the System Boundary and Operating Conditions.** State clearly what is *inside* the analysis (e.g., a brake-by-wire ECU) and what is *outside* (e.g., driver behavior, road conditions). This step defines the scope of the FTA.
2. **Define the Top Event.** The Top Event must be *precise, singular, and measurable* (e.g., "Loss of braking function while vehicle speed > 0 km/h"). Vague top events are the most common reason a fault tree is rejected.
3. **Identify Immediate Causes.** Using system knowledge (block diagrams, schematics, FMEA outputs), identify the immediate sub-events that directly cause the Top Event. Connect them via AND/OR gates.
4. **Iterate Down to Basic Events.** Continue decomposition for each sub-event until reaching **primitive failures** — events that cannot be logically broken down further. These become the leaf circles.
5. **Validate the Tree.** Cross-check by reviewing with system engineers, performing Boolean algebra simplification, and verifying against physical/system models (this is where MBSD integration excels — the system model *is* the source of truth for the tree).

## 2.2 Boolean Algebra Representation

A fault tree is mathematically equivalent to a Boolean function:

$$F = \text{Top Event Expression}$$

For a tree with an OR gate at the top feeding two AND-gated sub-branches:

$$F = (X_1 \land X_2) \lor (X_3 \land X_4)$$

Where $X_i$ are the Boolean indicators of basic events (1 = failed, 0 = functioning).

## 2.3 Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)

A **Cut Set** is a collection of basic events whose simultaneous failure causes the Top Event.
A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is a cut set from which no event can be removed without losing the property of causing the Top Event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MCS are the most important output of an FTA.** They pinpoint the *smallest combinations* of failures that bring down the system. In KTU exams, finding the MCS from a Boolean expression is a frequent 7-mark question.

### Method to Find MCS (Quine-McCluskey / Boolean Expansion):
1. Express the tree as a Sum-of-Products (SOP) Boolean expression.
2. Apply the distributive law: $(A \land B) \lor (A \land C) = A \land (B \lor C)$ — *absorption*.
3. Drop the redundant terms. The remaining products are the MCS.

## 2.4 Quantitative Analysis — Probability of the Top Event

For a Top Event expressed in terms of MCS, the probability is computed using the **Inclusion-Exclusion Principle** for OR-combined independent events:

$$P(T) = P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} M_i\right) = \sum_{i} P(M_i) - \sum_{i<j} P(M_i \cap M_j) + \sum_{i<j<k} P(M_i \cap M_j \cap M_k) - \ldots$$

For **small probabilities** ($P \ll 1$), the rare-event approximation is widely used in KTU problems:

$$P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(M_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{j \in M_i} q_j$$

Where $q_j$ is the failure probability of basic event $j$. This approximation holds when MCS intersection probabilities are negligible (typically $q < 10^{-2}$).

### AND-Gate Probability (Independent Inputs):

$$P_{\text{AND}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i$$

### OR-Gate Probability (Independent Inputs, rare-event):

$$P_{\text{OR}} \approx 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - q_i)$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | AND-gate output probability | $P = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i$ | Independent basic events |
| 2 | OR-gate (rare event) | $P \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i$ | When $q_i \ll 1$ |
| 3 | OR-gate (exact) | $P = 1 - \prod (1 - q_i)$ | Independent basic events |
| 4 | Top event via MCS (approx) | $P(T) \approx \sum_{i} \prod_{j \in M_i} q_j$ | Rare-event approximation |
| 5 | Top event (exact, incl-excl) | $P(T) = \sum P(M_i) - \sum P(M_i \cap M_j) + \ldots$ | General Inclusion-Exclusion |
| 6 | Unavailability $Q(t)$ | $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential, $\lambda$ = failure rate |
