> 1. **Confusing abstraction levels:** Many students write *PIM = high-level, PSM = low-level*. The correct phrasing is: *PIM = platform-neutral, PSM = platform-bound*. Low/High here refers to *abstraction*, not importance.
> 2. **Treating formality as binary:** Formality $F$ is a *real number in $[0,1]$*, not a Boolean. Saying "this model is formal" without a numeric $F$ loses 1–2 marks in Part-B numerical questions.
> 3. **Missing the tag-preservation axiom:** In monotonicity proofs, students often skip the *role of categorization tags*. Without tags, the transformation $\tau_i$ has no well-defined semantics — expect to lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Use-Case vs Class diagram scope:** Use-Case diagrams at System scope (correct), but a Class diagram of the *entire enterprise* is over-scoped — categorize it as **Component or Subsystem**.
> 5. **Forgetting the ceiling operator:** In $R_{\text{formal, required}} = \lceil F_{\min} \cdot R_{\text{total}} \rceil$, omitting the ceiling yields a fractional rule count, which is physically impossible. Always round *up*.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Categorization is six-axes-orthogonal** — every model receives exactly **one tag per axis** (Abstraction, Purpose, Aspect, Dynamics, Scope, Formality).
- [ ] **MDA hierarchy is monotonic**: $\text{CIM} \to \text{PIM} \to \text{PSM} \to \text{Code}$. No backward edges.
- [ ] **CIM = Why, PIM = What, PSM = How** — the canonical exam mnemonic.
- [ ] **Purpose taxonomy**: Descriptive (docs), Prescriptive (code generation), Predictive (simulation/analysis).
- [ ] **Formality Quotient** $F \in [0,1]$. SIL/DAL drives $F_{\min}$: SIL-A / DAL-A needs $F \ge 0.95$; SIL-D / DAL-E tolerates $F = 0$.
- [ ] **UML has 14 diagram types**; **SysML has 9**. This is a frequently asked 1-mark question.
- [ ] **Static models** never include time; **Dynamic models** always do. Memorize the matrix in Section 3.4.
- [ ] **Categorization tags are mandatory** for any M2M (model-to-model) transformation — they make $\tau_i$ a *total function*.
- [ ] **Scope containment** is set-theoretic: Component $\subseteq$ Subsystem $\subseteq$ System $\subseteq$ Enterprise.
- [ ] **Industry mapping**: AUTOSAR (automotive PIM $\to$ PSM), DO-178C (avionics DAL-A), IEC 62304 (medical Class A–C), MARTE (embedded UML).
- [ ] **Refinement is non-contracting**: $\lvert M_{i+1} \rvert \ge \lvert M_i \rvert$ — proof requires tag-preservation (Section 3.1).
- [ ] **Numeric exam tip**: When computing required formal rules, **always apply the ceiling** — fractional rules do not exist.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Generation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Generation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Model-Based Software Development (MBSD)**, **Code Generation** refers to the automated, semi-automated, or systematic transformation of abstract software models (expressed in languages such as **UML**, **SysML**, **MARTE**, or domain-specific modeling languages) into executable, deployable source code in one or more general-purpose programming languages (C, C++, Java, Python, Ada, VHDL, etc.).

According to the **OMG Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)** framework, code generation is a special case of a **Model-to-Text (M2T)** transformation. It operationalizes the **CIM → PIM → PSM → Code** refinement chain, where:

- **CIM** (Computation Independent Model)
- **PIM** (Platform Independent Model)
- **PSM** (Platform Specific Model)
- **Code** (Executable artifact)

The formal transformation is often expressed as a function:

$$
T : M_{\text{source}} \longrightarrow C_{\text{target}}
$$

where $M_{\text{source}}$ is a source model conforming to a metamodel $M_1$, and $C_{\text{target}}$ is the generated code artifact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Code generation is the *final concrete artifact* produced by an MBSD pipeline. It validates the round-trip traceability between design intent and executable behavior — a recurring **14-mark** question in the KTU ESE pattern.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Bakery"

Imagine a **high-tech industrial bakery** that produces pastries:

1. **The Recipe (Model)** — A precise, written specification of ingredients, quantities, oven temperature, and timing. It is *not* a pastry yet — it is a *description* of a pastry.
2. **The Industrial Oven (Code Generator)** — A configurable machine that takes the recipe and *automatically* produces the finished pastry.
3. **The Pastry (Generated Code)** — The final, consumable artifact (executable software).
4. **The Quality Inspector (Validator / Compiler)** — Checks that the pastry matches the recipe and is safe to eat.

In this analogy:

- The **recipe** can be a UML class diagram, a Simulink block diagram, or a state machine.
- The **oven** is a tool like **Acceleo**, **MATLAB Simulink Coder**, **TargetLink**, **IBM Rational Rhapsody**, or **dSPACE**.
- The **pastry** is C/C++ source code, an embedded binary, or a hardware description.

> [!NOTE]
> If you change the recipe, you regenerate the pastry. If you change the pastry (manual edit), you may *break* the link to the recipe. This is precisely why **round-trip engineering** is so important in MBSD.

---

## 1.3 Why Code Generation Matters in MBSD

- **Automation**: Eliminates repetitive boilerplate coding.
- **Consistency**: A single model produces uniform, standards-compliant code.
- **Traceability**: Every line of code can be traced back to a model element (a **KPI in safety-critical domains**).
- **Productivity**: Industry reports cite **40%–70% reduction** in development effort for embedded systems.
- **Certification Readiness**: In domains like **DO-178C (avionics)**, **ISO 26262 (automotive)**, and **IEC 61508**, model-based code generation with qualified tools (e.g., **SCADE Suite**) supports certification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The industry benchmark for code generator **accuracy** is measured by *Model-to-Code Coverage* (MCC), defined as the percentage of model elements that have a corresponding artifact in the generated code. **Target MCC ≥ 98%** for safety-critical applications.

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Generation Style | Input | Output | Tool Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Template-based (M2T)** | Model + Templates | Source Code / Docs | Acceleo, MOFScript, Xpand |
| **Model-to-Model (M2M)** | Source Model | Target (PSM) Model | QVT, ATL, ETL |
| **Diagram-based** | Simulink / Stateflow | C / HDL | MATLAB Coder, TargetLink |
| **DSL-based** | Domain Model | Code + Config | JetBrains MPS, Xtext |
| **AI-assisted** | Model + Context | Code (LLM) | GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain code generation techniques," students must mention at least **three distinct techniques** (template, transformation, diagram-based) and the **MDA layers** they operate on.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Code Generation Pipeline (Layered View)

A typical MBSD code generation pipeline is structured as a sequence of well-defined stages:

### Stage 1 — Model Acquisition
- The source model is loaded, parsed, and validated against its **metamodel** (e.g., the **UML 2.5 metamodel**).
- The model is stored in a standard interchange format such as **XMI (XML Metadata Interchange)**.

### Stage 2 — Model Normalization / Annotation
- The model is enriched with stereotypes, tagged values, and OCL constraints.
- For M2M transformations, the PIM may be refined into a **PSM** by adding platform-specific elements (e.g., OS tasking, memory mapping, communication ports).

### Stage 3 — Transformation Rule Application
- Transformation rules traverse the model and produce either intermediate model fragments or text fragments.
- Each rule has a **guard** (precondition) and a **body** (action).

### Stage 4 — Code Emission
- Generated fragments are assembled into complete source files.
- Header files, build scripts, and configuration files are also emitted.

### Stage 5 — Compilation & Validation
- The generated code is compiled and tested.
- Bidirectional traceability links are established for **round-trip engineering**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **OMG's MOF (Meta-Object Facility)** standard defines a four-layer metamodeling architecture. Code generation tools *consume* a model at **M1 layer** and produce artifacts whose semantics are interpreted by a metamodel at the **M2 layer**.

---

## 2.2 Core Code Generation Techniques (Deep Dive)

### 2.2.1 Template-Based Code Generation (M2T)

A **template engine** combines a static template (with placeholders) and a dynamic model to produce text.

The general form:

$$
\text{Output} = \text{Template}(S, P) \oplus M
$$

where $S$ is static boilerplate, $P$ is the set of placeholders, and $M$ is the model query result.

**Example tool flow:**

```
Model (UML)  →  Template Engine (Acceleo)  →  Java / C++ Source
```

**Advantages:**
- Easy to read, write, and review.
- Excellent for documentation generation.
- Strong tool support (Eclipse-based).

**Limitations:**
- Templates can become unmaintainable ("spaghetti templates").
- Weak type checking between model and template.

### 2.2.2 Model-to-Model (M2M) Transformation

M2M transformation takes a **PIM** and produces a **PSM**. The most prominent standard is **OMG QVT (Query/View/Transformation)**, which has three sub-languages:

| QVT Sub-language | Style | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **QVT-Operational** | Imperative (procedural) | Complex transformations with imperative logic |
| **QVT-Relations** | Declarative (relational) | Bidirectional, high-level model synchronization |
| **QVT-Core** | Minimal declarative core | Foundation for QVT-Relations |

The transformation is expressed as a **relation** $R$ between source metamodel $S$ and target metamodel $T$:

$$
R : S \longrightarrow T
$$

### 2.2.3 Diagram-Based / Visual Code Generation

Block-diagram tools (Simulink, SCADE, LabVIEW) generate code directly from **graphical block diagrams** or **statecharts**. This is the *de-facto* standard in **embedded control systems**.

Key features:
- **Discrete-time / continuous-time solvers** are baked into the generator.
- Code is **MISRA-C compliant** by default (with qualified tools).
- **Model-in-the-Loop (MIL)**, **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)**, and **Processor-in-the-Loop (PIL)** verification are integrated.

### 2.2.4 Generative Programming & Frameworks

A **generator** is a parameterized program that produces source code from a *configuration* (a domain model) and a *template* (a family of architectures). Examples include:

- **GenVoca** (Czarnecki & Eisenecker)
- **Feature-Oriented Programming (FOP)**
- **Stepwise Refinement** approaches

### 2.2.5 AI-Assisted / LLM-Based Code Generation

Modern pipelines increasingly integrate **Large Language Models (LLMs)** to assist in:
- Filling template placeholders intelligently.
- Auto-completing hand-written code segments.
- Generating test cases from behavioral models.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** LLMs are **not yet a KTU exam-expected** code generation technique for the PECNT634 syllabus. Mention them only as an "emerging trend" in 14-mark answers.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — it is a *guaranteed* hit in Part A or as a sub-part of Part B.

| **Concept** | **Definition / Formula** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Transformation Function | $T : M_{\text{source}} \to C_{\text{target}}$ | Formal definition of code gen |
| MDA Layers | $CIM \to PIM \to PSM \to Code$ | KTU-favorite diagram |
| Model Coverage | $MCC = \frac{\vert E_{\text{covered}} \vert}{\vert E_{\text{total}} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Quality metric |
| Generation Time | $T_g = T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{transform}} + T_{\text{emit}}$ | Performance |
| Traceability Ratio | $TR = \frac{\vert L_{\text{linked}} \vert}{\vert L_{\text{gen}} \vert}$ | Round-trip quality |
| Cyclomatic Code Complexity (avg) | $CC = E - N + 2P$ | Quality of generated code |
| Lines of Code (generated) | $LOC_{\text{gen}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} LOC_i$ | Productivity metric |
| Reusability Index | $RI = \frac{\text{Number of reused templates}}{\text{Total templates}} \times 100\%$ | Engineering efficiency |
| QVT Mapping | $R \subseteq S \times T$ | Declarative transformation |
| Round-Trip Delta | $\Delta = \text{Edit}(M) \oplus \text{Edit}(C)$ | Conflict detection |

> **Note on notation:** In the table, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes *cardinality* (number of elements), not absolute value, written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Code Generation Tool | Generated Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | SCADE Suite, Simulink | DO-178C certified C code |
| **Automotive** | TargetLink, ASCET | ISO 26262 compliant C code |
| **Industrial Automation** | IEC 61131-3 tools | PLC structured text |
| **Telecommunications** | Telelogic Rhapsody | SDL/C++ for protocol stacks |
| **Medical Devices** | MathWorks Simulink | FDA-validated C/C++ |
| **Web/Mobile** | JetBrains MPS, Acceleo | Java, TypeScript, Kotlin |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently cite **automotive (TargetLink)** and **aerospace (SCADE)** as real-world examples in 14-mark questions. Including these in your answer earns *high-impression* marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Transformation Walkthrough

## 3.1 Analytical Goal

In this section, we will **systematically derive the generated code** from a sample UML state machine using a **rule-based template engine** approach. The objective is to show, in *pedantic detail*, how a model element at the **M1 layer** is mapped to a construct in a target language (C).

---

## 3.2 Source Model: A Simple State Machine

Consider the following **UML Statechart** for an *Automatic Door Controller*:

- **States**: `Closed`, `Opening`, `Open`, `Closing`
- **Events**: `buttonPressed`, `obstacleDetected`, `doorFullyOpen`, `doorFullyClosed`
- **Actions**: `motorForward()`, `motorStop()`, `motorReverse()`, `buzzerOn()`

The metamodel of this state machine is:

$$
M = \{S, E, A, T\}
$$

where:
- $S$ = set of states, $S = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$
- $E$ = set of events, $E = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, e_4\}$
- $A$ = set of actions, $A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4\}$
- $T$ = set of transitions, $T \subseteq S \times E \times S \times A$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Mapping Derivation

### Step 1 — Enumerate the States

For each state $S_i \in S$, we emit a symbolic constant in C:

$$
T_{\text{state}}(S_i) = \texttt{\#define STATE\_} \oplus \text{upper}(S_i)
$$

Applying this to all states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{state}}(Closed) &= \texttt{\#define STATE\_CLOSED 0} \\
T_{\text{state}}(Opening) &= \texttt{\#define STATE\_OPENING 1} \\
T_{\text{state}}(Open) &= \texttt{\#define STATE\_OPEN 2} \\
T_{\text{state}}(Closing) &= \texttt{\#define STATE\_CLOSING 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each UML state becomes a unique enumerated constant, preserving the *identity* of the state in the executable.

---

### Step 2 — Enumerate the Events

For each event $e_j \in E$, we emit a numeric event ID:

$$
T_{\text{event}}(e_j) = \texttt{\#define EVT\_} \oplus \text{upper}(e_j)
$$

Application:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{event}}(buttonPressed) &= \texttt{\#define EVT\_BUTTON\_PRESSED 1} \\
T_{\text{event}}(obstacleDetected) &= \texttt{\#define EVT\_OBSTACLE\_DETECTED 2} \\
T_{\text{event}}(doorFullyOpen) &= \texttt{\#define EVT\_DOOR\_FULLY\_OPEN 3} \\
T_{\text{event}}(doorFullyClosed) &= \texttt{\#define EVT\_DOOR\_FULLY\_CLOSED 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Events become external interrupts or message IDs in the generated C code.

---

### Step 3 — Map Transitions

Each transition is a 4-tuple: $T_k = (S_{\text{src}}, e, S_{\text{dst}}, A)$.

The transformation rule is:

$$
T_{\text{trans}}(T_k) = \texttt{case } \oplus \text{STATE\_src} \oplus \texttt{ : if (e == EVT\_e) \{ } \oplus A \oplus \texttt{ ; state = STATE\_dst; \} break;}
$$

For example, for transition `(Closed, buttonPressed, Opening, motorForward)`:

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \texttt{case STATE\_CLOSED: if (evt == EVT\_BUTTON\_PRESSED) \{ motorForward(); state = STATE\_OPENING; \} break;}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Assemble the Full Switch Construct

Combining all transitions into the body of a `dispatch` function:

$$
T_{\text{dispatch}} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{\vert T \vert} T_{\text{trans}}(T_k) \oplus \texttt{default: break;}
$$

The complete generated function (logical view):

```c
void dispatch(int evt) {
    switch (current_state) {
        // All transitions emit here
        case STATE_CLOSED:
            if (evt == EVT_BUTTON_PRESSED) {
                motorForward();
                current_state = STATE_OPENING;
            }
            break;
        // ... other cases
        default: break;
    }
}
```

> **Note:** Code shown is *illustration of the generated artifact* (MBSD-specific), not a Python/algorithm question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Quality Matrix

After generation, the code is evaluated using the following matrix:

| **Metric** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Target Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model Coverage | $MCC$ | $\frac{\vert E_{\text{covered}} \vert}{\vert E_{\text{total}} \vert} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 98\%$ |
| Traceability | $TR$ | $\frac{\vert L_{\text{linked}} \vert}{\vert L_{\text{gen}} \vert}$ | $= 1.0$ |
| Avg. Cyclomatic Complexity | $\overline{CC}$ | $\frac{1}{n}\sum CC_i$ | $\leq 10$ |
| Dead-code Ratio | $DCR$ | $\frac{\text{unreachable LOC}}{\text{total LOC}}$ | $= 0$ |
| Compliance | $C$ | Boolean (MISRA) | $\text{True}$ |
| Generation Time | $T_g$ | wall-clock | $\leq 5 \text{ sec}$ |

---

## 3.5 Verification by Formula Substitution

For a model with 4 states, 4 events, and 5 transitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert E_{\text{total}} \vert &= \vert S \vert + \vert E \vert + \vert T \vert = 4 + 4 + 5 = 13 \\
\vert E_{\text{covered}} \vert &= 13 \quad (\text{all elements mapped}) \\
MCC &= \frac{13}{13} \times 100\% = 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The generated code is **fully model-covered**, meaning every model element has a counterpart in the executable artifact — a desirable property in safety-critical MBSD.

---

## 3.6 Round-Trip Delta Calculation

If a developer manually edits the generated code (e.g., adds a log statement), the **round-trip delta** is:

$$
\Delta = \text{Edit}(C) \oplus \text{Regenerate}(M) - C_{\text{old}}
$$

The conflict is detected by a **3-way merge** algorithm (common ancestor, model version, code version). KTU expects you to mention this concept as part of *round-trip engineering* in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — End-to-End Code Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Domain Model] --> B[Metamodel Validation]
    B --> C[PIM Creation]
    C --> D[M2M Transformation]
    D --> E[PSM Refinement]
    E --> F[Template Engine]
    F --> G[Generated Code C C++ Java]
    G --> H[Compiler]
    H --> I[Executable Artifact]
    I --> J[Verification and Traceability]
    J --> K[Round-Trip Engine]
    K --> C
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**

- The **Domain Model** (e.g., a UML class diagram) is the input.
- **Metamodel Validation** ensures syntactic correctness.
- **PIM** is platform-independent; **PSM** is platform-specific.
- The **Template Engine** produces code via M2T rules.
- The **Round-Trip Engine** maintains bidirectional synchronization.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Internal Architecture of a Code Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        A1[Model Loader XMI Parser]
        A2[Metamodel Repository]
        A3[OCL Constraint Engine]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Transformation Core]
        B1[Rule Engine QVT ATL]
        B2[Template Library]
        B3[Trace Link Manager]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        C1[C Code Emitter]
        C2[C++ Code Emitter]
        C3[Java Code Emitter]
        C4[Config and Build Files]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> C4
    C1 --> D1[Generated Source Files]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
```

**Functional Insight:** This block-level architecture separates *parsing*, *reasoning*, and *emission* — a clean separation-of-concerns pattern essential for maintainable code generators.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — MDA Transformation Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M3[M3 Meta-Metamodel Layer MOF] --> M2[M2 Metamodel Layer UML]
    M2 --> M1[M1 Model Layer PIM]
    M1 --> M0[M0 Real World Object]
    M1 --> PSM1[PSM Platform Specific Model]
    PSM1 --> CODE1[Generated Source Code]
    PSM1 --> CODE2[Generated Build Scripts]
```

**Insight for the Student:**

- The four-layer architecture (M3, M2, M1, M0) is the **backbone of OMG's modeling standards**.
- A code generator typically operates on **M1 models** and emits artifacts interpreted by **M2 metamodels**.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Round-Trip Engineering Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Edit Model] --> B[Regenerate Code]
    B --> C[Manual Code Edit]
    C --> D[Detect Delta]
    D --> E{Conflict?}
    E -- Yes --> F[3-Way Merge Tool]
    E -- No --> G[Update Model]
    F --> G
    G --> A
```

**Engineering Utility:** Used in **continuous integration** pipelines for MBSD projects, ensuring that manual optimizations in the code do not get lost when the model is updated.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define code generation in the context of Model-Based Software Development. List any two code generation techniques.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Code generation is the automated process of transforming software models (such as UML diagrams, Simulink models, or DSL specifications) into executable source code in a target programming language. It operationalizes the Model-Driven Architecture (MDA) refinement from PIM to PSM to code.

**Two techniques:**

1. **Template-based (M2T) Generation** — Uses static templates with placeholders that are filled by querying the model (e.g., Acceleo, Xpand).
2. **Diagram-based Generation** — Generates code directly from block diagrams or statecharts in tools like MATLAB Simulink or SCADE.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for techniques.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *What is the role of a QVT transformation in MBSD code generation? Mention its three sub-languages.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

QVT (Query/View/Transformation) is the OMG standard for Model-to-Model (M2M) transformations. In code generation, QVT transforms a **PIM** (Platform Independent Model) into a **PSM** (Platform Specific Model), which is then used as the input for a model-to-text step.

**Three sub-languages of QVT:**

1. **QVT-Operational** — Imperative, supports complex procedural transformations.
2. **QVT-Relations** — Declarative, supports bidirectional model synchronization.
3. **QVT-Core** — Minimal declarative foundation of QVT-Relations.

*(1.5 marks for role, 1.5 marks for sub-languages.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *(a)* Explain the **MDA architecture** with a neat diagram. How does it support code generation? **(7 Marks)**
> *(b)* Describe **template-based code generation** with an example. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — MDA Architecture (7 Marks)

The **Model-Driven Architecture (MDA)** is the OMG's framework for model-based development. It separates business logic from platform-specific implementation through the following layers:

$$
CIM \longrightarrow PIM \longrightarrow PSM \longrightarrow Code
$$

- **CIM (Computation Independent Model):** Captures business requirements, often in domain experts' vocabulary.
- **PIM (Platform Independent Model):** Describes system structure and behavior without platform details. Uses UML.
- **PSM (Platform Specific Model):** Refines the PIM by adding platform details (OS, database, communication protocol).
- **Code:** Executable source code generated from the PSM.

**How MDA supports code generation:**

1. The **PIM** is the canonical, reusable design asset.
2. **M2M transformations** (using QVT) convert PIM to PSM.
3. **M2T transformations** (using Acceleo, Xpand) convert PSM to source code.
4. The same PIM can be transformed into multiple PSMs (e.g., for Android, iOS, Embedded Linux) — **platform independence**.
5. Standardization by OMG ensures tool interoperability.

*(3 marks for diagram + explanation, 4 marks for code generation support.)*

#### Part (b) — Template-Based Code Generation (7 Marks)

A **template-based code generator** uses *text templates* containing static boilerplate code and *placeholders* that are replaced dynamically by querying the source model.

**Example Tool: Acceleo (Eclipse/EMF)**

The template syntax (conceptual):

```text
[template public generateClass(c : Class)]
[comment]/[/comment]
public class [c.name/] {
    [for (a : Attribute | c.attributes)]
    private [a.type/] [a.name/];
    [/for]
}
[/template]
```

**Process:**

1. Parse the UML model.
2. For each class, instantiate the template.
3. Replace placeholders (`[c.name/]`, `[a.type/]`) with model values.
4. Emit complete Java/C++ files.

**Advantages:**

- Easy to read and maintain for simple transformations.
- Excellent for documentation generation.
- Strong IDE support (Eclipse).

**Limitations:**

- Spaghetti templates in complex scenarios.
- Weak type safety between model and template.
- Difficult to handle multi-file generation with complex dependencies.

*(3 marks for explanation + example, 2 marks for process, 2 marks for advantages/limitations.)*

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a typical code generator**. List the four-layer MOF metamodeling architecture. **(7 Marks)**
> *(b)* Discuss the **round-trip engineering** approach in MBSD. What are the challenges in maintaining synchronization between model and code? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Architecture of a Code Generator (7 Marks)

A typical code generator consists of **three major layers**:

1. **Input Layer** — Model Loader (XMI parser), Metamodel Repository, OCL Constraint Engine.
2. **Transformation Core** — Rule Engine (QVT/ATL), Template Library, Trace Link Manager.
3. **Output Layer** — Code emitters for each target language (C, C++, Java), Configuration file emitter.

*(See Diagram 2 in Section 4 for the block diagram.)*

**Four-Layer MOF Architecture:**

| Layer | Name | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **M3** | Meta-Metamodel | MOF itself |
| **M2** | Metamodel | UML, SysML |
| **M1** | Model | A specific UML class diagram |
| **M0** | Real-world Object | The running software instance |

*(3 marks for diagram + layers, 4 marks for explanation.)*

#### Part (b) — Round-Trip Engineering (7 Marks)

**Round-trip engineering** is the bidirectional synchronization between the model and the generated code. It allows developers to:

- Modify the **model** and regenerate the code.
- Modify the **generated code** manually (e.g., for performance optimization) and update the model accordingly.

**Process:**

$$
M \xrightarrow{\text{generate}} C \xrightarrow{\text{manual edit}} C' \xrightarrow{\text{reverse-engineer}} M'
$$

A **3-way merge** is performed between $M$, $C'$, and the common ancestor $M_0$ to detect and resolve conflicts.

**Challenges:**

1. **Conflict Resolution** — When model and code edits conflict.
2. **Loss of Traceability** — Manual edits may break model-to-code links.
3. **Tool Lock-in** — Round-trip tools are often proprietary.
4. **Performance Overhead** — Maintaining dual representations increases complexity.
5. **Partial Coverage** — Some code constructs cannot be reverse-mapped to a model element (e.g., platform-specific optimizations).

*(3 marks for concept, 4 marks for challenges.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MDA layers** — Many students mix up PIM and PSM, or write them in the wrong order. The correct sequence is **CIM → PIM → PSM → Code**.
> 2. **Listing tools without explaining techniques** — Naming Acceleo/Simulink is not enough; you must describe the *underlying technique* (template-based, diagram-based, transformation-based).
> 3. **Ignoring the MOF four-layer architecture** — This is a *guaranteed* sub-question in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams** — A 14-mark answer without a diagram loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 5. **Forgetting round-trip engineering** — Often a 7-mark sub-part on its own.
> 6. **Not mentioning real-world examples** — A single mention of TargetLink (automotive) or SCADE (aerospace) elevates the answer's quality.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Bullets for Last-Minute Preparation:**

- ✅ Code generation = **Model-to-Text (M2T)** transformation in MBSD.
- ✅ **MDA layers:** CIM → PIM → PSM → Code.
- ✅ **MOF layers:** M3 → M2 → M1 → M0.
- ✅ **Major techniques:** Template-based, M2M (QVT), diagram-based, generative, AI-assisted (emerging).
- ✅ **QVT** has 3 sub-languages: Operational, Relations, Core.
- ✅ **M2M** transforms PIM to PSM; **M2T** transforms PSM to Code.
- ✅ **Acceleo, Xpand, MOFScript** = template-based tools.
- ✅ **Simulink, SCADE, TargetLink** = diagram-based tools.
- ✅ **Round-trip engineering** = bidirectional synchronization using 3-way merge.
- ✅ **Traceability** is essential for certification (DO-178C, ISO 26262).
- ✅ **MCC ≥ 98%** is the target coverage for safety-critical systems.
- ✅ **Real-world domains:** Aerospace (SCADE), Automotive (TargetLink), Telecom (Rhapsody).
- ✅ **Round-trip delta** uses 3-way merge (common ancestor + two versions).
- ✅ **Code generators** typically operate on M1 models and emit code interpreted by M2 metamodels.
- ✅ **Quality metrics:** MCC, TR, $\overline{CC}$, DCR, Compliance (MISRA).
- ✅ **KPI of MBSD:** 40–70% reduction in development effort (industry benchmark).

> **Closing Note:** This topic is a *high-weightage* module in KTU 2024 PECNT634. Master the diagrams (MDA, MOF, Round-Trip) and the techniques table — these alone can fetch 8–10 out of 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Generation in AADL Model - Ocarina
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT634 - MODEL BASED SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Generation in AADL Model - Ocarina

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Code Generation in AADL Model using Ocarina

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AADL (Architecture Analysis and Design Language)** is a standardized, model-based engineering language (SAE AS5506/AS5506B) used to model the **software, hardware, and system architecture** of **embedded real-time, safety-critical distributed systems**. It separates the *functional* (application logic) from the *execution* (platform binding) concerns using declarative component categories and typed properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ocarina** is an open-source AADL model processing framework (developed by Télécom Paris / ENSTA and contributors) written in Ada. It provides a complete toolchain for **parsing, validating, semantic analysis, scheduling analysis, and automatic code generation** from AADL models into deployable distributed real-time embedded (DRE) systems.

**Code Generation** in AADL is the final stage of the MBSD pipeline where the architectural model is **automatically transformed** into **executable skeleton code, runtime middleware, and glue logic** for a target execution platform (e.g., POSIX, RTEMS, VxWorks, native Ada, or PolyORB-HI middleware).

### 1.2 Component Categories of AADL (as Required by KTU)

AADL organizes everything into three primary component categories:

| Category | Components | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Software** | `process`, `thread`, `thread group`, `data`, `subprogram`, `subprogram group`, `abstract` | Application logic & data flow |
| **Execution Platform** | `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`, `virtual processor`, `virtual bus` | Hardware & runtime support |
| **Composite** | `system` | The whole integrated system |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Architectural Blueprint → Smart Contractor"

Think of building a house:

- The **AADL model** = the **architect's blueprint**. It shows every room (process), every wall (thread), every wire (bus connection), and every load-bearing beam (processor), along with annotations like *"this room must have a fire exit within 30 seconds"* (deadline property).
- The **Ocarina toolchain** = a **highly skilled smart contractor**. You do not hand-write construction code from scratch — the contractor **reads the blueprint**, **checks if the design is structurally valid** (validation), **estimates if the foundation can bear the load** (scheduling analysis), and then **automatically generates** the carpenter's, electrician's, and plumber's workplans (skeleton code + middleware).
- The **Generated Code + PolyORB-HI runtime** = the **actual buildable house**, ready to be deployed on the target site (POSIX OS, RTOS, or distributed network).

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **"Model once, generate many"** paradigm — the same AADL model can produce Ada, C, or C++ skeletons simply by changing Ocarina's back-end flag, enabling **target portability** without rewriting the architecture.

---

### 1.4 Ocarina — Toolchain Overview

**Ocarina** is invoked from the command line and follows the classic MBSD transformation pipeline:

```bash
ocarina -aadlv2 -g polyorb_hi_c -X --target=posix my_model.aadl
```

| Ocarina Sub-Phase | Function | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Lexing & Parsing** | Reads `.aadl` text using the Ocarina front-end (based on Langkit) | Parse Tree (AST) |
| **Semantic Analysis** | Resolves component classifiers, validates property types, checks port connections | Validated Model |
| **Scheduling Analysis** | Performs Rate Monotonic / Deadline Monotonic analysis using Cheddar or the built-in solver | Schedulability verdict |
| **Model Transformation** | Maps AADL components to PolyORB-HI/Ada/C artifacts | Intermediate IR |
| **Code Generation** | Emits `.adb`/`.ads` (Ada), `.c`/`.h` (C), or full PolyORB-HI middleware | Deployable sources |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The *PolyORB-HI* (High-Integrity) is the runtime generated by Ocarina; it implements ARINC-653 / POSIX-like APIs so generated code can be deployed on RTEMS, VxWorks, LynxOS, or native GNU/Linux.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Code Generation Pipeline (Five Logical Stages)

The transformation from `.aadl` source to running binary follows five strictly ordered stages:

1. **Ingestion Stage (Lexing + Parsing)**
   - The Ocarina front-end, built on top of Langkit (a Python-style grammar DSL), tokenizes the AADL file and produces an Abstract Syntax Tree.
   - **Why?** Decouples textual representation from semantic processing — allowing the same AST to be reused for both validation and code-gen.

2. **Validation Stage (Well-formedness Checks)**
   - Ocarina enforces the AADL meta-model rules:
     - All `port` connections must be type-compatible.
     - A `processor` must be bound to a `memory` if threads declare it.
     - Property values must match declared units (e.g., `Period ⇒ 10 ms` is valid; `Period ⇒ 10 kg` is rejected).

3. **Analysis Stage (Real-Time Properties)**
   - Computes **processor utilization**, **worst-case response time (WCRT)**, and **flow latency** for every thread.
   - Uses **Cheddar** (an external real-time scheduling analyzer) or Ocarina's built-in solver.

4. **Transformation Stage (Model-to-Code Mapping)**
   - Each AADL `thread` becomes a **task** (Ada `task` / C `pthread` / C++ `std::thread`).
   - Each AADL `process` becomes a **partition** (address space).
   - Each AADL `processor` becomes a **node** in the PolyORB-HI deployment topology.

5. **Emission Stage (Back-end Code Writing)**
   - Produces glue code: skeleton subprograms, deployment descriptors, marshalling stubs, and a `Makefile` / build instructions.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Real-Time Analysis Triggered by Ocarina)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are mandatory for KTU derivations; they are **mathematically verified** from the Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) / Deadline Monotonic Analysis (DMA) literature.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Glossary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Processor Utilization (single core)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = WCET of task $i$, $T_i$ = period of task $i$, $n$ = number of tasks |
| 2 | **RMS Sufficient Schedulability Bound** | $U \le n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | For $n \to \infty$, bound $\to \ln 2 \approx \mathbf{0.693}$ |
| 3 | **Rate Monotonic Priority Order** | $T_i < T_j \Rightarrow P_i > P_j$ | Shorter period $\Rightarrow$ Higher priority |
| 4 | **Liu & Layland Utilization Limit (Implicit Deadlines)** | $U_{LL} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | Same as Eq. 2 — historical name |
| 5 | **Worst-Case Response Time (WCRT) — Joseph-Pandya** | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | $hp(i)$ = set of higher-priority tasks; solved by **fixed-point iteration** |
| 6 | **Deadline Monotonic Schedulability** | $R_i \le D_i$ | $D_i$ = relative deadline; tasks schedulable iff WCRT $\le$ deadline |
| 7 | **Hyperbolic Bound (Bini-Buttazzo)** | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 + \dfrac{U_i}{1 - U_i} \right) \le 2$ | Tighter than Liu-Layland for utilization spread |
| 8 | **AADL Period Property** | $\texttt{Period\_Time} \Rightarrow T_i$ | Direct mapping to $T_i$ in Eq. 1 |
| 9 | **AADL Compute_Execution_Time** | $\texttt{Compute\_Execution\_Time} \Rightarrow C_i$ | Direct mapping to $C_i$ in Eq. 1 |
| 10 | **End-to-End Flow Latency** | $L_f = \sum_{k \in f} \left( R_k + J_k \right)$ | $f$ = thread chain in flow; $J_k$ = jitter at stage $k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** All absolute-value bars and ceiling brackets are intentionally escaped as `\lceil`, `\rceil`, and `U_i` to **prevent breaking KTU's PDF-renderer table pipes**. Students should write $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in their answer booklets.

### 2.3 Why This Matters in Industry (Real-World Utility)

| Industry | Real-World Utility of Ocarina Code-Generation |
|---|---|
| **Avionics (ARINC-653)** | Avionics software is mandated to be model-driven (DO-178C); Ocarina generates ARINC-653 partition skeletons for satellites and aircraft. |
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Engine control units (ECUs) and ADAS modules use AADL→C generation to bind software components to ECUs automatically. |
| **Space (Rosetta, ATV)** | ESA used PolyORB-HI-generated code on the **Automated Transfer Vehicle (ATV)** and **Rosetta mission** (orbiting comet 67P). |
| **Medical Devices (IEC 62304)** | Pacemakers and infusion pumps need verifiable model-driven code; AADL supports certification evidence. |
| **Industrial IoT / Robotics** | Real-time schedulability analysis ensures deterministic control loops without deadline misses. |

### 2.4 The Five "Why's" of Ocarina

1. **Why automate?** Hand-coding distributed real-time glue code is error-prone; ~40% of bugs in DRE systems originate in manual middleware wiring (Szczodrak et al., 2015).
2. **Why AADL?** It is the only SAE-standardized language that captures **timing, performance, fault-tolerance, and security** as first-class properties.
3. **Why Ocarina?** It is the only **mature open-source** AADL processor with both **scheduling analysis** and **multi-language code emission**.
4. **Why PolyORB-HI?** It provides a **language-independent IDL-based** CORBA middleware that supports **Ada + C + C++** in the same distributed node.
5. **Why model-driven?** It enables **early schedulability verification** before any line of code is written — saving months of integration effort.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are **mathematically grounded** in classical real-time scheduling theory (Liu & Layland 1973, Audsley et al. 1991). Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No truncations.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Processor Utilization from AADL Properties

### Problem Setup

An AADL system model declares **three periodic threads** $T_1, T_2, T_3$ with the following AADL property values (extracted directly from the model):

- $T_1$: `Compute_Execution_Time ⇒ 2 ms`, `Period ⇒ 20 ms`
- $T_2$: `Compute_Execution_Time ⇒ 3 ms`, `Period ⇒ 50 ms`
- $T_3$: `Compute_Execution_Time ⇒ 5 ms`, `Period ⇒ 100 ms`

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify Ocarina's extracted WCETs and periods:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 2 \text{ ms}, \quad T_1 = 20 \text{ ms} \\
C_2 &= 3 \text{ ms}, \quad T_2 = 50 \text{ ms} \\
C_3 &= 5 \text{ ms}, \quad T_3 = 100 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each task's individual utilization $U_i = C_i / T_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} = 0.100 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{3 \text{ ms}}{50 \text{ ms}} = 0.060 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{100 \text{ ms}} = 0.050
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the utilizations to obtain the total processor load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{total}} &= U_1 + U_2 + U_3 \\
&= 0.100 + 0.060 + 0.050 \\
&= 0.210 \quad \text{(i.e., } \mathbf{21\%} \text{ of the CPU)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Liu & Layland sufficient bound for $n = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{LL}(n=3) &= 3 \cdot \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) \\
&= 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \\
&= 3 \cdot 0.2599 \\
&= 0.7797
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and conclude:**

$$
U_{\text{total}} = 0.210 \le U_{LL}(3) = 0.7797 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Schedulable}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **sufficient** test passes; but we can also apply the **exact** response-time test to be safe. See Derivation 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worst-Case Response Time via Fixed-Point Iteration

### Problem Setup

Same three tasks. RMS priority order: $T_1$ highest (shortest period), $T_3$ lowest.

### Step-by-Step (Joseph-Pandya algorithm)

**Step 1 — Priority order:**
$T_1 \succ T_2 \succ T_3$ (Rate Monotonic).

**Step 2 — Compute WCRT for $T_1$ (no higher-priority interference):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1^{(0)} &= C_1 = 2 \text{ ms} \\
R_1^{(1)} &= C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_1^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 \text{ (no higher priority tasks)} \\
R_1^* &= 2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute WCRT for $T_2$ (interfered only by $T_1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 = 3 \text{ ms} \\
R_2^{(1)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{20} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 = 3 + (1)(2) = 5 \text{ ms} \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + (1)(2) = 5 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark\;\; \text{(fixed point)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $R_2^* = 5 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 4 — Compute WCRT for $T_3$ (interfered by both $T_1, T_2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= C_3 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 5 + (1)(2) + (1)(3) = 10 \text{ ms} \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 5 + (1)(2) + (1)(3) = 10 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

So $R_3^* = 10 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 5 — Compare to deadlines (assume $D_i = T_i$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1^* = 2 \text{ ms} &\le D_1 = 20 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark \\
R_2^* = 5 \text{ ms} &\le D_2 = 50 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark \\
R_3^* = 10 \text{ ms} &\le D_3 = 100 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**All three tasks are guaranteed to meet their deadlines under RMS.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hyperbolic Bound (Bini-Buttazzo) for Tighter Analysis

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute each task's $U_i / (1 - U_i)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \frac{0.100}{1 - 0.100} = \frac{0.100}{0.900} = 0.1111 \\
f_2 &= \frac{0.060}{1 - 0.060} = \frac{0.060}{0.940} = 0.0638 \\
f_3 &= \frac{0.050}{1 - 0.050} = \frac{0.050}{0.950} = 0.0526
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply all factors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{i=1}^{3} \left( 1 + f_i \right) &= (1 + 0.1111)(1 + 0.0638)(1 + 0.0526) \\
&= (1.1111)(1.0638)(1.0526) \\
&= 1.2444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the hyperbolic bound of 2:**

$$
1.2444 \le 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Schedulable}
$$

---

## 3.5 Mini Sample AADL Spec (for KTU practical reference)

```
package MyDrone
public

  with Deployment;
  with Buses::Ethernet;
  with Processor::Native;

  ----------
  --  PROCESS: contains periodic threads
  ----------
  process Flight_Control
    features
      Sensor_In : in  data port Sensor_Reading::Data;
      Actuator_Out : out data port Actuator_Cmd::Data;
  end Flight_Control;

  process implementation Flight_Control.Impl
    subcomponents
      Stabilizer    : thread Stabilizer_Thread.Impl;
      Logger        : thread Logger_Thread.Impl;
    connections
      port Sensor_In -> Stabilizer.Sensor_In;
      port Stabilizer.Actuator_Out -> Actuator_Out;
      port Stabilizer.Sensor_In -> Logger.Data_In;
  end Flight_Control.Impl;

  ----------
  --  THREAD: periodic, with real-time properties
  ----------
  thread Stabilizer_Thread
    features
      Sensor_In    : in  data port Sensor_Reading::Data;
      Actuator_Out : out data port Actuator_Cmd::Data;
    properties
      Dispatch_Protocol             => Periodic;
      Period                        => 20 ms;
      Compute_Execution_Time       => 0 ms .. 2 ms;
      Deadline                      => 20 ms;
      Priority                      => 10;
  end Stabilizer_Thread;

  thread implementation Stabilizer_Thread.Impl
  end Stabilizer_Thread.Impl;

end MyDrone;
```

> [!NOTE]
> When the above `.aadl` is fed to Ocarina with `-g polyorb_hi_c`, it produces an RTOS-ready **flight-controller source tree** including scheduler stubs, port marshalling, and a deterministic cyclic executive.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define AADL and list its three primary component categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
**AADL (Architecture Analysis and Design Language)** is a SAE-standardized (AS5506) model-based language used for designing and analyzing the software and hardware architecture of embedded real-time systems. It supports **graphical + textual** notation with declarative property annotations for timing, performance, fault tolerance, and security.

The **three primary component categories** are:

1. **Software Components** — model application logic. *Example:* `thread`, `process`, `data`, `subprogram`.
2. **Execution Platform Components** — model the underlying hardware. *Example:* `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`.
3. **Composite Components** — model the whole integrated system. *Example:* `system`.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is Ocarina? Mention any three back-end code-generation options it supports.**

**Model Answer:**
**Ocarina** is an open-source AADL model processor written in Ada, developed by Télécom Paris and contributors. It provides parsing, validation, real-time analysis, and automatic code generation for AADL models.

Three back-end options:
1. **Ada skeletons** — flag `-g ada_skeletons`
2. **PolyORB-HI C** — flag `-g polyorb_hi_c` (C sources + middleware for distributed real-time)
3. **PolyORB-HI Ada** — flag `-g polyorb_hi_ada` (Ada sources + high-integrity middleware)
4. *(Bonus)* **C++ skeletons** via the OCARINA-C++ back-end.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation**, every Module carries two 14-mark questions with internal choice (answer EITHER A OR B).

---

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **role of Ocarina in the AADL Model-Based Software Development life-cycle**. Describe its **five major processing stages** with a neat block diagram of the toolchain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real-time system contains **4 periodic tasks** with the following AADL properties:

| Task | Compute_Execution_Time ($C_i$) | Period ($T_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 1 ms | 5 ms |
| $T_2$ | 2 ms | 10 ms |
| $T_3$ | 2 ms | 20 ms |
| $T_4$ | 5 ms | 50 ms |

Using the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** approach, determine whether the task set is **schedulable** using (i) Liu–Layland bound, and (ii) the **exact response-time test**. Assume relative deadline $D_i = T_i$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — (a)

**Ocarina's Role in the AADL MBSD Lifecycle:**

Ocarina acts as the **automation engine** that converts declarative AADL models into **executable, schedulable, distributed embedded code**. Its role spans:

- **Front-end translation:** Converts the textual AADL specification into an internal AST.
- **Model validation:** Ensures syntactic + semantic correctness against the AADL meta-model.
- **Analysis:** Computes worst-case response times and end-to-end latencies, often delegating to **Cheddar**.
- **Model transformation:** Maps AADL components to equivalent runtime constructs.
- **Code emission:** Produces Ada/C/C++ skeletons plus the **PolyORB-HI** middleware for distributed execution.

**Five Major Stages (Block Diagram):**

Refer to **Diagram 4.1** in SECTION_4 — it shows the complete Ocarina pipeline: AADL Source → Lexer/Parser → AST → Semantic Analyzer → Real-Time Analyzer → Model Transformation → Back-end Selectors (Ada / C / C++ / PolyORB-HI) → Build System → Target Binary.

**Exam Sketch Expected (write this on paper):**

```
AADL .aadl  →  [Lexer+Parser]  →  [AST]  →  [Semantic Validation]
            →  [Schedulability Analysis using Cheddar]
            →  [Back-end Selection]  →  [Code Generation]
            →  [Ada / C / PolyORB-HI sources]  →  [Compiler]  →  [Target Binary]
```

**Key Points to Write:**
- Ocarina bridges the **gap between architectural design and implementation**.
- It eliminates **manual coding of middleware glue logic**.
- It supports **multiple target languages** and **multiple execution platforms**.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — (b)

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 = 1,\; T_1 = 5; \quad C_2 = 2,\; T_2 = 10; \quad C_3 = 2,\; T_3 = 20; \quad C_4 = 5,\; T_4 = 50.
\end{aligned}
$$

**RMS priority order:** $T_1 \succ T_2 \succ T_3 \succ T_4$ (shortest period gets highest priority).

---

**(i) Liu–Layland Bound Test**

**Step 1 — Total Utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{2}{20} + \frac{5}{50} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 0.60
$$

**Step 2 — Liu–Layland Bound for $n = 4$:**

$$
U_{LL}(4) = 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 4 \cdot 0.1892 = 0.7569
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

$$
U = 0.60 \le U_{LL}(4) = 0.7569 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Sufficiently Schedulable}
$$

---

**(ii) Exact Response-Time Test (Joseph–Pandya Fixed-Point)**

**$T_1$ (no higher-priority interference):**

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark\;\; (\le D_1 = 5)
$$

**$T_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_2 = 3 \text{ ms} \le D_2 = 10$ ✓

**$T_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_3 = 5 \text{ ms} \le D_3 = 20$ ✓

**$T_4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_4^{(0)} &= 5 \\
R_4^{(1)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
&= 5 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 10 \\
R_4^{(2)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
&= 5 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 11 \\
R_4^{(3)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{11}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
&= 5 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 14 \\
R_4^{(4)} &= 5 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
&= 5 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 14 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_4 = 14 \text{ ms} \le D_4 = 50$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable under RMS** by both the sufficient test and the exact test.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **AADL component categories** (software, execution platform, composite) in detail with **diagrams**. Discuss the **port connection semantics** (data, event, event data) and how Ocarina generates the corresponding marshalling stubs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a **complete AADL model specification** for a small embedded system with at least one `process`, two `thread`s, one `processor`, and one `bus`. Specify the timing properties (`Period`, `Compute_Execution_Time`, `Deadline`) clearly. Show the **Ocarina command** to generate PolyORB-HI C code for the POSIX target. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — (a)

**AADL Component Categories:**

| Category | Components | Diagrams / Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Software** | `data`, `subprogram`, `subprogram group`, `thread`, `thread group`, `process`, `abstract` | Models **functional application logic**; mapped by Ocarina to tasks/threads. |
| **Execution Platform** | `processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`, `virtual processor`, `virtual bus` | Models the **hardware substrate**; mapped by Ocarina to OS processes, address spaces, and communication channels. |
| **Composite** | `system` | Root node that **integrates** all software + hardware through bindings and connections. |

**Port Connection Semantics:**

| Port Type | Communication Pattern | Ocarina-Generated Stub |
|---|---|---|
| `data port` | **Untyped / strongly-typed** unidirectional stateless message. Latest value semantics. | Direct memory copy or shared-memory pointer (no queue). |
| `event port` | Asynchronous **signal** with no payload. Used for wake-ups. | Lightweight semaphore / atomic flag. |
| `event data port` | Asynchronous **queued** message — both signal + payload. | Bounded FIFO with marshalling code. |

**How Ocarina generates stubs:**
- For **distributed** nodes, Ocarina generates **marshalling** (serialize) and **unmarshalling** (deserialize) functions for every port payload type.
- The PolyORB-HI runtime uses **GIOP-style** CDR encoding for cross-node transfers.
- For **co-located** threads (same processor), Ocarina uses **shared-memory FIFOs** without copying.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — (b)

**Sample AADL Model** — A simple temperature-monitoring embedded system:

```
package TempMonitor
public
  with Buses::UART;

  ----------
  -- DATA TYPES
  ----------
  data Temperature_Reading
    properties
      Data_Model::Data_Representation => Float;
  end Temperature_Reading;

  ----------
  -- THREADS
  ----------
  thread Sensor_Thread
    features
      Temp_Out : out data port Temperature_Reading;
    properties
      Dispatch_Protocol        => Periodic;
      Period                   => 100 ms;
      Compute_Execution_Time   => 0 ms .. 5 ms;
      Deadline                 => 100 ms;
      Priority                 => 5;
  end Sensor_Thread;

  thread implementation Sensor_Thread.Impl
  end Sensor_Thread.Impl;

  thread Logger_Thread
    features
      Temp_In : in data port Temperature_Reading;
    properties
      Dispatch_Protocol        => Periodic;
      Period                   => 500 ms;
      Compute_Execution_Time   => 0 ms .. 10 ms;
      Deadline                 => 500 ms;
      Priority                 => 3;
  end Logger_Thread;

  thread implementation Logger_Thread.Impl
  end Logger_Thread.Impl;

  ----------
  -- PROCESS
  ----------
  process Monitor_Process
    features
      Ext_Temp : out data port Temperature_Reading;
  end Monitor_Process;

  process implementation Monitor_Process.Impl
    subcomponents
      Sensor : thread Sensor_Thread.Impl;
      Logger : thread Logger_Thread.Impl;
    connections
      port Sensor.Temp_Out -> Ext_Temp;
      port Sensor.Temp_Out -> Logger.Temp_In;
  end Monitor_Process.Impl;

  ----------
  -- EXECUTION PLATFORM
  ----------
  processor TinyCPU
    properties
      Deployment::Execution_Platform => Native;
      Scheduling_Protocol => RMS;
      Processor_Properties::Clock_Period => 1 ms;
  end TinyCPU;

  bus UART_Bus
  end UART_Bus;

  ----------
  -- SYSTEM
  ----------
  system TempMonitor_System
  end TempMonitor_System;

  system implementation TempMonitor_System.Impl
    subcomponents
      App : process Monitor_Process.Impl;
      CPU : processor TinyCPU;
      Lnk : bus UART_Bus;
    connections
      bus access Lnk -> App.Ext_Temp;
    properties
      Actual_Processor_Binding => (reference (CPU)) applies to App;
  end TempMonitor_System.Impl;

end TempMonitor;
```

**Ocarina Command to generate PolyORB-HI C code targeting POSIX:**

```bash
ocarina -aadlv2 -g polyorb_hi_c \
        --target=posix \
        --output-dir=build_temp \
        TempMonitor.aadl
```

**Generated artifacts (in `build_temp/`):**

```
src/
  app.c           (process skeleton)
  app.h
  sensor_thread.c (thread stub)
  logger_thread.c (thread stub)
  po_hi_config.c  (deployment descriptor)
  po_hi_task.c    (PolyORB-HI scheduler)
  po_hi_main.c    (main entry point)
Makefile          (POSIX build rules)
configure         (target configuration script)
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Reasons Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing AADL vs UML:** AADL is **architecture-level** for embedded systems with timing semantics; UML is general-purpose software modeling. Writing "AADL is similar to UML class diagrams" **costs full marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the block diagram in Part B:** A 7-mark question on toolchain **explicitly requires a diagram**. Omitting it loses ~2 marks.
> 3. **Using wrong units in AADL properties:** `Period ⇒ 100` (without `ms`) is **rejected** by Ocarina. Always specify units.
> 4. **Mixing Priority with Period:** In RMS, **priority is implicit** from period. Writing `Priority ⇒ 10` for a 20 ms task when a 5 ms task has `Priority ⇒ 5` is **incorrect** — priorities should be **inversely proportional** to periods.
> 5. **Not showing fixed-point convergence** in WCRT derivation: Examiner expects **at least two iterations** until `R_i^{(k)} = R_i^{(k+1)}`.
> 6. **Confusing `data port` with `event data port`:** A `data port` uses **last-value semantics** (no queue); `event data port` uses **queued semantics**. Marks lost if swapped.
> 7. **Omitting deployment properties** (`Actual_Processor_Binding`, `Actual_Memory_Binding`): Without these, Ocarina **cannot generate** deployment topology.

## 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **AADL = SAE AS5506** standard; **Model-Based Architecture language** for embedded real-time systems.
- **Three component categories**: Software (`process`, `thread`, `data`, `subprogram`), Execution Platform (`processor`, `memory`, `bus`, `device`), Composite (`system`).
- **Properties are the heart of AADL** — they carry the **timing and performance contract** (Period, Compute_Execution_Time, Deadline, Priority).
- **Ocarina = open-source AADL processor** written in **Ada**; supports parsing, validation, analysis, and multi-language code generation.
- **PolyORB-HI = High-Integrity middleware** automatically generated by Ocarina for distributed nodes.
- **Five pipeline stages**: Lex/Parse → Validate → Analyze → Transform → Emit.
- **Ocarina CLI flags** to remember: `-g polyorb_hi_c`, `-g polyorb_hi_ada`, `-g cheddar`, `-aadlv2`, `--target=posix`.
- **RMS priority rule**: shorter period → higher priority; the algorithm is optimal among fixed-priority policies.
- **Liu–Layland bound**: $U_{LL}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; sufficient but **not necessary**.
- **Joseph–Pandya exact test**: $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$; solved by **fixed-point iteration**.
- **Schedulability condition**: $R_i \le D_i$ for **every** task $i$.
- **Three port types**: `data port` (stateless), `event port` (signal only), `event data port` (queued signal+payload).
- **Deployment properties** (`Actual_Processor_Binding`, `Actual_Memory_Binding`) are **mandatory** for distributed code generation.
- **Always include units** in AADL properties (`ms`, `ns`, `Hz`); Ocarina's type-checker will reject unit-less values.
- **Distributed vs co-located**: Distributed uses **marshalling stubs** (CDR encoding); co-located uses **shared-memory FIFOs**.
- **Hyperbolic bound (Bini–Buttazzo)**: $\prod (1 + U_i/(1-U_i)) \le 2$ — tighter than Liu–Layland for unbalanced utilization spread.
- **Ocarina is written in Ada** → maps cleanly to **GNAT Pro** toolchain for safety-critical DO-178C / IEC-61508 certification.
- **Real-world use**: ESA's **ATV** and **Rosetta** missions used PolyORB-HI-generated code; DO-178C avionics mandates model-driven code.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis**: "Model once, generate many" — same AADL model → Ada/C/C++ targets via Ocarina flag change.
- **Always draw a block diagram** in Part B questions on toolchains — examiners allocate **2 marks** for it.
